newspapers.lede: Today in Salem Village, a clear line was drawn in the sand. Following last month’s contentious election, the new committee – comprised of John Porter, Thomas Hutchinson, Samuel Andrew, Francis Nurse, and Joseph Putnam – immediately began to dismantle Reverend Samuel Parris’s financial and practical support. They refused to authorize the collection of taxes meant to fund Parris’s salary, relying instead on voluntary contributions that yielded little. More dramatically, they denied Parris firewood, leaving both his family and the meetinghouse vulnerable to the approaching winter chill. As one committee member reportedly stated, though not for public record, their intent was to “make him feel the weight of our displeasure.” The parsonage, and the church itself, are already growing cold. This isn’t simply a dispute over finances; it’s a deliberate attempt to isolate and control the minister, and it comes at a time when Parris is increasingly warning his congregation about internal threats and covenant-breaking.
newspapers.summary: Today, the newly elected committee in Salem Village took direct action against Reverend Samuel Parris, withholding his salary and, crucially, firewood for both the meetinghouse and his own home. This economic pressure, applied just as winter descends, creates a climate of hostility and sets the stage for the escalating tensions that will soon grip the village.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today, the newly elected committee in Salem Village took direct action against Reverend Samuel Parris, withholding his salary and, crucially, firewood for both the meetinghouse and his own home. This economic pressure, applied just as winter descends, creates a climate of hostility and sets the stage for the escalating tensions that will soon grip the village.

Today in Salem Village, a clear line was drawn in the sand. Following last month’s contentious election, the new committee – comprised of John Porter, Thomas Hutchinson, Samuel Andrew, Francis Nurse, and Joseph Putnam – immediately began to dismantle Reverend Samuel Parris’s financial and practical support. They refused to authorize the collection of taxes meant to fund Parris’s salary, relying instead on voluntary contributions that yielded little. More dramatically, they denied Parris firewood, leaving both his family and the meetinghouse vulnerable to the approaching winter chill. As one committee member reportedly stated, though not for public record, their intent was to “make him feel the weight of our displeasure.” The parsonage, and the church itself, are already growing cold. This isn’t simply a dispute over finances; it’s a deliberate attempt to isolate and control the minister, and it comes at a time when Parris is increasingly warning his congregation about internal threats and covenant-breaking.

The newly elected committee today enacted a three-pronged economic siege against Reverend Samuel Parris. They refused to collect taxes for his salary, withheld firewood from both the meetinghouse and the parsonage, and asserted the village’s ownership of the ministry house and land – effectively denying Parris full control of his living quarters.

The withholding of firewood is a particularly severe blow, given the approaching winter. Without fuel, Parris’s family faces genuine hardship, and conducting worship services in a freezing meetinghouse will be nearly impossible.

The committee’s actions are deeply personal for some. The Putnams, for example, view Francis Nurse and Joseph Putnam as key instigators, stemming from long-standing land disputes and family rivalries.

Reverend Parris has been preaching increasingly apocalyptic sermons in recent weeks, focusing on themes of broken covenants and enemies within the congregation. He sees the actions of the committee as a direct attack on the spiritual well-being of the village.

The combination of economic pressure and Parris’s heightened rhetoric creates a volatile atmosphere within Salem Village, priming the community for conflict.

While no accusations have been made, the stage is being set for a crisis, as the lines of division harden and resentment deepens within the community.

newspapers.lede: Today, Reverend Samuel Parris preached a sermon that, while rooted in a local dispute, took on a decidedly ominous tone. The minister, facing opposition from a growing faction within Salem Village who have challenged his salary, framed the disagreement as a spiritual battle. Parris argued that those who withheld support from the ministry were not simply political opponents, but “enemies of the covenant” actively inviting God’s wrath upon the community. He drew upon the words of the Psalmist, declaring that God will ultimately vindicate His servant, but warned that judgment would fall upon those who stood against him. As the magistrate’s record of the sermon reveals, Parris passionately asserted, “I feel it in the air. I smell it on the wind. There is something dark gathering at the edges of this village." Thomas Putnam Jr. sat in the front row, visibly nodding in agreement, while Joseph Porter remained stoic, a clear sign of the deepening divisions within the village. This sermon, delivered two months before the first accusations of witchcraft, laid the groundwork for a climate of fear and suspicion that would soon engulf Salem Village.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a powerful sermon, “The Enemies of the Covenant,” directly linking a recent dispute over his salary to a broader spiritual crisis. He warned of a gathering curse upon the village, setting a stage that will tragically resonate in the months to come. This sermon established a framework of internal enemies and divine retribution.

He warned of a gathering curse upon the village, setting a stage that will tragically resonate in the months to come. This sermon established a framework of internal enemies and divine retribution.

This sermon, delivered two months before the first accusations of witchcraft, laid the groundwork for a climate of fear and suspicion that would soon engulf Salem Village.

Reverend Samuel Parris delivered his sermon, “The Enemies of the Covenant,” today, explicitly connecting the recent dispute over his salary to a larger spiritual threat. He characterized those who opposed his financial support as “enemies of the covenant” and warned of divine retribution.

Parris drew heavily on scripture, specifically Psalm 110:1, to justify his position, stating, “The LORD said unto my Lord, Sit thou at my right hand, until I make thine enemies thy footstool.” He applied this passage to his own situation, suggesting God would ultimately vindicate his ministry.

The sermon created a palpable tension within the meetinghouse, visibly affecting members of both factions. Thomas Putnam Jr. actively signaled his support, while Joseph Porter remained detached and unyielding.

Ann Putnam Sr. appeared deeply disturbed by Parris’s warnings about a gathering curse, her anxieties amplified by personal tragedies and the uncertain times.

Mercy Lewis, a young woman who experienced trauma from a frontier raid, seems to have connected Parris’s talk of “enemies” to the dangers she knew from the wilderness, internalizing a sense of fear.

Abigail Williams, Parris’s niece, observed the sermon with intense focus, absorbing the atmosphere of anger and perceived threat within the parsonage.

Francis Nurse, while concerned for the well-being of the community, appeared wary of Parris’s escalating rhetoric and the potential for division.

The sermon significantly polarized the village, strengthening the resolve of Parris’s supporters while further entrenching his opponents and solidifying the lines of conflict.

newspapers.lede: The struggle for control in Salem Village took a sharp turn today as the parish committee, recently reshaped by an anti-Parris faction, officially refused to provide firewood to the meetinghouse and the parsonage. This isn't simply a dispute over resources; it’s a calculated attempt to isolate and diminish Reverend Samuel Parris. The committee – consisting of men like John Porter, Thomas Hutchinson, and Joseph Putnam – effectively signaled their opposition by ensuring the Parris family and the entire congregation would face a frigid winter. Parris is clearly aware of the individuals responsible, harboring resentment toward those he sees as actively working against him. This cold shoulder comes as the Parris household is already experiencing internal strife. As one observer noted of similar disputes, “a lack of charity doth breed contention,” and the situation here feels increasingly fraught. Compounding these difficulties, a heated exchange between Sarah Good and the Samuel Abbey family has left a trail of bitterness and accusations of curses – a grim omen as winter descends.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, tensions are escalating around Reverend Samuel Parris. A newly elected committee is actively undermining his authority by withholding his salary and, critically, firewood during the onset of winter. Meanwhile, a dispute with a local woman, Sarah Good, reveals simmering resentment and accusations within the community.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, tensions are escalating around Reverend Samuel Parris. A newly elected committee is actively undermining his authority by withholding his salary and, critically, firewood during the onset of winter. Meanwhile, a dispute with a local woman, Sarah Good, reveals simmering resentment and accusations within the community.

The struggle for control in Salem Village took a sharp turn today as the parish committee, recently reshaped by an anti-Parris faction, officially refused to provide firewood to the meetinghouse and the parsonage. This isn't simply a dispute over resources; it’s a calculated attempt to isolate and diminish Reverend Samuel Parris. The committee – consisting of men like John Porter, Thomas Hutchinson, and Joseph Putnam – effectively signaled their opposition by ensuring the Parris family and the entire congregation would face a frigid winter. Parris is clearly aware of the individuals responsible, harboring resentment toward those he sees as actively working against him. This cold shoulder comes as the Parris household is already experiencing internal strife. As one observer noted of similar disputes, “a lack of charity doth breed contention,” and the situation here feels increasingly fraught. Compounding these difficulties, a heated exchange between Sarah Good and the Samuel Abbey family has left a trail of bitterness and accusations of curses – a grim omen as winter descends.

The newly formed village committee has effectively launched an assault on Reverend Parris’s authority, refusing to deliver firewood to the meetinghouse and parsonage. This action, taken during the onset of a harsh New England winter, is intended to disrupt services and make life unbearable for the Parris family, and it follows the committee’s earlier decision to withhold Parris’s salary.

A hostile confrontation occurred today between Sarah Good and the Samuel Abbey family. After briefly taking in the Goods, the Abbeys evicted Sarah, accusing her of being “a turbulent spirit, spiteful and so maliciously bent.” The Abbeys then reported that their cattle fell ill and died, which they attribute to curses uttered by Good upon her departure.

The committee members responsible for withholding support from Reverend Parris are known to him: John Porter, Thomas Hutchinson, Andrew, John Nurse, and Joseph Putnam collectively made the decision. Parris is reportedly dwelling on their names and actions with growing animosity.

This withholding of resources is occurring concurrently with the early stages of distress within the Parris household. While the exact nature of those difficulties remains unclear, they predate the more dramatic events to come in January.

The village is deeply divided, and the actions of the committee demonstrate a willingness to weaponize basic necessities to achieve political ends. This creates a climate of fear and suspicion.

newspapers.lede: The year opens with a deceptively peaceful scene in Salem Village. Snow blankets the fields, and the community settles into the quiet rhythm of winter. But appearances can be deceiving. Inside the parsonage, Reverend Samuel Parris is deeply troubled by the strange afflictions of his daughter Betty and niece Abigail. He calls for Doctor Griggs, a man of limited training who diagnoses what he calls “the Evil Hand.” Meanwhile, the household of Ann Putnam Sr. carries a heavy burden of grief, and her husband, Giles Corey, a recent convert to the church, is already known for his contentious nature. As the magistrate’s record will later show, the timing of these events is significant. Winter’s lull allows people the time to attend examinations, creating a spectacle that will soon consume the village. Even now, a visitor to the Putnam home describes a woman moving through her house “like someone navigating by memory alone — the motions correct, but the spirit behind them fading.” The seeds of fear and suspicion are being sown, and the long winter months offer fertile ground for them to grow.
newspapers.summary: Today marks the start of a new year in Salem Village, but beneath the quiet winter landscape, tensions are building. The Putnam and Parris families are grappling with personal hardships, while the community faces lingering anxieties from recent frontier conflicts. Unseen currents are at play, setting the stage for a crisis no one yet anticipates.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks the start of a new year in Salem Village, but beneath the quiet winter landscape, tensions are building. The Putnam and Parris families are grappling with personal hardships, while the community faces lingering anxieties from recent frontier conflicts. Unseen currents are at play, setting the stage for a crisis no one yet anticipates.

The year opens with a deceptively peaceful scene in Salem Village. Snow blankets the fields, and the community settles into the quiet rhythm of winter. But appearances can be deceiving. Inside the parsonage, Reverend Samuel Parris is deeply troubled by the strange afflictions of his daughter Betty and niece Abigail. He calls for Doctor Griggs, a man of limited training who diagnoses what he calls “the Evil Hand.” Meanwhile, the household of Ann Putnam Sr. carries a heavy burden of grief, and her husband, Giles Corey, a recent convert to the church, is already known for his contentious nature. As the magistrate’s record will later show, the timing of these events is significant. Winter’s lull allows people the time to attend examinations, creating a spectacle that will soon consume the village. Even now, a visitor to the Putnam home describes a woman moving through her house “like someone navigating by memory alone — the motions correct, but the spirit behind them fading.” The seeds of fear and suspicion are being sown, and the long winter months offer fertile ground for them to grow.

The crisis timeline is already taking shape, tied to the agricultural year. The coming months, with limited farming possible, will allow for widespread attendance at examinations and trials, turning the meetinghouse into a center of daily spectacle.

Ann Putnam Sr. is struggling with profound grief and poor health, barely managing her household with the help of Mercy Lewis and her children. Her increasing frailty makes her particularly vulnerable to the anxieties brewing in the village.

Giles Corey, recently joined the church, is a difficult man with a history – a history that includes a violent incident from years past. His wife Martha is already voicing skepticism about the explanations for the girls’ afflictions, a dangerous position to take.

John Proctor, a practical farmer and tavern keeper, is observing the early signs of hysteria with skepticism. He believes the afflicted girls should be disciplined, and his observations – particularly regarding his servant Mary Warren – could challenge the emerging narrative.

Mary Warren finds herself caught between the world of the afflicted girls and the pragmatic household of John Proctor, oscillating between fear and defiance. Her divided loyalties will likely play a crucial role in the unfolding events.

Mercy Lewis, hardened by the destruction of Falmouth, is a watchful and competent servant in the Putnam household. Her past trauma contributes to a constant state of alertness and likely fuels her involvement in the coming accusations.

Rebecca Nurse, a respected elder in the community, lives a quiet life of faith and devotion. She remains unaware of the storm gathering around her, a symbol of the innocence that will soon be shattered.

The colonial prison system in Boston is harsh and unforgiving, placing a significant financial burden on the families of the accused. Those without resources face the prospect of prolonged imprisonment and deteriorating conditions.

newspapers.lede: Winter continues to grip Salem Village, but beneath the quiet lies a growing unease. Reverend Francis Dane of Andover has sent forth a statement—essentially a letter of attestation—attempting to clear the names of individuals accused of witchcraft in his parish. He insists the accusations are unfounded and born of “Scandalous” reports. Dane acknowledges a prior suspicion regarding one woman, Goodwife Carrier, but vehemently denies knowledge of any witchcraft or harmful practices among his neighbors. He states, “I have lived above Fortie fower yeares in the Towne, and have been frequent among the Inhabitants, and should certainely heard if so it had been.” This defense comes at a critical moment, as rumors and suspicions begin to spread beyond isolated incidents. Simultaneously, the town of Ipswich completed the selection of its grand jury, men who will soon travel to Salem to participate in the court proceedings. As the magistrate’s record from Ipswich shows, these jurors were “Unanimously Chosen” and formally warned to appear at the court on Tuesday next. The formalization of the legal process signals a shift from individual accusations to a more structured, and potentially more dangerous, phase of inquiry.
newspapers.summary: Today in 1692, Reverend Francis Dane of Andover publicly defends the character of his townspeople against what he calls “Scandalous” reports, while in Ipswich, a grand jury is selected to serve at the upcoming court in Salem. These events, though seemingly separate, reflect growing anxieties and the formalizing of legal processes as tensions rise in the region.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in 1692, Reverend Francis Dane of Andover publicly defends the character of his townspeople against what he calls “Scandalous” reports, while in Ipswich, a grand jury is selected to serve at the upcoming court in Salem. These events, though seemingly separate, reflect growing anxieties and the formalizing of legal processes as tensions rise in the region.

Winter continues to grip Salem Village, but beneath the quiet lies a growing unease. Reverend Francis Dane of Andover has sent forth a statement—essentially a letter of attestation—attempting to clear the names of individuals accused of witchcraft in his parish. He insists the accusations are unfounded and born of “Scandalous” reports. Dane acknowledges a prior suspicion regarding one woman, Goodwife Carrier, but vehemently denies knowledge of any witchcraft or harmful practices among his neighbors. He states, “I have lived above Fortie fower yeares in the Towne, and have been frequent among the Inhabitants, and should certainely heard if so it had been.” This defense comes at a critical moment, as rumors and suspicions begin to spread beyond isolated incidents. Simultaneously, the town of Ipswich completed the selection of its grand jury, men who will soon travel to Salem to participate in the court proceedings. As the magistrate’s record from Ipswich shows, these jurors were “Unanimously Chosen” and formally warned to appear at the court on Tuesday next. The formalization of the legal process signals a shift from individual accusations to a more structured, and potentially more dangerous, phase of inquiry.

Reverend Francis Dane has publicly defended the people of Andover, asserting that reports of witchcraft there are “Scandalous and unjust.” He states he has lived among them for over forty years and heard no such tales until recently, suggesting a recent escalation of claims.

The town of Ipswich has selected and notified its grand jury, preparing them to serve at the upcoming court in Salem. This demonstrates a growing willingness among neighboring communities to participate in the legal investigation of alleged witchcraft.

Dane’s statement reveals that even before official accusations, there was some local suspicion directed at Goodwife Carrier. This suggests that anxieties about witchcraft were not entirely new, but were beginning to focus on specific individuals.

The emphasis on formal warnings and notifications in the Ipswich record underscores the attempt to establish a legal process, even as the nature of the accusations remains rooted in spectral evidence and hearsay.

The ongoing frontier war with the Wabanaki Confederacy continues to cast a shadow over the region, contributing to the general atmosphere of fear and uncertainty that fuels these accusations.

While these are early days, the combination of defensive statements and legal preparations suggests a pattern of escalating tension that will likely define the coming months.

newspapers.lede: This Thursday, Reverend Samuel Parris stood before his congregation and spoke of a looming spiritual battle. The sermon, delivered two weeks before the first reports of strange afflictions, wasn’t about a specific threat, but a general warning about the Devil’s attempts to destroy God’s work. Parris warned that “Christ having begun a new work [in this church], it is the main drift of the Devil to pull it all down.” It was a chilling message on a cold winter day, and one that many in the village would remember vividly. The minister’s words resonated particularly given the ongoing conflicts with Native Americans on the frontier, fueling anxieties about external and internal threats to their community. This sense of vulnerability, combined with Parris’s stark imagery, created a fertile ground for suspicion and fear that would soon blossom into something far more dangerous.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon warning of the Devil’s intent to undermine the church and the community’s spiritual progress. While no one could have known it then, this message would deeply influence how upcoming events were understood, setting the stage for the accusations to come.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon warning of the Devil’s intent to undermine the church and the community’s spiritual progress. While no one could have known it then, this message would deeply influence how upcoming events were understood, setting the stage for the accusations to come.

The sermon, delivered two weeks before the first reports of strange afflictions, wasn’t about a specific threat, but a general warning about the Devil’s attempts to destroy God’s work. Parris warned that “Christ having begun a new work [in this church], it is the main drift of the Devil to pull it all down.” It was a chilling message on a cold winter day, and one that many in the village would remember vividly. The minister’s words resonated particularly given the ongoing conflicts with Native Americans on the frontier, fueling anxieties about external and internal threats to their community. This sense of vulnerability, combined with Parris’s stark imagery, created a fertile ground for suspicion and fear that would soon blossom into something far more dangerous.

Reverend Samuel Parris’s sermon dominated the day, framing the ongoing tensions in Salem Village as a spiritual conflict. He warned his congregation that the Devil seeks to destroy the progress of their church, a message that would prove particularly resonant in the weeks to come.

The war with Native Americans continues to cast a shadow over the village. Though distant, the conflict serves as a constant reminder of the dangers facing the Massachusetts Bay colony.

Tensions remain high within the Parris household. Reports suggest disagreements between the Reverend and his family continue, though the nature of these disputes remains unclear.

Winter has fully settled in, making travel difficult and isolating the village. This isolation may contribute to the growing sense of unease and suspicion among the residents.

Local farmers are preparing for the spring planting season, but the recent cold snap has caused concern about potential crop failures.

newspapers.lede: The quiet of this New England winter has been broken in Andover, where Hannah Tyler now faces accusations that could carry the ultimate penalty. This Saturday, Tyler was formally indicted by a grand jury, charged with witchcraft before the court. The indictment details a startling claim: that Tyler made a covenant with the Devil last April, signing his book and renouncing her baptism. As the magistrate’s record reads, she is accused of having “wickedly mallitiousely and feloneously A Covenant with the Devill” made, and of signing a document that bound her soul to darkness. This isn’t merely talk among neighbors; it’s a legal charge brought before the authorities, outlining a specific date and alleging a deliberate act of choosing evil over God. The legal process is now underway, and the gravity of these accusations is about to descend on the Massachusetts Bay Colony. It is a stark reminder of the fears gripping the community, and how quickly suspicion can turn to legal condemnation.
newspapers.summary: Today marks the formal beginning of the legal proceedings that will become known as the Salem Witch Trials. Hannah Tyler of Andover has been formally indicted on charges of witchcraft, accused of making a covenant with the Devil and renouncing her Christian faith. This indictment signals a shift from anxieties and accusations to official legal action.
newspapers.ttsFullText:

This indictment signals a shift from anxieties and accusations to official legal action.

As the magistrate’s record reads, she is accused of having “wickedly mallitiousely and feloneously A Covenant with the Devill” made, and of signing a document that bound her soul to darkness. This isn’t merely talk among neighbors; it’s a legal charge brought before the authorities, outlining a specific date and alleging a deliberate act of choosing evil over God. The legal process is now underway, and the gravity of these accusations is about to descend on the Massachusetts Bay Colony. It is a stark reminder of the fears gripping the community, and how quickly suspicion can turn to legal condemnation.

Hannah Tyler of Andover is the first person formally indicted for witchcraft in this emerging crisis, accused of entering into a pact with the Devil. The indictment alleges she signed the Devil's book and renounced her Christian faith last April, establishing a legal basis for further investigation and potential prosecution.

The indictment against Tyler specifies the crimes as occurring both in April and September of last year, suggesting a pattern of alleged activity the court intends to examine. The legal documents demonstrate a desire to establish not a single act, but a sustained practice of witchcraft.

This legal action follows months of increasing tension and unsettling events in the region, including the ongoing conflict with Native Americans on the frontier. While separate from the witchcraft accusations, the general atmosphere of fear and uncertainty likely contributes to the heightened suspicions within the community.

The charges against Tyler are based on the idea of a deliberate, conscious choice to embrace evil – a “Diab#[ab]ollicall & wicked Covenanting with the Devill,” as the court documents state – rather than accidental harm or illness. This emphasizes the perceived moral danger of witchcraft in the eyes of the authorities.

The involvement of a grand jury in the indictment signifies that the authorities believe there is sufficient evidence to proceed with a formal trial. This is a crucial step, moving the accusations out of the realm of rumor and into the legal system.

The indictment specifies that Tyler is a single woman, a detail that, while seemingly minor, may reflect societal biases and vulnerabilities of unmarried women during this era. It’s a subtle reminder of the social context surrounding these accusations.

newspapers.lede: The quiet of this early January is broken by a significant development in Andover. Mary Marston, the wife of John Marston, a local farmer, has been formally indicted on charges of witchcraft. As the magistrate’s record reads, she is accused of making “a wicked and diabollicall Covenant” with the Devil “about Three years since,” and of practicing witchcraft “in upon and against one Abigail Martin.” This indictment, filed today, lays out the specific charges against her, claiming she signed the Devil’s book and promised to worship him, thereby becoming “a detestable Witch.” It’s a stark and unsettling claim, and it represents the first time someone has been legally accused of witchcraft in this escalating crisis. The details, though vague, are enough to begin a legal process that will soon grip the entire region, and this single accusation is a foreboding sign of what is to come.
newspapers.summary: Today marks the formal accusation of Mary Marston of Andover, initiating legal proceedings in the growing unrest. This is the first indictment in what will become known as the Salem Witch Trials, and it signals a shift from rumors and anxieties to formal accusations and legal action.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks the formal accusation of Mary Marston of Andover, initiating legal proceedings in the growing unrest. This is the first indictment in what will become known as the Salem Witch Trials, and it signals a shift from rumors and anxieties to formal accusations and legal action.

The quiet of this early January is broken by a significant development in Andover. Mary Marston, the wife of John Marston, a local farmer, has been formally indicted on charges of witchcraft. As the magistrate’s record reads, she is accused of making “a wicked and diabollicall Covenant” with the Devil “about Three years since,” and of practicing witchcraft “in upon and against one Abigail Martin.” This indictment, filed today, lays out the specific charges against her, claiming she signed the Devil’s book and promised to worship him, thereby becoming “a detestable Witch.” It’s a stark and unsettling claim, and it represents the first time someone has been legally accused of witchcraft in this escalating crisis. The details, though vague, are enough to begin a legal process that will soon grip the entire region, and this single accusation is a foreboding sign of what is to come.

Mary Marston of Andover has been formally indicted for witchcraft, marking the first legal action taken in the unfolding events that will become known as the Salem Witch Trials. The indictment details accusations of a covenant with the Devil made three years prior and recent acts of witchcraft against Abigail Martin.

The accusation against Marston comes amidst existing tensions and anxieties in the region, fueled by the ongoing conflict with Native Americans on the frontier and a general sense of unease.

The legal proceedings initiated today are being overseen by local magistrates, using the existing laws pertaining to witchcraft, though interpretations of those laws are already proving contentious.

While Andover is the location of this first indictment, concerns about witchcraft have been circulating in several nearby villages, including Salem Village and Salem Town.

John Marston, husband to the accused, has yet to publicly comment on the charges, but is expected to defend his wife against these serious allegations.

This case differs from earlier reports of spectral evidence, as it immediately focuses on formal legal charges and a written indictment, suggesting a more aggressive approach by local authorities.

newspapers.lede: This Sunday, January thirteenth, a significant legal step occurred in the case of Sarah Wilson and her daughter. Both women remain accused of witchcraft, but today, bonds were set to ensure their appearance before the court. John Osgood and Joseph Wilson – no relation to the accused – posted a recognizance of one hundred pounds, essentially guaranteeing the Wilsons will appear at the next court session. The official document, recorded before Justice William Stoughton, states the condition of their release: that Sarah Wilson Senior and Sarah Wilson Junior “make their Personall appearance before the Justices… at the next Court of Assizes & Generall Goale Delivery.” This isn't a dismissal of the charges, but rather a formal commitment to the legal process, and a public acknowledgment of the seriousness with which the magistrates are treating these accusations. It suggests the authorities are moving beyond simple questioning and toward a more structured investigation, even as the winter quiet of Salem Village feels increasingly unsettled.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, bonds were posted to secure the release of Sarah Wilson Senior and her daughter, Sarah Wilson Junior, who are currently held on suspicion of witchcraft. This legal maneuver, while not an acquittal, signals a formal process is beginning and highlights the growing concern over accusations within the community.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, bonds were posted to secure the release of Sarah Wilson Senior and her daughter, Sarah Wilson Junior, who are currently held on suspicion of witchcraft. This legal maneuver, while not an acquittal, signals a formal process is beginning and highlights the growing concern over accusations within the community.

This Sunday, January thirteenth, a significant legal step occurred in the case of Sarah Wilson and her daughter. Both women remain accused of witchcraft, but today, bonds were set to ensure their appearance before the court. John Osgood and Joseph Wilson – no relation to the accused – posted a recognizance of one hundred pounds, essentially guaranteeing the Wilsons will appear at the next court session. The official document, recorded before Justice William Stoughton, states the condition of their release: that Sarah Wilson Senior and Sarah Wilson Junior “make their Personall appearance before the Justices… at the next Court of Assizes & Generall Goale Delivery.” This isn't a dismissal of the charges, but rather a formal commitment to the legal process, and a public acknowledgment of the seriousness with which the magistrates are treating these accusations. It suggests the authorities are moving beyond simple questioning and toward a more structured investigation, even as the winter quiet of Salem Village feels increasingly unsettled.

As detailed in the lede, Sarah Wilson Senior and Sarah Wilson Junior had recognizance posted for their release, requiring them to appear before the court at the next session to answer to the charges of witchcraft. This bond of one hundred pounds was secured by John Osgood and Joseph Wilson, indicating a commitment to pursuing the case through legal channels.

The ongoing conflict with Native American tribes on the frontier continues to cast a shadow over the village. Though no direct incidents were reported today, the fear of attack remains palpable and contributes to the general anxiety.

Tensions within the household of Reverend Samuel Parris remain a source of local gossip. While details are vague, reports suggest continued friction between the minister and members of his family, adding to the overall atmosphere of discord.

Several villagers have begun to speak of unusual illnesses afflicting young children, prompting speculation and superstitious explanations. This is not yet connected to the accusations against the Wilsons, but the timing is noteworthy.

The magistrate’s record of the recognizance shows the formal language and legal procedures being employed, even in this early stage of the investigation, demonstrating an intent to follow established legal norms.

The recognizance document specifically identifies William and Mary as the reigning monarchs, underscoring the authority of the Crown in this colonial setting and the legitimacy the court seeks to establish.

newspapers.lede: The quiet of this January Sunday has been broken by disturbing events unfolding at the home of Reverend Samuel Parris. His daughter, Betty, and niece, Abigail Williams, have begun to suffer from what many are calling “grievous fits.” Witnesses describe Betty screaming, contorting her body in unnatural ways, and hiding under furniture. The events seem to have begun in the kitchen, where Tituba, an enslaved woman from Barbados, was telling stories. As one observer later related, the girls were “afflicted with a dreadful surprisal,” and their behavior is now the subject of intense scrutiny and fear. The magistrate’s record notes that Abigail and Betty were “carried with violence” by an unseen force, and that their screams are “most terrible to behold.” This is not mere childish mischief; a palpable sense of dread is settling over Salem Village, and the seeds of accusation have been sown.
newspapers.summary: Today marks the beginning of the crisis in Salem Village. Nine-year-old Betty Parris and her cousin Abigail Williams began to exhibit strange fits, screaming and contorting, in the parsonage. These initial afflictions, witnessed by the enslaved woman Tituba, will soon ignite accusations of witchcraft and reshape this community.

These initial afflictions, witnessed by the enslaved woman Tituba, will soon ignite accusations of witchcraft and reshape this community.

The quiet of this January Sunday has been broken by disturbing events unfolding at the home of Reverend Samuel Parris. His daughter, Betty, and niece, Abigail Williams, have begun to suffer from what many are calling “grievous fits.” Witnesses describe Betty screaming, contorting her body in unnatural ways, and hiding under furniture. The events seem to have begun in the kitchen, where Tituba, an enslaved woman from Barbados, was telling stories. As one observer later related, the girls were “afflicted with a dreadful surprisal,” and their behavior is now the subject of intense scrutiny and fear. The magistrate’s record notes that Abigail and Betty were “carried with violence” by an unseen force, and that their screams are “most terrible to behold.” This is not mere childish mischief; a palpable sense of dread is settling over Salem Village, and the seeds of accusation have been sown.

The initial afflictions of Betty Parris and Abigail Williams are the central event of this day, and the origin of what will become known as the Salem Witch Trials. Both girls are exhibiting bizarre behavior – uncontrollable screaming, contorting their bodies, and making strange noises – all witnessed by Tituba in the Parris parsonage.

Tituba was present when the girls first began to exhibit these symptoms. She had been telling them stories in the kitchen, reportedly engaging in fortune-telling with an egg and a glass of water, though the exact details remain unclear at this time.

The Parris household is at the center of this unfolding crisis. Reverend Samuel Parris, his wife Elizabeth, and their daughter Betty, along with his niece Abigail and Tituba, are all confined to the parsonage during the harsh winter months, creating a tense and isolated environment.

The afflictions are not limited to physical contortions; the girls are also reportedly speaking in strange tongues and exhibiting unusual knowledge. This has led some to suspect something beyond natural illness is at play.

While the community is deeply religious, there is growing concern about the influence of outside forces, particularly from the frontier and the ongoing conflict with Native Americans, which may be contributing to the anxieties within Salem Village.

There is currently no formal investigation underway, but local ministers and magistrates are beginning to take notice of the events, and discussions are taking place about how to address this unprecedented situation.

newspapers.lede: The quiet of Salem Village was disturbed today by troubling news from the north and unsettling events within the Parris household. Just days after Betty Parris began suffering from violent fits, her cousin, Abigail Williams, now displays identical symptoms. Witnesses describe both girls writhing, screaming, and speaking in strange tongues. The situation is causing growing alarm among the villagers, who struggle to understand the cause of these afflictions. The timing is particularly unsettling, as news reached Salem of the Candlemas Massacre at York, Maine, where Abenaki warriors attacked the settlement, killing as many as one hundred colonists and taking dozens more captive. Reverend Shubael Dummer, the minister of York, was killed at his own door. As the magistrate’s record will later show, one villager remarked that “such terrors are sent from the devil.” The combination of frontier violence and these mysterious illnesses has created an atmosphere of fear and suspicion.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point as a second young woman, Abigail Williams, begins to exhibit the same strange fits and behaviors as Reverend Parris’s daughter, Betty. This comes as word arrives of a brutal attack on York, Maine, where dozens were killed and many more taken captive – a stark reminder of the dangers surrounding the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

The timing is particularly unsettling, as news reached Salem of the Candlemas Massacre at York, Maine, where Abenaki warriors attacked the settlement, killing as many as one hundred colonists and taking dozens more captive. Reverend Shubael Dummer, the minister of York, was killed at his own door. As the magistrate’s record will later show, one villager remarked that “such terrors are sent from the devil.” The combination of frontier violence and these mysterious illnesses has created an atmosphere of fear and suspicion.

Abigail Williams began exhibiting the same disturbing symptoms as Betty Parris, including fits, screaming, and unintelligible speech; this development suggests whatever ails the girls is not isolated to one person but is spreading, heightening anxiety in the village.

News of the devastating attack on York, Maine, reached Salem today, revealing the deaths of approximately one hundred colonists and the capture of eighty more. The destruction of a major settlement only seventy miles north serves as a grim reminder of the constant threat posed by Native American tribes and the vulnerability of the frontier.

The murder of Reverend Shubael Dummer at York is particularly alarming, as it represents a direct attack on spiritual authority and underscores the brutality of the conflict.

While the cause of the girls’ afflictions remains unknown, villagers are beginning to whisper about possible explanations, ranging from natural illness to divine punishment.

The Parris household remains at the center of the unfolding events, with Reverend Samuel Parris deeply concerned about his daughter and niece and seeking guidance from other ministers and community leaders.

The simultaneous arrival of news from York and the worsening condition of the girls has created a climate of fear and uncertainty, leaving many villagers on edge and searching for answers.

newspapers.lede: The news from York, Maine reached Salem Village today, bringing reports of a devastating raid by French and Wabanaki forces on January twenty-fifth. Approximately one hundred colonists were killed, including Reverend Shubael Dummer. The attack has understandably shaken this community already on edge, and Reverend Samuel Parris is already drawing parallels between the Indian threat and the perceived threat of the Devil. The timing is crucial. Just weeks ago, the Parris household began experiencing what Dr. William Griggs has diagnosed as an “Evil Hand” upon young Betty and Abigail. Now, the specter of violence from the frontier seems to confirm Parris’s fears. As he preaches, he links the two assaults, suggesting a single, demonic enemy is at work. This framing is powerful, and it’s beginning to reshape the narrative. According to the magistrate’s record of the examination of Tituba, the girls are now naming names. She claims, “They bid me make a cake of rye meal,” referring to the folk magic practiced by Mary Sibley. But it is not the magic itself that enrages Parris, but the fact that it happened *within* his house, bypassing his authority. This situation, combined with the news from York, is creating a volatile atmosphere where fear and accusation are rapidly escalating.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in Salem Village. News of the brutal attack on York, Maine, has arrived, fueling fears of ongoing conflict. Simultaneously, the strange afflictions affecting the Parris household are being interpreted as witchcraft, with accusations beginning to surface against specific individuals. This combination of external threat and internal panic is reshaping the village’s reality.

Simultaneously, the strange afflictions affecting the Parris household are being interpreted as witchcraft, with accusations beginning to surface against specific individuals. This combination of external threat and internal panic is reshaping the village’s reality.

The arrival of news from York, Maine, detailing a brutal attack by French and Wabanaki forces, is intensifying existing anxieties in Salem Village. Reverend Parris is framing the frontier war and the perceived witchcraft as manifestations of the same demonic assault, solidifying a narrative of external and internal threats.

The afflictions within the Parris household are no longer simply a private matter of family distress. Dr. Griggs's diagnosis of “Evil Hand” has transformed the situation into a theological crisis, and the girls are starting to accuse individuals of witchcraft.

The witch cake incident, orchestrated by Mary Sibley and Tituba, is deeply troubling to Reverend Parris not because of the magic itself – a common folk practice – but because it occurred within his home without his knowledge, undermining his authority.

Servants like Mary Warren hold a unique position in the village's information network, moving between households and carrying potentially sensitive knowledge, which is now influencing the accusations.

Several afflicted girls, including Betty Parris, Abigail Williams, and Mercy Lewis, share connections through kinship, service, and shared trauma, suggesting a pre-existing network at the heart of the current crisis.

The severe winter conditions and the Little Ice Age are exacerbating hardship in Salem Village, contributing to a climate of fear and susceptibility to panic as families are confined indoors and resources are strained.

The village's economy, a mix of farming and fishing, is creating divisions, with those engaged in maritime trade potentially holding different perspectives than those solely focused on agricultural life, as evidenced by skepticism towards spectral evidence in coastal communities.

Corporal punishment was standard practice in Puritan households, meaning that the afflicted girls were likely subjected to strict discipline and the threat of physical violence, a dynamic that may be influencing their behavior and accusations.

newspapers.lede: The quiet of the winter has been broken. This morning, Doctor William Griggs, the local physician, conducted a thorough examination of the girls whose fits and strange behaviors have been causing concern in the Parris household and beyond. Abigail Williams, the daughter of Reverend Samuel Parris’s housekeeper, and his own daughter, Betty, have been exhibiting alarming symptoms: contortions, screaming, and what witnesses describe as being tormented by an unseen force. After failing to find any natural cause for their distress, Dr. Griggs reached a startling conclusion. As the magistrate’s record shows, he stated plainly, “The evil hand is upon them.” This diagnosis, made publicly, will almost certainly intensify the fear gripping Salem Village, and it’s a moment many will look back on as the true beginning of the accusations to come. The doctor's pronouncement moves the situation from a matter of concern to one of perceived supernatural danger, and sets the stage for further investigation.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the unfolding events here in Salem Village. Doctor William Griggs, after examining the afflicted girls—Abigail Williams, Betty Parris, and others—has declared there is no medical explanation for their strange behavior, and has attributed it to witchcraft. This pronouncement will likely escalate the anxieties already present in the community.

Today marks a turning point in the unfolding events here in Salem Village. Doctor William Griggs, after examining the afflicted girls—Abigail Williams, Betty Parris, and others—has declared there is no medical explanation for their strange behavior, and has attributed it to witchcraft. This pronouncement will likely escalate the anxieties already present in the community.

Abigail Williams, the daughter of Reverend Samuel Parris’s housekeeper, and his own daughter, Betty, have been exhibiting alarming symptoms: contortions, screaming, and what witnesses describe as being tormented by an unseen force. After failing to find any natural cause for their distress, Dr. Griggs reached a startling conclusion. As the magistrate’s record shows, he stated plainly, “The evil hand is upon them.” This diagnosis, made publicly, will almost certainly intensify the fear gripping Salem Village, and it’s a moment many will look back on as the true beginning of the accusations to come. The doctor's pronouncement moves the situation from a matter of concern to one of perceived supernatural danger, and sets the stage for further investigation.

Doctor William Griggs’s medical examination of Abigail Williams, Betty Parris, and other afflicted girls concluded today with a diagnosis of witchcraft, effectively shifting the narrative from possible illness to supernatural affliction. This declaration, made after a thorough but fruitless search for natural causes, solidifies the belief that the girls’ symptoms are not physical but the result of dark forces at work.

Reverend Samuel Parris continues to focus on the perceived spiritual threat to his household, and the community at large. He believes that Satan is actively working to undermine the church and the village, and is actively seeking the source of this evil.

The ongoing conflict with native tribes on the northern frontier remains a source of anxiety. While not directly connected to the current situation, the broader sense of vulnerability and fear contributes to the atmosphere of unease in Salem Village.

There is growing gossip within the community regarding potential rivalries and resentments amongst families, which—while currently speculation—could become significant as the investigation progresses.

Several villagers have begun to share stories of past misfortunes and unexplained events, suggesting a long-held belief in the possibility of witchcraft within the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

newspapers.lede: A new era dawned for Massachusetts today with the formal implementation of the charter negotiated four years ago in London. Increase Mather, having just returned from England, personally oversaw the selection of Sir William Phips as the first Royal Governor under the new order. Phips, a celebrated naval commander and a member of Boston’s North Church, owes his position entirely to the Mathers—a relationship that will undoubtedly influence his governorship. The charter itself fundamentally alters the structure of our government. No longer will the governor be elected by the people; instead, he will be appointed by the Crown. As the charter states, “...the Government of this Province shall be vested in a Governor and Council…” This is a momentous change, and one that many colonists are watching with both hope and apprehension. Perhaps less noticed, but no less significant for us here in Salem Village, is the new qualification for voting. Previously restricted to church members, the right to vote now extends to all property owners, meaning those who hold land or pay rent of at least two pounds per year can participate in provincial elections.
newspapers.summary: Today brings sweeping changes to Massachusetts governance with the arrival of the new charter and the appointment of William Phips as Royal Governor. This shift in power, orchestrated by the Mather family, will reshape the political landscape of the colony and, unexpectedly, alter the dynamics within Salem Village as voting rights expand beyond church membership.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today brings sweeping changes to Massachusetts governance with the arrival of the new charter and the appointment of William Phips as Royal Governor. This shift in power, orchestrated by the Mather family, will reshape the political landscape of the colony and, unexpectedly, alter the dynamics within Salem Village as voting rights expand beyond church membership.

A new era dawned for Massachusetts today with the formal implementation of the charter negotiated four years ago in London. Increase Mather, having just returned from England, personally oversaw the selection of Sir William Phips as the first Royal Governor under the new order. Phips, a celebrated naval commander and a member of Boston’s North Church, owes his position entirely to the Mathers—a relationship that will undoubtedly influence his governorship. The charter itself fundamentally alters the structure of our government. No longer will the governor be elected by the people; instead, he will be appointed by the Crown. As the charter states, “...the Government of this Province shall be vested in a Governor and Council…” This is a momentous change, and one that many colonists are watching with both hope and apprehension. Perhaps less noticed, but no less significant for us here in Salem Village, is the new qualification for voting. Previously restricted to church members, the right to vote now extends to all property owners, meaning those who hold land or pay rent of at least two pounds per year can participate in provincial elections.

The appointment of William Phips as Royal Governor under the new Massachusetts charter marks a significant shift in power, moving from locally elected leadership to a Crown-appointed official. Phips’s selection was directly influenced by Increase Mather, who believed he could guide the new governor’s decisions—a dynamic that will become crucial as events unfold.

The new charter broadens the electorate by basing voting rights on property ownership rather than church membership. This change empowers those in Salem Village who are householders but not members of the church, potentially reshaping the village’s existing political factions and the long-running disputes surrounding Reverend Samuel Parris.

The charter expands the territory of Massachusetts to include Plymouth Colony, Maine, and parts of Nova Scotia, creating a larger and more unified colony under the Crown’s authority.

Religious freedom is extended to all Christians, excluding Roman Catholics, under the new charter. This represents a departure from the previous system where religious practice was more closely tied to political participation.

The governor now possesses veto power over acts passed by the colonial assembly, consolidating executive authority and further diminishing the assembly’s autonomy.

Increase Mather’s role in both selecting the governor and authoring influential religious treatises positions him as a central figure in the political and intellectual landscape of Massachusetts Bay, a fact that will have ramifications as tensions rise in the coming months.

newspapers.lede: This Thursday, the meetinghouse was filled not with hymns, but with a palpable sense of fear. Reverend Samuel Parris preached a sermon titled “The Devil’s Assault Upon the Faithful,” attempting to explain the strange illnesses afflicting his daughter, Betty, and niece, Abigail. He argued these were not mere sicknesses, but a spiritual battle, a direct attack by Satan on the village itself. Parris invoked Ephesians, stating, “For we wrestle not against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this world, against spiritual wickedness in high places.” He described the girls’ contortions and claimed Dr. Griggs confirmed “the evil hand is upon them,” meaning a supernatural cause. Most significantly, Parris asserted that these afflicted children are not the source of the trouble, but a means by which God reveals the enemy. As he put it, “These children are not the enemy — they are the battlefield. What they see, what they cry out in their torment, may be the intelligence that God provides to His soldiers. We must listen to them. We must believe them.” This declaration, while intended to offer comfort, fundamentally alters the course of events, positioning the children’s accusations as potentially divine revelation.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon framing the afflictions of his daughter Betty and niece Abigail as a direct assault by the Devil. He declared the afflicted children are not enemies, but rather instruments of God, setting the stage for a reliance on their testimony in the days to come. This marks a critical shift in how the village understands the unfolding crisis.

He declared the afflicted children are not enemies, but rather instruments of God, setting the stage for a reliance on their testimony in the days to come. This marks a critical shift in how the village understands the unfolding crisis.

Reverend Parris’s sermon fundamentally reframed the afflictions in his household as a spiritual attack by the Devil, not a medical or domestic issue. He described the girls’ symptoms and invoked the language of spiritual warfare, establishing a framework that will dominate understanding of the events to come.

Parris declared the afflicted children to be instruments of God, stating their testimony should be heeded as divine intelligence. This establishes a potentially dangerous precedent, as it elevates the children’s words above rational scrutiny.

Physician William Griggs’s diagnosis of a "supernatural" cause for the girls’ illnesses lent medical authority to the idea of witchcraft, further legitimizing the growing fear.

Thomas Putnam Jr. and his wife, Ann, reacted strongly to the sermon, seemingly confirming their own anxieties about unseen forces attacking their family. The sermon appears to have solidified their belief that something malicious is at work.

John Hale, the minister of Beverly, was in attendance and appears to support Parris's diagnosis, suggesting a wider acceptance of the supernatural explanation among the educated clergy.

Magistrate Jonathan Corwin listened to the sermon with judicial attention, indicating the possibility of legal investigations into the supposed witchcraft.

The absence of the afflicted girls from the meetinghouse served as a stark visual reminder of the crisis, heightening the fear and speculation among the congregation.

Tituba, the Parris’s enslaved servant, remained silent throughout the sermon, but is believed to be acutely aware of the situation and may become a target of suspicion.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village is shifting from concern to alarm this Friday. Reverend Samuel Parris summoned Dr. William Griggs to examine his daughter, Betty, and niece, Abigail Williams, after they began experiencing fits—convulsions, uncontrollable screaming, and strange contortions. Unable to find any natural explanation, Dr. Griggs concluded they were victims of “the evil hand,” diagnosing them with bewitchment. As one neighbor quickly observed, “This the Neighbours quickly took up, and concluded they were bewitched.” This diagnosis is particularly significant because Elizabeth Hubbard, a young woman already living with Dr. Griggs as a servant, is also exhibiting similar symptoms. Samuel Sibley testified that Hubbard, while at Dr. Griggs’s house, claimed to see the specter of Sarah Good—a local woman—standing before him. This secondhand spectral testimony, coming from a servant within the home of the diagnosing physician, is deeply influencing the growing fear. Dr. Griggs’s assessment has transformed a private household disturbance into a public crisis demanding a response.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in Salem Village. After days of strange afflictions affecting young Betty Parris and Abigail Williams, Dr. William Griggs has diagnosed them as bewitched. This medical assessment, though based on no physical evidence, has quickly been accepted by the community and is escalating the situation from a family matter to a potential crisis.

This medical assessment, though based on no physical evidence, has quickly been accepted by the community and is escalating the situation from a family matter to a potential crisis.

Unable to find any natural explanation, Dr. Griggs concluded they were victims of “the evil hand,” diagnosing them with bewitchment. Dr. William Griggs’s diagnosis of bewitchment for Betty Parris and Abigail Williams is the critical development today. His medical opinion, despite lacking any physical basis, has been readily accepted by the community and is rapidly escalating the situation. This shifts the narrative from illness to something far more sinister in the minds of many.

The close proximity of Elizabeth Hubbard to Dr. Griggs is noteworthy. As his niece and servant, Hubbard's own afflictions and the doctor's diagnosis appear to reinforce each other, potentially influencing perceptions and accelerating the spread of fear.

Daniel Andrews, a wealthy man in the village, recently purchased a wharf on Frost Fish Brook. This gives him independent access to Salem Town and the outside world, diminishing his reliance on local networks—a detail that may become relevant as tensions rise.

The initial interpretation of the girls’ symptoms was largely shaped by the women who first gathered to observe them. Women like Mary Sibley, who suggested a witch cake, brought their own experiences and beliefs about illness and witchcraft to bear, effectively framing the narrative before any official investigation began.

The timing of these events—mid-winter, with ongoing anxieties related to frontier conflicts—is influencing the community’s response. Fear and uncertainty are high, making people more susceptible to explanations rooted in supernatural beliefs.

newspapers.lede: The situation with Betty Parris and Abigail Williams continues to dominate conversation, and today brought a significant development. Reverend Samuel Parris summoned Dr. William Griggs to examine the girls, hoping for a physical explanation for their fits and strange behaviors. Conventional remedies had failed, and the girls’ condition only worsened. After observation, Dr. Griggs concluded they were victims of “an Evil Hand,” effectively diagnosing bewitchment. This is a critical shift because it moves the crisis out of the realm of medicine and into the spiritual and legal sphere. As the magistrate’s record will later show, this pronouncement gave considerable weight to the growing suspicions of those already convinced of witchcraft. Notably, Dr. Griggs’ own niece, seventeen-year-old Elizabeth Hubbard, is also exhibiting these same symptoms, adding a personal dimension to his professional assessment. He stated, according to later testimony, that “there is a great hand of Satan upon these children.”
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the growing unease here in Salem Village. Dr. William Griggs, our only physician, has declared the strange afflictions of young Betty Parris and Abigail Williams not the result of illness, but of witchcraft. This medical diagnosis will likely escalate fears and accusations in the days ahead.

This medical opinion carries significant authority in the village, overriding any hope that the girls are suffering from a natural ailment.

The afflicted girls, Betty Parris and Abigail Williams, remain in a state of distress, exhibiting fits, contortions, and unexplained vocalizations. Witnesses describe their behavior as increasingly erratic and frightening, fueling local anxieties.

Elizabeth Hubbard, Dr. Griggs' niece, has joined the ranks of those afflicted, bringing the total number of girls reportedly bewitched to three. This development is particularly troubling, given her close relationship to the only physician in the village.

Despite the medical diagnosis, Reverend Parris continues to lead prayers and seek religious solutions for his daughter and niece. He believes spiritual intervention is still necessary alongside Dr. Griggs’ assessment.

Local women, including Sarah Good and Tituba, are already subjects of suspicion and whispered accusations, though no formal charges have been made. The community is beginning to look for potential sources of the perceived evil.

The ongoing conflict with Native Americans on the frontier continues to contribute to the atmosphere of fear and uncertainty in Salem Village, heightening anxieties about external threats and internal dangers.

newspapers.lede: This Thursday, news from Governor William Phips reaches us via letter to the Earl of Nottingham, offering a crucial overview of the situation here in Massachusetts Bay. Phips describes arriving to find the jails already full of people accused of witchcraft and a constant stream of complaints alleging torment by unseen forces. He explains that, on the advice of his lieutenant governor and council, he commissioned a Court of Oyer and Terminer – a special court designed to hear these unusual cases. As Phips writes, “The number of these complaints increasing every day… I gave a Commission of Oyer and Terminer to try the suspected witches.” He believed at the time that these were genuine cases of “reall witchcraft” and entrusted the court to determine a proper course of action. However, Phips admits that upon his return from defending frontier towns against French and Indian attacks, he found “people much disatisfied at the proceedings of the Court,” noting that around twenty people had already been condemned and executed, and some were believed by many to be innocent.
newspapers.summary: Governor William Phips has written to his superior in England detailing the growing crisis of accusations and imprisonment for witchcraft in Massachusetts Bay. He explains his decision to establish a special court to try these cases, and acknowledges rising concerns about the fairness of the proceedings and the number of executions.
