He explains his decision to establish a special court to try these cases, and acknowledges rising concerns about the fairness of the proceedings and the number of executions.

This Thursday, news from Governor William Phips reaches us via letter to the Earl of Nottingham, offering a crucial overview of the situation here in Massachusetts Bay. Phips describes arriving to find the jails already full of people accused of witchcraft and a constant stream of complaints alleging torment by unseen forces. He explains that, on the advice of his lieutenant governor and council, he commissioned a Court of Oyer and Terminer – a special court designed to hear these unusual cases. As Phips writes, “The number of these complaints increasing every day… I gave a Commission of Oyer and Terminer to try the suspected witches.” He believed at the time that these were genuine cases of “reall witchcraft” and entrusted the court to determine a proper course of action. However, Phips admits that upon his return from defending frontier towns against French and Indian attacks, he found “people much disatisfied at the proceedings of the Court,” noting that around twenty people had already been condemned and executed, and some were believed by many to be innocent.

Governor Phips’ letter confirms the severity of the situation, with prisons filled and accusations escalating rapidly. He initiated the Court of Oyer and Terminer hoping to address the complaints of torment, but now expresses concern over the court’s judgments and the growing number of executions.

The ongoing conflict with French and Indian forces continues to draw resources and attention away from Salem Village. Phips’ absence commanding the army likely contributed to the unrest and dissatisfaction with the court’s proceedings.

The letter suggests a growing divide within the community regarding the nature of these accusations. While Phips initially believed in “reall witchcraft,” he acknowledges widespread doubts about the guilt of those condemned.

This is the first official communication from the governor regarding the trials to reach England, signaling that the crisis is now considered a matter of significant concern for the colonial administration.

The reference to twenty condemned and executed individuals illustrates the swiftness and severity of the legal response to the accusations, even before this Friday’s developments.

The magistrate’s record shows a pattern of spectral evidence being admitted – testimony based on visions and dreams – which is likely fueling the accusations and contributing to the growing dissatisfaction.

newspapers.lede: We stand near the Parris parsonage, a place of increasing distress. This morning, Mary Sibley, aunt to one of the afflicted girls, Mary Walcott, took a desperate step. Seeking to understand the source of the fits and visions plaguing young Betty Parris and Abigail Williams, Sibley instructed Tituba and John Indian to bake what’s known as a “witch cake.” This was a traditional English remedy – rye meal mixed with the urine of the afflicted, given to a dog, with the belief that the dog would then reveal the witch responsible. But when Reverend Parris discovered this happening in his own home, he was incensed. He publicly rebuked Sibley from the pulpit, declaring, “going to the Devil for help against the Devil!” The incident reveals a surprising reliance on folk magic even among those who profess piety. And as John Hale would later write, this may well be “the origin of the mischief,” as the girls’ symptoms seemed to worsen after the cake was baked.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, a neighbor’s attempt to use folk magic to diagnose the cause of the girls’ strange afflictions backfired spectacularly. Mary Sibley’s actions, intended to find a solution outside of prayer, ignited the anger of Reverend Parris and exposed a hidden undercurrent of magical belief within the community. This incident marks a turning point, escalating tensions and setting the stage for formal accusations.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, a neighbor’s attempt to use folk magic to diagnose the cause of the girls’ strange afflictions backfired spectacularly. Mary Sibley’s actions, intended to find a solution outside of prayer, ignited the anger of Reverend Parris and exposed a hidden undercurrent of magical belief within the community. This incident marks a turning point, escalating tensions and setting the stage for formal accusations.

We stand near the Parris parsonage, a place of increasing distress. This morning, Mary Sibley, aunt to one of the afflicted girls, Mary Walcott, took a desperate step. Seeking to understand the source of the fits and visions plaguing young Betty Parris and Abigail Williams, Sibley instructed Tituba and John Indian to bake what’s known as a “witch cake.” This was a traditional English remedy – rye meal mixed with the urine of the afflicted, given to a dog, with the belief that the dog would then reveal the witch responsible. But when Reverend Parris discovered this happening in his own home, he was incensed. He publicly rebuked Sibley from the pulpit, declaring, “going to the Devil for help against the Devil!” The incident reveals a surprising reliance on folk magic even among those who profess piety. And as John Hale would later write, this may well be “the origin of the mischief,” as the girls’ symptoms seemed to worsen after the cake was baked.

As we discussed, Mary Sibley’s attempt to use a witch cake to diagnose the cause of the girls’ afflictions proved disastrous. She directed Tituba and John Indian to bake the cake using the afflicted girls’ urine and rye meal, a folk practice intended to reveal the witch through the reaction of a dog. However, this act of counter-magic sparked outrage from Reverend Parris, who saw it as a pact with the very forces they were fighting against.

Reverend Parris reacted with fury when he discovered the witch cake. He publicly reprimanded Mary Sibley, condemning her actions as a descent into the same darkness she sought to combat, and exposing the practice to the entire congregation.

This incident underscores the coexistence of traditional folk beliefs and Puritan theology in Salem Village. The quick resort to counter-magic before seeking purely religious solutions reveals a complex and sometimes contradictory worldview.

Abigail Williams has now named Tituba as one of those afflicting her, a particularly cruel betrayal considering Tituba provided a degree of maternal comfort to the orphaned girl. This demonstrates the power dynamics at play and the vulnerability of those on the margins of the community.

Mary Sibley’s connection to the Walcott family, through her relationship with Mary Walcott, places her at the very center of the initial events, linking her household to the origins of the crisis.

The first names being offered by the afflicted girls – Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne – are telling. These are the most vulnerable members of the community: a slave, a beggar, and an elderly woman already ostracized for her absence from church and a property dispute. This suggests a deliberate targeting of those least able to defend themselves.

newspapers.lede: We stand near the Parris parsonage, a modest home that has become the unlikely center of the unfolding crisis. Just days ago, on February 25th, neighbor Mary Sibley directed Tituba and John Indian to bake what’s known as a ‘witch cake’. It was a folk remedy, common in England for generations – rye flour mixed with the urine of the afflicted girls, then fed to a dog, with the hope it would reveal the witch tormenting them. Reverend Parris, however, was enraged. From the pulpit, he publicly rebuked Sibley, declaring, “Is it not because there is not a God in Israel?” and condemning the practice as “going to the Devil for help against the Devil.” This wasn’t merely theological disagreement; it signaled a hardening of lines. The witch cake wasn’t sanctioned by the church, but it *was* a reflection of the beliefs held by many in this very village – a world of charms, divination, and folk remedies existing alongside, and sometimes in tension with, the strict Calvinism preached from this meetinghouse. The incident, as the magistrate’s record of the examinations will later show, appears to have coincided with the girls beginning to name names, suggesting the folk diagnostic gave shape to their accusations.
newspapers.summary: Today we uncover the hidden world of folk magic coexisting with Puritan beliefs in Salem Village. The recent ‘witch cake’ incident – a traditional English remedy using urine to diagnose witchcraft – has shaken Reverend Parris and revealed a tension between accepted practice and religious doctrine. This pre-crisis moment is a crucial turning point as the community navigates fear and superstition.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today we uncover the hidden world of folk magic coexisting with Puritan beliefs in Salem Village. The recent ‘witch cake’ incident – a traditional English remedy using urine to diagnose witchcraft – has shaken Reverend Parris and revealed a tension between accepted practice and religious doctrine. This pre-crisis moment is a crucial turning point as the community navigates fear and superstition.

We stand near the Parris parsonage, a modest home that has become the unlikely center of the unfolding crisis. Just days ago, on February 25th, neighbor Mary Sibley directed Tituba and John Indian to bake what’s known as a ‘witch cake’. It was a folk remedy, common in England for generations – rye flour mixed with the urine of the afflicted girls, then fed to a dog, with the hope it would reveal the witch tormenting them. Reverend Parris, however, was enraged. From the pulpit, he publicly rebuked Sibley, declaring, “Is it not because there is not a God in Israel?” and condemning the practice as “going to the Devil for help against the Devil.” This wasn’t merely theological disagreement; it signaled a hardening of lines. The witch cake wasn’t sanctioned by the church, but it *was* a reflection of the beliefs held by many in this very village – a world of charms, divination, and folk remedies existing alongside, and sometimes in tension with, the strict Calvinism preached from this meetinghouse. The incident, as the magistrate’s record of the examinations will later show, appears to have coincided with the girls beginning to name names, suggesting the folk diagnostic gave shape to their accusations.

As we’ve discussed, the witch cake incident reveals a deep undercurrent of folk magic in Salem Village. While Reverend Parris condemns such practices as “going to the Devil for help against the Devil,” it’s important to understand that these traditions – brought from England – were widespread and often seen as practical solutions for everyday problems.

Beyond the witch cake, almanacs were also common in colonial New England, offering farmers guidance based on lunar cycles and astrological forecasts. These blurred the line between acceptable natural philosophy and what the church considered forbidden knowledge.

The Parris parsonage itself is a cramped and austere space, revealing the family’s modest circumstances. The home is filled with the necessities of life, but also with the intellectual tools of a minister struggling to maintain authority and order.

Mary Sibley’s actions, while condemned by Parris, demonstrate the desperation felt by some villagers to find a solution to the perceived affliction. She felt guilt about Tituba suffering for her instruction, a disparity that highlights the power dynamics of the colony.

Tituba, as an enslaved woman, bore the brunt of the consequences for a folk practice initiated by a white woman. This injustice is lost on no one, and shapes her perspective on the unfolding events.

Reverend Parris secretly regrets the witch cake ever happening, as it implicates his own household in the practice of folk magic and undermines his authority. He is, understandably, anxious about the situation.

Counter-magic wasn't limited to witch cakes; practices like hanging horseshoes and burying iron were believed to offer protection against witchcraft, demonstrating the pervasive fear of the unseen.

Parris’s condemnation of the witch cake wasn’t just a theological dispute; it was a strategic move to solidify his control over the crisis and establish the church and the court as the sole arbiters of truth.

newspapers.lede: This Friday, February 29th, 1692, the invisible world of accusations has become terrifyingly real. Following weeks of strange afflictions plaguing young women in Salem Village, formal complaints have been filed with the magistrates in Salem Town. Thomas Putnam, along with Edward Putnam, Thomas Preston, and Joseph Hutchinson, formally accused Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne of witchcraft. Constable Joseph Herrick acted on those warrants, bringing the three women into custody. As the magistrate’s record reads, the complaint alleges “suspition of witchcraft…much injury don to Elizabeth Parris Abigail Williams Anna Putnam and Elizabeth Hubert.” These are the first legal steps in what will become known as the Salem Witch Trials, and the atmosphere in the village is thick with dread. Tituba, an enslaved woman in the household of Reverend Samuel Parris, was the first named, a devastating betrayal given the warmth and care she had shown the young girls. The accusations against Good and Osborne, already marginalized women in the community, suggest a pattern of targeting the vulnerable.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in Salem Village. Following complaints filed by local men, the first arrest warrants have been issued for Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne, accused of witchcraft. This sets in motion a legal process fueled by fear and spectral evidence, and foreshadows a crisis that will engulf the community.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a turning point in Salem Village. Following complaints filed by local men, the first arrest warrants have been issued for Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne, accused of witchcraft. This sets in motion a legal process fueled by fear and spectral evidence, and foreshadows a crisis that will engulf the community.

This Friday, February 29th, 1692, the invisible world of accusations has become terrifyingly real. Following weeks of strange afflictions plaguing young women in Salem Village, formal complaints have been filed with the magistrates in Salem Town. Thomas Putnam, along with Edward Putnam, Thomas Preston, and Joseph Hutchinson, formally accused Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne of witchcraft. Constable Joseph Herrick acted on those warrants, bringing the three women into custody. As the magistrate’s record reads, the complaint alleges “suspition of witchcraft…much injury don to Elizabeth Parris Abigail Williams Anna Putnam and Elizabeth Hubert.” These are the first legal steps in what will become known as the Salem Witch Trials, and the atmosphere in the village is thick with dread. Tituba, an enslaved woman in the household of Reverend Samuel Parris, was the first named, a devastating betrayal given the warmth and care she had shown the young girls. The accusations against Good and Osborne, already marginalized women in the community, suggest a pattern of targeting the vulnerable.

Today, the first arrest warrants were issued for Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne, marking the official beginning of the legal proceedings against those accused of witchcraft. These warrants, filed by Thomas Putnam and others, transformed spectral visions and complaints of affliction into formal charges.

Tituba, an enslaved woman in the Parris household, appears to be the central figure in the initial accusations. She was named by the afflicted girls, and her confession—likely coerced—will shape the narrative of the crisis, describing spectral flights and demonic influences.

Sarah Good, a homeless beggar, and Sarah Osborne, an elderly woman who hadn’t attended church in over a year, are also accused. Both women were already on the fringes of Salem Village society, making them easy targets for suspicion.

Constable Joseph Herrick carried out the arrests, finding Sarah Good begging and Sarah Osborne ill in her home. Tituba was taken from the Parris parsonage, illustrating the reach of these accusations even into the most private of homes.

The accusations reveal a pattern of targeting the most vulnerable members of the community: the poor, the homeless, the non-churchgoing, and those without strong social connections. This suggests the crisis will likely prey on existing social tensions and anxieties.

The men who filed the complaints—Putnam, Preston, and Hutchinson—are establishing themselves as key figures in the unfolding events. Their involvement in initiating the legal process will significantly influence the direction of the trials.

The Parris household is at the center of the unfolding drama. Tituba’s position as an enslaved woman within the home, coupled with the afflictions experienced by Betty Parris and Abigail Williams, places the family squarely within the narrative of witchcraft and demonic influence.

Servants and enslaved people, like Tituba, possessed a unique access to information within Salem Village households. They overheard private conversations and witnessed daily life, making them both valuable sources of intelligence and potential targets for accusation.

newspapers.lede: The meetinghouse in Salem Village was packed today as magistrates John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin began the public examination of Sarah Good, Sarah Osborne, and Tituba. Hundreds of villagers crowded in, eager – and fearful – to witness the proceedings. As the magistrates pressed the accused, the afflicted girls, including Abigail Williams and Ann Putnam Junior, began to convulse and cry out, claiming to be tormented by the presence of the women. The scene was chaotic, and the tension palpable. When questioned about who was harming the children, Sarah Good shockingly pointed to Sarah Osborne, establishing a dangerous pattern of the accused turning on one another. But it was Tituba who provided the most dramatic testimony. Facing relentless questioning, she confessed to witchcraft, describing a tall man from Boston who commanded her to harm the children. As the magistrate’s record reads, Tituba claimed this figure instructed her to “hurt the children,” and spoke of a book with nine names. This confession, though fantastical, provided the authorities with the narrative they were seeking and fueled the growing hysteria. Tituba’s testimony, detailed and seemingly crafted to satisfy her examiners, has set a dangerous precedent.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the unfolding crisis. The first public examinations of Sarah Good, Sarah Osborne, and Tituba took place in Salem Village, drawing a huge crowd. Tituba, facing intense questioning, offered a confession that implicated others and provided details of spectral encounters, escalating the fear and accusations.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a turning point in the unfolding crisis. The first public examinations of Sarah Good, Sarah Osborne, and Tituba took place in Salem Village, drawing a huge crowd. Tituba, facing intense questioning, offered a confession that implicated others and provided details of spectral encounters, escalating the fear and accusations.

The meetinghouse in Salem Village was packed today as magistrates John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin began the public examination of Sarah Good, Sarah Osborne, and Tituba. Hundreds of villagers crowded in, eager – and fearful – to witness the proceedings. As the magistrates pressed the accused, the afflicted girls, including Abigail Williams and Ann Putnam Junior, began to convulse and cry out, claiming to be tormented by the presence of the women. The scene was chaotic, and the tension palpable. When questioned about who was harming the children, Sarah Good shockingly pointed to Sarah Osborne, establishing a dangerous pattern of the accused turning on one another. But it was Tituba who provided the most dramatic testimony. Facing relentless questioning, she confessed to witchcraft, describing a tall man from Boston who commanded her to harm the children. As the magistrate’s record reads, Tituba claimed this figure instructed her to “hurt the children,” and spoke of a book with nine names. This confession, though fantastical, provided the authorities with the narrative they were seeking and fueled the growing hysteria. Tituba’s testimony, detailed and seemingly crafted to satisfy her examiners, has set a dangerous precedent.

Today’s examinations of Sarah Good, Sarah Osborne, and Tituba were the first public proceedings of the Salem witch trials, attracting a large and anxious crowd to the Salem Village meetinghouse. The afflicted girls exhibited violent fits during the questioning, seemingly reacting to the presence of the accused, bolstering the belief in spectral attacks.

Tituba’s confession is the most significant development of the day. She named names, described a demonic figure, and provided details of supposed rituals, giving substance to the accusations and providing a framework for the magistrates' understanding of events.

Sarah Good, under questioning, directly accused Sarah Osborne of afflicting the children, marking the first instance of one accused person implicating another. This sets a concerning precedent and suggests a desperate attempt to deflect blame.

Nathaniel Ingersoll spent eight pounds, ten shillings, and nine pence today hosting the magistrates, marshals, constables, and their horses, highlighting how the tavern is becoming central to the logistical infrastructure of the trials. He is currently under-reimbursed for these expenses.

Sarah Good is reportedly pregnant, and her infant child was born while she is imprisoned. The conditions in the jail are harsh, raising concerns about the health and well-being of both mother and child.

The afflictions are beginning to spread beyond the initial group of accusers. Ann Putnam Senior is expected to enter the accusing circle soon, which is predicted to drastically escalate the crisis.

Giles Corey is reportedly fearful that his wife, Martha, will be accused, and may be providing information against her to protect himself, demonstrating the growing panic within the community.

John Proctor continues to publicly question the validity of the accusations and the methods used by the magistrates, making him a potential target for further scrutiny.

newspapers.lede: The events unfolding here in Salem Village are becoming increasingly unsettling. Constable Joseph Herrick Sr. and his wife Mary testified today about their experience transporting Sarah Good to the Ipswich jail yesterday. According to their deposition, Good somehow vanished while in their custody, escaping despite being barefoot and with no apparent means of travel. Herrick found her the next morning, bloodied and claiming no memory of her travels. This seemingly impossible escape has only fueled the belief that she is aided by supernatural forces. Later today, Tituba underwent a second, lengthy examination before magistrates. In her own words, she described a covenant made with a man she initially believed to be God: “he Tell me he god, & I must beleive him & Serve him Six yeares & he would give me many fine things.” Tituba’s testimony detailed a network of witchcraft, suggesting that Good and Sarah Osborne were also participants in a larger, organized effort to harm the afflicted girls. The scope of this alleged conspiracy is rapidly expanding.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the accusations against Sarah Good took a disturbing turn, with reports of her escaping custody and returning injured. Meanwhile, Tituba’s second examination revealed a wider conspiracy involving multiple familiars and a clear pact with the Devil, solidifying fears of a growing threat to the community.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, the accusations against Sarah Good took a disturbing turn, with reports of her escaping custody and returning injured. Meanwhile, Tituba’s second examination revealed a wider conspiracy involving multiple familiars and a clear pact with the Devil, solidifying fears of a growing threat to the community.

The events unfolding here in Salem Village are becoming increasingly unsettling. Constable Joseph Herrick Sr. and his wife Mary testified today about their experience transporting Sarah Good to the Ipswich jail yesterday. According to their deposition, Good somehow vanished while in their custody, escaping despite being barefoot and with no apparent means of travel. Herrick found her the next morning, bloodied and claiming no memory of her travels. This seemingly impossible escape has only fueled the belief that she is aided by supernatural forces. Later today, Tituba underwent a second, lengthy examination before magistrates. In her own words, she described a covenant made with a man she initially believed to be God: “he Tell me he god, & I must beleive him & Serve him Six yeares & he would give me many fine things.” Tituba’s testimony detailed a network of witchcraft, suggesting that Good and Sarah Osborne were also participants in a larger, organized effort to harm the afflicted girls. The scope of this alleged conspiracy is rapidly expanding.

As reported in today’s examination, Tituba has elaborated on her initial confession, describing a clear pact with a demonic figure. She claims this entity promised her “many fine things” in exchange for six years of service, and that she was compelled to write in a book containing nine signatures, described as marks “like a:C” for some.

Constable Herrick’s account of Sarah Good’s escape from his custody and subsequent return with injuries has deeply alarmed the community. The implication is that Good was out committing dark deeds while seemingly beyond the reach of earthly laws.

Tituba identified specific familiars belonging to both Sarah Good and Sarah Osborne. Good allegedly had a yellow bird and a cat, while Osborne’s familiar was described as a creature with a woman’s head and wings.

Tituba claimed she was sometimes blinded during attacks and unable to identify who was causing harm to the afflicted children, further complicating the investigation.

During her examination, Tituba spoke of seeing a white-haired man in Boston and a woman in a black and white silk hood, suggesting the reach of this alleged witchcraft extends beyond Salem Village.

The magistrates are taking Tituba’s testimony as strong evidence of a coordinated conspiracy, reinforcing the growing fear that Salem Village is under attack by powerful and malevolent forces.

newspapers.lede: The jail in Salem Town, on Prison Lane, is overflowing. William Dounton, the jailer, holds more than 150 prisoners within its walls, a building meant for a fraction of that number. And these aren't held as guests. They pay for everything – their food, their lodging, even the irons that bind them. Sarah Good, already destitute before her arrest, now faces impossible debts. As the Boston jailer’s account shows, she’s accrued charges for twelve weeks of imprisonment, the cost of chains for herself and Sarah Osborne, and even blankets for her infant child, “by order.” It’s a system where poverty and accusation become indistinguishable. This week, the court found Lydia Dustin not guilty of witchcraft, yet she remains imprisoned, unable to pay the fees accumulated during her detention. As one observer noted, “innocence meant nothing without money.” The starkness of this reality is becoming increasingly apparent as the trials continue.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a grim picture of the jail system in Salem Town, where over 150 accused witches are held, forced to pay for their own imprisonment. Even those acquitted, like Lydia Dustin, can remain trapped by debt. The economic realities of 1692 Salem are becoming tragically clear – justice isn’t equal for those without means.

Today brings a grim picture of the jail system in Salem Town, where over 150 accused witches are held, forced to pay for their own imprisonment. Even those acquitted, like Lydia Dustin, can remain trapped by debt. The economic realities of 1692 Salem are becoming tragically clear – justice isn’t equal for those without means.

The jail in Salem Town, on Prison Lane, is overflowing. William Dounton, the jailer, holds more than 150 prisoners within its walls, a building meant for a fraction of that number. And these aren't held as guests. They pay for everything – their food, their lodging, even the irons that bind them. Sarah Good, already destitute before her arrest, now faces impossible debts. As the Boston jailer’s account shows, she’s accrued charges for twelve weeks of imprisonment, the cost of chains for herself and Sarah Osborne, and even blankets for her infant child, “by order.” It’s a system where poverty and accusation become indistinguishable. This week, the court found Lydia Dustin not guilty of witchcraft, yet she remains imprisoned, unable to pay the fees accumulated during her detention. As one observer noted, “innocence meant nothing without money.” The starkness of this reality is becoming increasingly apparent as the trials continue.

The Salem Town jail is severely overcrowded, holding over 150 prisoners in a space designed for far fewer. These prisoners are not provided for; they must pay for their food, lodging, and even to have their chains removed, creating a desperate situation for those without financial resources.

Salem Town, with a population of 1,500 to 2,000, is a bustling port and the center of legal and commercial life in Essex County, a stark contrast to the smaller, more isolated Salem Village. This disparity in population and social structure is important to understand as the trials unfold.

Lydia Dustin was acquitted of witchcraft today, but remains in jail because she cannot afford to pay the required fees, highlighting the impact of economic status on justice in this time. Her case is a particularly tragic example of how the system can punish even the innocent.

Sarah Good is burdened with substantial jail fees, including charges for her lodging, chains, and provisions for her infant child, demonstrating the financial hardship faced by accused individuals and their families. Her infant daughter, Mercy, died in prison.

The prevailing economic system of “book credit” relies on detailed ledger keeping and mutual trust, but also creates opportunities for disputes over debts and accusations of dishonesty. This system informs all aspects of life, from commerce to legal proceedings.

Most transactions in colonial Massachusetts occur through “country pay,” the exchange of goods and provisions instead of coin. This system, while common, introduces issues of quality, timing, and subjective valuation, adding complexity to economic life.

The journey between Salem Village and Salem Town is a significant undertaking, taking between one and a half to two and a half hours to walk, and requiring a full day for those attending court or visiting the jail. This distance adds to the hardship faced by villagers involved in the legal proceedings.

newspapers.lede: The afflictions plaguing the young women of Salem Village have spread, and with them, the accusations. Ann Putnam Junior, Mary Walcott, and Mercy Lewis have joined Abigail Williams and Betty Parris in experiencing what they describe as being pinched, bitten, and otherwise tormented. These fits are not random, however. The girls are now identifying those they claim are afflicting them. Today, the magistrate’s record shows, the girls named more individuals, expanding the circle of the accused beyond Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne. As one observer described the scene, “They did fall into fits, and turned and tossed, and cried out terribly.” This expansion of accusations, particularly within the Putnam household, suggests a growing panic and a broadening scope to the unfolding events. The household of Reverend Samuel Parris and the Putnams are now at the very center of this unsettling drama, and the accusations are becoming increasingly specific and alarming.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a dangerous escalation in Salem Village. More young women are exhibiting what they describe as fits and torments, and crucially, they are now accusing additional people of witchcraft. The focus of these accusations is shifting toward the Putnam family and their household, deepening the crisis.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a dangerous escalation in Salem Village. More young women are exhibiting what they describe as fits and torments, and crucially, they are now accusing additional people of witchcraft. The focus of these accusations is shifting toward the Putnam family and their household, deepening the crisis.

The afflictions plaguing the young women of Salem Village have spread, and with them, the accusations. Ann Putnam Junior, Mary Walcott, and Mercy Lewis have joined Abigail Williams and Betty Parris in experiencing what they describe as being pinched, bitten, and otherwise tormented. These fits are not random, however. The girls are now identifying those they claim are afflicting them. Today, the magistrate’s record shows, the girls named more individuals, expanding the circle of the accused beyond Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne. As one observer described the scene, “They did fall into fits, and turned and tossed, and cried out terribly.” This expansion of accusations, particularly within the Putnam household, suggests a growing panic and a broadening scope to the unfolding events. The household of Reverend Samuel Parris and the Putnams are now at the very center of this unsettling drama, and the accusations are becoming increasingly specific and alarming.

The number of afflicted girls has increased significantly today with Ann Putnam Junior, Mary Walcott, and Mercy Lewis joining the initial group of accusers, intensifying the hysteria and expanding the reach of the accusations. This suggests the possibility of influence or shared experience among the girls, but also complicates efforts to understand the source of their distress.

The Putnam family is now deeply implicated in the accusations, with Ann Jr. among those claiming to be afflicted and naming new individuals. This is significant because of the family’s prominence and influence within the village, and could quickly escalate the situation.

Mary Walcott's testimony is becoming increasingly important, as she appears to corroborate the claims of other accusers. Her role suggests a network of shared experience, or at least shared narrative, among the girls.

While the initial accusations focused on marginalized members of the community, the expanding list now includes individuals with stronger ties to Salem Village society. This broadening of the scope raises questions about the motivations and patterns behind the accusations.

The magistrate, William Stoughton, continues to take depositions and gather information from the afflicted girls and other witnesses, attempting to establish a clear understanding of the events. The investigation remains largely focused on establishing the presence of witchcraft through spectral evidence and the girls’ testimonies.

Concern is growing among some villagers regarding the reliability of the girls’ accusations, but fear of being accused themselves appears to be silencing dissenting voices. This climate of fear is hindering open discussion and critical examination of the unfolding events.

newspapers.lede: The accusations against Martha Corey are sending shockwaves through Salem Village. Unlike Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne, Corey is a full covenanted church member, a woman of standing in the community. This morning, after Ann Putnam Junior named her specter as afflicting her, investigators Edward Putnam and Ezekiel Cheever went to question Mrs. Corey. Before they could even state their purpose, she declared, “I know what ye are coming for… Ye are come to talk with me about being a witch.” She then astounded them by asking, “But does she tell you what clothes I have on?”—a pointed reference to Ann Putnam, whom they had asked to describe Corey’s spectral attire. As the magistrate’s record shows, the child had claimed to be blinded and unable to see. Corey’s foreknowledge, coupled with what visitors described as a dismissive smile, is being interpreted as proof of her connection to the Devil. News of this encounter is, as one villager put it, “upon every tongue” already. This accusation expands the circle of suspicion beyond those already considered outsiders and introduces a dangerous new precedent.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the Salem Village crisis. Martha Corey, a respected member of the church, has been accused of witchcraft, expanding the scope of the accusations beyond the marginalized. Her immediate denial and foreknowledge of the investigators’ questions are raising suspicions, but also prompting questions about how information is traveling within the village.

Unlike Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne, Corey is a full covenanted church member, a woman of standing in the community. This morning, after Ann Putnam Junior named her specter as afflicting her, investigators Edward Putnam and Ezekiel Cheever went to question Mrs. Corey. Before they could even state their purpose, she declared, “I know what ye are coming for… Ye are come to talk with me about being a witch.” She then astounded them by asking, “But does she tell you what clothes I have on?”—a pointed reference to Ann Putnam, whom they had asked to describe Corey’s spectral attire. As the magistrate’s record shows, the child had claimed to be blinded and unable to see. Corey’s foreknowledge, coupled with what visitors described as a dismissive smile, is being interpreted as proof of her connection to the Devil. News of this encounter is, as one villager put it, “upon every tongue” already. This accusation expands the circle of suspicion beyond those already considered outsiders and introduces a dangerous new precedent.

Martha Corey, a respected church member, was accused of witchcraft today by Ann Putnam Junior, fundamentally changing the nature of the accusations. This expands the crisis beyond the previously accused outcasts and into the heart of the community, raising fears that anyone could be susceptible to the Devil’s influence.

Before investigators could question her, Martha Corey knew why they had come, stating, “I know what ye are coming for. Ye are come to talk with me about being a witch.” She then shrewdly inquired about the details of Ann Putnam’s vision, a question that has fueled suspicions about her supernatural knowledge.

The investigators, Edward Putnam and Ezekiel Cheever, noted that Corey “seemed to smile at it as if shee had showed us a pretty trick” when informed Ann Putnam claimed to be blinded, further solidifying their belief in her guilt. This reaction, while perhaps a display of defiance, is being interpreted as evidence of supernatural power.

Giles Corey, Martha’s husband, initially testified against her, reportedly speaking of disruptions to his prayers and strange occurrences in their home. This betrayal weighs heavily on him, and sources suggest it stems from fear and a desperate attempt to protect himself and his farm.

Martha Corey has publicly questioned the validity of the girls’ afflictions, stating, “We must not believe all that these distracted children say,” a position that has drawn the ire of Reverend Samuel Parris. Parris views her skepticism as direct evidence of her allegiance to the Devil.

The image of a “yellow bird” appears to be a recurring motif in the accusations, with Ann Putnam Jr. reporting seeing one on Martha Corey’s hand, similar to a previous sighting involving Goodwife Cloyce. This spectral familiar is a common trope in witchcraft lore, believed to be a demonic creature serving the witch.

Accounts suggest Martha Corey’s skepticism isn't based on blind faith, but on careful observation of the afflicted girls. She reportedly told others, “I watched them. I watched their eyes,” believing their behavior to be manufactured and amplified by adult attention.

The relationships between the accused are becoming clearer. Both Martha Corey and Giles share the experience of being condemned alongside Rebecca Nurse. Their shared fate speaks to a growing realization among the accused that church standing offers no protection from these accusations.

newspapers.lede: The gravity of the situation deepened today with the accusation against Rebecca Nurse, a woman widely regarded as pious and upstanding. Ann Putnam Junior, during a public examination, named Nurse as afflicting her, claiming to see her spirit tormenting the girl. This claim quickly gained traction, fueled by the existing tensions between the Nurse and Putnam families over land. Ann Putnam Senior then testified that the specter of Rebecca Nurse confessed to killing her sister, Mary Carr, several years prior. According to Senior’s testimony, this specter revealed, “I did kill her,” referring to her deceased sister. This accusation is particularly troubling, as it extends beyond simple affliction and introduces the charge of murder, linking the supposed witchcraft to real, historical grief and family disputes. The weight of this adult testimony lends a dangerous new credibility to the proceedings, potentially broadening the scope of accusations beyond the initial claims of young girls. The community is visibly shaken, as Nurse is known for her devout faith and good character.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the unfolding tragedy. Rebecca Nurse, a respected member of Salem Village, has been publicly accused of witchcraft by Ann Putnam Junior. This accusation, coming from a prominent family and alleging a long-held grievance, dramatically escalates the crisis and signals a shift toward accusations leveled by adults.

This accusation, coming from a prominent family and alleging a long-held grievance, dramatically escalates the crisis and signals a shift toward accusations leveled by adults.

According to Senior’s testimony, this specter revealed, “I did kill her,” referring to her deceased sister. This accusation is particularly troubling, as it extends beyond simple affliction and introduces the charge of murder, linking the supposed witchcraft to real, historical grief and family disputes. The weight of this adult testimony lends a dangerous new credibility to the proceedings, potentially broadening the scope of accusations beyond the initial claims of young girls. The community is visibly shaken, as Nurse is known for her devout faith and good character.

Rebecca Nurse, a seventy-one-year-old parishioner and wife of farmer Francis Nurse, was publicly accused of witchcraft today by Ann Putnam Junior. This accusation is especially significant because Nurse is a long-standing, respected member of the community, and her family has been embroiled in a land dispute with the Putnams for some time.

Ann Putnam Senior testified that Rebecca Nurse’s spirit confessed to the murder of her sister, Mary Carr, who died in 1688 after the loss of three children. This testimony connected the witchcraft accusations to a very real and longstanding family grievance.

The accusation against Nurse represents a shift in the nature of the complaints. Until today, the accusations primarily came from young girls. Ann Putnam Senior is the first adult woman to publicly accuse another of witchcraft, potentially lending more weight to the proceedings.

The testimony regarding Mary Carr’s death reveals a complicated family history. George Carr, Mary’s father and Ann’s father, disinherited Ann in his will, leaving his estate to his wife and sons. This familial conflict adds another layer of complexity to the accusations.

Despite the growing accusations, Rebecca Nurse maintains her innocence, proclaiming, as the magistrate’s record shows, “I can say before my Eternal Father I am innocent, and God will clear my innocence.”

Thirty-nine neighbors have already begun to circulate a petition defending Rebecca Nurse, attesting to her good character and piety. This demonstrates a growing division within the community regarding the validity of the accusations.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village continues to thicken with fear and accusation, but today saw a rare moment of direct challenge to the proceedings. John Proctor, a respected farmer, took matters into his own hands when his servant, Mary Warren, began to exhibit the same fits and contortions as other young women in the village. Proctor put Warren to work at the spinning wheel, declaring he would beat her if she continued her performance. Remarkably, the fits ceased under this strict supervision. He then openly questioned the entire spectacle, stating at Ingersoll’s tavern, “If they were let alone we should all be Devils and witches quickly. They should rather be had to the whipping post.” This pragmatism, however, has painted a target on Proctor’s back. As the magistrate’s record shows, he even went so far as to say, “Hang them! Hang them!” – a sentiment that will undoubtedly be used against him. Meanwhile, the girls themselves appear to be shifting blame, with accusations flying back and forth regarding who first identified Rebecca Nurse.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, John Proctor took a bold and controversial stand against the growing hysteria. He publicly questioned the afflicted girls and even threatened to beat his servant, Mary Warren, to stop her fits – a tactic that briefly worked. This defiance, however, has made Proctor a target, and the accusations are becoming increasingly circular among the girls themselves.

He publicly questioned the afflicted girls and even threatened to beat his servant, Mary Warren, to stop her fits – a tactic that briefly worked. This defiance, however, has made Proctor a target, and the accusations are becoming increasingly circular among the girls themselves.

John Proctor, a respected farmer, took matters into his own hands when his servant, Mary Warren, began to exhibit the same fits and contortions as other young women in the village. Proctor put Warren to work at the spinning wheel, declaring he would beat her if she continued her performance. Remarkably, the fits ceased under this strict supervision. He then openly questioned the entire spectacle, stating at Ingersoll’s tavern, “If they were let alone we should all be Devils and witches quickly. They should rather be had to the whipping post.” This pragmatism, however, has painted a target on Proctor’s back. As the magistrate’s record shows, he even went so far as to say, “Hang them! Hang them!” – a sentiment that will undoubtedly be used against him. John Proctor’s direct confrontation with Mary Warren and his public dismissal of the afflicted girls’ claims are the most significant developments today. He stopped Warren’s fits with hard labor and openly called the girls frauds, actions that have already marked him as a potential suspect in the eyes of the accusers.

The dynamic among the accusing girls is increasingly chaotic and self-serving. When questioned about how Ann Putnam Jr. first learned of Rebecca Nurse’s alleged involvement, Mercy Lewis and Ann Putnam each blamed the other for the identification, revealing a pattern of deflection and a lack of concrete evidence.

Reverend Samuel Parris has removed his daughter, Betty, from the parsonage and sent her to live with her cousin, Stephen Sewall, in Salem Town. Parris appears motivated by a desire to protect Betty from the escalating crisis within his own home, and thankfully, Betty is already reported to be recovering.

Proctor's actions suggest the afflictions may be influenced by external factors. The temporary cessation of Mary Warren’s fits when forced to work supports the idea that the symptoms are performative, or at least susceptible to social control.

The Tarbell deposition reveals that the girls’ accusations aren’t based on reliable testimony. The circular blaming between Mercy Lewis and Ann Putnam demonstrates the fragility and potentially fabricated nature of their claims.

Betty Parris's removal from the parsonage and subsequent recovery is a noteworthy development, hinting at the environmental factors contributing to the initial outbreak of affliction. Her eventual long life – she lived to be 77 years old – stands in stark contrast to the fate of others caught in this crisis.

newspapers.lede: The accusations in Salem Village took a somber and unsettling turn today. Until now, the afflicted have been primarily young women, their testimonies often dismissed as the product of youthful imagination or hysteria. But this afternoon, Ann Putnam Sr., a wife, mother, and respected member of the community, testified to being tormented by the specter of Rebecca Nurse. She described a terrifying encounter, saying, “On the 18th March 1692, I being wearied out in helping to tend my poor afflicted child and maid… I was almost pressed and choked to death… and presently I saw the shape of Rebecca Nurse… and she did immediately set upon me in a most dreadful manner.” This isn’t simply a claim of being frightened; Mrs. Putnam describes a physical assault by a spectral figure. The fact that an adult woman of her standing now corroborates the girls’ stories will undoubtedly deepen the fear and suspicion gripping the village. It also reveals a heart-wrenching context: Mrs. Putnam has recently suffered the loss of children and a sister, and now accuses Nurse’s specter of responsibility for those deaths.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the unfolding crisis. For the first time, an adult woman, Ann Putnam Sr., has come forward claiming to be afflicted by the specter of Rebecca Nurse. This lends significant weight to the accusations already made by the young girls and dramatically escalates the situation.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a turning point in the unfolding crisis. For the first time, an adult woman, Ann Putnam Sr., has come forward claiming to be afflicted by the specter of Rebecca Nurse. This lends significant weight to the accusations already made by the young girls and dramatically escalates the situation.

The accusations in Salem Village took a somber and unsettling turn today. Until now, the afflicted have been primarily young women, their testimonies often dismissed as the product of youthful imagination or hysteria. But this afternoon, Ann Putnam Sr., a wife, mother, and respected member of the community, testified to being tormented by the specter of Rebecca Nurse. She described a terrifying encounter, saying, “On the 18th March 1692, I being wearied out in helping to tend my poor afflicted child and maid… I was almost pressed and choked to death… and presently I saw the shape of Rebecca Nurse… and she did immediately set upon me in a most dreadful manner.” This isn’t simply a claim of being frightened; Mrs. Putnam describes a physical assault by a spectral figure. The fact that an adult woman of her standing now corroborates the girls’ stories will undoubtedly deepen the fear and suspicion gripping the village. It also reveals a heart-wrenching context: Mrs. Putnam has recently suffered the loss of children and a sister, and now accuses Nurse’s specter of responsibility for those deaths.

Ann Putnam Sr. became the first adult to claim spectral afflictions today, dramatically shifting the dynamic of the accusations. Until this point, the claims of girls like Abigail Williams and Betty Parris were often viewed with skepticism, but an adult woman of full church standing experiencing torment lends the crisis a new level of credibility and theological weight.

Mrs. Putnam’s deposition focuses heavily on the loss of loved ones. She accuses Rebecca Nurse’s specter not only of attacking her but also of causing the deaths of her children, her sister Mary Carr, and other villagers like Benjamin Holton, John Fuller, and Rebecca Shepherd. This suggests a deep well of personal grief is fueling the accusations.

The timing of Mrs. Putnam’s accusations is significant. Her sister, Mary Carr, died shortly before the trials began, and her deposition details how Nurse’s specter confessed to causing her sister’s death. This projection of real family loss onto the accused reveals a complex psychological dynamic at play.

The Putnam family has experienced considerable hardship in recent years, with multiple children dying young and a decline in their family fortune following King Philip’s War. This context underscores the depth of their grief and may explain the intensity of their accusations.

Ann Putnam Sr.’s testimony provides a third explanation for her family’s suffering, beyond the unbearable theological alternatives of God testing her faith or punishing her for sin. Witchcraft, in her view, offers a tangible, if terrifying, reason for the tragedies that have befallen her family.

The magistrate’s record of today’s examinations shows a clear effort to connect the spectral accusations to known deaths in the community. This strategy aims to demonstrate a pattern of malice and solidify the case against those accused of witchcraft.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village tightened significantly today as the authorities arrested Martha Corey, wife of farmer Giles Corey. Unlike earlier accused women like Tituba or Sarah Good, Goodwife Corey was a full and active member of the church, publicly questioning the girls’ claims of witchcraft. Constable Herrick took her from her home this afternoon. The arrest sent a clear message: respectability offers no protection. As the magistrate’s record reads from the warrant issued this day, the complaint alleges that Goodwife Corey has caused “much hurt and injury” to several young women, including Ann Putnam Jr. and Abigail Williams. Reverend Deodat Lawson arrived at Ingersoll’s Ordinary, summoned to observe these afflictions firsthand. Shortly after his arrival, Mary Walcott presented Lawson and others with what she claimed were bite marks on her wrist, inflicted by a spectral attacker. Lawson examined the marks by candlelight, a chilling moment in the village’s central gathering place, and documented his observations, setting the stage for the first published account of these events.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the accusations widened dramatically with the arrest of Martha Corey, a respected, full-communing member of the church. This signifies a dangerous new phase of the trials, proving that no one—not even pillars of the community—is safe from suspicion. Reverend Lawson also arrived in the village and witnessed what appeared to be physical evidence of the girls’ afflictions.

This signifies a dangerous new phase of the trials, proving that no one—not even pillars of the community—is safe from suspicion. Reverend Lawson also arrived in the village and witnessed what appeared to be physical evidence of the girls’ afflictions.

Unlike earlier accused women like Tituba or Sarah Good, Goodwife Corey was a full and active member of the church, publicly questioning the girls’ claims of witchcraft. Constable Herrick took her from her home this afternoon. The arrest sent a clear message: respectability offers no protection. As the magistrate’s record reads from the warrant issued this day, the complaint alleges that Goodwife Corey has caused “much hurt and injury” to several young women, including Ann Putnam Jr. and Abigail Williams. Reverend Deodat Lawson arrived at Ingersoll’s Ordinary, summoned to observe these afflictions firsthand. Shortly after his arrival, Mary Walcott presented Lawson and others with what she claimed were bite marks on her wrist, inflicted by a spectral attacker. Lawson examined the marks by candlelight, a chilling moment in the village’s central gathering place, and documented his observations, setting the stage for the first published account of these events.

The arrest of Martha Corey marks a turning point in the Salem Village crisis. Until today, those accused – Tituba, Sarah Good, Sarah Osborne – were social outsiders. Goodwife Corey’s standing as a full church member demonstrates that the accusations are now reaching into the heart of the community, and that anyone could be named.

Reverend Deodat Lawson witnessed what appeared to be physical evidence of the girls’ afflictions. Upon arriving at Ingersoll’s Ordinary, Mary Walcott displayed alleged bite marks to Lawson, adding a disturbing element to the already tense situation.

Giles Corey testified against his wife, Martha. He described strange occurrences, including his inability to pray when she was present and unsettling behavior from his ox, contributing to the evidence used against her. He would later deeply regret this testimony.

Ann Putnam Jr. was the first to name Martha Corey as an afflicting spirit, publicly accusing her of witchcraft and triggering her arrest. This accusation, coming from a respected young woman, carried significant weight within the community.

The Tarbell deposition reveals a level of coordination – or perhaps simply shared delusion – among the afflicted girls. When questioned about how Ann Putnam Jr. identified Rebecca Nurse as her tormentor, the girls shifted blame onto each other, saying “it was you that told her.”

Former minister Deodat Lawson is documenting the events, having returned to Salem Village after learning Tituba implicated witchcraft in the deaths of his wife and child. He is expected to preach a sermon tomorrow and will likely publish an account of the unfolding crisis.

The distance between the accusers, like the Putnam family, and the accused, like Rebecca Nurse, was surprisingly small. Their farms were often within sight of each other, and a walk between the properties took only 30 to 40 minutes, revealing the close-knit and often contentious nature of the community.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village is growing increasingly volatile. This morning, former minister Deodat Lawson attempted to preach a sermon, but was repeatedly interrupted by Abigail Williams and other afflicted girls. During the service, Williams pointed at Martha Corey, accusing her of spectral mischief, shouting, “Look where Goodwife Cloyce sits on the beam suckling her yellow bird between her fingers!” and claiming the creature then flew onto Lawson’s hat. This public display marked a turning point, as the girls demonstrated their willingness to disrupt even a religious service and accuse a full church member. The spectacle unfolded before a packed meetinghouse, filled with villagers and visitors alike. As the magistrate’s record shows, the girls’ behavior signaled a new level of boldness, suggesting no one—not even a minister in his pulpit—was safe from their accusations. Later, John Proctor vehemently denounced the trials, declaring, “If they are let alone we should all be Devils and witches quickly. They should rather be had to the whipping post. Hang them! Hang them!”—a testament to the growing desperation and anger within the village.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the crisis deepened as accusations broadened to include respected members of the church. During Reverend Deodat Lawson’s sermon, the afflicted girls disrupted the service, targeting Martha Corey, while John Proctor publicly denounced the trials and attempted to control his servant, Mary Warren. Samuel Parris’s sermon authorized accusing church members, escalating the fear and uncertainty gripping the community.

Today in Salem Village, the crisis deepened as accusations broadened to include respected members of the church. During Reverend Deodat Lawson’s sermon, the afflicted girls disrupted the service, targeting Martha Corey, while John Proctor publicly denounced the trials and attempted to control his servant, Mary Warren. Samuel Parris’s sermon authorized accusing church members, escalating the fear and uncertainty gripping the community.

The atmosphere in Salem Village is growing increasingly volatile. This morning, former minister Deodat Lawson attempted to preach a sermon, but was repeatedly interrupted by Abigail Williams and other afflicted girls. During the service, Williams pointed at Martha Corey, accusing her of spectral mischief, shouting, “Look where Goodwife Cloyce sits on the beam suckling her yellow bird between her fingers!” and claiming the creature then flew onto Lawson’s hat. This public display marked a turning point, as the girls demonstrated their willingness to disrupt even a religious service and accuse a full church member. The spectacle unfolded before a packed meetinghouse, filled with villagers and visitors alike. As the magistrate’s record shows, the girls’ behavior signaled a new level of boldness, suggesting no one—not even a minister in his pulpit—was safe from their accusations. Later, John Proctor vehemently denounced the trials, declaring, “If they are let alone we should all be Devils and witches quickly. They should rather be had to the whipping post. Hang them! Hang them!”—a testament to the growing desperation and anger within the village.

As we’ve just heard, the accusations have now extended to Martha Corey, a respected member of the church, during a disruptive sermon by Deodat Lawson. Abigail Williams publicly accused Corey of witchcraft, demonstrating an alarming escalation of the crisis and signaling that no one is safe from accusation.

Samuel Parris delivered a sermon today that effectively authorized the accusation of church members, arguing that the Devil prefers to use respectable people as instruments. He stated, “In this very church, Christ knows how many devils there are,” and warned that more names would be revealed, further fueling the paranoia and fear within the community.

John Proctor attempted to control his servant, Mary Warren, fearing she would report information to the authorities. According to testimony, he “kept her close to the spinning wheel & threatened to thresh her,” revealing his desperation to contain her and prevent her from contributing to the growing accusations.

Ann Putnam Sr. revealed that she believes Rebecca Nurse’s specter caused the death of her sister, Mary Carr, a loss stemming from the hardships of King Philip’s War. This suggests a pattern of projecting personal grief and resentment onto the accused, framing past tragedies as spectral attacks.

The afflicted girls disrupted Reverend Lawson’s sermon not only with verbal accusations but also with physical fits, turning the meetinghouse into a stage for their accusations. This was the first time such behavior occurred during worship, transforming a sacred space into a center of fear and suspicion.

Parris referenced Tituba’s confession, stating that she named nine individuals connected to the Devil, but only three have been identified so far. This created a sense of urgency and expectation for further accusations, as the community now believes six hidden witches remain among them.

John Hale was in attendance at Parris's sermon, observing the proceedings with a grave expression. While aligned with the core belief in spectral evidence, some indication suggests he is beginning to question the methods being employed.

Villagers reacted to Parris’s sermon with fear and anxiety, rushing to leave the meetinghouse as soon as it concluded. The sermon’s message—that even church members could be in league with the Devil—deepened the sense of paranoia and distrust within the community.

newspapers.lede: The meetinghouse was packed this morning as Martha Corey faced accusations of witchcraft. This was the first examination of a woman in full communion with the church, and the proceedings were unlike any seen before. As Magistrate Hathorne questioned her, the afflicted girls began to mimic Corey’s every move. When she clasped her hands, they cried out in pain, claiming their own hands were being crushed. When she bit her lip, they screamed they were being bitten. Corey, a woman of strong character, maintained her innocence, declaring, “I am an innocent person: I never had to do with Witchcraft since I was born. I am a Gospel Woman.” But her composure and even laughter were twisted into evidence against her. According to Reverend Deodat Lawson, who witnessed the examination, the scene was chaotic, with spectators even resorting to physical aggression. Goodwife Bathsheba Pope, one of the afflicted, “threw her Muff at her, and then her Shoe” during the questioning. This day reveals how easily observation could be manipulated and how quickly skepticism could be deemed a sign of guilt.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Martha Corey, a respected church member, underwent a grueling examination before magistrates and a large crowd. The afflicted girls claimed her movements caused them pain, mirroring her gestures in dramatic fashion. This marks a significant escalation as accusations now target individuals previously considered beyond suspicion.

Today in Salem Village, Martha Corey, a respected church member, underwent a grueling examination before magistrates and a large crowd. The afflicted girls claimed her movements caused them pain, mirroring her gestures in dramatic fashion. This marks a significant escalation as accusations now target individuals previously considered beyond suspicion.

This was the first examination of a woman in full communion with the church, and the proceedings were unlike any seen before. As Magistrate Hathorne questioned her, the afflicted girls began to mimic Corey’s every move. When she clasped her hands, they cried out in pain, claiming their own hands were being crushed. When she bit her lip, they screamed they were being bitten. Corey, a woman of strong character, maintained her innocence, declaring, “I am an innocent person: I never had to do with Witchcraft since I was born. I am a Gospel Woman.” But her composure and even laughter were twisted into evidence against her. According to Reverend Deodat Lawson, who witnessed the examination, the scene was chaotic, with spectators even resorting to physical aggression. Goodwife Bathsheba Pope, one of the afflicted, “threw her Muff at her, and then her Shoe” during the questioning. This day reveals how easily observation could be manipulated and how quickly skepticism could be deemed a sign of guilt.

Martha Corey’s examination today represented a turning point in the trials. Previously, accusations had largely focused on marginalized members of the community. Corey, a respected member of the church, now finds herself accused, signaling a dangerous expansion of the scope of these proceedings.

The girls’ mirroring of Corey’s movements was a key element of the prosecution’s case. Every gesture she made, from clasping her hands to simply moving her feet, was instantly reflected in the afflicted girls’ fits, creating an impossible situation for Corey to defend herself against.

The atmosphere inside the meetinghouse was volatile and hostile. Goodwife Pope’s outburst, throwing her muff and shoe at Corey, demonstrates the level of animosity and the breakdown of decorum during the examination.

Mercy Lewis claimed to have witnessed a spectral attack during the examination, shouting "There's a man he whispered in her ear," adding to the mounting accusations and fueling the hysteria.

Ann Putnam Jr. accused Corey of having a yellow bird suckling on her hand, a bizarre detail that highlights the fantastical nature of the accusations and the willingness of the accusers to present outlandish claims as evidence.

Corey repeatedly asserted her innocence, stating, “We must not believe all that these distracted children say,” but her pleas were largely ignored by the magistrates and the assembled crowd.

Reverend Lawson’s detailed account of the examination provides a valuable near-contemporaneous record of the events, free from obvious bias, and confirms the extraordinary nature of the proceedings.

newspapers.lede: This morning, Constable Joseph Herrick arrived at the home of seventy-one-year-old Rebecca Nurse, a woman long known for her devotion and good standing in Salem Village. She was taken into custody on a warrant alleging witchcraft, a charge stemming from accusations made by both Ann Putnam Senior and her daughter, Ann Putnam Junior. The arrest has sent shockwaves through the community, as Nurse is hardly the sort one would expect to be accused. As the magistrate’s record reads, the warrant states the complaint was made “against Rebeca Nurce…for vehement Suspition, of haveing Committed Sundry acts of Witchcraft.” This is not simply an accusation against an outsider or a social pariah; this is a challenge to the very foundations of our community’s understanding of who might be susceptible to the Devil’s influence. Rebecca herself, reportedly confused and partially deaf, is said to have responded to the charge with a plea of innocence, stating she was “as innocent as the child unborn.” The arrest of Rebecca Nurse signifies a dangerous escalation of this crisis.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the Salem crisis. Rebecca Nurse, a highly respected member of this community, has been accused of witchcraft and arrested. This development is deeply unsettling, as Nurse is considered a pious woman, and her accusation suggests the reach of these claims is widening beyond the marginalized.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a turning point in the Salem crisis. Rebecca Nurse, a highly respected member of this community, has been accused of witchcraft and arrested. This development is deeply unsettling, as Nurse is considered a pious woman, and her accusation suggests the reach of these claims is widening beyond the marginalized.

This morning, Constable Joseph Herrick arrived at the home of seventy-one-year-old Rebecca Nurse, a woman long known for her devotion and good standing in Salem Village. She was taken into custody on a warrant alleging witchcraft, a charge stemming from accusations made by both Ann Putnam Senior and her daughter, Ann Putnam Junior. The arrest has sent shockwaves through the community, as Nurse is hardly the sort one would expect to be accused. As the magistrate’s record reads, the warrant states the complaint was made “against Rebeca Nurce…for vehement Suspition, of haveing Committed Sundry acts of Witchcraft.” This is not simply an accusation against an outsider or a social pariah; this is a challenge to the very foundations of our community’s understanding of who might be susceptible to the Devil’s influence. Rebecca herself, reportedly confused and partially deaf, is said to have responded to the charge with a plea of innocence, stating she was “as innocent as the child unborn.” The arrest of Rebecca Nurse signifies a dangerous escalation of this crisis.

The arrest of Rebecca Nurse is the most significant development today. The accusations against her, coming from both Ann Putnam Senior and Junior, have deeply shaken the village, as she was widely considered a pious and respected woman.

Four- and five-year-old Dorcas Good is now imprisoned, accused of witchcraft and reportedly confessed to having a familiar – a small snake – as evidenced by a mark on her finger. She has been held in chains for months and is said to be permanently damaged by the experience.

The roots of this conflict run deeper than simple belief. A long-standing land dispute between the Nurse and Putnam families appears to be fueling the accusations, with the Putnam patriarch seemingly motivated by resentment over property lines.

Rebecca Nurse is part of a larger network of accused women from the Towne family of Topsfield: her sisters, Mary Easty and Sarah Cloyce, have also been named as witches, suggesting a targeted pattern of accusation.

Constable Herrick’s arrest of Rebecca Nurse marks a shift in his role – from simply serving warrants to arresting a pillar of the community, a task that likely weighed heavily on him.

Before her arrest, Rebecca Nurse spent her afternoons reading her Bible and sewing, with her sisters often visiting. These quiet, domestic scenes stand in stark contrast to the accusations now leveled against her.

Ann Putnam Senior’s grief over the loss of her own children seems to be intertwined with her accusations, as she claims Nurse’s specter was responsible for the deaths of others.

A woman’s reputation in Salem Village is carefully constructed and maintained by other women, and can quickly become a matter of life and death when accusations of witchcraft arise.

newspapers.lede: The events of today demonstrate how quickly the accusations are spreading and reaching into the most respected families in Salem Village. This morning, four-year-old Dorcas Good was taken into custody, accused of witchcraft. Authorities are interpreting her childish utterances as a confession, a disturbing development given her age. Later, Rebecca Nurse faced examination by magistrates. She vehemently denied the charges, declaring, “I can say before my Eternal father I am innocent, & God will clear my innocence.” However, during the proceedings, the afflicted girls reportedly mimicked her movements—when Nurse turned her head, they turned theirs—a spectacle that fueled the accusations. Reverend Deodat Lawson, who served as minister here years ago, was present for the examination and has since confirmed the afflictions are real. As Parris notes, Lawson's return and confirmation of events are critical, offering outside validation to a community consumed by fear. The crisis is no longer confined to the margins; it has reached the heart of Salem Village.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the unfolding crisis. Four-year-old Dorcas Good was arrested, and Rebecca Nurse, a respected member of the community, underwent a rigorous examination. Reverend Deodat Lawson, a former minister, returned to Salem Village and witnessed the afflictions firsthand, lending outside validation to the growing panic.

Today marks a turning point in the unfolding crisis. Four-year-old Dorcas Good was arrested, and Rebecca Nurse, a respected member of the community, underwent a rigorous examination. Reverend Deodat Lawson, a former minister, returned to Salem Village and witnessed the afflictions firsthand, lending outside validation to the growing panic.

The events of today demonstrate how quickly the accusations are spreading and reaching into the most respected families in Salem Village. This morning, four-year-old Dorcas Good was taken into custody, accused of witchcraft. Authorities are interpreting her childish utterances as a confession, a disturbing development given her age. Later, Rebecca Nurse faced examination by magistrates. She vehemently denied the charges, declaring, “I can say before my Eternal father I am innocent, & God will clear my innocence.” However, during the proceedings, the afflicted girls reportedly mimicked her movements—when Nurse turned her head, they turned theirs—a spectacle that fueled the accusations. Reverend Deodat Lawson, who served as minister here years ago, was present for the examination and has since confirmed the afflictions are real. As Parris notes, Lawson's return and confirmation of events are critical, offering outside validation to a community consumed by fear. The crisis is no longer confined to the margins; it has reached the heart of Salem Village.

Rebecca Nurse underwent examination today, maintaining her innocence despite accusations and the strange behavior of the afflicted girls. She declared herself “as clear as the child unborn,” but the girls reportedly mirrored her movements, further solidifying the belief that she was tormenting them.

Dorcas Good, only four years old, was arrested and imprisoned. Her words are being interpreted as a confession, raising serious concerns about the fairness of the proceedings and the vulnerability of children in this climate of fear.

Reverend Deodat Lawson returned to Salem Village today, preaching a sermon and witnessing the afflictions firsthand. His presence and confirmation of the events lend outside validation to the crisis and are likely to exacerbate the growing panic.

The petition in defense of Rebecca Nurse has gathered 39 signatures, demonstrating a significant level of support for the accused amongst her neighbors. This includes Captain John Putnam Sr., who is breaking with his own family’s stance on the matter.

We are learning more about those involved. Francis Nurse, Rebecca’s husband, is a seventy-four-year-old farmer who has spent decades building a prosperous life. His hands bear the marks of hard work, and he is now tirelessly gathering support for his wife.

Daniel Andrew, a wharf builder and member of the committee that opposed Reverend Parris’s salary, is now himself accused of witchcraft and has fled the village. This illustrates the connection between political opposition and the current accusations.

Jurors are now physically examining accused women, searching for what they believe are “preternatural marks” or witch’s teats—a practice that blends medical assessment with folk magic and lacks any basis in sound reasoning.

The Nurse family has a long history of defending their reputation through legal means, having won defamation judgments in the past. However, the current situation is different—the court is no longer protecting reputation but actively dismantling it.

newspapers.lede: Tensions escalated sharply today as skepticism met accusation head-on. John Proctor, a farmer and tavern keeper known for his independent spirit, publicly denounced the afflicted girls, going so far as to say they “should be hanged” and threatening his servant, Mary Warren, with a beating if she continued her fits. Witnesses reported Proctor believed that keeping Warren occupied with work stopped the fits, and he openly expressed his disbelief in the entire process, stating, “if they were let alone so we should all be Devils & witches quickly.” His defiance has undoubtedly marked him as a target. Simultaneously, Reverend Samuel Parris publicly rebuked Mary Sibley during Sunday service for directing Tituba to bake the so-called witch cake. According to accounts, Parris condemned her actions as “going to the Devil for help against the Devil,” prompting Sibley to publicly weep and confess her error. This public shaming underscores the village’s determination to control not only accusations of witchcraft but also the methods used to diagnose it.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the fallout from Rebecca Nurse’s examination continues to widen the circle of accusation. John Proctor openly challenged the proceedings, drawing attention to himself, while Mary Sibley faced public rebuke for her involvement with the initial witch cake—a troubling sign of how deeply folk remedies are implicated in the current crisis.
