Rebecca Nurse, facing renewed scrutiny, reportedly responded to the accusations with, “What, do these persons give in evidence against me now? They used to come among us,” a statement the court twisted to suggest attendance at witch meetings.

Deliverance Hobbs confessed to witchcraft today and will now serve as a key witness against others, particularly in the case of Rebecca Nurse. Her testimony at Nurse’s trial proved pivotal, with a confusing exchange regarding who “came among us” leading to the jury reversing their initial not-guilty verdict.

Edward and Sarah Bishop Sr. of Salem Village were accused today, demonstrating how the accusations are cascading through families. Sarah Bishop was already under suspicion due to her practice of folk healing, which brought unwanted attention from the afflicted.

Nehemiah Abbott Jr. of Topsfield is the only person thus far to be released after examination. The afflicted girls could not unanimously identify him as the source of their torment, leading to his freedom—a unique occurrence in these proceedings.

Mary Esty, sister to Rebecca Nurse and Sarah Cloyce, firmly maintained her innocence during her examination, declaring, “I can say before Christ Jesus, I am free.” However, like others, her fate remains uncertain, as the accusers can always revive fits and demand a re-arrest.

Sarah Wildes was examined alongside several others, and the afflicted girls immediately fell into fits upon seeing her, confirming their belief in her guilt. Her stepdaughter and stepdaughter-in-law have also been accused, illustrating how family connections are intensifying suspicion.

William Hobbs was also examined today, and while he protested his innocence, stating he was “as clear as a new born babe,” the afflicted girls fell into fits during the questioning, suggesting their continued torment.

The examination of Sarah Wildes, Mary Easty, and Nehemiah Abbott took place as part of a mass examination, illustrating the scale of the ongoing investigations and the pressure on the court to find those responsible for the afflictions.

newspapers.lede: The reach of the accusations continues to expand beyond Salem Village, now drawing in residents of Woburn and Beverly. This morning, news arrived that Ann Sears of Woburn has been accused of witchcraft, her case connected to the earlier accusations involving the Dustin family of Reading. Details remain scarce, but the pattern is clear: the crisis is no longer contained. Simultaneously, reports confirm that Sarah Morrill of Beverly is also facing charges. As the magistrate’s record reads from a preliminary hearing, a witness claimed that Morrill’s “specter did afflict” several individuals in the town. These accusations, coming from outside of Salem, highlight the growing hysteria and suggest that the supposed affliction is spreading beyond the immediate vicinity. It seems no community is safe from suspicion, and the number of the accused is growing daily.
newspapers.summary: Today brings more accusations from neighboring towns, widening the scope of the trials beyond Salem Village and Salem Town. Ann Sears of Woburn and Sarah Morrill of Beverly join the growing number of those accused of witchcraft, demonstrating how fear is spreading throughout Massachusetts Bay. These cases, though less documented, reflect the escalating panic.

Ann Sears of Woburn and Sarah Morrill of Beverly join the growing number of those accused of witchcraft, demonstrating how fear is spreading throughout Massachusetts Bay. These cases, though less documented, reflect the escalating panic.

These accusations, coming from outside of Salem, highlight the growing hysteria and suggest that the supposed affliction is spreading beyond the immediate vicinity. It seems no community is safe from suspicion, and the number of the accused is growing daily.

Ann Sears of Woburn and Sarah Morrill of Beverly were both accused of witchcraft today, extending the reach of the trials into neighboring communities and indicating a broader panic than previously understood. These accusations, though currently lacking detailed documentation, further demonstrate the rapidly expanding scope of the crisis.

The connection of Ann Sears’s case to the Dustin family of Reading suggests that accusations are spreading along existing social networks and potentially fueled by pre-existing disputes. This points toward the complex origins of the charges, beyond simple religious belief.

Sarah Morrill of Beverly is currently imprisoned, awaiting further examination, and represents yet another individual caught in the web of accusations. The lack of formal indictment at this stage does little to alleviate the fear surrounding her case.

While the examinations in Salem Village continue to draw the most attention, these new accusations signal that the crisis is becoming a regional phenomenon, straining the resources of the courts and magistrates.

The limited documentation available regarding both Sears and Morrill’s cases demonstrates that not every accusation receives the same level of scrutiny, raising questions about the consistency of the proceedings.

Despite the growing number of accused, no trials have yet concluded, and the afflicted parties continue to offer their testimonies, further fueling the frenzy of accusations.

newspapers.lede: The accusations are multiplying rapidly. This morning, warrants were issued for the arrest of several new individuals, including Reverend George Burroughs, a former minister here in Salem Village. Burroughs, who left Salem Village several years ago after a dispute over his salary, is now accused of being ‘the ringleader of them all,’ according to the afflicted girls. The complaint, filed by Thomas Putnam and Jonathan Walcott, speaks to a longstanding resentment. Also accused today are Susannah Martin, a widow from Amesbury, and Philip English, a wealthy merchant from Salem Town. English, however, appears to have anticipated the warrant. As the magistrate’s record reads regarding his case, “Phillip English not beeing to bee found,” indicating he’s already evaded arrest. This highlights a growing pattern: those with resources may be able to avoid the legal process, while others are left vulnerable. The warrants detail the charges – ‘Sundry acts of Witchcraft’ – and demand the accused be brought before the magistrates for examination. The language is broad, and the evidence, so far, relies heavily on the testimonies of the afflicted girls.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a surge of new accusations in the widening crisis. Reverend George Burroughs, formerly of Salem Village, has been formally accused, as have several individuals from neighboring towns, including Reading and Beverly. The reach of these accusations is expanding, demonstrating how fear is spreading beyond Salem Village itself.

Reverend George Burroughs, formerly of Salem Village, has been formally accused, as have several individuals from neighboring towns, including Reading and Beverly. The reach of these accusations is expanding, demonstrating how fear is spreading beyond Salem Village itself.

As the magistrate’s record reads regarding his case, “Phillip English not beeing to bee found,” indicating he’s already evaded arrest. This highlights a growing pattern: those with resources may be able to avoid the legal process, while others are left vulnerable. The warrants detail the charges – ‘Sundry acts of Witchcraft’ – and demand the accused be brought before the magistrates for examination. The language is broad, and the evidence, so far, relies heavily on the testimonies of the afflicted girls.

Reverend George Burroughs was formally accused of witchcraft today, reopening old wounds from his time as minister in Salem Village. The accusations stem from the afflicted girls and suggest a long-held animosity, possibly connected to financial disputes and a perceived challenge to the Putnam family’s authority.

Philip English, a wealthy merchant and the owner of numerous properties and vessels, has been accused but appears to have fled before authorities could apprehend him. This demonstrates that economic means may offer a path to escape the accusations—a path unavailable to most.

Susannah Martin, a 71-year-old widow from Amesbury, was also accused today, illustrating how the reach of the accusations extends far beyond Salem Village and into neighboring communities. She has faced prior accusations.

Dorcas Hoar of Beverly has confessed to witchcraft, reportedly to save her life before a scheduled execution. This confession, though obtained under duress, adds to the growing narrative of demonic influence.

Bethia Carter Sr. and Bethia Carter Jr. of Woburn have been accused, showing the accusations are radiating outwards along social and geographic networks from the initial focus in Salem Village and Reading.

Lydia Dustin of Reading, already accused in the past, faces renewed charges today. Her case, along with that of her daughter Sarah, highlights how longstanding reputations for witchcraft can resurface during times of crisis.

Sheriff Corwin is actively seizing the property of the accused, including Philip English’s extensive holdings. This practice raises questions about potential conflicts of interest and the economic motivations driving the investigations.

The arrest warrants issued today demonstrate the broad scope of the accusations and the reliance on the testimonies of a small group of young women as primary evidence.

newspapers.lede: The air here in Salem Village is thick with uncertainty, and not just from the spring planting. For three years, since the overthrow of Governor Andros in 1689, Massachusetts has existed in a kind of legal limbo. The old charter was gone, the Dominion of New England overthrown, but no new authority had arrived to take its place. Courts operated with questionable legitimacy, and land titles remained in dispute. This vacuum fueled the anxieties that have gripped the village, and it's why Governor Phips’s arrival is so keenly anticipated. But even with him coming, there is no court authorized to try those accused of witchcraft. Meanwhile, tensions are escalating. Deputy Constable John Willard has refused to make further arrests, believing the accused are innocent, and has himself been accused. As the magistrate’s record reads, Ann Putnam Jr. and others promptly named his specter, escalating the cycle of accusation. And Nathaniel Ingersoll, a deacon and tavern keeper, continues to file complaints against those accused, even as his own wife reportedly heard the afflicted girls admit their claims might be false — “for sport, they could say such-and-such about so-and-so, or that person bewitched them.” The contradictions are stark, and the consequences, dire.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the crisis. With Governor Phips expected any day, the legal landscape of Massachusetts remains unstable after three years without a valid charter. Accusations continue, but now intersect with a desperate need for planting season labor, and a growing defiance of the legal process itself.

Today marks a turning point in the crisis. With Governor Phips expected any day, the legal landscape of Massachusetts remains unstable after three years without a valid charter. Accusations continue, but now intersect with a desperate need for planting season labor, and a growing defiance of the legal process itself.

The air here in Salem Village is thick with uncertainty, and not just from the spring planting. For three years, since the overthrow of Governor Andros in 1689, Massachusetts has existed in a kind of legal limbo. The old charter was gone, the Dominion of New England overthrown, but no new authority had arrived to take its place. Courts operated with questionable legitimacy, and land titles remained in dispute. This vacuum fueled the anxieties that have gripped the village, and it's why Governor Phips’s arrival is so keenly anticipated. But even with him coming, there is no court authorized to try those accused of witchcraft. Meanwhile, tensions are escalating. Deputy Constable John Willard has refused to make further arrests, believing the accused are innocent, and has himself been accused. As the magistrate’s record reads, Ann Putnam Jr. and others promptly named his specter, escalating the cycle of accusation. And Nathaniel Ingersoll, a deacon and tavern keeper, continues to file complaints against those accused, even as his own wife reportedly heard the afflicted girls admit their claims might be false — “for sport, they could say such-and-such about so-and-so, or that person bewitched them.” The contradictions are stark, and the consequences, dire.

As we discussed, Massachusetts has been operating under a legal cloud for three years. The overthrow of Andros in 1689 left a gap in governance, with no new charter issued until Governor Phips’s expected arrival. This lack of legal clarity has exacerbated the existing anxieties and contributed to the escalating accusations.

Philip English, a prominent Salem Town merchant, has lost a staggering 1,183 pounds worth of property seized by Sheriff Corwin. This seizure reveals the economic impact of the accusations, and how readily property is taken from those accused, disrupting trade and livelihoods. His goods list shows Salem Town’s extensive commerce, with sloops carrying goods to Virginia and Barbados, and returning with staples.

John Willard’s refusal to continue making arrests has led to his own accusation. This highlights the escalating paranoia and the way anyone questioning the process quickly becomes a target. He reportedly laughed nervously while reciting the Lord’s Prayer after being captured, saying, “I think I am bewitcht as well as they.”

Mercy Lewis continues to play a pivotal role, providing testimony against both George Burroughs and Captain John Alden Jr. She strategically targeted Alden, possibly due to a past grievance involving ammunition sales to Native Americans. Yet, notably, she spared a relative by marriage, Sarah Cloyce, demonstrating a complex understanding of village relationships.

Mary Warren’s testimony against John and Elizabeth Proctor reveals the tragic consequences of the afflicted girls’ power. After briefly speaking out against the accusations, she has now helped condemn the very people who tried to support her, stating, in her later reflection, “I helped kill the man who tried to save me.”

The timing of this crisis coincides with the most crucial planting season. Every hour lost to court proceedings, accusations, or fear is an hour taken away from ensuring the colony’s food supply. Farmers serving as constables or witnesses are neglecting their fields, creating a slow-motion catastrophe for the village economy.

Rogation Sunday, traditionally a time for the community to walk field boundaries and bless the crops, is taking on a new, tense significance. In a village fractured by land disputes, even this ancient practice has become fraught with political implications.

The arrival of the new charter will inevitably bring changes to Massachusetts governance. While it confirms land titles, it also ends the era of self-governance, with the Crown now appointing the governor and other key officials, a fact that many colonists view with apprehension.

newspapers.lede: The crisis deepens as the Court of Oyer and Terminer officially began its work here in Salem Village today. The court, led by magistrates John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin, immediately focused on examining those already accused. The most remarkable case unfolded with Susannah Martin, a woman from Amesbury, over thirty miles distant. Upon entering the meetinghouse, Martin’s presence immediately triggered fits among the afflicted girls. Ann Putnam Jr. even threw a glove at her. When questioned about the girls’ suffering, Martin offered a defiant reply: “I do not know.” And when pressed if she believed they were bewitched, she shockingly stated, “No, I do not think they are.” Even more telling was her response to accusations of malice. As the magistrate's record reads, when accused of laughing at the afflicted, Martin retorted, “Well I may at such folly.” This wasn't the panicked confession the court expected, but a cool, almost philosophical dismissal of the proceedings. Martin’s refusal to validate the girls’ claims, combined with her open contempt, sealed her fate, even as it revealed the dangerous logic at the heart of these trials.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the Salem trials. The newly appointed Court of Oyer and Terminer has convened, and the first examinations are underway, most notably that of Susannah Martin of Amesbury, who boldly challenged the court and the accusations against her. Several others were arrested and committed to Boston jail.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a turning point in the Salem trials. The newly appointed Court of Oyer and Terminer has convened, and the first examinations are underway, most notably that of Susannah Martin of Amesbury, who boldly challenged the court and the accusations against her. Several others were arrested and committed to Boston jail.

The crisis deepens as the Court of Oyer and Terminer officially began its work here in Salem Village today. The court, led by magistrates John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin, immediately focused on examining those already accused. The most remarkable case unfolded with Susannah Martin, a woman from Amesbury, over thirty miles distant. Upon entering the meetinghouse, Martin’s presence immediately triggered fits among the afflicted girls. Ann Putnam Jr. even threw a glove at her. When questioned about the girls’ suffering, Martin offered a defiant reply: “I do not know.” And when pressed if she believed they were bewitched, she shockingly stated, “No, I do not think they are.” Even more telling was her response to accusations of malice. As the magistrate's record reads, when accused of laughing at the afflicted, Martin retorted, “Well I may at such folly.” This wasn't the panicked confession the court expected, but a cool, almost philosophical dismissal of the proceedings. Martin’s refusal to validate the girls’ claims, combined with her open contempt, sealed her fate, even as it revealed the dangerous logic at the heart of these trials.

The examination of Susannah Martin dominated the day, revealing a new level of defiance from the accused. Martin, a widow from Amesbury, not only denied bewitching the girls but questioned the very premise of the accusations, asserting, “No, I do not think they are” bewitched—an answer that angered the court.

Authorities ordered the arrest of Lydia Dustin and Dorcas Hoar today, along with Sarah Morey, and committed them to the jail in Boston. A warrant was issued for Dustin, and all four women now await further examination or trial.

Dorcas Hoar’s examination also took place today, with the afflicted girls claiming she had afflicted them for over a week and even accusing her of murdering her husband; Hoar vehemently denied the charges.

The case of Susannah Martin is significant because it demonstrates how far the reach of these accusations has extended beyond Salem Village. Her home in Amesbury is over thirty miles away, showing that geography is no barrier to these charges.

Abigail Williams testified against Susannah Martin today, adding her voice to the growing chorus of accusations. Her testimony, like those of the other afflicted girls, relies on spectral evidence – visions of Martin allegedly tormenting her.

Sarah Morey of Beverly was examined today but, unlike the others, was released and never formally indicted, an unusual outcome in this escalating crisis.

The swift action of the court, with examinations and arrests occurring within days of each other, highlights the speed and fervor with which these proceedings are unfolding.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with anticipation today as Sir William Phips, the newly appointed governor of Massachusetts Bay, has arrived to oversee the unfolding crisis. Simultaneously, the Court of Oyer and Terminer – a special court established to hear the cases of suspected witchcraft – has begun its sessions. Inside the Salem prison, the examination of Deliverance Hobbs unfolded, shedding light on the complex claims of spectral influence. Hobbs repeatedly insisted she had resisted temptation, but admitted to signing a document at the urging of Goody Oliver, also known as Bishop. As the magistrate’s record reads, when questioned about further trouble, Hobbs stated, “yes S’r, but I have nott done itt, nor will nott doe itt.” But pressed further, she confessed to being tempted by Goody Wilds and ultimately to signing the document initially, admitting, “Yes. I did itt is True.” The court seems determined to understand not just *if* Hobbs engaged in witchcraft, but *how* she yielded to temptation and who influenced her actions.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the trials. Sir William Phips has arrived in Salem, and the Court of Oyer and Terminer has formally convened, signaling a new, more official phase of the proceedings. Meanwhile, the examination of Deliverance Hobbs reveals continued accusations of spectral temptation and coercion.

Meanwhile, the examination of Deliverance Hobbs reveals continued accusations of spectral temptation and coercion.

The atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with anticipation today as Sir William Phips, the newly appointed governor of Massachusetts Bay, has arrived to oversee the unfolding crisis. Simultaneously, the Court of Oyer and Terminer – a special court established to hear the cases of suspected witchcraft – has begun its sessions. Inside the Salem prison, the examination of Deliverance Hobbs unfolded, shedding light on the complex claims of spectral influence. Hobbs repeatedly insisted she had resisted temptation, but admitted to signing a document at the urging of Goody Oliver, also known as Bishop. As the magistrate’s record reads, when questioned about further trouble, Hobbs stated, “yes S’r, but I have nott done itt, nor will nott doe itt.” But pressed further, she confessed to being tempted by Goody Wilds and ultimately to signing the document initially, admitting, “Yes. I did itt is True.” The court seems determined to understand not just *if* Hobbs engaged in witchcraft, but *how* she yielded to temptation and who influenced her actions.

Sir William Phips’ arrival and the convening of the Court of Oyer and Terminer represent a significant escalation of the legal response to the accusations of witchcraft. This new court is intended to bring order to the proceedings, but also likely to accelerate the pace of examinations and trials.

The examination of Deliverance Hobbs highlights the recurring theme of spectral coercion, with Hobbs claiming she was pressured into signing a "book" by other accused witches, notably Goody Oliver. This suggests a network of alleged conspirators.

Hobbs’ testimony reveals a pattern of initial denial followed by admission of past actions, indicating the pressure placed upon the accused during examination. She claims to have been tempted “about fryday or Saturday nig[ht]” but insists she resisted.

Goody Wilds has been named by Hobbs as one who tempted her, adding another name to the growing list of individuals accused of spectral assault and witchcraft.

The court is intensely focused on determining the extent to which Hobbs yielded to temptation, seeking to understand the precise nature of her involvement. They want to know if she simply signed a document or actively participated in harming others.

The use of the term “Goody” – a contraction of “Goodwife” – is common for addressing women of lower social status. It's a reminder of the social dynamics at play during these trials, and how accusations often targeted marginalized members of the community.

newspapers.lede: This Sunday, the reach of the court extends far beyond Salem Village. Field Marshal Jonathan Partridge traveled over one hundred miles to Wells, Maine, and arrested Reverend George Burroughs while he was at dinner. Burroughs, a former minister here in Salem Village, is now accused of being the ringleader of all witchcraft in the area. The afflicted girls claim he is the “black man” who leads diabolical sacraments. This arrest represents a new and frightening turn in the proceedings. As the magistrate’s record shows, one of the afflicted stated, “Goody Burroughs does carry me up into the air,” and others corroborate a pattern of spectral attacks led by the former minister. The sheer distance traveled to apprehend Burroughs underscores the expanding scope of the investigations and the determination of those pursuing these charges. It also reveals how deeply rooted the accusations are becoming—reaching even those who have left the village. The weight of military authority behind these actions is also becoming apparent, with prominent figures like Sergeant Thomas Putnam and Captain Jonathan Walcott consistently involved in the complaints.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a dramatic escalation in the witch hunt with the arrest of Reverend George Burroughs, dragged from Maine and accused of leading the witches. The Putnam and Walcott families continue to exert significant influence over the accusations, and we're learning more about the economic pressures and personal grievances fueling this crisis.

This Sunday, the reach of the court extends far beyond Salem Village. Field Marshal Jonathan Partridge traveled over one hundred miles to Wells, Maine, and arrested Reverend George Burroughs while he was at dinner. Burroughs, a former minister here in Salem Village, is now accused of being the ringleader of all witchcraft in the area. The afflicted girls claim he is the “black man” who leads diabolical sacraments. This arrest represents a new and frightening turn in the proceedings. As the magistrate’s record shows, one of the afflicted stated, “Goody Burroughs does carry me up into the air,” and others corroborate a pattern of spectral attacks led by the former minister. The sheer distance traveled to apprehend Burroughs underscores the expanding scope of the investigations and the determination of those pursuing these charges. It also reveals how deeply rooted the accusations are becoming—reaching even those who have left the village. The weight of military authority behind these actions is also becoming apparent, with prominent figures like Sergeant Thomas Putnam and Captain Jonathan Walcott consistently involved in the complaints.

The arrest of Reverend George Burroughs is the most significant development today. He was taken from Maine, over 100 miles away, and brought back to Salem Village to face accusations of witchcraft and leading the alleged coven. This demonstrates the far-reaching nature of the investigation and the perceived threat posed by Burroughs.

The Putnam and Walcott families are central to the accusations. Their military rank and consistent involvement in complaints give weight to the proceedings, suggesting a structured effort to identify and prosecute those deemed enemies.

Puritan ministers, though educated and morally authoritative, were economically vulnerable. Several former Salem Village ministers, including Burroughs, struggled financially, and relied on the community for basic necessities, creating potential for resentment and dispute.

Mercy Lewis’s past trauma shapes her present accusations. Orphaned in a frontier raid and placed in the household of Thomas Putnam Jr., she has become a prolific accuser, and her testimony appears to align with the Putnam family’s grievances.

Thomas Putnam Jr. appears to be driven by long-standing land disputes. He filed a remarkable 43 complaints, targeting families who had previously opposed the Putnams in property claims.

Thomas Beadle’s tavern is serving as an informal jail. He is housing prisoners like Burroughs, Jacobs, and Easty upstairs and receiving payment for their upkeep, highlighting the economic incentives at play during the trials.

In the absence of sufficient coin, promissory notes functioned as currency, creating a network of debt and obligation that connected households. This system of credit, and the potential for default, may have contributed to existing tensions.

Prisoners are transported by horse and cart, often chained, to prevent “spectral flight.” The journey to Boston jail alone takes a full day, demonstrating the logistical challenges and harsh conditions faced by the accused.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with anticipation today as Sir William Phips, the newly appointed governor, has arrived in Massachusetts Bay. Simultaneously, the Court of Oyer and Terminer, established to try these cases of alleged witchcraft, has begun its work. One of its first warrants targeted Philip English, a Salem merchant accused by Captain Jonathan Walcott and Thomas Putnam. Authorities found English attempting to hide from arrest, “under a pile of dirty laundry,” according to the officer’s return on the warrant. Despite his efforts, both Philip and his wife, Mary, were apprehended and taken to Boston. It’s noteworthy that the couple later escaped to New York, a colony known to be far more skeptical of these proceedings, suggesting they anticipated little justice here. The warrant itself, dated this day, details the accusations brought by Walcott and Putnam, naming Ann Putnam, Marcy Lewis, and Susannah Sheldon as among those allegedly afflicted by English’s witchcraft.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the trials as Sir William Phips arrives in Massachusetts Bay and the Court of Oyer and Terminer officially convenes. The court’s first actions included a warrant for the arrest of merchant Philip English, who attempted to evade authorities—highlighting the growing fear and desperation within Salem Village.

Despite his efforts, both Philip and his wife, Mary, were apprehended and taken to Boston. The warrant itself, dated this day, details the accusations brought by Walcott and Putnam, naming Ann Putnam, Marcy Lewis, and Susannah Sheldon as among those allegedly afflicted by English’s witchcraft.

As we’ve just discussed, Philip English and his wife Mary were arrested today on charges of witchcraft. English’s attempt to evade capture underscores the escalating panic and the desperation of those accused. The warrant for his arrest, issued by the Court of Oyer and Terminer, specifically names several accusers and their alleged afflictions.

The official convening of the Court of Oyer and Terminer represents a major escalation of the crisis. With Sir William Phips now in the colony, the trials will proceed under a new level of authority, though whether that authority will bring fairness remains to be seen.

The arrival of Governor Phips is expected to bring some order to the proceedings, but also increases pressure to resolve the situation quickly. Many villagers hope for a swift end to the accusations and a return to normalcy, while others are eager to see the accused punished.

The fact that Philip and Mary English fled to New York demonstrates that not everyone believes the accusations, and that some are willing to risk everything to escape the court’s reach. New York’s relative skepticism towards witch trials offered a glimmer of hope for those seeking refuge.

Captain Jonathan Walcott and Thomas Putnam continue to be central figures in the accusations, consistently bringing forward new complaints and identifying supposed witches. Their influence on the court and the community remains significant.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem is thick with anticipation as the newly appointed Court of Oyer and Terminer prepares to convene. But today’s most striking development is the arrival of George Burroughs, brought from Wells, Maine, after his arrest three days ago. The warrant traveled seventy miles north, a testament to how thoroughly the accusations have spread beyond Salem Village itself. Burroughs had been living in Maine for some time, having left Salem Village years earlier, but old grievances clearly followed him. As the magistrate's record will later show, Thomas Putnam Junior remembered a debt dispute from 1683, a matter that now appears to be fueling these new charges. The speed with which accusations are now moving, and the distance they now cover, is deeply unsettling. One witness claimed, “he is a man of great pride and malice,” echoing the sentiment driving these proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a significant escalation of the trials. George Burroughs, a former minister of Salem Village, has been arrested in Maine and brought back to Salem, demonstrating how far the reach of these accusations extends. His arrival signals a new phase as the Court of Oyer and Terminer prepares to begin its work.

His arrival signals a new phase as the Court of Oyer and Terminer prepares to begin its work.

The speed with which accusations are now moving, and the distance they now cover, is deeply unsettling. One witness claimed, “he is a man of great pride and malice,” echoing the sentiment driving these proceedings.

George Burroughs’s arrival in Salem after his arrest in Maine dramatically expands the scope of the trials, proving the accusations are not limited to residents of Salem Village. The lengthy journey back to Salem—over difficult and dangerous roads—highlights the determination of those pursuing these charges and the geographic reach of their suspicions.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer is expected to begin sessions very soon. The appointment of this special court signifies a shift towards more formal legal proceedings, though the standards of evidence remain deeply problematic.

Thomas Putnam Junior continues to be a central figure in the accusations. His long-held grudge against George Burroughs, stemming from a debt dispute years earlier, appears to be a key driver of the charges against the former minister.

The arrest of someone living so far from Salem Village suggests that accusers are actively seeking out individuals to implicate, rather than simply responding to local complaints. This proactive approach raises serious questions about the fairness of the process.

Travel remains perilous. The roads between Salem and Maine are still recovering from King William’s War, making Burroughs’s transport a difficult undertaking and underscoring the instability of this time.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with anticipation and fear this Thursday as the newly appointed court begins its work. Governor William Phips is present, overseeing the proceedings, and has authorized the arrest of three more women: Ann Sears, Bethiah Carter Senior, and Bethiah Carter Junior. A warrant, issued by magistrates John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin, details complaints brought by Thomas and John Putnam, alleging witchcraft practiced upon Ann Putnam, Mercy Lewis, and Mary Walcot. The document states, “Whereas Complaint hath benne this day Exhibited…on behalfe of theire Majesties Against Ann Seeres…and Bethiah Carter…for high Suspition of Sundry acts of Witchcraft.” Constable Ephraim Bouck of Woburn has already taken Sears and the elder Carter into custody, transporting them to Lieutenant Nathaniel Ingersoll’s house for examination. Meanwhile, Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon this day, warning his congregation about the dangers of spiritual corruption. He preached, “Ye cannot drink the Cup of the Lord, & the cup of Devils,” underscoring his belief that the afflictions are a direct result of demonic influence.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a significant escalation in the trials. Governor William Phips has arrived in Salem Village and, crucially, the Court of Oyer and Terminer has officially convened, signaling a formal legal process is now underway – and new arrests have been made based on the accusations of afflicted girls.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a significant escalation in the trials. Governor William Phips has arrived in Salem Village and, crucially, the Court of Oyer and Terminer has officially convened, signaling a formal legal process is now underway – and new arrests have been made based on the accusations of afflicted girls.

The atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with anticipation and fear this Thursday as the newly appointed court begins its work. Governor William Phips is present, overseeing the proceedings, and has authorized the arrest of three more women: Ann Sears, Bethiah Carter Senior, and Bethiah Carter Junior. A warrant, issued by magistrates John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin, details complaints brought by Thomas and John Putnam, alleging witchcraft practiced upon Ann Putnam, Mercy Lewis, and Mary Walcot. The document states, “Whereas Complaint hath benne this day Exhibited…on behalfe of theire Majesties Against Ann Seeres…and Bethiah Carter…for high Suspition of Sundry acts of Witchcraft.” Constable Ephraim Bouck of Woburn has already taken Sears and the elder Carter into custody, transporting them to Lieutenant Nathaniel Ingersoll’s house for examination. Meanwhile, Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon this day, warning his congregation about the dangers of spiritual corruption. He preached, “Ye cannot drink the Cup of the Lord, & the cup of Devils,” underscoring his belief that the afflictions are a direct result of demonic influence.

As detailed in the warrant issued today, Ann Sears and both Bethiah Carters have been arrested on accusations of witchcraft brought by the Putnames, based on the afflictions of several young women in Salem Village. This signals a widening of the net of accusations beyond those already in custody.

The official convening of the Court of Oyer and Terminer today, with Governor Phips in attendance, establishes a formal legal framework for addressing the accusations, replacing the earlier, more informal examinations.

Constable Ephraim Bouck confirmed he has served the warrant and taken Ann Sears and the elder Bethiah Carter into custody, bringing them to Lieutenant Ingersoll for questioning, as required by the court order.

Reverend Samuel Parris, in a sermon delivered today, warned against the dangers of associating with evil and drew a sharp distinction between Christian and demonic influences, reinforcing the prevailing belief in the supernatural causes of the afflictions.

The magistrate’s record shows the complaints against Sears and the Carters specifically cite “Sundry acts of Witchcraft” causing harm to Ann Putnam, Mercy Lewis, and Mary Walcot, laying out the basis for the legal charges.

This day’s events mark a clear shift towards a more formalized and potentially more aggressive prosecution of those accused of witchcraft in Salem Village and surrounding areas.

newspapers.lede: The arrival of Sir William Phips as the new governor of Massachusetts Bay did little to slow the escalating crisis today. The Court of Oyer and Terminer convened, and one of the most startling examinations took place: that of George Burroughs. Burroughs, who served as minister here in Salem Village from 1680 to 1683, was brought before magistrates including Hathorne, Corwin, Sewall, and Stoughton. He was pressed on his religious practices, specifically his infrequent participation in communion. When asked about the afflicted girls’ fits during the examination, Burroughs stated, “it was an amazing & humbling Providence, but he understood nothing of it.” The magistrate’s record shows that as Burroughs entered the room, “many (if not all of the Bewitched) were greivously tortured,” and he reportedly knocked down several of the afflicted simply by looking at them. His arrest, coming nine years after he left this village under a cloud of debt and resentment, has deeply shaken the community – if a former minister can consort with the Devil, who is safe? The constables took Burroughs into custody, beginning a seven-week imprisonment that will cost the colony seventeen shillings and six pence.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the trials. Reverend George Burroughs, a former minister of Salem Village, faced intense questioning and arrest, sending fear through the community. Meanwhile, accusations continued to spread, with servants turning against their masters in desperate attempts to save themselves.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a turning point in the trials. Reverend George Burroughs, a former minister of Salem Village, faced intense questioning and arrest, sending fear through the community. Meanwhile, accusations continued to spread, with servants turning against their masters in desperate attempts to save themselves.

The arrival of Sir William Phips as the new governor of Massachusetts Bay did little to slow the escalating crisis today. The Court of Oyer and Terminer convened, and one of the most startling examinations took place: that of George Burroughs. Burroughs, who served as minister here in Salem Village from 1680 to 1683, was brought before magistrates including Hathorne, Corwin, Sewall, and Stoughton. He was pressed on his religious practices, specifically his infrequent participation in communion. When asked about the afflicted girls’ fits during the examination, Burroughs stated, “it was an amazing & humbling Providence, but he understood nothing of it.” The magistrate’s record shows that as Burroughs entered the room, “many (if not all of the Bewitched) were greivously tortured,” and he reportedly knocked down several of the afflicted simply by looking at them. His arrest, coming nine years after he left this village under a cloud of debt and resentment, has deeply shaken the community – if a former minister can consort with the Devil, who is safe? The constables took Burroughs into custody, beginning a seven-week imprisonment that will cost the colony seventeen shillings and six pence.

As detailed in the lede, George Burroughs underwent a rigorous private examination today and was subsequently arrested. His past as a Salem Village minister and his seemingly composed demeanor under questioning have made his case particularly alarming to many.

Sarah Churchill, a twenty-year-old servant in the household of George Jacobs Senior, confessed to witchcraft and implicated her master and his granddaughter, Margaret Jacobs. However, later in the day, she privately admitted to Ingersoll’s niece that she had “belied herself,” revealing the impossible position of servants caught between their own survival and loyalty to their employers.

Mercy Lewis, an orphaned girl who once served in Burroughs’s household in Maine, also testified against him today. She claimed Burroughs showed her a “new fashion book” and possessed the power to raise the devil, accusations rooted in a past she shared with the accused.

The cost of imprisoning George Burroughs has already reached seventeen shillings and six pence for approximately seven weeks of keeping, demonstrating the growing financial strain the trials are placing on the colony.

Burroughs admitted he had not partaken in communion for some time, stating he was at meetings in Boston and Charlestown when the sacrament was offered but did not receive it. This detail, while seemingly minor, was seized upon by the magistrates as evidence of his spiritual shortcomings.

Susannah Sheldon testified that Burroughs’s two wives appeared to her in winding sheets, a spectral vision meant to implicate him. This type of spectral evidence continues to play a significant role in the accusations.

The afflicted girls reportedly fell into violent fits upon Burroughs’s entry into the examination room, and again when he looked at them – further fueling the belief in his power to harm through supernatural means.

newspapers.lede: The weight of accusation fell heavily today on George Jacobs Sr., a seventy-two-year-old farmer from Salem Town. Brought before the magistrates, Jacobs vehemently denied the charges leveled against him by Ann Putnam Jr., Mercy Lewis, and Mary Walcott. He even dismissed the afflicted girls with a harsh epithet. As the magistrate’s record reads, when questioned about the accusations, Jacobs laughed and declared, “I am as innocent as the Child born to night.” But the court was not swayed by his protestations. Compounding the tragedy, his own granddaughter, Margaret Jacobs, testified against him, a desperate act she would later regret. Abigail Williams also testified, claiming the old man appeared to her and pressed her to sign the Devil’s book. The pattern is becoming clear: accusation often comes from within families, and spectral evidence is accepted as fact. This morning, Sarah Osborne died in a Boston jail cell, another victim of the escalating panic, and without ever facing a formal trial. Her death underscores the swift and often merciless nature of these proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a grim milestone in the unfolding crisis. Sarah Osborne died in Boston prison, never receiving a trial. Meanwhile, George Jacobs Sr. was accused of witchcraft, and his own granddaughter, Margaret Jacobs, implicated him – though she would later recant her testimony. The seeds of accusation are sown deep within family and community ties.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a grim milestone in the unfolding crisis. Sarah Osborne died in Boston prison, never receiving a trial. Meanwhile, George Jacobs Sr. was accused of witchcraft, and his own granddaughter, Margaret Jacobs, implicated him – though she would later recant her testimony. The seeds of accusation are sown deep within family and community ties.

The weight of accusation fell heavily today on George Jacobs Sr., a seventy-two-year-old farmer from Salem Town. Brought before the magistrates, Jacobs vehemently denied the charges leveled against him by Ann Putnam Jr., Mercy Lewis, and Mary Walcott. He even dismissed the afflicted girls with a harsh epithet. As the magistrate’s record reads, when questioned about the accusations, Jacobs laughed and declared, “I am as innocent as the Child born to night.” But the court was not swayed by his protestations. Compounding the tragedy, his own granddaughter, Margaret Jacobs, testified against him, a desperate act she would later regret. Abigail Williams also testified, claiming the old man appeared to her and pressed her to sign the Devil’s book. The pattern is becoming clear: accusation often comes from within families, and spectral evidence is accepted as fact. This morning, Sarah Osborne died in a Boston jail cell, another victim of the escalating panic, and without ever facing a formal trial. Her death underscores the swift and often merciless nature of these proceedings.

George Jacobs Sr. was accused of witchcraft this morning and examined by the magistrates. He vehemently denied the charges, dismissing the afflicted girls and proclaiming his innocence, but his granddaughter, Margaret Jacobs, testified against him under pressure.

Sarah Osborne died in Boston prison today, never having been formally tried or convicted. She was the first of the accused to die in custody, and her death highlights the harsh conditions and rapid progression of the legal process.

Margaret Jacobs later recanted her testimony against her grandfather, writing a heartfelt letter from prison to express her regret, but this came too late to save George Jacobs Sr. from further proceedings.

Sarah Churchill, a servant in the Jacobs household, also confessed to witchcraft but later told a friend she had “belied herself,” revealing the immense pressure placed on individuals to confess, even if falsely accused.

The Wilkins family is fracturing as a result of the accusations. John Willard refused to serve arrest warrants and was himself accused, creating a deep rift within the family and community.

Several of the afflicted girls, including Mercy Lewis and Sarah Churchill, were “put out” children – orphaned or sent to live and work in other households – making them particularly vulnerable and potentially influencing their accusations.

The property dispute involving Sarah Osborne and the Putnam family adds another layer of complexity to the accusations, suggesting that personal conflicts may be playing a role in the unfolding events.

George Jacobs Sr., despite his age and infirmity, maintains a defiant spirit, telling the magistrates, “You tax me for a wizard; you may as well tax me for a buzzard.” His refusal to cooperate with the court is seen by some as further proof of his guilt.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with accusation as the newly formed Court of Oyer and Terminer began its work this morning. Testimony against George Jacobs Senior dominated the day. Several afflicted girls, including Abigail Williams, claimed to be tormented by his apparition, as Abigail Williams testified, stating she had “severall times seen, & been very much afflicted by the Apparition of Geo: Jacobs Sen'r.” Thomas and John Putnam, father and son, added their accounts, describing how the afflicted children writhed in agony during Jacobs’ examination, “their bones would have ben disjoynted.” But the day also brought forth accusations against Susannah Martin, reaching back years—even decades—into the past. Bernard Peach testified that Martin entered his bedroom at night, sitting on his chest and paralyzing him. He claimed to have bitten her fingers “to the bone” in desperation. This pattern of long-held grudges and spectral accusations is becoming increasingly clear as the court proceedings unfold.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the Court of Oyer and Terminer has officially convened with Judge William Stoughton presiding. Testimony against George Jacobs Senior intensified with multiple accusers claiming direct affliction. New accusations also surfaced against Susannah Martin, detailing decades-old grievances and disturbing nighttime experiences.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, the Court of Oyer and Terminer has officially convened with Judge William Stoughton presiding. Testimony against George Jacobs Senior intensified with multiple accusers claiming direct affliction. New accusations also surfaced against Susannah Martin, detailing decades-old grievances and disturbing nighttime experiences.

The atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with accusation as the newly formed Court of Oyer and Terminer began its work this morning. Testimony against George Jacobs Senior dominated the day. Several afflicted girls, including Abigail Williams, claimed to be tormented by his apparition, as Abigail Williams testified, stating she had “severall times seen, & been very much afflicted by the Apparition of Geo: Jacobs Sen'r.” Thomas and John Putnam, father and son, added their accounts, describing how the afflicted children writhed in agony during Jacobs’ examination, “their bones would have ben disjoynted.” But the day also brought forth accusations against Susannah Martin, reaching back years—even decades—into the past. Bernard Peach testified that Martin entered his bedroom at night, sitting on his chest and paralyzing him. He claimed to have bitten her fingers “to the bone” in desperation. This pattern of long-held grudges and spectral accusations is becoming increasingly clear as the court proceedings unfold.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer formally convened today under the leadership of Judge William Stoughton, marking a significant escalation in the legal pursuit of those accused of witchcraft. The court's purpose is to try those accused and determine guilt or innocence, a process that has already begun to divide the community.

Testimony against George Jacobs Senior was particularly strong today, with multiple accusers claiming to be directly afflicted by his spectral presence. Ann Putnam, Jr. testified that Jacobs “dreadfully tormented” her, reinforcing the claims of the other afflicted girls and solidifying the charges against him.

The deposition of John Pressy revealed a decades-old grievance against Susannah Martin. Approximately 24 years ago, Pressy became repeatedly lost near Martin's property, and she allegedly cursed him, stating, “we should never have but too cows.” He claims his herd never exceeded two cattle for the following 20 years.

William Brown testified that Susannah Martin threatened his wife approximately 30 years ago, after a dispute at court. Brown’s wife then suffered strange afflictions, including being “peking her Legs” with what seemed like bird’s beaks. This testimony reveals the long memory held by those who believe they have been wronged.

Bernard Peach's deposition details a disturbing nighttime encounter with Susannah Martin, which he described as a classic “night hag” experience—a feeling of paralysis and chest pressure. While modern understanding attributes such experiences to sleep paralysis, seventeenth-century observers viewed it as evidence of spectral attack.

The pattern of accusations against Susannah Martin, spanning decades and involving personal slights and alleged curses, highlights the role of long-held resentments in fueling the witch hunt. These testimonies suggest that old feuds are now being recast as evidence of witchcraft.

The court records reveal increasing reliance on spectral evidence, with accusers claiming direct harm from the apparitions of the accused. This reliance on subjective experiences is proving to be a central, and controversial, element of the proceedings.

newspapers.lede: This Monday, May twelfth, the magistrates issued warrants for the arrest of Alice Parker and Ann Pudeator, adding their names to the growing list of the accused. The warrants, signed by John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin, formally charge them with witchcraft based on the testimonies collected so far. Mary Warren, during her own examination, implicated both women, stating in her testimony, “I did sett my hand to the Devills book… my Master Procter brought to me, & he Tould me if I would Sett my hand to that book I should be well.” Alongside Parker and Pudeator, John Willard, a former constable who had begun to question the proceedings, has also been accused. Willard’s flight from Salem, seen as definitive proof of guilt by the court, only solidified the charges against him. These arrests signal a troubling turn, with accusations extending beyond initial disputes within Salem Village and encompassing residents of Salem Town as well.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a significant escalation in the accusations of witchcraft as three individuals – Alice Parker, Ann Pudeator, and John Willard – are formally charged. These accusations, fueled by the testimonies of afflicted girls and expanding beyond Salem Village, demonstrate the widening scope of the crisis and the growing fear gripping the community.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a significant escalation in the accusations of witchcraft as three individuals – Alice Parker, Ann Pudeator, and John Willard – are formally charged. These accusations, fueled by the testimonies of afflicted girls and expanding beyond Salem Village, demonstrate the widening scope of the crisis and the growing fear gripping the community.

This Monday, May twelfth, the magistrates issued warrants for the arrest of Alice Parker and Ann Pudeator, adding their names to the growing list of the accused. The warrants, signed by John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin, formally charge them with witchcraft based on the testimonies collected so far. Mary Warren, during her own examination, implicated both women, stating in her testimony, “I did sett my hand to the Devills book… my Master Procter brought to me, & he Tould me if I would Sett my hand to that book I should be well.” Alongside Parker and Pudeator, John Willard, a former constable who had begun to question the proceedings, has also been accused. Willard’s flight from Salem, seen as definitive proof of guilt by the court, only solidified the charges against him. These arrests signal a troubling turn, with accusations extending beyond initial disputes within Salem Village and encompassing residents of Salem Town as well.

As we’ve just discussed, warrants were issued today for the arrest of Alice Parker, Ann Pudeator, and John Willard, bringing the total number of formally accused to over thirty. These arrests underscore the rapid expansion of the accusations beyond the initial circle of accusers and the internal conflicts of Salem Village.

Alice Parker, the wife of a Salem Town fisherman, is accused based on the testimonies of Mary Warren and Margaret Jacobs, as well as other afflicted girls. Her case illustrates how the trials are now reaching into Salem Town, affecting individuals not directly involved in the village’s disputes.

Ann Pudeator, a widow who worked as a nurse and midwife, faces accusations stemming from the death of a patient she attended, John Best’s wife. Her medical supplies, including ointments and herbs, are being presented as evidence of witchcraft, demonstrating the dangerous suspicion surrounding women’s healing practices.

John Willard, a former deputy constable, is accused not for any acts of witchcraft, but for refusing to continue arresting those accused. His flight to Lancaster, which the court interprets as proof of guilt, highlights the increasingly precarious situation for anyone who expresses doubt about the proceedings.

The magistrate’s record details the formal charges, stating that Parker, Pudeator, and Willard are accused of “sundry acts of Witchcraft” causing harm to several individuals in Salem Village and surrounding areas. This broad language reflects the lack of specific evidence and the reliance on spectral testimony.

Mary Warren’s examination revealed her claim that John Procter brought her a “Devill’s book” and told her signing it would make her well. This demonstrates the desperate attempts to explain the afflicted girls’ behavior and the involvement of prominent figures like Procter in the unfolding crisis.

The mittimus, or warrant, for the accused specifically names the jailer and instructs him to securely hold these individuals until legally released, indicating the court's intention to pursue prosecution and conviction.

newspapers.lede: The crisis deepens here in Salem Village as another resident, Abigail Soames, was taken into custody today. Constable Peter Osgood, acting under warrant from magistrates John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin, apprehended Soames at the house of Samuel Gaskill in Salem Town. During her examination, Mary Warren reportedly suffered a violent fit, immediately identifying Soames as the source of her torment. As the magistrate’s record reads, Warren “continually Crying out that it was this very Woman tho she knew her not before.” A curious detail emerged during the examination – a large sewing needle was found hidden in Soames’ apron, the origin of which she claims to be unknown. This arrest, following so closely on the heels of others, underscores the growing fear and suspicion gripping the community. It’s a troubling day, and the court’s actions signal a continued escalation of these proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Abigail Soames of Salem Town was arrested on charges of witchcraft. The magistrates began her examination, and accusations flew involving afflictions against Mary Warren. Meanwhile, testimonies continue to surface against Susannah Martin, detailing alleged encounters stretching back years.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, Abigail Soames of Salem Town was arrested on charges of witchcraft. The magistrates began her examination, and accusations flew involving afflictions against Mary Warren. Meanwhile, testimonies continue to surface against Susannah Martin, detailing alleged encounters stretching back years.

The crisis deepens here in Salem Village as another resident, Abigail Soames, was taken into custody today. Constable Peter Osgood, acting under warrant from magistrates John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin, apprehended Soames at the house of Samuel Gaskill in Salem Town. During her examination, Mary Warren reportedly suffered a violent fit, immediately identifying Soames as the source of her torment. As the magistrate’s record reads, Warren “continually Crying out that it was this very Woman tho she knew her not before.” A curious detail emerged during the examination – a large sewing needle was found hidden in Soames’ apron, the origin of which she claims to be unknown. This arrest, following so closely on the heels of others, underscores the growing fear and suspicion gripping the community. It’s a troubling day, and the court’s actions signal a continued escalation of these proceedings.

Abigail Soames was arrested today on the charge of witchcraft and is currently imprisoned in Salem jail. The warrant cites “Sundry acts of Witchcraft” and suspicion related to the affliction of Mary Warren, with Warren immediately identifying Soames as her tormentor during the examination.

Testimony from Jarvis Ring, dating back seven to eight years, accuses Susannah Martin of physically attacking him while he was asleep. Ring claims Martin bit his finger, and the mark remained for a long time, as he swore under oath today.

Joseph Ring provided two separate depositions today concerning Susannah Martin and Thomas Hardy. One details a nighttime encounter in a deserted house where Martin allegedly transformed into a black hog, and the other recounts an earlier incident near Casco Bay fort, where Hardy and others allegedly demanded money from him under threatening circumstances.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer has officially convened, marking a significant escalation in the legal response to the accusations. This court, with its appointed judges, is intended to bring order to the proceedings, though many remain skeptical of its impartiality.

While details are scarce, the arrest of Abigail Soames, a resident of Salem Town, highlights how the reach of these accusations extends beyond Salem Village itself, broadening the scope of the crisis.

The continued reliance on spectral evidence – testimonies about visions and apparitions – remains a central, and controversial, element of the examinations. The validity of these claims is hotly debated, but they are consistently accepted by the magistrates.

newspapers.lede: Governor Phips stepped ashore in Boston this morning, bringing with him not only a new charter for the Massachusetts Bay Colony, but also a deepening crisis. For three years, the colony has operated without a clear legal framework, a consequence of revoked charters and political upheaval. Now, with the jails overflowing with those accused of witchcraft, Phips must establish a court capable of addressing the charges. As the magistrate’s record shows, warrants were issued today for multiple individuals, including Daniel Andrew, George Jacobs Jr., and Rebecca Jacobs, all connected to the growing accusations in Salem Village. One complaint reads, “high Suspition of Sundry acts of Witchcraft by them Committed or donne lately on the body of Ann puttnam Marcy Lewis Mary Walcot And Abigail Williams & others.” Phips’s arrival doesn’t resolve the legal limbo, but rather formalizes it with the intention of creating a Court of Oyer and Terminer. This new court will determine the fate of those already imprisoned, and those still being accused. The timing is critical: the new government's legitimacy is now irrevocably tied to the outcome of these trials.
newspapers.summary: Today, Sir William Phips arrived from Boston with the new Massachusetts charter, becoming royal governor. He finds a colony in crisis, with jails overflowing with those accused of witchcraft, and no functioning court to hear their cases. Phips immediately begins the process of establishing a Court of Oyer and Terminer, setting the stage for the trials to come.

Today, Sir William Phips arrived from Boston with the new Massachusetts charter, becoming royal governor. He finds a colony in crisis, with jails overflowing with those accused of witchcraft, and no functioning court to hear their cases. Phips immediately begins the process of establishing a Court of Oyer and Terminer, setting the stage for the trials to come.

Governor Phips stepped ashore in Boston this morning, bringing with him not only a new charter for the Massachusetts Bay Colony, but also a deepening crisis. One complaint reads, “high Suspition of Sundry acts of Witchcraft by them Committed or donne lately on the body of Ann puttnam Marcy Lewis Mary Walcot And Abigail Williams & others.” Phips’s arrival doesn’t resolve the legal limbo, but rather formalizes it with the intention of creating a Court of Oyer and Terminer. This new court will determine the fate of those already imprisoned, and those still being accused. The timing is critical: the new government's legitimacy is now irrevocably tied to the outcome of these trials.

Governor Phips arrived in Boston today, finding the jails overflowing with accused witches. The lack of a functioning court system, due to years of charter disputes, has created an untenable situation. Phips’s immediate priority is establishing a Court of Oyer and Terminer to address the growing number of accusations.

Several new warrants were issued today, targeting individuals connected to the accusations in Salem Village. Among those named are Daniel Andrew, a prominent village figure who evaded arrest, and George Jacobs Jr., who has fled, mirroring his father’s fate.

Rebecca Jacobs, wife of the fleeing George Jacobs Jr., was also accused and warrants issued for her arrest, along with Sarah Buckley and her daughter Mary Witheridge. Records indicate Rebecca Jacobs is described as being mentally unwell, a common descriptor for those accused.

Constable John Parker reported that Elizabeth Colson, accused by several of the afflicted girls, has fled to Boston, presumably to escape arrest and potentially leave the colony altogether. Witnesses claimed she ran, fell, and rose repeatedly before disappearing near some bushes with a large cat.

Thomas Farrar Sr., an elderly farmer known as “Old Pharaoh,” was also accused based on the testimony of Ann Putnam Jr., who claimed a “great nose” appeared before her and tormented her. He will be examined by the court later this week.

The new Massachusetts charter, negotiated by Increase Mather, confirms land titles and shifts governance to a royally appointed governor and property-based voting. While offering stability, its arrival coincides with the height of the witch hunt, inextricably linking the new administration to the trials.

Phips’s appointment of William Stoughton as Lieutenant Governor is a significant development. Stoughton, a former clergyman with no legal training, will become Chief Justice of the Court of Oyer and Terminer, potentially shaping the trials’ outcomes with his unwavering belief in the accusations.

newspapers.lede: The net is tightening here in Salem Village. Today, a second warrant was issued for John Willard, a man who had been actively involved in arresting others accused of witchcraft. But Willard himself began to question the proceedings, and refused to continue serving warrants. Now, he’s on the run, and his flight is being used as evidence against him. As the magistrate’s record reads, the warrant instructs officers to apprehend Willard, “if he may be found in your precints,” charging him with witchcraft relating to the afflictions of the Wilkins family. This flight, of course, confirms in the eyes of many that he is guilty. But the day’s events reveal a disturbing pattern: accusations are fracturing families, and even confessions are being called into question. Sarah Churchill, a servant, confided to friends that she felt trapped, stating, “If I told Mr. Noyes but once I had set my hand to the Book he would believe me, but if I told the truth a hundred times that I had not, he would not believe me.” This highlights the impossible bind the accused find themselves in – a confession, however false, is accepted, while any claim of innocence is dismissed.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the pressure mounts as more people flee arrest and accusations spread even within families. John Willard, a man once tasked with enforcing the law, is now a fugitive. Meanwhile, confessions continue, but doubts are emerging about their validity, revealing the impossible situation faced by the accused.

Today in Salem Village, the pressure mounts as more people flee arrest and accusations spread even within families. John Willard, a man once tasked with enforcing the law, is now a fugitive. Meanwhile, confessions continue, but doubts are emerging about their validity, revealing the impossible situation faced by the accused.

The net is tightening here in Salem Village. Today, a second warrant was issued for John Willard, a man who had been actively involved in arresting others accused of witchcraft. But Willard himself began to question the proceedings, and refused to continue serving warrants. Now, he’s on the run, and his flight is being used as evidence against him. As the magistrate’s record reads, the warrant instructs officers to apprehend Willard, “if he may be found in your precints,” charging him with witchcraft relating to the afflictions of the Wilkins family. This flight, of course, confirms in the eyes of many that he is guilty. But the day’s events reveal a disturbing pattern: accusations are fracturing families, and even confessions are being called into question. Sarah Churchill, a servant, confided to friends that she felt trapped, stating, “If I told Mr. Noyes but once I had set my hand to the Book he would believe me, but if I told the truth a hundred times that I had not, he would not believe me.” This highlights the impossible bind the accused find themselves in – a confession, however false, is accepted, while any claim of innocence is dismissed.

As we discussed, John Willard fled after the first warrant for his arrest was issued. A second, county-wide warrant was issued today, and Willard was apprehended three days later in Nashaway, some forty miles west of Salem. His escape is being presented as proof of his guilt, despite his prior role in enforcing these very accusations.

Adding to the turmoil, George Jacobs Jr. has also fled, leaving his elderly father, George Jacobs Sr., vulnerable to the accusations of his granddaughter, Margaret. This abandonment leaves the senior Jacobs without family support to defend against the growing charges.

Fifteen-year-old Margaret Jacobs confessed to witchcraft today, and testified against both her grandfather, George Jacobs Sr., and Reverend George Burroughs. She admitted under duress that she feared “being put down into the dungeon and… hanged” if she did not comply.

Disturbingly, even family members are turning against one another. Members of the Wilkins family, the in-laws of John Willard, testified against him after he refused to serve further warrants, demonstrating the deep divisions being created by these proceedings.

Sarah Churchill, a servant in the Jacobs household, privately recanted her earlier confession, revealing to friends that her words were coerced by the magistrates. This adds to the growing questions about the reliability of the confessions being obtained.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer is now fully convened, and is expected to hear more cases in the coming days, potentially escalating the crisis even further as more individuals are brought before the court.

newspapers.lede: The newly appointed Court of Oyer and Terminer officially began its work today, adding urgency to the already tense atmosphere in Salem Village. Much of the day’s attention centered on the testimony of John Kimball, a forty-five-year-old farmer from Amesbury, regarding his dealings with Susannah Martin some twenty-three years prior. Kimball claims that after he refused to sell two cows to Martin, she declared, “you had ben as good you had,” a sentiment he interprets as a curse. Shortly thereafter, one of the reserved cows died suddenly, followed by the deaths of other livestock, costing Kimball the equivalent of thirty pounds. Then, years later, when Kimball sought to purchase puppies from Martin, she was again rebuffed. As Kimball recalls, Martin then stated, “If I Live Ile give him pupeys enough.” That very night, he claims, a demonic puppy appeared and attacked him. These events, presented as evidence of malice, are driving the narrative of Martin’s guilt as the court seeks to uncover witchcraft in the community.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the Court of Oyer and Terminer has convened, and accusations against Susannah Martin are gaining traction. A deposition from John Kimball details incidents of perceived maleficium—harmful magic—after he refused to trade with Martin, painting a picture of retaliatory supernatural events. This evidence underscores the pattern of accusations linking denied requests to subsequent misfortune.

Today in Salem Village, the Court of Oyer and Terminer has convened, and accusations against Susannah Martin are gaining traction. A deposition from John Kimball details incidents of perceived maleficium—harmful magic—after he refused to trade with Martin, painting a picture of retaliatory supernatural events. This evidence underscores the pattern of accusations linking denied requests to subsequent misfortune.

The newly appointed Court of Oyer and Terminer officially began its work today, adding urgency to the already tense atmosphere in Salem Village. Much of the day’s attention centered on the testimony of John Kimball, a forty-five-year-old farmer from Amesbury, regarding his dealings with Susannah Martin some twenty-three years prior. Kimball claims that after he refused to sell two cows to Martin, she declared, “you had ben as good you had,” a sentiment he interprets as a curse. Shortly thereafter, one of the reserved cows died suddenly, followed by the deaths of other livestock, costing Kimball the equivalent of thirty pounds. Then, years later, when Kimball sought to purchase puppies from Martin, she was again rebuffed. As Kimball recalls, Martin then stated, “If I Live Ile give him pupeys enough.” That very night, he claims, a demonic puppy appeared and attacked him. These events, presented as evidence of malice, are driving the narrative of Martin’s guilt as the court seeks to uncover witchcraft in the community.

As detailed in the deposition of John Kimball, the court is hearing accusations of direct harm—maleficium—against Susannah Martin. Kimball testified to a pattern of refused requests followed by misfortune, specifically the death of livestock and an alleged attack by a demonic creature, suggesting Martin used witchcraft to retaliate against perceived slights.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer is now fully in session, marking a significant escalation in the legal proceedings surrounding the witchcraft accusations. This court, appointed by Governor Phips, has the authority to try cases of witchcraft and potentially deliver convictions.

The testimony of John Kimball suggests a specific pattern in the accusations: a refusal of a commercial transaction or request, followed by a negative outcome for the person who refused. This echoes similar claims made in other cases and highlights the perceived link between denial and supernatural punishment.

Kimball’s account of Martin’s words—"you had ben as good you had" and "If I Live Ile give him pupeys enough"—are particularly striking as they appear to be direct recollections of remembered speech, lending a degree of authenticity to his claims.

The depositions reveal the importance of livestock in the economic life of the Village. The loss of several animals represents a significant financial hardship for Kimball, further intensifying the perceived severity of Martin’s actions.

Edmund Eliot corroborated part of Kimball's story, stating he overheard Martin ask why Kimball wouldn’t take her puppies and her subsequent claim that she would provide him with enough puppies if she lived. This secondary testimony reinforces the narrative presented by Kimball.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere here in Salem Village is thick with fear and accusation. This morning, a twelve-man jury completed its inquest into the death of Daniel Wilkins, a young man who had been ailing for several days. They examined his body and found disturbing evidence – bruises and what they described as puncture wounds. The jury’s verdict was unequivocal: Wilkins did not die a natural death, but rather “an unnaturall death by sume cruell hands of wicthcraft.” This is a significant development, as it’s one of the few times a jury has explicitly linked a death to witchcraft, lending legal weight to the growing accusations. Constable John Putnam reported securing several accused individuals, including John Willard, whom he apprehended while working in Nashaway. According to the report, “the afflicted persons made such an out Crye that I was forced to pinion him.” Susannah Sheldon also testified today, claiming to have been repeatedly afflicted by several individuals, including Willard, and even stabbed by Elizabeth Colson. The intensity of these claims is fueling the frenzy, as more and more people are implicated in what is now widely believed to be a widespread conspiracy.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, a coroner’s jury delivered a startling verdict: Daniel Wilkins’ death was caused by witchcraft. This rare legal finding, combined with new accusations and arrests, intensifies the crisis and sets the stage for the Court of Oyer and Terminer to begin its work.

Today in Salem Village, a coroner’s jury delivered a startling verdict: Daniel Wilkins’ death was caused by witchcraft. This rare legal finding, combined with new accusations and arrests, intensifies the crisis and sets the stage for the Court of Oyer and Terminer to begin its work.

The atmosphere here in Salem Village is thick with fear and accusation.
