They examined his body and found disturbing evidence – bruises and what they described as puncture wounds. The jury’s verdict was unequivocal: Wilkins did not die a natural death, but rather “an unnaturall death by sume cruell hands of wicthcraft.” This is a significant development, as it’s one of the few times a jury has explicitly linked a death to witchcraft, lending legal weight to the growing accusations. Constable John Putnam reported securing several accused individuals, including John Willard, whom he apprehended while working in Nashaway. According to the report, “the afflicted persons made such an out Crye that I was forced to pinion him.” Susannah Sheldon also testified today, claiming to have been repeatedly afflicted by several individuals, including Willard, and even stabbed by Elizabeth Colson. The intensity of these claims is fueling the frenzy, as more and more people are implicated in what is now widely believed to be a widespread conspiracy.

As we heard in the lede, the coroner’s jury’s finding in the case of Daniel Wilkins is a pivotal moment. The jury detailed their findings, noting bruises and marks resembling puncture wounds, and concluded his death was caused by “sume cruell hands of wicthcraft.” This provides a legal basis beyond spectral evidence for pursuing those accused of harming Wilkins.

John Willard was arrested today while at work in Nashaway, further connecting the accusations to individuals outside of Salem Village. The constable reported the “afflicted persons” reacted violently to his capture, requiring him to be restrained.

Multiple warrants were issued today for the arrest of additional individuals, including George Jacobs, Jr., Daniel Andrew, and Elizabeth Colson. Authorities believe Colson has fled to Boston or Charlestown and are actively seeking her apprehension.

Susannah Sheldon testified that Elizabeth Colson, along with several others, continuously torments her. Sheldon claimed Colson physically attacked her, stating, “Ellizebeth Colson stabbing of hur one the back Right against the other woundes.”

The accusations are expanding; today’s testimonies and warrants name a wider range of individuals as being involved in witchcraft, including Phillip English and his wife, and Rebecca Jacobs.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer is expected to convene shortly, and with the increasing number of accusations and this new legal precedent, a wave of trials seems inevitable.

George Herrick and Benjamin Wilkins submitted a statement detailing the events surrounding Willard's arrest and the condition of Daniel Wilkins prior to his death, further supporting the claims of witchcraft.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with fear and accusation as the Court of Oyer and Terminer began its work today. John Willard, a resident of Salem Village, was subjected to intense questioning. The magistrates pressed him to confess, but Willard maintained his innocence, declaring, “I know nothing of appearance, & the God of Heaven will clear me.” However, his inability to readily recite the Lord’s Prayer under pressure was taken as damning evidence. He later stated, seemingly bewildered, “I think I am bewitcht as well,” a statement that could be read as an admission of guilt or a desperate plea for understanding. The afflicted girls fell into fits as Willard was present, and their testimony continues to drive these proceedings. This pattern of spectral evidence and coerced reactions is becoming increasingly common, raising serious questions about the fairness of the trials.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the Court of Oyer and Terminer formally convened, and several new accusations were made. Roger Toothaker, a man with some medical knowledge, was arrested, and John Willard faced a grueling examination. The power of the afflicted girls to influence legal proceedings was again on display with the case of Mary Easty.

Roger Toothaker, a man with some medical knowledge, was arrested, and John Willard faced a grueling examination. The power of the afflicted girls to influence legal proceedings was again on display with the case of Mary Easty.

John Willard, a resident of Salem Village, was subjected to intense questioning. The magistrates pressed him to confess, but Willard maintained his innocence, declaring, “I know nothing of appearance, & the God of Heaven will clear me.” However, his inability to readily recite the Lord’s Prayer under pressure was taken as damning evidence. He later stated, seemingly bewildered, “I think I am bewitcht as well,” a statement that could be read as an admission of guilt or a desperate plea for understanding. The afflicted girls fell into fits as Willard was present, and their testimony continues to drive these proceedings. This pattern of spectral evidence and coerced reactions is becoming increasingly common, raising serious questions about the fairness of the trials.

John Willard underwent a lengthy and difficult examination today. As we heard in the lede, Willard vehemently denied any involvement with witchcraft, appealing to God for vindication. However, his struggles reciting the Lord’s Prayer, and his later statement that he might himself be bewitched, were interpreted as evidence of guilt by the court and the afflicted girls.

Roger Toothaker, a self-styled physician from Billerica, was arrested today on accusations from Elizabeth Hubbard, Ann Putnam Jr., and Mary Walcott. Toothaker had previously boasted about teaching his daughter Martha Emerson his trade and claiming she had “killed a witch,” a statement that likely drew unwanted attention.

Mary Easty experienced a brief release from prison, only to be immediately re-arrested at the insistence of the accusers. This demonstrates the significant influence the afflicted girls wield over the legal process, and how easily accusations can lead to continued imprisonment.

A warrant was issued for the arrest of Roger Toothaker, John Willard, Thomas Farrer, and Elizabeth Hart, ordering them to be taken to Boston jail, as the magistrate’s record shows.

Elizabeth Booth testified against Mary Warren and Daniel Andrew, claiming Andrew brought a book for her to sign while under compulsion and that Warren attempted to influence her.

Sarah Buckley was examined, with the afflicted girls alleging she bit and tormented them. Susan Sheldon even claimed to see a “black man” whispering in Buckley’s ear during the questioning.

newspapers.lede: The tenuous hope offered to Mary Easty just two days ago has been brutally extinguished. After a brief period home with her children, she was re-arrested this morning following a dramatic episode involving Mercy Lewis. Witnesses George Herrick and John Putnam Jr. testified they found Lewis in what they described as a “Dreadfull and Solemn Condition,” seemingly near death from her fits. Lewis, in this state, reportedly spoke to the specter of Mary Easty, asking, “what have you brought me the winding Sheet?” This reference to a burial shroud, a winding sheet, is being interpreted as a direct threat on Lewis’s life. The magistrate’s record shows this incident occurred after Easty’s temporary release and is now being used to justify her renewed imprisonment. As Easty herself has reportedly come to understand, the court seems less interested in establishing truth than in continuing the arrests.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the cycle of accusation and re-arrest continues. Mary Easty, briefly released from prison, has been taken back into custody based on the claims of Mercy Lewis, who alleges spectral torment. This highlights a disturbing pattern and raises serious questions about the fairness of the proceedings.

This highlights a disturbing pattern and raises serious questions about the fairness of the proceedings.

Mary Easty’s re-arrest underscores the precarious situation faced by the accused. Released on May 18th, she experienced a fleeting moment of freedom before being plunged back into captivity based solely on the afflicted girls’ claims of spectral attacks. This cycle of release and re-arrest demonstrates the power of suggestion and the readily accepted evidence of spectral visions.

The testimony of Mercy Lewis is central to Easty’s re-arrest. Her dramatic affliction and the alleged communication with Easty’s specter are being presented as irrefutable evidence of guilt. The court appears to be relying heavily on the testimony of the afflicted girls, despite the subjective nature of their experiences.

A deposition taken today involves an older grievance. Bernard Peach testified about an incident from approximately ten years ago involving William Osgood and an ox. Peach claims Osgood was denied some beef, and shortly after, one of Osgood’s cows became violently ill and died, exhibiting strange symptoms.

Peach describes the cow having “Atter under her eyes as bigg as wallnutts” before it died, a detail likely intended to suggest supernatural involvement. This deposition, though recounting an event from a decade past, is being presented as evidence of Susannah Martin's malice, demonstrating the broadening scope of accusations.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer is now fully in session, and the pace of examinations and arrests is accelerating. Governor Phips’ arrival has seemingly legitimized the proceedings, despite growing concerns about the validity of the evidence being presented.

The afflicted girls continue to be the primary drivers of the accusations, and their claims are rarely challenged. The community is increasingly divided, with some questioning the methods of the court and others fully believing in the reality of witchcraft.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village remains tense as more individuals are brought forward under suspicion. This morning, accusations turned towards Sarah Bassett of Lynn and Susannah Roots of Beverly, both sisters-in-law of Elizabeth Proctor, who herself was accused earlier this month. The complaints, filed by Thomas Putnam and John Putnam, allege witchcraft committed against several of the afflicted girls – Mary Walcott, Abigail Williams, Mercy Lewis, and Ann Putnam, among others. As the magistrate’s record reads, the Putnams claim these women have caused “Sundry acts of Witchcraft” resulting in “great hurt & Injury” to the girls. Warrants have been issued for their arrest, and Susannah Roots is already in custody. The pattern is becoming clear: accusations are not random, but are spreading through networks of association, particularly those connected to the Proctor family. This systematic approach raises serious questions about the nature of these proceedings and the motivations behind them.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the accusations are expanding beyond those initially named. Sarah Bassett and Susannah Roots, both connected to the Proctor family, have been accused of witchcraft by the afflicted girls. This widening net of suspicion reveals a disturbing pattern and suggests a systematic targeting of individuals associated with Elizabeth Proctor.

The pattern is becoming clear: accusations are not random, but are spreading through networks of association, particularly those connected to the Proctor family. This systematic approach raises serious questions about the nature of these proceedings and the motivations behind them.

As we’ve just discussed, Sarah Bassett and Susannah Roots were accused of witchcraft today, expanding the scope of the accusations and revealing a clear connection to the Proctor family. The complaint alleges harm done to the afflicted girls, and warrants have been issued for the women’s arrest.

A formal complaint was filed by Thomas and John Putnam today, detailing the accusations against Bassett, Roots, and Sarah Proctor. This document, now part of the court record, outlines the specific charges of witchcraft and requests justice be served.

Susannah Roots of Beverly has already been apprehended by Constable Jonathan Biles, acting as deputy to the Marshall of Essex, following the issuance of the warrant this morning. She will be brought before the court for examination.

The accusations against Bassett and Roots are part of a larger pattern of targeting those associated with Elizabeth Proctor, suggesting a deliberate attempt to dismantle her family network through these trials.

While no indictments have been issued against Bassett or Roots at this time, their accusations mark a significant escalation in the number of individuals drawn into the investigations and further fuels the growing hysteria gripping Salem Village.

newspapers.lede: The weight of accusation fell heavily on several families this Friday. Benjamin Proctor, son of John and Elizabeth Proctor—already deeply embroiled in these proceedings—was brought forward on complaint by Nathaniel Ingersall and Thomas Rayment. The charge, like so many others, centers on bewitchment and harm inflicted upon the afflicted girls. As the magistrate’s record reads, Ingersall and Rayment claim Proctor, along with Mary De Rich and Sarah Pease, have committed “Sundry acts of Witchcraft” causing “great hurt” to Mary Warren, Abigail Williams, and Elizabeth Hubbard. Meanwhile, Mary Walcott testified against Sarah Proctor, claiming a spectral figure “come and choake me and pincht me” and demanded she sign a book. In her own words, Walcott swore Proctor “tormented me dreadfully.” These accusations, piling upon existing ones, demonstrate the ever-widening net of suspicion cast over the community and the mounting pressure as the court prepares to hear cases.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, accusations continue to spread, ensnaring more members of established families. Benjamin Proctor, son of John and Elizabeth, has been accused, as have Sarah Pease and Sarah Buckley. The newly arrived Governor Phips has convened the Court of Oyer and Terminer, signaling a formal escalation of the legal proceedings.

In her own words, Walcott swore Proctor “tormented me dreadfully.” These accusations, piling upon existing ones, demonstrate the ever-widening net of suspicion cast over the community and the mounting pressure as the court prepares to hear cases.

Benjamin Proctor, son of John and Elizabeth, was accused today by Nathaniel Ingersall and Thomas Rayment, joining his family in the accusations. While released, his involvement highlights the far-reaching impact of the trials.

Sarah Buckley, after months of imprisonment, was acquitted by the Superior Court, but the cost was steep. She was forced to pay the jail fees herself, leaving her family destitute.

Sarah Pease of Salem Town was also accused today, and along with Mary Easty, will be transferred to Boston prison as local jails overflow with the accused.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer has officially convened with Governor Phips presiding, marking a formal escalation of the legal process and promising swift trials.

Testimony from Elizabeth Booth details alleged physical attacks by Sarah Proctor and Mary De Rich, claiming they pinched and afflicted her grievously.

Further, a deposition involving Roger Toothaker reveals a disturbing claim that his daughter “kild a witch” using a method involving urine and a hot oven—a detail that speaks to the strange and increasingly desperate beliefs of the time.

newspapers.lede: Sir William Phips, newly appointed royal governor of Massachusetts Bay, officially opened the Court of Oyer and Terminer here in Salem today. This court, intended to bring order to the escalating accusations of witchcraft, began its work even as the existing jails reached a breaking point. The sheer number of people accused – not just here in Salem, but also in Boston, Cambridge, and Ipswich – has created a crisis of confinement. As the magistrate’s record reads, a warrant was issued this day for Sarah Good and Tituba, ordering them to be taken to Boston jail: “To the Keeper of theire Majests Goale in Boston You are in theire Majests names hereby required, to take into your care and safe Custody the Bodys of Sarah Good… and Titiba an Indian Woman… who stand Charged… for theire feloniously Committing Sundry acts of Witchcraft.” But the cost of justice extends beyond the courtroom; prisoners are now required to pay for their own food and lodging while incarcerated, and even after acquittal, some remain imprisoned simply because they cannot afford the fees.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a grim turning point in the trials as Sir William Phips arrives and formally convenes the Court of Oyer and Terminer. Simultaneously, the jails are overflowing with the accused, and a disturbing pattern emerges: even those found innocent are held captive until they can pay their jail fees.

Simultaneously, the jails are overflowing with the accused, and a disturbing pattern emerges: even those found innocent are held captive until they can pay their jail fees.

Sir William Phips, newly appointed royal governor of Massachusetts Bay, officially opened the Court of Oyer and Terminer here in Salem today. This court, intended to bring order to the escalating accusations of witchcraft, began its work even as the existing jails reached a breaking point. The sheer number of people accused – not just here in Salem, but also in Boston, Cambridge, and Ipswich – has created a crisis of confinement. As the magistrate’s record reads, a warrant was issued this day for Sarah Good and Tituba, ordering them to be taken to Boston jail: “To the Keeper of theire Majests Goale in Boston You are in theire Majests names hereby required, to take into your care and safe Custody the Bodys of Sarah Good… and Titiba an Indian Woman… who stand Charged… for theire feloniously Committing Sundry acts of Witchcraft.” But the cost of justice extends beyond the courtroom; prisoners are now required to pay for their own food and lodging while incarcerated, and even after acquittal, some remain imprisoned simply because they cannot afford the fees.

Sir William Phips has formally convened the Court of Oyer and Terminer, signaling a new phase in the prosecution of alleged witchcraft. This court is intended to bring a more structured legal process to the trials, but concerns remain about the fairness and scope of the proceedings.

Jails across Massachusetts are severely overcrowded with those accused of witchcraft, creating a logistical and financial strain. The accused are now responsible for paying for their own upkeep in jail, a practice that disproportionately affects the poor and vulnerable.

The case of Lydia Dustin illustrates the economic injustice within the system; though acquitted in February, she died in March unable to pay her jail fees. This highlights how even proven innocence doesn’t guarantee release, and the machinery of justice can punish individuals long after any legal wrongdoing.

Tituba, an enslaved woman belonging to Reverend Samuel Parris, and Sarah Good, the wife of a local farmer, were both committed to Boston jail today under the authority of magistrates John Hathorne and Jonathan Corwin.

The warrants for Good and Tituba specifically charge them with afflicting Elizabeth Parris, Elizabeth Hubbard, Abigail Williams, and Ann Putnam—the same girls whose initial accusations sparked this crisis.

Reports indicate that after Phips’s planned pardon in May of 1693, some individuals, like Tituba, may have remained in jail until outstanding fees were settled, further demonstrating the financial burdens placed upon those caught in the legal system.

newspapers.lede: The Court of Oyer and Terminer continues its work here in Salem Village, and today brought accusations against Mary Bradbury, a woman of Salisbury and wife of Captain Thomas Bradbury. She’s approximately seventy-five years old, and a respected member of her community, but that didn’t shield her from the accusations levied by the afflicted girls. Constable Joseph Neal brought the accusers – Mary Walcott, Ann Putnam, Mercy Lewis, and others – before a gathering where they claimed to be tormented by Bradbury’s spectral presence. As the magistrate’s record reads from this day, “Beeing at Salem village w’th Constable Josp Neale the persons under written was afflicted much and Complained ag’st viz Mary Walcott Ann Putnam upon Capt Bradberys wife of Salsbury…” This testimony, combined with similar claims against Sarah Rice, Wilmott Redd, and Elizabeth Fosdick, underscores the relentless pace of these examinations and the power of the accusing girls’ pronouncements. Despite the widespread support for Bradbury – a petition defending her boasts over a hundred names – the court appears poised to proceed with her case.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a grim milestone as Mary Bradbury was formally accused of witchcraft, joining the growing number of individuals facing trial. Despite a substantial petition of over one hundred signatures attesting to her good character, Bradbury was named in the afflictions of several young women, raising fears and escalating the crisis.

Despite the widespread support for Bradbury – a petition defending her boasts over a hundred names – the court appears poised to proceed with her case.

Mary Bradbury of Salisbury was formally accused of witchcraft today by several of the afflicted girls, including Mary Walcott and Ann Putnam. This accusation, despite a large petition in her defense, highlights the severity of the situation and the court’s reliance on spectral evidence.

Constable Joseph Neal played a key role in gathering the afflicted girls and presenting their accusations before the authorities, solidifying the pattern of accusations being driven by these young women.

Sarah Rice of Reading, Wilmott Redd of Marblehead, and Elizabeth Fosdick were also named in complaints today, indicating a broadening scope of accusations beyond Salem Village itself.

The sheer number of signatures on the petition defending Mary Bradbury – exceeding one hundred – is unprecedented and suggests a growing unease with the proceedings amongst those witnessing the trials.

Captain Thomas Bradbury submitted a statement vouching for his wife's character, describing her as "a loveing & faithfull wife"— a desperate plea mirroring the efforts of other families to protect their loved ones from these accusations.

The court continues to focus on individuals accused of tormenting others through spectral means, relying heavily on the testimony of the afflicted girls as evidence of wrongdoing.

newspapers.lede: Governor William Phips officially established the Court of Oyer and Terminer today, a move intended to bring legal order to the escalating accusations of witchcraft. He named William Stoughton as Chief Justice, entrusting him with the weighty responsibility of presiding over the trials. The court’s very name – meaning “to hear and to determine” – reflects its purpose. Phips believed a formal court was necessary, stating it was comprised of men “of the best prudence and figure that could then be pitched upon.” However, the timing is striking. May is the most crucial month for planting, and the labor of the fields is now divided between securing crops and facing accusations. The creation of this court, while intended to bring justice, comes at a steep economic cost. As the magistrate’s record shows, the afflicted claimed they were tormented by the specters of the accused, reasoning that the Devil could not assume a person’s shape “without their consent.” This chilling logic will underpin the court’s proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the unfolding tragedy here in Salem. Governor Phips has formally established the Court of Oyer and Terminer, a special commission tasked with trying the accused witches. William Stoughton has been appointed Chief Justice, setting the stage for trials that will irrevocably alter the lives of many in Massachusetts Bay.

The court’s very name – meaning “to hear and to determine” – reflects its purpose. Phips believed a formal court was necessary, stating it was comprised of men “of the best prudence and figure that could then be pitched upon.” However, the timing is striking. May is the most crucial month for planting, and the labor of the fields is now divided between securing crops and facing accusations. The creation of this court, while intended to bring justice, comes at a steep economic cost. As the magistrate’s record shows, the afflicted claimed they were tormented by the specters of the accused, reasoning that the Devil could not assume a person’s shape “without their consent.” This chilling logic will underpin the court’s proceedings.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer is now formally convened, with William Stoughton as Chief Justice. This court was established by Governor Phips to address the growing crisis of witchcraft accusations and will operate as a special commission, not a permanent legal body.

The timing of the court’s establishment coincides directly with the peak planting season, diverting vital labor from the fields and threatening the upcoming harvest. This disruption will have significant economic consequences for the entire region.

Judge Bartholomew Gedney, a man of many trades and a personal acquaintance of Captain John Alden, will preside over the trials, despite the clear conflict of interest in judging someone he knows.

The kinship ties among the judges are remarkably strong: several are connected through marriage, including Judge Peter Sergeant, whose brother-in-law is Sheriff George Corwin – the man responsible for executing the court’s sentences and seizing the property of those convicted.

Thomas Newton, the only individual with formal legal training, has been appointed as the initial prosecutor for the Court of Oyer and Terminer. His legal expertise will be crucial, though temporary, in navigating the complex and unprecedented nature of these trials.

The court operates under the belief that the Devil requires consent to assume a person’s form, meaning spectral evidence – seeing someone’s spirit tormenting another – is considered proof of a covenant with Satan. This theological justification will heavily influence the proceedings.

Stephen Sewall, the Clerk of the Court, has a unique connection to the beginning of the accusations, as he and his wife hosted Betty Parris in their home, where her afflictions mysteriously ceased – a detail that raises questions about the origins of the hysteria.

Waitstill Winthrop, grandson of a founding governor and commander of the Massachusetts militia, sits among the judges, bringing significant political weight and influence to the Court of Oyer and Terminer.

newspapers.lede: The pace of accusations quickened dramatically today, with a large cluster of new names added to the growing list of the accused. Among those arrested was Captain John Flood, a veteran military man and Indian fighter, accused by the same girls who have driven much of this frenzy – Mary Walcott, Abigail Williams, and others. The girls reportedly convulsed and claimed he afflicted them, despite his years of service defending the frontier. A warrant for his arrest, and for that of William Proctor, reads simply, “Whoe stands Charged w’th Sundry Acts of Witchcraft…to theyr great hurt.” This illustrates the ease with which accusations are now made, and the speed with which the court acts upon them. Also accused today was Wilmot Redd, the only person from Marblehead to face these charges. The constable's return states he “brought her to the house of L't Ingersalls,” ready for examination. The accusations are spreading beyond Salem Village, now reaching into neighboring communities and extending to individuals from all walks of life.
newspapers.summary: Today saw a surge in accusations across several towns, bringing the total number of those accused to over thirty. New names include a captain, a healer, a boy, and several women from Andover, Reading, and Marblehead. The Court of Oyer and Terminer is now fully convened, and arrests are happening rapidly.

Today saw a surge in accusations across several towns, bringing the total number of those accused to over thirty. New names include a captain, a healer, a boy, and several women from Andover, Reading, and Marblehead. The Court of Oyer and Terminer is now fully convened, and arrests are happening rapidly.

The pace of accusations quickened dramatically today, with a large cluster of new names added to the growing list of the accused. Among those arrested was Captain John Flood, a veteran military man and Indian fighter, accused by the same girls who have driven much of this frenzy – Mary Walcott, Abigail Williams, and others. The girls reportedly convulsed and claimed he afflicted them, despite his years of service defending the frontier. A warrant for his arrest, and for that of William Proctor, reads simply, “Whoe stands Charged w’th Sundry Acts of Witchcraft…to theyr great hurt.” This illustrates the ease with which accusations are now made, and the speed with which the court acts upon them. Also accused today was Wilmot Redd, the only person from Marblehead to face these charges. The constable's return states he “brought her to the house of L't Ingersalls,” ready for examination. The accusations are spreading beyond Salem Village, now reaching into neighboring communities and extending to individuals from all walks of life.

Today’s surge in accusations brought the total number of accused to over thirty, marking a significant escalation in the crisis. The court is actively issuing warrants and making arrests across multiple towns.

Martha Carrier of Andover was accused by multiple afflicted girls, as well as by neighbor Benjamin Abbot. The community already held a grudge against the Carrier family, blaming them for a smallpox outbreak in 1690, which killed thirteen people—a charge that appears to be resurfacing in the accusations against her.

Roger Toothaker, a folk healer from Billerica, was arrested today. His claim to be able to kill witches with counter-magic likely drew the attention of the accusers and led to his arrest, despite the irony of his profession. He died in prison before a trial could be held.

Elizabeth Cary, wife of a wealthy ship captain, was accused and is now imprisoned, though her husband is attempting to have the case moved to Charlestown. She and her husband ultimately fled Massachusetts to avoid the court.

Arthur Abbott of Ipswich was accused, along with ten others, in a mass complaint. He was released, but no record of a trial or examination survives, illustrating the arbitrary nature of these proceedings.

Elizabeth How of Topsfield, a woman in her late fifties, was accused and arrested. The depositions mention longstanding accusations made by neighbors, suggesting a history of resentment and suspicion long before the current crisis.

Captain John Alden, a military officer who recently ransomed captives from a frontier raid, was himself accused of witchcraft and treason – specifically, of selling weapons to the enemy. The girls initially pointed to the wrong man before identifying him, a detail noted by observers and raising questions about the reliability of their testimony.

Margaret Jacobs formally recanted her testimony against her grandfather, George Jacobs Sr., after his conviction and sentence. While it came too late to save him – he was hanged yesterday – this act of repentance highlights the coercive nature of the confessions being obtained and the devastating impact on family bonds.

Sarah Rice of Reading and Mary Toothaker of Billerica were also accused today, adding to the growing number of individuals caught in the web of accusations. Mary Toothaker confessed and implicated others, including her sister, Martha Carrier.

newspapers.lede: The crisis deepened today with the arrest of Elizabeth Howe, a woman from the neighboring town of Topsfield. Constable Ephraim Wildes carried out the warrant, issued yesterday, placing Howe in custody on the complaint of Joseph Holton and John Walcott. The accusations stem from the afflicted girls – Mary Walcott and Abigail Williams among them – who continue to claim they are tormented by spectral evidence. This case is particularly poignant, as Wildes’s own mother, Sarah Wildes, was accused over a month ago on April twenty-first. He was thus compelled to arrest a neighbor under the same cloud of suspicion that now hangs over his family. As the magistrate’s record reads from the warrant, Howe is accused of “high suspicion of Witchcraft by grievous hurtings of these persons named, and by Spectrals and other evil ways.” The situation underscores the increasing personal toll this wave of accusations is taking on families within the Massachusetts Bay.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the reach of the accusations widened with the arrest of Elizabeth Howe of Topsfield. This arrest is particularly troubling because it was carried out by Constable Ephraim Wildes, whose own mother stands accused, highlighting the growing fear and desperation gripping the community.

This arrest is particularly troubling because it was carried out by Constable Ephraim Wildes, whose own mother stands accused, highlighting the growing fear and desperation gripping the community.

The accusations stem from the afflicted girls – Mary Walcott and Abigail Williams among them – who continue to claim they are tormented by spectral evidence. This case is particularly poignant, as Wildes’s own mother, Sarah Wildes, was accused over a month ago on April twenty-first. He was thus compelled to arrest a neighbor under the same cloud of suspicion that now hangs over his family. As the magistrate’s record reads from the warrant, Howe is accused of “high suspicion of Witchcraft by grievous hurtings of these persons named, and by Spectrals and other evil ways.” The situation underscores the increasing personal toll this wave of accusations is taking on families within the Massachusetts Bay.

Elizabeth Howe’s arrest expands the scope of the trials beyond Salem Village itself, demonstrating the spread of fear and accusation to neighboring communities. Constable Wildes’s difficult position – arresting a neighbor while his own mother faces similar charges – illustrates the personal conflicts tearing apart the social fabric.

Joseph Holton and John Walcott formally filed the complaint against Howe, relying on the testimony of the afflicted girls. Their continued involvement highlights the central role these young women play in driving the accusations.

Mary Walcott and Abigail Williams remain key accusers, consistently naming new individuals as tormentors. Their pronouncements are currently accepted as evidence by the Court of Oyer and Terminer, which convened earlier this month.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer continues to meet, preparing to hear further cases and examine the evidence presented. Governor William Phips arrived in Massachusetts Bay earlier this month and authorized the court’s creation, hoping to bring order to the escalating situation.

Sarah Wildes remains in custody, awaiting examination. Her case, along with that of Elizabeth Howe, demonstrates the vulnerability of women, especially those who deviate from societal expectations, to these accusations.

Concern is growing among some residents regarding the reliance on spectral evidence – visions and dreams – to convict individuals. The validity of such evidence is increasingly questioned, but so far the court has shown no sign of discounting it.

newspapers.lede: The Court of Oyer and Terminer has formally convened, and the pace of accusations is quickening. This Friday, complaints were filed against Elizabeth Fosdick and Elizabeth Paine, both women from outside Salem Village, extending the reach of these proceedings beyond our immediate community. They were accused by several of the afflicted girls, including Mary Walcott and Mercy Lewis, and by Peter Tufts of Charlestown, who claims witchcraft harmed an enslaved woman. The accusations echo those already leveled against others: spectral evidence and unexplained misfortune. William Stacy’s deposition today offered a particularly detailed account of his long-standing animosity toward Bridget Bishop. He claims Bishop’s spirit visited him in the night, years ago, and that misfortune followed. As Stacy recalls, “about midnight this Deponent felt something between his lips Pressing hard ag't his teeth… she having then a black cap & a black hat, and a Red Coat with two [Eakes] of two Coulers.” This red coat, Stacy notes, was a distinctive feature of Bishop’s appearance, and has become a recurring detail in the accusations against her. The pattern is becoming clear: past grievances, now interpreted through the lens of witchcraft, are fueling these charges.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village and neighboring towns, the accusations continue to spread. Two more women, Elizabeth Fosdick of Malden and Elizabeth Paine of Charlestown, have been formally accused of witchcraft, joining the growing number of individuals facing questioning. Meanwhile, testimony against Bridget Bishop is mounting, detailing a long history of perceived offenses.

Two more women, Elizabeth Fosdick of Malden and Elizabeth Paine of Charlestown, have been formally accused of witchcraft, joining the growing number of individuals facing questioning. Meanwhile, testimony against Bridget Bishop is mounting, detailing a long history of perceived offenses.

This Friday, complaints were filed against Elizabeth Fosdick and Elizabeth Paine, both women from outside Salem Village, extending the reach of these proceedings beyond our immediate community. They were accused by several of the afflicted girls, including Mary Walcott and Mercy Lewis, and by Peter Tufts of Charlestown, who claims witchcraft harmed an enslaved woman. The accusations echo those already leveled against others: spectral evidence and unexplained misfortune. William Stacy’s deposition today offered a particularly detailed account of his long-standing animosity toward Bridget Bishop. He claims Bishop’s spirit visited him in the night, years ago, and that misfortune followed. As Stacy recalls, “about midnight this Deponent felt something between his lips Pressing hard ag't his teeth… she having then a black cap & a black hat, and a Red Coat with two [Eakes] of two Coulers.” This red coat, Stacy notes, was a distinctive feature of Bishop’s appearance, and has become a recurring detail in the accusations against her. The pattern is becoming clear: past grievances, now interpreted through the lens of witchcraft, are fueling these charges.

As detailed in the lede, Elizabeth Fosdick of Malden and Elizabeth Paine of Charlestown were formally accused of witchcraft today, broadening the scope of the investigations beyond Salem Village. Both women were named in complaints filed by Lieutenant Nathaniel Putnam and Joseph Whipple, and accusations came from the afflicted girls as well as Peter Tufts.

William Stacy provided a lengthy deposition detailing fourteen years of alleged harm caused by Bridget Bishop. Stacy described a spectral visit from Bishop’s form, claiming it occurred while he was in bed, and linked his daughter’s subsequent illness and death to Bishop’s actions.

Stacy’s testimony reveals a pattern of attributing long-held resentment and misfortune to witchcraft. He recounts a seemingly minor transaction—Bishop paying him three pence for a service—followed by a series of setbacks, which he now believes were caused by her.

The description of Bishop’s appearance during the alleged spectral visit is incredibly specific. Stacy claims she wore “a black cap & a black hat, and a Red Coat with two [Eakes] of two Coulers,” highlighting the significance of her clothing as a memorable and perhaps unsettling detail.

Peter Tufts, a resident of Charlestown, filed a complaint against both Elizabeth Fosdick and Elizabeth Paine, alleging they afflicted an enslaved woman under his care. This demonstrates the widespread fear and the extension of accusations to those perceived as vulnerable within the community.

Both Elizabeth Fosdick and Elizabeth Paine were apprehended following the complaints, though neither has yet faced a trial. Their cases, like many others, remain under investigation by the court.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with tension as the Court of Oyer and Terminer began its work today. Captain John Alden Jr., a respected merchant from Boston, was among those examined by Judges Hathorne, Corwin, and Gedney. The accusations against him came from the now-familiar group of afflicted girls – Mary Walcott, Mercy Lewis, and Abigail Williams – but the proceedings took a strange turn when one accuser initially pointed to a different man entirely before being corrected by someone whispering in her ear. As Alden himself would later write, describing this scene, “a company of poor distracted, or possessed creatures” were at play. This incident raises serious questions about the validity of the accusations and the methods being used to obtain them. Alongside Alden, Martha Carrier presented a defiant front, vehemently denying the charges leveled against her and even suggesting the accusers were themselves unwell. Carrier’s bold resistance marks her as a particularly compelling figure in this unfolding drama.
newspapers.summary: Today saw the first official session of the Court of Oyer and Terminer, with several prominent figures brought before the magistrates for examination. Captain John Alden Jr. faced accusations and revealed evidence of coaching among the accusers, while Martha Carrier boldly defended herself against charges of witchcraft and even accused her tormentors of madness.

Today saw the first official session of the Court of Oyer and Terminer, with several prominent figures brought before the magistrates for examination. Captain John Alden Jr. faced accusations and revealed evidence of coaching among the accusers, while Martha Carrier boldly defended herself against charges of witchcraft and even accused her tormentors of madness.

The atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with tension as the Court of Oyer and Terminer began its work today. Captain John Alden Jr., a respected merchant from Boston, was among those examined by Judges Hathorne, Corwin, and Gedney. The accusations against him came from the now-familiar group of afflicted girls – Mary Walcott, Mercy Lewis, and Abigail Williams – but the proceedings took a strange turn when one accuser initially pointed to a different man entirely before being corrected by someone whispering in her ear. As Alden himself would later write, describing this scene, “a company of poor distracted, or possessed creatures” were at play. This incident raises serious questions about the validity of the accusations and the methods being used to obtain them. Alongside Alden, Martha Carrier presented a defiant front, vehemently denying the charges leveled against her and even suggesting the accusers were themselves unwell. Carrier’s bold resistance marks her as a particularly compelling figure in this unfolding drama.

Captain John Alden Jr. underwent examination today, and the proceedings revealed evidence of coaching among the accusers. One girl initially identified the wrong man before being corrected by a voice prompting her from behind, suggesting manipulation during the examinations.

Martha Carrier was examined and offered a particularly forceful defense, denying the accusations against her and claiming her accusers were insane. She famously stated, “It is false the Devil is a liar,” refusing to concede any ground.

Elizabeth How was also examined today, alongside Carrier, Captain Alden, Wilmot Redd, and Philip English. She maintained her innocence, supported by extensive testimony from neighbors and ministers vouching for her character. She declared, “If it was the last moment I was to live, God knows I am innocent of any thing in this nature.”

During her examination, Martha Carrier dismissed the fits of the afflicted girls, saying, “They will dissemble if I look upon them,” and asserted that she had not looked at anyone since entering the room.

Nehemiah Abbott Jr. of Topsfield was the only accused person acquitted at examination today. The afflicted girls were unable to agree that he was the man they claimed to see.

Cotton Mather penned a letter to Judge John Richards, cautioning against relying too heavily on spectral evidence, noting that “the Devils have sometimes represented the Shapes of persons not only innocent, but also very virtuous.” However, he also urged the judges to consider such evidence, creating a dangerous ambiguity.

Judge Bartholomew Gedney, one of those presiding over the examinations, had prior business dealings with Captain John Alden Jr., yet made no effort to recuse himself from the case, highlighting the blurring of personal relationships and legal duty.

A warrant for Captain Alden’s arrest was issued earlier this week, and he was brought before the court today. The document details the complaints against him and authorizes his immediate apprehension.

newspapers.lede: The foundations of the prosecutions are shaking today as Sarah Churchill, a servant previously accusing her master George Jacobs and others, has recanted her confession. Churchill confided in Sarah Ingersoll that she lied under duress, fearing the consequences if she didn’t comply with the demands of Reverend Samuel Noyes. As she reportedly told Ingersoll, “If I told Mr. Noyes but once I had set my hand to the Book, he would believe me, but if I told the truth and said I had not set my hand to the Book a hundred times, he would not believe me.” This admission, made privately, exposes the pressure tactics being used to secure convictions and raises serious concerns about the reliability of the afflicted girls’ accusations. It suggests that fear, not spectral evidence, may be driving much of the testimony. The court, however, seems determined to continue its course, with Bridget Bishop already convicted and awaiting execution.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem, a crucial confession has been cast into doubt. Sarah Churchill, a key accuser, has admitted her testimony against several individuals was false, claiming she was coerced by Reverend Noyes. This revelation throws the validity of much of the recent evidence into question, even as the Court of Oyer and Terminer continues its work.

This revelation throws the validity of much of the recent evidence into question, even as the Court of Oyer and Terminer continues its work.

Churchill confided in Sarah Ingersoll that she lied under duress, fearing the consequences if she didn’t comply with the demands of Reverend Samuel Noyes. As she reportedly told Ingersoll, “If I told Mr. Noyes but once I had set my hand to the Book, he would believe me, but if I told the truth and said I had not set my hand to the Book a hundred times, he would not believe me.” This admission, made privately, exposes the pressure tactics being used to secure convictions and raises serious concerns about the reliability of the afflicted girls’ accusations. It suggests that fear, not spectral evidence, may be driving much of the testimony. The court, however, seems determined to continue its course, with Bridget Bishop already convicted and awaiting execution.

Sarah Churchill’s recantation is the most important development of the day. She confessed under pressure and implicated several individuals, including Bridget Bishop and George Jacobs. Now she claims her testimony was entirely fabricated, revealing a pattern of coercion by Reverend Noyes to secure confessions.

Giles Corey remains defiant in prison, but his focus has shifted. He is studying the law and appears to be calculating the implications of refusing to enter a plea – a strategy that could prevent forfeiture of his land to Sheriff Corwin.

Rebecca Nurse, imprisoned since late March, finds strength in her faith amidst her suffering. She prays for her sister and fellow accused, demonstrating a spiritual fortitude that unsettles the court. Francis Nurse continues gathering signatures for a petition in her defense.

Tituba, despite confessing to save her life, is now utterly alone in prison. She reflects on the consequences of her confession and the flood of accusations it unleashed, realizing she constructed the narrative that fueled the entire crisis.

Philip and Mary English have fled to New York, escaping arrest and the reach of the court. This act of defiance has enraged the Salem authorities and led to the aggressive seizure of their considerable property by Sheriff Corwin.

Mary Warren, while still publicly supporting the accusations, privately admitted to fellow prisoners that the afflicted girls’ testimony is unreliable, comparing it to the ravings of a long-term invalid. She claims her own afflicted visions were often the result of a "distempered" head.

The economic cost of the trials is becoming increasingly apparent. Families of the accused are losing valuable labor, and the county is accruing significant expenses for travel, court fees, and prison upkeep, all while officials profit from fines and property seizures.

Constable Putnam and Sheriff Corwin are personally benefiting from the trials. Constables are paid per arrest, and Sheriff Corwin is seizing property worth over 1,183 pounds, retaining a portion of the value for himself.

newspapers.lede: The trials have officially begun. This morning, Bridget Bishop stood before the magistrates and was found guilty of witchcraft. The evidence against her was a combination of prior accusations, her unconventional lifestyle – she ran a tavern without a license and was known for wearing a red bodice – and, most disturbingly, the testimony regarding alleged ‘witch’s marks’ found on her body. A jury of women, under the direction of Sheriff Corwin, conducted a physical examination, claiming to find “apreternathurall Excresence of flesh” between Bishop’s legs, which they interpreted as teats for a familiar spirit. As the magistrate’s record reads from the oath administered to those conducting the trial, they swore to act “truly and faithfully…as to Law and Justice doth appertain.” However, the methods employed, particularly this invasive examination, raise serious questions about the fairness and objectivity of these proceedings. Bishop’s fate is sealed, and she is scheduled to be hanged next week, setting a terrifying precedent for those accused.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a grim milestone in Salem Village. Bridget Bishop has become the first person condemned and sentenced to hang for witchcraft. The court, fueled by accusations and spectral evidence, has moved with alarming speed, and physical examinations of the accused are becoming commonplace.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a grim milestone in Salem Village. Bridget Bishop has become the first person condemned and sentenced to hang for witchcraft. The court, fueled by accusations and spectral evidence, has moved with alarming speed, and physical examinations of the accused are becoming commonplace.

The trials have officially begun. This morning, Bridget Bishop stood before the magistrates and was found guilty of witchcraft. The evidence against her was a combination of prior accusations, her unconventional lifestyle – she ran a tavern without a license and was known for wearing a red bodice – and, most disturbingly, the testimony regarding alleged ‘witch’s marks’ found on her body. A jury of women, under the direction of Sheriff Corwin, conducted a physical examination, claiming to find “apreternathurall Excresence of flesh” between Bishop’s legs, which they interpreted as teats for a familiar spirit. As the magistrate’s record reads from the oath administered to those conducting the trial, they swore to act “truly and faithfully…as to Law and Justice doth appertain.” However, the methods employed, particularly this invasive examination, raise serious questions about the fairness and objectivity of these proceedings. Bishop’s fate is sealed, and she is scheduled to be hanged next week, setting a terrifying precedent for those accused.

Bridget Bishop was convicted of witchcraft today and sentenced to hang, making her the first official victim of the Salem trials. The speed of the conviction, from indictment to sentencing in a single day, demonstrates the urgency and fervor driving the court.

A particularly disturbing practice was revealed today: the physical examination of accused witches. Nine women, acting as a jury, searched the bodies of Bishop, Elizabeth Proctor, Rebecca Nurse, Alice Parker, Susannah Martin, and Sarah Good for “witch’s marks” – evidence of a physical connection to demonic forces.

John Louder testified that he chased a deformed creature to Bridget Bishop’s orchard, providing further “evidence” of her involvement in witchcraft. This reliance on spectral evidence and subjective experiences continues to be a central feature of the trials.

The grand jury, composed of prominent men from Salem Town and surrounding areas, is wielding significant power in determining who will face trial. John Ruck, a merchant with ties to a currently accused man, Philip English, served as foreman today.

Testimony from Sarah Andrews reveals a long-held grievance against Elizabeth How, dating back seven years. Andrews claims How afflicted her sister with sickness and threatened her, highlighting how personal disputes are being woven into the fabric of these accusations.

John Westgate testified about a strange incident involving a black hog and a knife wound, linking it to Alice Parker and suggesting malicious intent. This illustrates the tendency to interpret everyday occurrences as evidence of witchcraft.

The economic impact of the trials is becoming increasingly apparent. Salem Town's harbor commerce is being disrupted as ships divert to Boston, and the prison system is placing a financial strain on both the accused and the community.

Thomas Brattle, a respected merchant and member of the Royal Society, is beginning to question the methods and evidence used in the trials, foreshadowing potential opposition to the proceedings. He believes the “touch test” itself is superstitious and lacks any rational basis.

newspapers.lede: The court continued its examinations today, but cracks are beginning to show in the foundation of these proceedings. Reverend Samuel Phillips of Rowley testified that Hannah Perley, whose death years ago fueled the accusations against Elizabeth Howe, actually recanted her claims that Howe had harmed her *before* she died. Phillips also revealed that Hannah’s brother attempted to pressure her into naming Howe. This is a stunning reversal and should, by all rights, dismantle the case against Howe, built as it was on this long-held grudge. Yet, the court appears inclined to disregard this crucial testimony. Meanwhile, other accusers continued to present their stories. Ann Putnam, Jr., in her deposition, stated, “I saw the Apperishtion of gooddy Nurs: and she did immediatly afflect me… pinching and pricking me.” Similar accounts were given by Mary Walcott and Elizabeth Hubbard, detailing how the apparitions of Rebecca Nurse tormented them, urging them to write in a book. Sarah Bibber also testified, claiming she witnessed the apparition of John Willard harming other afflicted girls, stating that Willard "fel upon me & tormented me grevesly & pinched me & threw me down.” The pattern of spectral evidence and the vulnerability of the accusers are becoming increasingly clear, even as doubts about the motivations behind these accusations grow.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, testimony emerged questioning the accusations against Elizabeth Howe, revealing potential coaching of accusers and a recanted claim of affliction. Despite this evidence, the examinations and depositions continue, with several young women reiterating their claims of being tormented by the apparitions of those accused.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, testimony emerged questioning the accusations against Elizabeth Howe, revealing potential coaching of accusers and a recanted claim of affliction. Despite this evidence, the examinations and depositions continue, with several young women reiterating their claims of being tormented by the apparitions of those accused.

The court continued its examinations today, but cracks are beginning to show in the foundation of these proceedings. Reverend Samuel Phillips of Rowley testified that Hannah Perley, whose death years ago fueled the accusations against Elizabeth Howe, actually recanted her claims that Howe had harmed her *before* she died. Phillips also revealed that Hannah’s brother attempted to pressure her into naming Howe. This is a stunning reversal and should, by all rights, dismantle the case against Howe, built as it was on this long-held grudge. Yet, the court appears inclined to disregard this crucial testimony. Meanwhile, other accusers continued to present their stories. Ann Putnam, Jr., in her deposition, stated, “I saw the Apperishtion of gooddy Nurs: and she did immediatly afflect me… pinching and pricking me.” Similar accounts were given by Mary Walcott and Elizabeth Hubbard, detailing how the apparitions of Rebecca Nurse tormented them, urging them to write in a book. Sarah Bibber also testified, claiming she witnessed the apparition of John Willard harming other afflicted girls, stating that Willard "fel upon me & tormented me grevesly & pinched me & threw me down.” The pattern of spectral evidence and the vulnerability of the accusers are becoming increasingly clear, even as doubts about the motivations behind these accusations grow.

As detailed in the lede, Reverend Samuel Phillips testified that Hannah Perley recanted her accusation against Elizabeth Howe before her death, and that her brother attempted to coerce the accusation. This evidence directly challenges the basis of the prosecution’s case against Howe, yet the court has so far shown no indication of dismissing the charges.

Several young women, including Ann Putnam, Jr., Mary Walcott, and Elizabeth Hubbard, provided depositions today detailing their experiences with the apparitions of Rebecca Nurse, claiming she pinched, pricked, and even attempted to choke them. These testimonies rely heavily on spectral evidence, which is increasingly debated but still accepted by the court.

Sarah Bibber testified that she saw the apparition of John Willard harming Mary Walcott and Marcy Lewis, adding to the growing list of accused individuals and demonstrating the escalating number of accusers and alleged victims.

The testimony of Reverend Phillips and Edward Payson revealed that they rebuked young Samuel Perley for urging his sister to accuse Elizabeth Howe, offering direct evidence of potential manipulation and coaching of accusers within families.

The depositions consistently mention the accused urging the afflicted to write in a “book,” a detail that remains unexplained but suggests a symbolic act of submission or allegiance being demanded by the alleged witches.

The sheer volume of depositions taken today underscores the intensity of the investigations and the relentless pursuit of those suspected of witchcraft within Salem Village and surrounding communities.

newspapers.lede: The meeting house was filled with tension this afternoon as Job Tookey of Beverly was questioned about accusations of witchcraft. Several young women, including Mary Warren, Ann Putnam Jr., and Susannah Sheldon, testified they saw Tookey afflict them, and claimed he boasted of his power to control the Devil. According to the magistrate’s record, the girls swore Tookey “did this day tell them…that he had Learneing and could Raise the divele when he pleased.” Tookey himself maintained his innocence, attributing the harm to the Devil acting in his form. John Louder testified that Tookey openly admitted to communicating with the Devil, saying he “can as freely discourse the Divell as well as he speaking to him.” The magistrates, John Hathorne, Bartholomew Gedney, and Jonathan Corwin, recorded these statements, adding to the growing weight of evidence – or, at least, testimony – against the accused. The day also saw a warrant issued for Mary Ireson, a woman from the neighboring town of Lynn, broadening the reach of these investigations.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, accusations continue to mount. Job Tookey underwent a rigorous examination before magistrates, and a warrant was issued for the arrest of Mary Ireson of Lynn, expanding the scope of the investigations beyond Salem itself. These events signal a deepening crisis as authorities attempt to understand the source of the afflictions plaguing young women.

Today in Salem Village, accusations continue to mount. Job Tookey underwent a rigorous examination before magistrates, and a warrant was issued for the arrest of Mary Ireson of Lynn, expanding the scope of the investigations beyond Salem itself. These events signal a deepening crisis as authorities attempt to understand the source of the afflictions plaguing young women.

The meeting house was filled with tension this afternoon as Job Tookey of Beverly was questioned about accusations of witchcraft. Several young women, including Mary Warren, Ann Putnam Jr., and Susannah Sheldon, testified they saw Tookey afflict them, and claimed he boasted of his power to control the Devil. According to the magistrate’s record, the girls swore Tookey “did this day tell them…that he had Learneing and could Raise the divele when he pleased.” Tookey himself maintained his innocence, attributing the harm to the Devil acting in his form. John Louder testified that Tookey openly admitted to communicating with the Devil, saying he “can as freely discourse the Divell as well as he speaking to him.” The magistrates, John Hathorne, Bartholomew Gedney, and Jonathan Corwin, recorded these statements, adding to the growing weight of evidence – or, at least, testimony – against the accused. The day also saw a warrant issued for Mary Ireson, a woman from the neighboring town of Lynn, broadening the reach of these investigations.

As we heard in the lede, Job Tookey faced intense questioning today, with multiple accusers claiming he directly caused their afflictions and admitted to dealings with the Devil. The girls testified they witnessed these events firsthand, adding to the gravity of the accusations and the pressure on Tookey to confess.

A warrant was issued for the arrest of Mary Ireson of Lynn, broadening the scope of the witch hunt beyond Salem Village. This indicates that the afflicted girls, or those claiming to be, are identifying individuals from surrounding communities as the source of their troubles.

Several witnesses, including John Louder, Samuel King, and Daniel Bacon, corroborated claims that Tookey spoke of his ability to communicate with the Devil. This testimony, while concerning, relies heavily on secondhand accounts and the interpretation of Tookey’s words.

John Stacy testified that he heard Tookey declare his support for a Mr. Burrows, immediately following which the afflicted girls began to suffer more intensely. Stacy’s statement attempts to connect Tookey’s statements to specific instances of affliction, further fueling the accusations.

Constable Henry Collings of Lynn confirmed he apprehended Mary Ireson and transported her to Salem for examination, demonstrating the cooperation between towns in addressing this perceived threat.

The magistrates continued their practice of recording sworn oaths from accusers, lending an air of legal formality to what is, at its core, a series of accusations based on spectral evidence and the testimonies of young women.

newspapers.lede: The accusations against Rebecca Nurse took a new turn this Thursday as her daughters, Rebecca Preston and Mary Tarbell, petitioned the court for a second physical examination. They argue the initial assessment, which found evidence of spectral affliction, may stem from a natural cause—specifically, the long-term effects of repeated childbirth. This is a remarkable step, considering the momentum behind the trials, and represents one of the earliest documented attempts at a formal defense. The daughters’ statement notes their mother “have bene trobled with an Infirmity of body for many years,” suggesting a pre-existing condition could explain the symptoms presented to the court. As the magistrate’s record shows, the family hopes a fresh examination by different women will reveal “Arise from A naturall Cause” rather than witchcraft, a plea that could significantly alter the course of the proceedings. This challenge, though likely to be met with resistance, signals growing unease with the increasingly rapid pace of the trials.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the family of Rebecca Nurse formally challenged the medical findings against her, requesting a second examination. This is a rare instance of direct opposition to the court’s proceedings and a sign that some are beginning to question the accusations fueling this crisis.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, the family of Rebecca Nurse formally challenged the medical findings against her, requesting a second examination. This is a rare instance of direct opposition to the court’s proceedings and a sign that some are beginning to question the accusations fueling this crisis.

The accusations against Rebecca Nurse took a new turn this Thursday as her daughters, Rebecca Preston and Mary Tarbell, petitioned the court for a second physical examination. They argue the initial assessment, which found evidence of spectral affliction, may stem from a natural cause—specifically, the long-term effects of repeated childbirth. This is a remarkable step, considering the momentum behind the trials, and represents one of the earliest documented attempts at a formal defense. The daughters’ statement notes their mother “have bene trobled with an Infirmity of body for many years,” suggesting a pre-existing condition could explain the symptoms presented to the court. As the magistrate’s record shows, the family hopes a fresh examination by different women will reveal “Arise from A naturall Cause” rather than witchcraft, a plea that could significantly alter the course of the proceedings. This challenge, though likely to be met with resistance, signals growing unease with the increasingly rapid pace of the trials.

Rebecca Nurse’s daughters formally petitioned for a second physical examination of their mother, arguing her affliction may be due to a long-standing physical weakness rather than witchcraft. This petition is an unprecedented attempt to challenge the court’s findings and introduces the possibility of natural causes being overlooked in the examinations.

This action by the Nurse family marks one of the first documented instances of organized defense against the witchcraft accusations, a notable departure from the largely passive acceptance of the court’s authority seen thus far.

The request specifically asks for a different group of women to conduct the examination, suggesting a lack of confidence in the initial assessment and a belief that a biased perspective may have influenced the results.

Several other individuals remain in custody, awaiting examination or trial, including Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne, whose cases continue to be central to the unfolding events.

Preparations are underway for the upcoming court sessions, where more accused individuals are expected to appear before the magistrates and face questioning.

Local ministers continue to preach sermons emphasizing the dangers of witchcraft and the importance of rooting out evil within the community, further fueling the atmosphere of fear and suspicion.

newspapers.lede: The pace of accusations and examinations continues to quicken here in Salem Village. This Friday, both Ann Dolliver and Mary Ireson faced their accusers before magistrates Bartholomew Gedney, John Hathorne, and Jonathan Corwin. The afflicted girls—Susanna Sheldon, Mary Warren, and Elizabeth Booth among them—were brought forward to identify the accused. According to the magistrate’s record of Ann Dolliver’s examination, when asked if she had ever practiced witchcraft, she responded, “not with intent to hurt any body with it.” However, she admitted to spending nights in the woods, and the girls affirmed that she was the one afflicting them. The girls claimed Dolliver was present even when she appeared to be elsewhere, and even identified a spirit alongside her. While the court recorded the girls’ vehement accusations, the grand jury found insufficient evidence to indict Mary Ireson, returning an “Ignoramus” – a finding of no true bill – in the case of Elizabeth Booth. This is a rare instance of a reprieve, but it does little to slow the momentum of the trials.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the examinations of Ann Dolliver and Mary Ireson took place before magistrates Gedney, Hathorne, and Corwin. Both women were accused of witchcraft based on the afflicted girls’ accusations, though the grand jury found no cause to indict Ireson. A warrant was issued for Dolliver’s arrest, marking a rapid escalation in the legal proceedings.

Today in Salem Village, the examinations of Ann Dolliver and Mary Ireson took place before magistrates Gedney, Hathorne, and Corwin. Both women were accused of witchcraft based on the afflicted girls’ accusations, though the grand jury found no cause to indict Ireson. A warrant was issued for Dolliver’s arrest, marking a rapid escalation in the legal proceedings.

The pace of accusations and examinations continues to quicken here in Salem Village. This Friday, both Ann Dolliver and Mary Ireson faced their accusers before magistrates Bartholomew Gedney, John Hathorne, and Jonathan Corwin. The afflicted girls—Susanna Sheldon, Mary Warren, and Elizabeth Booth among them—were brought forward to identify the accused. According to the magistrate’s record of Ann Dolliver’s examination, when asked if she had ever practiced witchcraft, she responded, “not with intent to hurt any body with it.” However, she admitted to spending nights in the woods, and the girls affirmed that she was the one afflicting them. The girls claimed Dolliver was present even when she appeared to be elsewhere, and even identified a spirit alongside her. While the court recorded the girls’ vehement accusations, the grand jury found insufficient evidence to indict Mary Ireson, returning an “Ignoramus” – a finding of no true bill – in the case of Elizabeth Booth. This is a rare instance of a reprieve, but it does little to slow the momentum of the trials.

As detailed in the lede, Ann Dolliver and Mary Ireson were both examined today on charges of witchcraft. The afflicted girls were brought into the room to visually identify the accused, and repeatedly fell into fits during the proceedings, claiming the women were causing them harm.

A warrant was issued this morning for the arrest of Ann Dolliver, wife of William Dolliver of Gloucester, based on accusations from Mary Warren and Susanna Sheldon. The warrant, as the magistrate’s record shows, specifically charges her with witchcraft that has caused harm to the bodies of Warren and Sheldon.

Despite vehement accusations from three of the afflicted girls, the grand jury found no cause to indict Mary Ireson for afflicting Elizabeth Booth. This contrasts sharply with the usual course of events, where accusations have almost always led to further legal action.

During her examination, Mary Ireson admitted to having a bad temper but denied practicing witchcraft. She stated she “might be left to this affliction for her other sins,” but maintained her innocence regarding witchcraft.

The testimony revealed a strange detail about Ireson’s attire—she wore a riding hood during the examination, and the girls claimed they had never seen her before, yet recognized her as the source of their afflictions.

Ann Dolliver confessed to spending nights in the woods, explaining she sometimes did so to escape conflict with her mother. She also recounted an incident where a Black man frightened her, making her unsure if she could distinguish a spirit from a man in the dark.

newspapers.lede: The village is reeling from a deposition given today by John Allen, a forty-five-year-old farmer from Salisbury. Allen testified that Susannah Martin, wife of George Martin, requested he haul staves – wood for barrels – for her, but he refused, citing his oxen were too weak. According to Allen, Martin responded with a chilling prediction: “I had been as good; for my oxen should never do me much more service.” Allen claims he retorted, angered by what he perceived as a threat, saying he would throw her into a nearby brook. Shortly after this confrontation, Allen reports that fourteen of his oxen stampeded and drowned in the sea, leaving him with only one animal saved. As Allen stated in his deposition, this was no accident, but a direct result of Martin’s malice. This dramatic testimony, with the image of fourteen animals lost to the waves, is quickly spreading through the village and fueling the growing fear.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the accusations against Susannah Martin intensified with startling testimony about a threat and a devastating loss of livestock. Meanwhile, the examination of Job Tookey revealed further claims of spectral evidence and alleged harm caused by the accused. These events signal a rapid escalation in the trials.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, the accusations against Susannah Martin intensified with startling testimony about a threat and a devastating loss of livestock. Meanwhile, the examination of Job Tookey revealed further claims of spectral evidence and alleged harm caused by the accused. These events signal a rapid escalation in the trials.

The village is reeling from a deposition given today by John Allen, a forty-five-year-old farmer from Salisbury. Allen testified that Susannah Martin, wife of George Martin, requested he haul staves – wood for barrels – for her, but he refused, citing his oxen were too weak. According to Allen, Martin responded with a chilling prediction: “I had been as good; for my oxen should never do me much more service.” Allen claims he retorted, angered by what he perceived as a threat, saying he would throw her into a nearby brook. Shortly after this confrontation, Allen reports that fourteen of his oxen stampeded and drowned in the sea, leaving him with only one animal saved. As Allen stated in his deposition, this was no accident, but a direct result of Martin’s malice. This dramatic testimony, with the image of fourteen animals lost to the waves, is quickly spreading through the village and fueling the growing fear.

John Allen’s deposition details a confrontation with Susannah Martin and the subsequent loss of fourteen oxen, which he attributes to her witchcraft. This is a particularly striking example of maleficium—harm directly caused by witchcraft—and has deeply shaken the community with its scale and the vivid imagery of the drowned animals.

The examination of Job Tookey today revealed new claims of spectral evidence. According to the testimony, Tookey’s gaze affected the afflicted girls, and Mary Warren, in a trance, claimed Tookey had killed Gamaliel Hawkins in Barbados by sticking a pin into him.

Testimony from Susanna Shelden alleged that Tookey’s apparition warned her he would no longer reveal what he knew. This claim further supports the belief in spectral evidence, where spirits are believed to be able to communicate and act in the physical world.

Mary Walcot reported seeing apparitions of three men, three women, and two children all calling for vengeance against Tookey, adding to the chorus of accusations based on visions and trances.

Elizabeth Booth, a young afflicted girl, reportedly pointed to the same location as Walcot, but was unable to speak, further demonstrating the physical manifestations the girls claim to experience during these examinations.

The magistrate’s record shows Job Tookey denied the accusations, stating he “knew not what [he] said” when questioned about seeing the Devil, but his denials did little to counter the girls’ increasingly elaborate testimonies.

newspapers.lede: The courtroom in Salem Town saw a significant development this morning as Associate Judge Nathaniel Saltonstall tendered his resignation from the Court of Oyer and Terminer. Saltonstall, a respected man of approximately fifty-three years from Haverhill, appears to have been deeply troubled by the recent conviction and sentencing of Bridget Bishop, who is to be hanged on Wednesday next. Though details are scarce, his decision to step away is the first public break from the court and suggests that not everyone is convinced of the justice being served. As Thomas Brattle would later write, Saltonstall was “much troubled” with the proceedings, and his departure is a clear indication of his dissatisfaction. However, even in withdrawing from the court, Saltonstall has not escaped suspicion. We have learned that accusations of witchcraft have already been leveled against him, based on spectral evidence, a troubling sign of how quickly these charges can spread. The magistrate’s record shows a growing pattern of accusers claiming to see the shapes, or specters, of the accused afflicting them—evidence that many find dubious, yet the court continues to accept.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the trials as Judge Nathaniel Saltonstall resigned from the Court of Oyer and Terminer following the conviction of Bridget Bishop. His departure, a rare act of dissent, signals growing unease with the proceedings, though it also made him a target of accusation.
