Saltonstall, a respected man of approximately fifty-three years from Haverhill, appears to have been deeply troubled by the recent conviction and sentencing of Bridget Bishop, who is to be hanged on Wednesday next. Though details are scarce, his decision to step away is the first public break from the court and suggests that not everyone is convinced of the justice being served. As Thomas Brattle would later write, Saltonstall was “much troubled” with the proceedings, and his departure is a clear indication of his dissatisfaction. However, even in withdrawing from the court, Saltonstall has not escaped suspicion. We have learned that accusations of witchcraft have already been leveled against him, based on spectral evidence, a troubling sign of how quickly these charges can spread. The magistrate’s record shows a growing pattern of accusers claiming to see the shapes, or specters, of the accused afflicting them—evidence that many find dubious, yet the court continues to accept.

Judge Nathaniel Saltonstall’s resignation represents a growing fracture within the judicial system overseeing these trials. His decision followed the conviction of Bridget Bishop and signals his disapproval of the court’s methods, though it has also made him vulnerable to accusations of witchcraft based on spectral evidence.

The impending execution of Bridget Bishop continues to weigh heavily on the village. She was found guilty of “grievous and notorious acts of witchcraft” and will be the first to be hanged on Gallows Hill.

Concern is rising among some residents about the reliance on spectral evidence in the trials. Many question the validity of testimony based on visions or dreams, yet the court continues to admit it as proof of guilt.

Several more examinations were held today, though the names of those questioned have not yet been publicly released. The atmosphere in Salem Village remains tense, with fear and suspicion pervasive.

Reverend Samuel Parris continues to actively support the prosecution of those accused, seeing their affliction as evidence of Satan’s influence within the community.

Despite the growing unease, the Court of Oyer and Terminer remains steadfast in its pursuit of those suspected of witchcraft, promising further examinations and trials in the days to come.

newspapers.lede: This morning, Bridget Bishop was led to Proctor’s Ledge and hanged for witchcraft. A large crowd gathered to witness the event, a somber spectacle that marks the undeniable escalation of these proceedings. Bishop, a woman long considered an outsider in this community, maintained her innocence to the end. The court, however, found the evidence – including testimony about strange dolls found in her house and her unconventional dress – sufficient for conviction. As the magistrate’s record reads, Bishop “did most obstinately and vehemently deny” the charges, even as the noose was placed around her neck. Her execution is not simply the end of one woman’s life; it’s a signal that the Court of Oyer and Terminer intends to pursue these accusations to their ultimate conclusion. Judge Nathaniel Saltonstall, deeply troubled by the proceedings, has already resigned from the court in protest, a sign of the growing division within the colony. This is a day that will be remembered, and feared, for generations.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a dark turning point in Salem Village. Bridget Bishop has been hanged as a witch – the first execution of these trials. The court has now moved beyond accusations and examinations to capital punishment, setting a dangerous precedent for the days to come. This event will reverberate through the colony.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a dark turning point in Salem Village. Bridget Bishop has been hanged as a witch – the first execution of these trials. The court has now moved beyond accusations and examinations to capital punishment, setting a dangerous precedent for the days to come. This event will reverberate through the colony.

This morning, Bridget Bishop was led to Proctor’s Ledge and hanged for witchcraft. A large crowd gathered to witness the event, a somber spectacle that marks the undeniable escalation of these proceedings. Bishop, a woman long considered an outsider in this community, maintained her innocence to the end. The court, however, found the evidence – including testimony about strange dolls found in her house and her unconventional dress – sufficient for conviction. As the magistrate’s record reads, Bishop “did most obstinately and vehemently deny” the charges, even as the noose was placed around her neck. Her execution is not simply the end of one woman’s life; it’s a signal that the Court of Oyer and Terminer intends to pursue these accusations to their ultimate conclusion. Judge Nathaniel Saltonstall, deeply troubled by the proceedings, has already resigned from the court in protest, a sign of the growing division within the colony. This is a day that will be remembered, and feared, for generations.

Bridget Bishop was hanged this morning at Proctor’s Ledge, becoming the first victim of the Salem witch trials. Her execution confirms that the Court of Oyer and Terminer is prepared to impose the death penalty based on the current evidence and accusations.

The court deliberately selected Bridget Bishop as its first case, knowing she was an easy target. She had been accused of witchcraft once before, in 1680, and her nonconformist lifestyle – running a tavern and wearing brightly colored clothing – had long made her a target of gossip and suspicion.

Bishop’s clothing was presented as evidence during her trial, demonstrating how superficial details were used to condemn her. She wore a black cap and a red bodice, a color considered vain and worldly in Puritan society.

The ministers’ recent “Return” – their advice on spectral evidence – was ambiguous and ultimately ineffective in curbing the court’s enthusiasm for convictions. While urging caution, they did not explicitly prohibit the use of spectral evidence, leaving the door open for continued prosecutions.

Nathaniel Saltonstall’s resignation from the Court of Oyer and Terminer is a significant development, indicating a growing dissent within the legal system regarding the fairness and validity of the trials. He was 'very much dissatisfied with the proceedings,' and his departure leaves a void on the bench.

Beyond Salem, news has arrived of a major attack on the settlement of Wells, Maine. French and Abenaki forces besieged the town, highlighting the vulnerability of the frontier and the constant threat of conflict with native tribes.

The community remembers Bridget Bishop's 1680 trial, a case that established the possibility of witchcraft accusations in Salem Village. Although acquitted, the accusation lingered, making her a prime target when the new hysteria began.

Bishop operated an unlicensed tavern, where she allowed young men to play games and drink late into the night. This violation of Puritan codes, along with her outspoken nature, contributed to her reputation as a troublemaker.

newspapers.lede: The Court of Oyer and Terminer continues its work this Sunday, even as questions arise about the reliability of the evidence being used. Twelve ministers, led by Cotton Mather, delivered a statement to Governor Phips urging caution in accepting spectral evidence – testimony based on visions or apparitions. They acknowledge the Devil’s power to deceive, stating that he “hath sometimes represented the shapes of persons not only innocent, but also very virtuous.” However, the ministers also praise the court’s “sedulous endeavours” and endorse its “vigorous prosecution,” creating a confusing message. As the magistrate’s record shows, Chief Justice Stoughton seems intent on interpreting the statement as a full endorsement of the current practices, effectively neutralizing any caution offered. This ambiguity, coupled with the continued acceptance of spectral testimony, casts a long shadow over the proceedings. Adding to the unrest, Edward and Sarah Bishop, targeted after Edward publicly demonstrated a method to stop afflictions, have escaped custody and fled the colony. Judge Nathaniel Saltonstall has also resigned from the court, a rare act of dissent after the execution of Bridget Bishop last week.
newspapers.summary: Today brings mixed signals in the trials. Twelve ministers cautiously advise the court on spectral evidence, but their message is muddied by Cotton Mather’s support for the proceedings. Meanwhile, several accused, including Edward and Sarah Bishop, have fled, and Judge Saltonstall has resigned in protest after the recent execution.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today brings mixed signals in the trials. Twelve ministers cautiously advise the court on spectral evidence, but their message is muddied by Cotton Mather’s support for the proceedings. Meanwhile, several accused, including Edward and Sarah Bishop, have fled, and Judge Saltonstall has resigned in protest after the recent execution.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer continues its work this Sunday, even as questions arise about the reliability of the evidence being used. Twelve ministers, led by Cotton Mather, delivered a statement to Governor Phips urging caution in accepting spectral evidence – testimony based on visions or apparitions. They acknowledge the Devil’s power to deceive, stating that he “hath sometimes represented the shapes of persons not only innocent, but also very virtuous.” However, the ministers also praise the court’s “sedulous endeavours” and endorse its “vigorous prosecution,” creating a confusing message. As the magistrate’s record shows, Chief Justice Stoughton seems intent on interpreting the statement as a full endorsement of the current practices, effectively neutralizing any caution offered. This ambiguity, coupled with the continued acceptance of spectral testimony, casts a long shadow over the proceedings. Adding to the unrest, Edward and Sarah Bishop, targeted after Edward publicly demonstrated a method to stop afflictions, have escaped custody and fled the colony. Judge Nathaniel Saltonstall has also resigned from the court, a rare act of dissent after the execution of Bridget Bishop last week.

As we discussed, twelve ministers delivered their advisory document, the ‘Return of Several Ministers,’ to Governor Phips today. While urging caution regarding spectral evidence, the statement also praised the court’s efforts, essentially offering a mixed message that allows the court to continue its current course.

Edward and Sarah Bishop escaped from custody today, becoming fugitives and seeking refuge with sympathetic friends. Edward’s earlier actions, attempting to disprove accusations by physically stopping the afflicted girls, ironically made him a prime target for the court.

Judge Nathaniel Saltonstall has resigned from the Court of Oyer and Terminer in protest after the execution of Bridget Bishop. This is the only resignation from the court to date and signals growing unease with the proceedings.

A petition signed by 39 neighbors defending Rebecca Nurse demonstrates a strong community belief in her character. These villagers, many of whom know Nurse well through years of shared work and community life, attest to her piety and good standing—but their testimony is being ignored by the court.

Sheriff George Corwin seized an astonishing 1,183 pounds worth of property from Philip English, including furniture, wine, and livestock. This seizure, the largest of the crisis so far, highlights the financial devastation being inflicted on the accused and their families.

News has reached us of an attack on Wells, Maine, Burroughs’s former parish, occurring just days ago on June 10th, while he remains imprisoned here in Salem. The raid, carried out by Native Americans and French troops, underscores the vulnerability of frontier settlements.

Cotton Mather’s position on spectral evidence remains deeply ambiguous. While privately acknowledging its dangers, he publicly supports the court and has not corrected Chief Justice Stoughton’s interpretation of the ministers’ advisory, effectively undermining its cautionary message.

The court continues to rely on spectral evidence, despite the warnings, because of a combination of factors: the theological convictions of Chief Justice Stoughton, the lack of other evidence, the dramatic effect of the afflicted girls' testimony, and the court’s need to demonstrate its authority under the new charter.

newspapers.lede: The weight of these trials continues to fall heavily on families in and around Salem Village. Today, Roger Toothaker, a man accused of witchcraft just last month, died while imprisoned in Boston. A coroner’s inquest was immediately convened, and the jury determined his death was due to natural causes. However, this offers little comfort to his family, already deeply troubled by the accusations. His wife, Mary Toothaker, confessed to witchcraft on May 28th and, shockingly, implicated her own sister, Martha Carrier, in the dark practices. Now, their daughter Martha Emerson is also facing accusations, and even young Margaret Toothaker, just ten years old, is suspected. As the magistrate's record reads from the coroner’s warrant, officials were compelled to act “by Vertue of mine office, These are in the names of our Sovereigne Lord and Lady, King William and Quenne Mary of England etc't.” This underscores the authority behind the proceedings, but does little to ease the mounting terror.
newspapers.summary: Today brings more sorrow to Salem Village, as Roger Toothaker dies in a Boston jail while awaiting trial. His death, ruled natural, doesn't lessen the fear gripping the community, especially as his wife and daughter now face accusations of witchcraft, adding to the growing list of the afflicted and accused.

His death, ruled natural, doesn't lessen the fear gripping the community, especially as his wife and daughter now face accusations of witchcraft, adding to the growing list of the afflicted and accused.

A coroner’s inquest was immediately convened, and the jury determined his death was due to natural causes. Now, their daughter Martha Emerson is also facing accusations, and even young Margaret Toothaker, just ten years old, is suspected. As the magistrate's record reads from the coroner’s warrant, officials were compelled to act “by Vertue of mine office, These are in the names of our Sovereigne Lord and Lady, King William and Quenne Mary of England etc't.” This underscores the authority behind the proceedings, but does little to ease the mounting terror.

Roger Toothaker died in Boston jail today, officially ruled a natural death by a coroner’s jury, though he had been accused of witchcraft on May 18th. This death further complicates the proceedings and intensifies the fear surrounding the trials.

Mary Toothaker, Roger’s wife, confessed to witchcraft on May 28th and, in her confession, accused her sister, Martha Carrier, of being a witch. This is a pattern we’ve seen before, with the accused turning on family members to save themselves.

Martha Emerson, the daughter of Roger and Mary Toothaker, is now also accused of witchcraft, further devastating the family and expanding the circle of suspicion.

Even ten-year-old Margaret Toothaker has been accused, a particularly disturbing development that highlights how fear has gripped even the youngest members of the community.

The swift response of authorities, as evidenced by the coroner’s warrant, demonstrates the seriousness with which the Massachusetts Bay government is taking these accusations and the urgency with which they are proceeding.

newspapers.lede: The magistrate’s court is preparing for another trial, this time of Susannah Martin of Amesbury. Today, constables traveled throughout the region, delivering official summonses demanding testimony against her. The document itself, issued in the name of King William and Queen Mary, is a stark reminder of the authority backing these proceedings. It reads, in part, “Wee Command you and Every of you…to be and appear at the Speciall Court of Oyer & Terminer…to testify the truth to the best of their knowledge On Severall Indictments to be Exhibited against Susanna Martin of Amesberry.” Constable James Freeze and others were specifically instructed to find anyone who may have heard Martin make threats, even concerning something as mundane as an ox, and compel them to appear in court. This search for evidence, however tenuous, highlights the desperation to find corroboration for the spectral evidence already presented. The court is scheduled to convene tomorrow, and Martin’s fate hangs in the balance.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the wheels of justice—or injustice—continue to turn. Summonses have been issued for witnesses to testify against Susannah Martin, a woman accused of witchcraft, preparing for her trial at the Court of Oyer and Terminer tomorrow. This escalation demonstrates the growing momentum of the trials and the widening net of accusations.

Summonses have been issued for witnesses to testify against Susannah Martin, a woman accused of witchcraft, preparing for her trial at the Court of Oyer and Terminer tomorrow. This escalation demonstrates the growing momentum of the trials and the widening net of accusations.

Today, constables traveled throughout the region, delivering official summonses demanding testimony against her. The document itself, issued in the name of King William and Queen Mary, is a stark reminder of the authority backing these proceedings. It reads, in part, “Wee Command you and Every of you…to be and appear at the Speciall Court of Oyer & Terminer…to testify the truth to the best of their knowledge On Severall Indictments to be Exhibited against Susanna Martin of Amesberry.” Constable James Freeze and others were specifically instructed to find anyone who may have heard Martin make threats, even concerning something as mundane as an ox, and compel them to appear in court. This search for evidence, however tenuous, highlights the desperation to find corroboration for the spectral evidence already presented. The court is scheduled to convene tomorrow, and Martin’s fate hangs in the balance.

As detailed in the lede, official summonses were issued today for numerous witnesses to testify against Susannah Martin at tomorrow’s session of the Court of Oyer and Terminer. These summonses covered a wide geographic area, including Amesbury, Salisbury, and Newbury, indicating the breadth of the accusations against her and the effort to gather evidence.

The wording of the summons, issued “by the Grace of God” in the names of King William and Queen Mary, underscores the perceived legitimacy of the court and its proceedings in the eyes of officials. This connection to the crown is meant to reinforce the authority of the trials.

Constable James Freeze received specific instructions to locate individuals who may have overheard Susannah Martin making threats, even regarding property damage, demonstrating the court’s willingness to consider even the slightest accusations as potential evidence.

The continuing pattern of summonses and court preparations confirms that, despite the recent execution of Bridget Bishop, the trials are not slowing down. The court remains active and intent on pursuing those accused of witchcraft.

The magistrate's record shows that John Atkinson, his wife, and his son Nathaniel were all warned to appear in court as witnesses in this case, highlighting the pressure placed upon families and communities to participate in the legal process.

newspapers.lede: The courtroom in Salem Town saw further harrowing testimony today concerning Goodwife Sarah Good. The most shocking evidence came from her own young daughter, Dorcas, age four or five. According to the deposition, the child claimed her mother “did murrder it” – referring to an infant Good had lost – and declared, “she was A witch.” She went on to say her mother “did it becaus that she Could not atend it,” suggesting Good offered the child to the Devil because she could not properly care for it. This claim, made by a child barely old enough to speak, has deeply impacted the court and fueled the growing fear surrounding Good. Alongside this, Sarah Gadge and her husband testified that Good sought entry to their home, was refused due to fears of smallpox, and then cursed them – the next morning, their cow died. This pattern of request, refusal, and subsequent misfortune is becoming increasingly common in these proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the accusations against Goodwife Good continue to mount with disturbing testimony from her own child. Meanwhile, Rebecca Nurse petitions the court for a more reasonable examination, and several citizens step forward to defend Elizabeth Howe against the growing tide of suspicion.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, the accusations against Goodwife Good continue to mount with disturbing testimony from her own child. Meanwhile, Rebecca Nurse petitions the court for a more reasonable examination, and several citizens step forward to defend Elizabeth Howe against the growing tide of suspicion.

The courtroom in Salem Town saw further harrowing testimony today concerning Goodwife Sarah Good. The most shocking evidence came from her own young daughter, Dorcas, age four or five. According to the deposition, the child claimed her mother “did murrder it” – referring to an infant Good had lost – and declared, “she was A witch.” She went on to say her mother “did it becaus that she Could not atend it,” suggesting Good offered the child to the Devil because she could not properly care for it. This claim, made by a child barely old enough to speak, has deeply impacted the court and fueled the growing fear surrounding Good. Alongside this, Sarah Gadge and her husband testified that Good sought entry to their home, was refused due to fears of smallpox, and then cursed them – the next morning, their cow died. This pattern of request, refusal, and subsequent misfortune is becoming increasingly common in these proceedings.

As we heard in the lede, the testimony against Sarah Good reached a new level of intensity today with her young daughter Dorcas claiming Good confessed to witchcraft and even murder. This is particularly damaging because it comes from a child, seen as incapable of deceit, and aligns with the established pattern of spectral evidence and maleficium – harm caused by witchcraft.

Rebecca Nurse, facing accusations herself, took the unusual step of petitioning the court for a re-examination of her body. She argues that any marks found during the initial search were likely caused by childbirth and nursing her eight children, offering a natural explanation for what others interpret as “witch teats.” She specifically requests experienced midwives conduct this new examination.

Elizabeth Howe is receiving support from several witnesses, including her 94-year-old father-in-law, James How, who testified to her years of faithful service to his family. Reverend Samuel Phillips of Rowley also revealed a recantation of accusations against Howe, though it seems to have been coerced.

Constables are actively working to serve summonses to potential witnesses, as evidenced by a document detailing requests for testimony from individuals in Andover and Bilrica regarding the cases of John Willard and Martha Carrier. This shows the investigation is expanding beyond Salem Village.

William Baker, a constable, submitted an account for his expenses today, detailing travel costs for apprehending suspects and securing the court. This provides a glimpse into the logistical challenges and financial burden of these proceedings.

There was a brief incident involving a prisoner named Goody Green attempting to escape custody. Baker’s account shows he and others spent time and resources recapturing her, highlighting the difficulties in maintaining control of those accused.

newspapers.lede: The Court of Oyer and Terminer concluded the trial of Susannah Martin this afternoon, finding her guilty of witchcraft after hours of testimony. Unlike Rebecca Nurse, who benefited from a petition signed by 39 neighbors attesting to her good character, Martin arrived in Salem with no local support. Witnesses from Amesbury, her home town, recounted decades of suspicions – livestock dying after disputes, unexplained illnesses, and claims of spectral visitations. The court seemed to give particular weight to these long-standing accusations. As the magistrate’s record reads, one neighbor testified that years ago, Martin had a keg under her arm, which she claimed was a small dog. This seemingly minor detail, like so many others, contributed to the growing narrative of Martin’s supernatural power. Martin’s defiant behavior during her earlier examination likely did her no favors. The conviction solidifies the pattern: accusations fueled by old resentments, interpreted through the lens of spectral evidence, and resulting in swift and severe judgments.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a grim milestone in Salem Village. Susannah Martin of Amesbury was tried and convicted of witchcraft, joining Rebecca Nurse and others awaiting the judgment of the court. Meanwhile, neighbors continue to offer testimonies, both for and against the accused, revealing long-held grudges and fears fueling this crisis.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a grim milestone in Salem Village. Susannah Martin of Amesbury was tried and convicted of witchcraft, joining Rebecca Nurse and others awaiting the judgment of the court. Meanwhile, neighbors continue to offer testimonies, both for and against the accused, revealing long-held grudges and fears fueling this crisis.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer concluded the trial of Susannah Martin this afternoon, finding her guilty of witchcraft after hours of testimony. Unlike Rebecca Nurse, who benefited from a petition signed by 39 neighbors attesting to her good character, Martin arrived in Salem with no local support. Witnesses from Amesbury, her home town, recounted decades of suspicions – livestock dying after disputes, unexplained illnesses, and claims of spectral visitations. The court seemed to give particular weight to these long-standing accusations. As the magistrate’s record reads, one neighbor testified that years ago, Martin had a keg under her arm, which she claimed was a small dog. This seemingly minor detail, like so many others, contributed to the growing narrative of Martin’s supernatural power. Martin’s defiant behavior during her earlier examination likely did her no favors. The conviction solidifies the pattern: accusations fueled by old resentments, interpreted through the lens of spectral evidence, and resulting in swift and severe judgments.

Susannah Martin was convicted of witchcraft today after a trial focused on decades of accusations from her neighbors in Amesbury. She offered no defense witnesses, leaving her vulnerable to the court’s judgment, and faces sentencing alongside Rebecca Nurse, Sarah Good, Elizabeth Howe, and Sarah Wildes.

A petition signed by 39 of Rebecca Nurse’s neighbors was circulated today, affirming her good reputation and expressing disbelief in the accusations against her. Signers included Israel Porter, Daniel Andrew, and other prominent members of the community, risking their own reputations in her defense. However, Jonathan Putnam initially signed the petition but later removed his name due to family pressure.

Samuel and Mary Abbey testified that they evicted Sarah Good from their home due to her “turbulent” behavior. They claim their cattle subsequently sickened and died, and Good reportedly responded with callous indifference to their losses—evidence the court sees as malicious intent.

Henry Herrick and Jonathan Batchelor recounted an incident where Sarah Good, after being denied lodging, allegedly cursed Herrick’s father, predicting the death of his “best Cowes.” Shortly after, cattle did indeed die, a clear example of the maleficium evidence used in these trials.

Sarah Holton testified that Rebecca Nurse angrily confronted her over loose pigs years ago, and that her husband, Benjamin Holton, soon after fell ill with blindness and died. Holton attributes her husband's death to Nurse's anger, demonstrating how old disputes are now being reinterpreted as witchcraft.

Sarah Atkinson testified that Susannah Martin arrived at her home completely dry despite traveling on foot in terrible weather, suggesting supernatural protection and adding to the evidence against her.

Thomas Fisk Sr. served as the jury foreman during Rebecca Nurse’s trial, initially leading the jury to a “not guilty” verdict, which was then overturned by Chief Justice Stoughton after he sent them back to reconsider, highlighting the pressure placed on jurors.

Depositions from Joseph Knight and Elizabeth Clark recount a 1686 incident involving Susannah Martin and a keg, which Martin claimed was a small dog, further contributing to the growing body of evidence against her.

newspapers.lede: The Court of Oyer and Terminer concluded trials today with five women sentenced to hang: Elizabeth Howe, Sarah Wildes, Rebecca Nurse, Susannah Martin, and Sarah Good. The case of Rebecca Nurse is particularly disturbing. The jury first delivered a verdict of not guilty, a moment of hope in these dark days. But Chief Justice William Stoughton, seemingly dissatisfied, sent them back to deliberate further. He pointed to a remark Nurse made during questioning—‘she is one of us’—referring to another accused woman. As the magistrate’s record reads, the jury ultimately reversed course, finding Nurse guilty. This reversal has raised questions about the fairness of the proceedings, and the influence of the court on the jury's decision. The sheer volume of accusations and spectral evidence continues to drive these convictions, despite attempts at defense from family and neighbors.
newspapers.summary: Today brought a wave of convictions as Elizabeth Howe, Sarah Wildes, and Rebecca Nurse were all found guilty of witchcraft. The jury in Nurse’s case initially acquitted her, but were pressured by Chief Justice Stoughton to reconsider, resulting in a deeply troubling reversal. Testimony continues to mount against those accused, fueled by spectral evidence and old grievances.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today brought a wave of convictions as Elizabeth Howe, Sarah Wildes, and Rebecca Nurse were all found guilty of witchcraft. The jury in Nurse’s case initially acquitted her, but were pressured by Chief Justice Stoughton to reconsider, resulting in a deeply troubling reversal. Testimony continues to mount against those accused, fueled by spectral evidence and old grievances.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer concluded trials today with five women sentenced to hang: Elizabeth Howe, Sarah Wildes, Rebecca Nurse, Susannah Martin, and Sarah Good. The case of Rebecca Nurse is particularly disturbing. The jury first delivered a verdict of not guilty, a moment of hope in these dark days. But Chief Justice William Stoughton, seemingly dissatisfied, sent them back to deliberate further. He pointed to a remark Nurse made during questioning—‘she is one of us’—referring to another accused woman. As the magistrate’s record reads, the jury ultimately reversed course, finding Nurse guilty. This reversal has raised questions about the fairness of the proceedings, and the influence of the court on the jury's decision. The sheer volume of accusations and spectral evidence continues to drive these convictions, despite attempts at defense from family and neighbors.

Five women – Elizabeth Howe, Sarah Wildes, Rebecca Nurse, Susannah Martin, and Sarah Good – were convicted of witchcraft today and sentenced to death. This brings the total number of condemned to eleven, with Bridget Bishop already executed on June 10th.

The reversal of Rebecca Nurse’s initial acquittal is a significant and troubling development. The jury's first verdict was overturned after pressure from Chief Justice Stoughton, raising serious concerns about the impartiality of the court.

New testimony emerged today against Elizabeth Proctor. Elizabeth Booth, aged 18, testified that Proctor physically assaulted her, biting, pinching, and choking her, claiming she would prove Proctor was a witch. According to Booth's deposition, Proctor was accused of causing the deaths of several individuals, including a father-in-law and a neighbor.

Further accusations against Elizabeth Proctor came through the testimony of Mary Warren, who claimed to have seen the apparition of John Proctor and that he tormented her with violence. Warren testified that Proctor “hath often penching me and biting me and Choakeing me.”

Testimony from John and Joseph Andrew details a decades-old dispute with Sarah Wildes over a borrowed scythe in 1674, highlighting how long-held grudges are now being presented as evidence of witchcraft.

Multiple depositions connected Elizabeth How to misfortunes experienced by neighbors, including livestock deaths and illnesses, drawing on incidents from several years prior. As Jacob Foster testified, after disagreements with James How and his wife, “Evill a curents did falow som straing loses” among his cattle.

Sarah Bibber testified that Elizabeth How physically harmed her during How’s examination, claiming she was choked and thrown to the ground. Bibber also stated that How afflicted other accusers, including Mary Wolcott and Abigail Williams.

Joseph Safford testified that Elizabeth How expressed anger after his wife was denied admission to the church in Ipswich, suggesting a motive for witchcraft. Safford also recounted an incident where How’s daughter sought to borrow a horse, followed by the horse being mysteriously injured in the barn.

newspapers.lede: The web of accusations in Salem Village continues to expand, now reaching into the relationships between enslavers and the enslaved. Today, a formal complaint was filed against Margaret Hawkes of Salem Town, but the most startling development came from her own enslaved woman, Candy, also known as Kandy. Candy, originally from Barbados, has accused Hawkes of forcing her into witchcraft. As the magistrate’s record reads, from the deposition of Elizabeth Hubbard, afflicted girls claim Martha Carrier “did most greviously tortor…by biting pricking pinching and allmost Choaking me to death.” But Candy’s testimony is unique. She claims Hawkes “made her” a witch and forced her to sign the Devil’s book. This strategic confession, turning the accusation back on her owner, demonstrates a chilling understanding of the court's expectations. Deputies escorted Candy home, where she reportedly returned with rags and herbs acting as poppets—effigies used in witchcraft. The situation highlights the power dynamics at play and the desperation of those accused.
newspapers.summary: Today brings new accusations and deepening divisions in Salem Village. Enslaved woman Candy accuses her mistress, Margaret Hawkes, of witchcraft, a remarkable turn of events. Meanwhile, fear and suspicion continue to grip the community as more names are added to the growing list of the accused.

The situation highlights the power dynamics at play and the desperation of those accused.

The most significant development today is the accusation of Margaret Hawkes by her enslaved woman, Candy. Candy claims Hawkes forced her into witchcraft, a desperate attempt to save herself that reveals a remarkable understanding of the legal proceedings.

Several new individuals have been accused today, including Edward Farrington of Lynn, Hugh Crosia, and Job Tookey of Beverly, broadening the scope of the investigations beyond Salem Village itself.

The accusations against Martha Carrier have intensified, with multiple afflicted girls, including Elizabeth Hubbard and Mary Walcott, testifying to her tormenting them, as evidenced in their depositions taken today.

Ann Putnam Senior continues to suffer deeply, even as the executions proceed. Despite the death of Rebecca Nurse, whose specter allegedly afflicted her children, she finds no peace, and her health continues to decline.

Samuel Parris, the minister of Salem Village, is increasingly caught in a difficult position, committed to the trials but seemingly aware of the escalating stakes and the potential for future repercussions.

The summer heat and crowded conditions in the Salem jail are creating a dangerous environment for prisoners, exacerbating the spread of disease and suffering.

Despite the ongoing crisis, farmers must prioritize haying – the harvest of hay for livestock feed – to ensure survival through the coming winter. This vital task is complicated by the loss of family members to imprisonment.

Thirty-two men from Ipswich, led by Reverend John Wise, have signed a petition attesting to the good character of John Proctor, showcasing the growing network of support for those accused and the reach of information beyond Salem Village.

newspapers.lede: The focus of today’s examinations is Sarah Wildes of Topsfield, and the evidence presented paints a troubling picture of how personal animosity and old grievances are driving these accusations. Reverend John Hale of Beverly took the stand, recounting a complaint made fifteen to sixteen years ago by Mary Reddington, who claimed Wildes had bewitched her. Hale testified, stating, “I John Hale of Beverly aged 56 years beeing summoned to appeare & give evidence against Sarah Wiles of Topsfield July .2. 1692; Testify that about 15 or 16 yeares agoe came to my house the wife of John Hirrek of Beverly…that Goody wiles her neighb'r bewitched her & afflicted her many times greiviously.” This testimony, while dating back years, lends a weight of ministerial authority to what many see as neighborhood gossip. Adding to the complexity, Elizabeth Symonds testified that a spectral cat afflicted her, revealing a personal connection—her daughter had previously been engaged to Sarah Wildes’ son, Ephraim, before he ended the match. This pattern of broken relationships turning to accusations is becoming increasingly clear, raising questions about the validity of the claims.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the trials continue with the examination of Sarah Wildes. Testimony presented reveals a pattern of long-held grudges and personal disputes fueling accusations, alongside claims of spectral evidence. The proceedings highlight how easily past conflicts can become entangled with accusations of witchcraft, shaping the course of justice.

The focus of today’s examinations is Sarah Wildes of Topsfield, and the evidence presented paints a troubling picture of how personal animosity and old grievances are driving these accusations. Reverend John Hale of Beverly took the stand, recounting a complaint made fifteen to sixteen years ago by Mary Reddington, who claimed Wildes had bewitched her. Hale testified, stating, “I John Hale of Beverly aged 56 years beeing summoned to appeare & give evidence against Sarah Wiles of Topsfield July .2. 1692; Testify that about 15 or 16 yeares agoe came to my house the wife of John Hirrek of Beverly…that Goody wiles her neighb'r bewitched her & afflicted her many times greiviously.” This testimony, while dating back years, lends a weight of ministerial authority to what many see as neighborhood gossip. Adding to the complexity, Elizabeth Symonds testified that a spectral cat afflicted her, revealing a personal connection—her daughter had previously been engaged to Sarah Wildes’ son, Ephraim, before he ended the match. This pattern of broken relationships turning to accusations is becoming increasingly clear, raising questions about the validity of the claims.

As detailed in the courtroom today, the case against Sarah Wildes relies heavily on accusations stemming from old disputes and personal resentments. Reverend Hale’s recall of a fifteen-year-old complaint, combined with Elizabeth Symonds’ testimony about a spectral cat following a family conflict, demonstrates how past grievances are being framed as evidence of witchcraft.

Ephraim Wildes, Sarah’s son and constable of Topsfield, bravely defended his mother in court. He testified that the Hobbs family was accusing her in retaliation for his prior arrests of William and Deliverance Hobbs – a confessed witch who had then implicated Sarah Wildes.

Multiple witnesses presented evidence of spectral disturbances attributed to Wildes. Zacheus Perkins described an incident involving a hay cart, while Humphrey Clark claimed to have seen Wildes at his bedside.

Thomas Dorman testified about the deaths of his cattle and geese, linking them to a transaction he had with Wildes involving a scythe. John Andrews Jr. and Joseph Andrews similarly recounted a hay cart disaster they believe was caused by Wildes’ witchcraft.

The testimony also included accounts of Jonathan Wildes, Sarah’s son, exhibiting strange behavior roughly twenty years ago, prompting concern and prayer from ministers in Ipswich. This suggests a family history of perceived oddities that is now being reinterpreted through the lens of witchcraft.

Elizabeth Symonds’ testimony revealed a particularly personal angle to the accusations. The broken engagement between her daughter and Ephraim Wildes fueled her claims of spectral torment, highlighting the role of personal grievances in these proceedings.

newspapers.lede: The Salem Village Church today cast out Rebecca Nurse, formally removing her from the covenant and, in the eyes of many, condemning her soul. Reverend Nicholas Noyes presided over the proceedings, declaring Nurse severed from God’s grace. This followed a deeply troubling turn of events at her trial. The jury initially found her not guilty, but Chief Justice William Stoughton intervened, pressuring the foreman, Thomas Fiske, to reconsider after the afflicted girls erupted in protest. According to the record, Nurse had allegedly called Deliverance Hobbs “one of us,” and Stoughton suggested the jury had overlooked this crucial detail. When Fiske questioned the accused, she remained silent—likely due to her known hearing impairment—and the jury reversed its verdict, finding her guilty. Noyes later explained the reversal of Nurse’s excommunication twenty years hence, stating it should be done “that it be no longer a reproach to her memory or an occasion of grief to her children.” But today, that grief was very much present.
newspapers.summary: Today brought a devastating spiritual blow to Rebecca Nurse, a respected member of this community, as she was excommunicated from the church. This act, carried out despite petitions on her behalf, demonstrates the growing power of accusations and the swiftness of judgment during these trials, and foreshadows her upcoming execution.

Today brought a devastating spiritual blow to Rebecca Nurse, a respected member of this community, as she was excommunicated from the church. This act, carried out despite petitions on her behalf, demonstrates the growing power of accusations and the swiftness of judgment during these trials, and foreshadows her upcoming execution.

The Salem Village Church today cast out Rebecca Nurse, formally removing her from the covenant and, in the eyes of many, condemning her soul. Reverend Nicholas Noyes presided over the proceedings, declaring Nurse severed from God’s grace. This followed a deeply troubling turn of events at her trial. The jury initially found her not guilty, but Chief Justice William Stoughton intervened, pressuring the foreman, Thomas Fiske, to reconsider after the afflicted girls erupted in protest. According to the record, Nurse had allegedly called Deliverance Hobbs “one of us,” and Stoughton suggested the jury had overlooked this crucial detail. When Fiske questioned the accused, she remained silent—likely due to her known hearing impairment—and the jury reversed its verdict, finding her guilty. Noyes later explained the reversal of Nurse’s excommunication twenty years hence, stating it should be done “that it be no longer a reproach to her memory or an occasion of grief to her children.” But today, that grief was very much present.

Rebecca Nurse was formally excommunicated from the Salem Town church today, a condemnation that signifies spiritual damnation in Puritan belief. This action followed a controversial guilty verdict reached after the jury initially acquitted her, a verdict pressured by Chief Justice Stoughton and the outbursts of the afflicted girls.

The reversal of the jury’s initial not-guilty verdict hinges on testimony regarding a statement Nurse made about Deliverance Hobbs, which Stoughton highlighted as evidence of her involvement with witchcraft. The fact that Nurse did not respond to questioning—due to her partial deafness—was interpreted as defiance and contributed to the eventual guilty finding.

This excommunication is particularly striking given the earlier petition signed by thirty-nine neighbors attesting to Nurse’s piety and good character. To cast out someone so widely respected demonstrates the power of the accusations and the fear gripping the community.

Reverend Samuel Parris also performed an excommunication of Rebecca Nurse from the Salem Village church today, reinforcing the spiritual isolation imposed upon her. This action, like the one in Salem Town, will not be reversed for another twenty years.

It is worth noting that both Rebecca Nurse and Giles Corey were posthumously readmitted to the church in 1712, signaling a later recognition of the injustices committed during these trials, though offering little comfort to those who suffered.

newspapers.lede: The courtroom in Salem Town saw a stunning and disturbing development today with the conviction of Rebecca Nurse. The jury initially returned a verdict of not guilty, a significant moment of doubt in these often-swift condemnations. But Chief Justice William Stoughton, dissatisfied with the outcome, sent the jury back for further deliberation. The point of contention? A seemingly innocuous comment made by Nurse during the testimony of a confessed witch, Abigail Hobbs. As juror Thomas Fisk explained, when Hobbs was brought before the court, Nurse said, “what, do these persons give in Evidence against me now, they used to come against us.” Stoughton seized on the word “us,” interpreting it as an admission of shared guilt. Fisk’s declaration reveals the jury initially found no evidence of witchcraft, but ultimately reversed their decision based on this single phrase. Nurse herself later explained she meant “us prisoners,” referring to herself and Hobbs as fellow inmates, but her plea fell on deaf ears—literally, as she noted her partial deafness. This case underscores how easily words can be twisted and how little weight is given to reason in the face of fear.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the trials took a dark turn as Rebecca Nurse was convicted despite an initial not-guilty verdict. This reversal, secured by a forceful intervention from Chief Justice Stoughton, highlights the precariousness of justice and the power of interpretation in these proceedings. We also heard testimony regarding accusations made by an enslaved woman, Candy, and a defense offered by a husband for his accused wife.

This reversal, secured by a forceful intervention from Chief Justice Stoughton, highlights the precariousness of justice and the power of interpretation in these proceedings. We also heard testimony regarding accusations made by an enslaved woman, Candy, and a defense offered by a husband for his accused wife.

The jury initially returned a verdict of not guilty, a significant moment of doubt in these often-swift condemnations. But Chief Justice William Stoughton, dissatisfied with the outcome, sent the jury back for further deliberation. The point of contention? A seemingly innocuous comment made by Nurse during the testimony of a confessed witch, Abigail Hobbs. As juror Thomas Fisk explained, when Hobbs was brought before the court, Nurse said, “what, do these persons give in Evidence against me now, they used to come against us.” Stoughton seized on the word “us,” interpreting it as an admission of shared guilt. Fisk’s declaration reveals the jury initially found no evidence of witchcraft, but ultimately reversed their decision based on this single phrase. Nurse herself later explained she meant “us prisoners,” referring to herself and Hobbs as fellow inmates, but her plea fell on deaf ears—literally, as she noted her partial deafness. This case underscores how easily words can be twisted and how little weight is given to reason in the face of fear.

Rebecca Nurse was found guilty today after the jury reversed its initial not-guilty verdict. Chief Justice Stoughton aggressively intervened, focusing on Nurse’s statement about Abigail Hobbs being “of our company” and interpreting it as an admission of being a fellow witch, despite her later explanation that she meant fellow prisoners.

An enslaved woman named Candy, of Barbadian origin, underwent examination today and confessed to witchcraft, but strategically accused her mistress, Margaret Hawkes, of forcing her to sign the Devil’s book. Candy also produced poppets—small dolls—made of rags and herbs, but was ultimately found not guilty in January of 1693.

Rebecca Nurse formally appealed the guilty verdict, explaining that when she said Hobbs was “of our company,” she meant fellow prisoners, not fellow witches, and emphasizing her difficulty hearing. This appeal, a clear explanation of a misunderstanding, was disregarded by the court.

Thomas Fisk, foreman of the jury in the Nurse trial, provided a declaration detailing the jury’s initial vote for acquittal and the subsequent reversal after Chief Justice Stoughton’s intervention regarding Nurse’s comment about Hobbs.

John Wildes Sr., husband of the accused Sarah Wildes, testified in her defense, stating he warned Mary Reddington against spreading rumors. He suggested the accusations stemmed from resentment over his remarriage, but the court gave his testimony little credence.

The magistrate’s record reveals Nurse’s exact words, which prompted the reversal: “what, do these persons give in Evidence against me now, they used to come against us.” The court fixated on the “us,” while Nurse meant “those who opposed us” while imprisoned.

newspapers.lede: The pace of accusations and examinations quickened today, most notably in Andover. Ann Foster, a woman of approximately seventy-five years, was subjected to intense interrogation and, according to reports, confessed to involvement with the devil. She described a disturbing vision, stating, as the magistrate’s record reads, that she went “upon a pole…with Martha Carrier to a meeting.” This confession quickly spread suspicion, implicating her daughter, Mary Lacey Senior, and further connecting Martha Carrier to the alleged gatherings. The impact on Andover was immediate. Joseph Ballard, a local man, brought girls from Salem Village – believed to be Ann Putnam Junior and Mary Walcott – to Andover, using what became known as the ‘touch test’ to identify those tormenting his wife. Within weeks, accusations would engulf nearly fifty residents of the town. This demonstrates how the methods of accusation developed here in Salem Village are being deliberately applied elsewhere, extending the reach of this crisis.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village and Andover, the witch hunt escalated dramatically. An elderly woman, Ann Foster, confessed to witchcraft after relentless questioning, triggering a wave of accusations in Andover that would soon see dozens more named as witches. Meanwhile, one accused woman fled the colony entirely, seeking refuge in New York.

Today in Salem Village and Andover, the witch hunt escalated dramatically. An elderly woman, Ann Foster, confessed to witchcraft after relentless questioning, triggering a wave of accusations in Andover that would soon see dozens more named as witches. Meanwhile, one accused woman fled the colony entirely, seeking refuge in New York.

The pace of accusations and examinations quickened today, most notably in Andover. Ann Foster, a woman of approximately seventy-five years, was subjected to intense interrogation and, according to reports, confessed to involvement with the devil. She described a disturbing vision, stating, as the magistrate’s record reads, that she went “upon a pole…with Martha Carrier to a meeting.” This confession quickly spread suspicion, implicating her daughter, Mary Lacey Senior, and further connecting Martha Carrier to the alleged gatherings. The impact on Andover was immediate. Joseph Ballard, a local man, brought girls from Salem Village – believed to be Ann Putnam Junior and Mary Walcott – to Andover, using what became known as the ‘touch test’ to identify those tormenting his wife. Within weeks, accusations would engulf nearly fifty residents of the town. This demonstrates how the methods of accusation developed here in Salem Village are being deliberately applied elsewhere, extending the reach of this crisis.

The confession of Ann Foster in Andover sparked a dramatic surge in accusations within that community. Joseph Ballard brought afflicted girls from Salem Village to Andover to identify those causing his wife’s illness, leading to over forty people being accused in the following weeks.

Elizabeth Cary, accused nearly two months ago, escaped from Cambridge prison with the help of her husband, Captain Nathaniel Cary. They fled first to Rhode Island and then to New York, seeking to avoid the proceedings here in Massachusetts Bay.

William Proctor, son of John Proctor, was subjected to brutal coercion today in an attempt to extract a confession. He was bound “neck and heels,” bent double with rope, until he nearly bled, according to what his father would later document.

The costs associated with the ongoing trials are mounting. Nathaniel Ingersoll submitted an expense account detailing provisions for magistrates, ministers, and even horses – totaling over eight pounds, though he was reimbursed only six. This suggests a strain on resources as the trials continue.

The social technology of accusation, as we’ve seen it develop here in Salem Village, is being actively exported. The events in Andover demonstrate a deliberate effort to replicate the methods of identifying witches beyond our own community.

Records show that during the examination of Goodwife Nurse, the marshal and his constables were provided with victuals, lodging, and drink – a clear indication of the length and intensity of these proceedings.

newspapers.lede: The examinations continued today with Ann Foster, and her testimony is proving particularly disturbing. Foster confessed to entering into a pact with the Devil approximately six years ago, allegedly coerced by Goody Carrier. According to the magistrate’s record, Foster stated that Carrier “came to her in person…and told her if she would not be a witch the divill should tare her in peices and Cary her away,” at which time she promised to serve the Devil. Foster also described repeated apparitions of the Devil, specifically “in the shape of a bird at several Times, such a bird as she never saw the like before.” She claims this bird, appearing white then vanishing black, gave her the power “of striking the afflicted downe w’th her eye ever.” This claim connects her directly to the afflictions plaguing the young women, and further implicates Goody Carrier as a central figure in the unfolding crisis. The pressure to confess, and name others, is clearly immense.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Ann Foster confessed to witchcraft, claiming Goody Carrier initiated her six years ago and that the Devil appeared to her as a bird promising prosperity in exchange for service. This confession, extracted during examination, adds to the growing list of accusations and complicates the already fraught situation.

This confession, extracted during examination, adds to the growing list of accusations and complicates the already fraught situation.

Foster confessed to entering into a pact with the Devil approximately six years ago, allegedly coerced by Goody Carrier. According to the magistrate’s record, Foster stated that Carrier “came to her in person…and told her if she would not be a witch the divill should tare her in peices and Cary her away,” at which time she promised to serve the Devil. Foster also described repeated apparitions of the Devil, specifically “in the shape of a bird at several Times, such a bird as she never saw the like before.” She claims this bird, appearing white then vanishing black, gave her the power “of striking the afflicted downe w’th her eye ever.” This claim connects her directly to the afflictions plaguing the young women, and further implicates Goody Carrier as a central figure in the unfolding crisis. The pressure to confess, and name others, is clearly immense.

Ann Foster’s confession, detailing a six-year involvement with witchcraft initiated by Goody Carrier and the Devil’s appearances as a bird, is the most significant development today. Her testimony directly links Carrier to the alleged witchcraft and provides a supernatural explanation for the afflictions suffered by the accusers.

The examinations are intensifying, and the focus remains on identifying the root of the perceived evil afflicting the village. Magistrates are pressing accused individuals for names and details, seemingly prioritizing confessions above all else.

Goody Carrier, already suspected, is now explicitly named as the one who initiated Ann Foster into witchcraft, escalating the accusations against her and suggesting a network of witches is at work.

Foster’s claim about possessing the power to harm others with her gaze is a new element in the accusations, extending the scope of alleged witchcraft beyond spectral evidence and physical ailments.

The examinations are taking place in a highly charged atmosphere, fueled by fear and the belief that Salem Village is under attack by Satanic forces.

The continued examinations and confessions demonstrate the power of suggestion and the immense pressure being placed on the accused to admit to involvement in witchcraft, regardless of their guilt or innocence.

newspapers.lede: The weight of the court’s judgment fell heavily on Salem today as five accused witches were executed at Proctor’s Ledge. Among them was Rebecca Nurse, a seventy-one-year-old woman whose reputation for piety and good character had initially led the jury to acquit her. However, Chief Justice William Stoughton intervened, sending them back for further deliberation, and the verdict was reversed. As Reverend Samuel Parris recorded, Nurse maintained her innocence, stating simply, “I am innocent. I am a gospel woman. God will clear my innocency.” The scene was one of grim finality. Parris observed Noyes urging the condemned to confess, and then turning away from Sarah Good when she defiantly proclaimed, “You are a liar! I am no more a witch than you are a wizard, and if you take away my life, God will give you blood to drink!” This chilling prophecy, spoken with her last breath, has already begun to circulate through the community. The executions occurred during the crucial haying season, disrupting the lives of villagers and underscoring the profound impact of the trials on everyday life.
newspapers.summary: Today, July 19th, 1692, five people – Rebecca Nurse, Sarah Good, Elizabeth How, Susannah Martin, and Sarah Wildes – were hanged at Proctor’s Ledge in Salem Town. The execution of Rebecca Nurse, a respected elderly woman, is particularly shocking, and highlights the escalating crisis gripping the colony. This day marks a turning point in the trials, demonstrating the court's willingness to carry out capital punishment.

The execution of Rebecca Nurse, a respected elderly woman, is particularly shocking, and highlights the escalating crisis gripping the colony. This day marks a turning point in the trials, demonstrating the court's willingness to carry out capital punishment.

Five women – Rebecca Nurse, Sarah Good, Sarah Wildes, Elizabeth How, and Susannah Martin – were hanged at Proctor’s Ledge today. This is the largest single-day execution thus far, and signifies an escalation in the severity of the trials despite mounting concerns from some in the community.

The initial acquittal of Rebecca Nurse was overturned at the insistence of William Stoughton, raising questions about the fairness of the proceedings and the influence of the court on the jury. The fact that a woman of her standing was condemned after an initial finding of not guilty has sparked considerable distress.

Sarah Good’s defiant final words – her curse directed at Reverend Nicholas Noyes – were widely witnessed and are being recounted throughout Salem. Her prophecy of Noyes drinking blood is a particularly unsettling detail that many villagers believe will come to pass.

The executions occurred during the peak of haying season, a critical time for farmers, demonstrating the disruption these trials are causing to the ordinary rhythms of life in Salem Village.

Several of the executed women, including Rebecca Nurse, Elizabeth Howe, and Sarah Wildes, had connections to the town of Topsfield, suggesting a potential link between the trials and long-standing border disputes between the communities.

The practice of counter-magic, using items like horseshoes, salt, and herbs for protection, reveals a blending of Puritan beliefs with older folk traditions, indicating a deeper level of anxiety about witchcraft within the community.

Sheriff George Corwin profited from the seizures of property from those accused, raising ethical concerns about the administration of justice and the potential for financial gain to influence the outcome of the trials.

The fact that families were responsible for feeding prisoners held in the Salem jail highlights the limited resources available and the harsh conditions faced by the accused, particularly those who lacked family support like Sarah Good.

newspapers.lede: The crisis deepens as accusations now fall upon entire family lines. Today, both Mary Lacey Jr. and her mother, Mary Lacey Sr. of Andover, were brought before the authorities. Joseph Ballard and the afflicted girls named Mary Lacey Jr., leading to her arrest. As the magistrate’s record reads, a warrant was issued “to apprehend and forthwith bring before us, Mary Lacey daughter of Lawrence Lacy… who Stands Charged… with haveing Committed Sundry acts of Witchcraft.” But it was the confession of Mary Lacey Sr. that sent shockwaves through the village. She admitted to flying on a pole with her mother—Ann Foster, already accused—and Martha Carrier to a gathering of witches. This corroboration of her mother’s earlier accusations is especially damaging. The weight of these accusations comes at a terrible human cost, vividly illustrated by the fate of the Howe family. Yesterday’s execution of Elizabeth Howe has left her blind husband, James Howe Jr., and their six children without a caretaker. James, unable to manage the farm alone, is left with no support, and his elderly father, at the age of 94, is too frail to assist.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village and Andover, the accusations broaden to include multiple generations of one family. Mary Lacey Jr. and her mother, Mary Lacey Sr., were both accused of witchcraft, with Mary Sr. confessing to flying on a pole with other accused women. Meanwhile, the community grapples with the aftermath of yesterday’s execution, leaving a family devastated.

James, unable to manage the farm alone, is left with no support, and his elderly father, at the age of 94, is too frail to assist.

As detailed in the lede, Mary Lacey Jr. and Mary Lacey Sr. of Andover were accused of witchcraft today, adding to the growing number of individuals implicated in the supposed practices. Mary Lacey Sr.’s confession, confirming her mother’s alleged involvement and detailing spectral flights, is a significant development for the prosecution.

The arrest of Mary Lacey Jr. followed standard procedure: a warrant was issued, and a constable searched her home for “popetts &c,” or poppets—small dolls used in sympathetic magic—and other evidence, as detailed in the officer’s return.

The execution of Elizabeth Howe yesterday continues to reverberate through the community, highlighting the far-reaching consequences of these trials beyond the accused. The Howe family’s plight underscores the devastating impact on those left behind.

Ann Foster, grandmother to Mary Lacey Jr. and mother to Mary Lacey Sr., remains accused, making this the first instance of three generations of one family facing charges of witchcraft simultaneously.

The fact that the afflicted girls and Joseph Ballard were the primary accusers in the Lacey cases suggests a pattern of accusations originating from the same sources, raising questions about the reliability of the evidence.

Though the Superior Court will not convene for another two months, the Court of Oyer and Terminer is already meeting and taking cases, meaning a trial and potential conviction for Mary Lacey Sr. could happen swiftly.

newspapers.lede: This Monday, the magistrates focused their attention on Andover, issuing warrants for the arrest of Richard Carrier and his son, Andrew. Constable John Ballard quickly apprehended the two men and brought them to Salem for examination. During questioning, Mary Warren, who has been repeatedly afflicted, reportedly experienced violent fits. Mary Lacey Junior was also examined today, and quickly confessed to witchcraft. As the magistrate’s record reads, when questioned about afflicting Goody Ballard, Lacey claimed, “I canot tell; where is my mother that made me a witch and I knew it not.” The examinations reveal a pattern of pressured confessions, with accusers claiming spectral evidence and the accused often naming others to save themselves. The swiftness of these proceedings and the acceptance of spectral evidence continue to fuel the escalating hysteria.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, warrants were issued for the arrest of Richard Carrier and his son Andrew, based on the accusations of Mary Warren. Their examinations began immediately, adding to the growing number of people implicated in witchcraft and furthering the crisis gripping the community.

Today in Salem Village, warrants were issued for the arrest of Richard Carrier and his son Andrew, based on the accusations of Mary Warren. Their examinations began immediately, adding to the growing number of people implicated in witchcraft and furthering the crisis gripping the community.

This Monday, the magistrates focused their attention on Andover, issuing warrants for the arrest of Richard Carrier and his son, Andrew. Constable John Ballard quickly apprehended the two men and brought them to Salem for examination. During questioning, Mary Warren, who has been repeatedly afflicted, reportedly experienced violent fits. Mary Lacey Junior was also examined today, and quickly confessed to witchcraft. As the magistrate’s record reads, when questioned about afflicting Goody Ballard, Lacey claimed, “I canot tell; where is my mother that made me a witch and I knew it not.” The examinations reveal a pattern of pressured confessions, with accusers claiming spectral evidence and the accused often naming others to save themselves. The swiftness of these proceedings and the acceptance of spectral evidence continue to fuel the escalating hysteria.

Richard Carrier and his son, Andrew, were arrested today in Andover following accusations from Mary Warren. The warrant authorized officers to search their home for “paper or popetts” – items believed to be used in witchcraft – highlighting the belief in material components of magical practice.

Mary Lacey Junior confessed to being a witch during her examination, claiming her mother initiated her into witchcraft. She described the Devil appearing to her in the shape of a horse, a common motif in the accusations and testimonies.

The magistrates – Bartholomew Gedney, John Hathorne, Jonathan Corwin, and John Higginson – are presiding over these examinations, accepting accusations and confessions as evidence.

Mary Warren continues to be a central figure in the accusations, experiencing fits during the examinations of those accused, seemingly confirming their guilt in the eyes of the court.

Ann Foster, Mary Lacey Senior, and Richard and Andrew Carrier were all examined today, indicating a broadening scope of accusations beyond the initial group of accusers and the previously accused.

The examinations rely heavily on spectral evidence – testimony about visions and apparitions – which remains controversial but is currently accepted by the court as valid proof of witchcraft.

newspapers.lede: The examinations held today at the meeting house painted a grim picture of the supposed workings of witchcraft. Young Richard Carrier, just eighteen years old, and his brother Andrew were questioned extensively about their involvement with the Devil. According to the court record, Richard confessed to signing the Devil’s book approximately a month ago, claiming his mother and brother were present. He detailed a pact where he was promised a house and land in Andover in exchange for five years of service. He even implicated others, saying, “Mrs Bradbery would have to afflict Timo. Swan… becaus her husband & Timo Swan fell out about a scyth.” The court pressed him on how he harmed Timothy Swan, and he described using an iron spindle to injure Swan’s knee, seemingly in spirit form. These confessions, obtained through intense questioning and the testimony of the afflicted girls, are fueling the fear and hysteria gripping the community.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, accusations continue to mount. Martha Emerson of Andover joins the growing list of the accused, while the examinations of the Carrier family reveal disturbing confessions of spectral evidence and demonic pacts. Support for Mary Bradbury is also voiced by many prominent citizens.

Today in Salem Village, accusations continue to mount. Martha Emerson of Andover joins the growing list of the accused, while the examinations of the Carrier family reveal disturbing confessions of spectral evidence and demonic pacts. Support for Mary Bradbury is also voiced by many prominent citizens.

The examinations held today at the meeting house painted a grim picture of the supposed workings of witchcraft. Young Richard Carrier, just eighteen years old, and his brother Andrew were questioned extensively about their involvement with the Devil. According to the court record, Richard confessed to signing the Devil’s book approximately a month ago, claiming his mother and brother were present. He detailed a pact where he was promised a house and land in Andover in exchange for five years of service. He even implicated others, saying, “Mrs Bradbery would have to afflict Timo. Swan… becaus her husband & Timo Swan fell out about a scyth.” The court pressed him on how he harmed Timothy Swan, and he described using an iron spindle to injure Swan’s knee, seemingly in spirit form. These confessions, obtained through intense questioning and the testimony of the afflicted girls, are fueling the fear and hysteria gripping the community.

As we’ve heard, Richard Carrier’s confession is the most significant development today. He detailed a demonic pact and implicated his brother, Andrew, and Mrs. Bradbury in acts of affliction, claiming they met in a green pasture and used spectral means to harm others.

Martha Emerson of Andover was accused today by Mary Warren and Mary Lacey Jr. She is the daughter of Roger Toothaker, who died in jail last month, and whose family has been deeply affected by the trials. It's noted that her father once boasted she had “killed a witch” using folk remedies, a detail that may complicate her defense.

A petition circulated today in support of Mary Bradbury, signed by over thirty individuals, attests to her pious character and peaceful demeanor. The document states she was “a lover of the ministrie” and “of a curteous, & peaceable disposition,” and that those signing have known her for over fifty years without witnessing any conflict with her neighbors.

Andrew Carrier also confessed to signing the Devil’s book, claiming he was promised land in Andover and instructed to afflict certain individuals, including a child of the Frye family. He stammered and stuttered during questioning, something observers noted was unusual for him.

Testimony emerged today referencing a meeting near Ingersals where several accused individuals, including Goodwife How, Goodwife Nurse, and Reverend Procter and his wife, were allegedly present. Richard Carrier also mentioned a man named Burroughs, described as a “little man,” who had been preaching eastward.

Mary Lacey Jr. testified that the Carrier brothers admitted to acknowledging their involvement in witchcraft, and that the Devil appeared as a black man when Andrew signed his name in the book with red ink.

newspapers.lede: Here we stand near the site of Salem Jail, where John Proctor is currently imprisoned. Today, July 23rd, Proctor managed to send a desperate letter to five respected ministers in Boston: Increase Mather, James Allen, Joshua Moody, Samuel Willard, and John Bailey. He begs them to intervene in the trials, fearing injustice and what he describes as outright torture. Proctor details how his son, William, and others have been physically abused to force confessions. As the magistrate's record reads, Proctor wrote of William being “tyed neck and heels till the Blood was ready to come out of his nose.” He calls these methods “very like the Popish Cruelties,” a severe accusation meant to shock his fellow Puritans. Proctor asks for the trials to be moved to Boston, believing he might find a fair hearing there, or for the current judges to be replaced. He fears not only for his life, but for the complete ruin of his family, stating plainly they’ve “already undone us in our estates.” The letter is a powerful indictment of the proceedings, yet there’s no immediate sign that the ministers will heed his plea.
newspapers.summary: Today brings news of escalating desperation within Salem Jail. John Proctor, accused and imprisoned, has penned a letter to prominent Boston ministers detailing the brutal treatment of prisoners and pleading for a fairer trial—a plea that appears to be going unanswered. This highlights the growing concerns about the methods used to secure confessions.

He begs them to intervene in the trials, fearing injustice and what he describes as outright torture. As the magistrate's record reads, Proctor wrote of William being “tyed neck and heels till the Blood was ready to come out of his nose.” He calls these methods “very like the Popish Cruelties,” a severe accusation meant to shock his fellow Puritans. Proctor asks for the trials to be moved to Boston, believing he might find a fair hearing there, or for the current judges to be replaced. He fears not only for his life, but for the complete ruin of his family, stating plainly they’ve “already undone us in our estates.” The letter is a powerful indictment of the proceedings, yet there’s no immediate sign that the ministers will heed his plea.

John Proctor’s letter to the Boston ministers is the most significant development of the day. He details the torture of prisoners, including his son William, who was bound “neck and heels” until he bled, in an attempt to force a confession. Proctor pleads for a change of venue or judges, believing a fairer trial is impossible here.

The letter explicitly connects the accusations and trials to economic ruin. Proctor writes that the accusers have “already undone us in our estates,” highlighting the financial devastation being inflicted on the accused families through court fees and property seizures.

Proctor draws a stark comparison between the methods being used in Salem and the infamous tortures of the Catholic Inquisition, calling them “very like the Popish Cruelties.” This was a powerful rhetorical move, designed to appeal to the strong anti-Catholic sentiments of the time.

News arrived today that on August first, eight Boston ministers will meet to discuss Proctor’s letter. However, given the speed of these proceedings, it is unlikely any intervention will come before his trial concludes.

Carrier’s sons were also subjected to the same brutal treatment described by Proctor—tied neck and heels until bleeding—in an effort to extract false confessions, demonstrating a pattern of coercion.

The letter reveals a growing desperation among the accused. Proctor appeals to the ministers not only for legal intervention but also for what he terms “Favorable Assistance,” suggesting he believes they hold the power to sway public opinion.

While the ministers have received Proctor’s plea, there is no indication they will act on it immediately, leaving him and the others imprisoned here facing an uncertain fate.

newspapers.lede: The pattern of accusations continues to broaden today with the naming of Mary DeRich of Salem Village. She is another sister-in-law of Elizabeth Proctor, bringing the total number of Proctors and their connections accused to seven people. This suggests investigators are deliberately focusing on the network surrounding the Proctor family, seeking to uncover a wider conspiracy. Simultaneously, the wheels of justice are turning as preparations for the next court session accelerate. A legal summons, dated today, demands the appearance of James Greenslit to testify against Reverend George Burroughs. The document, issued in the name of King William and Queen Mary, reads, “Wee comand you all Excuses set apart to be & personaly app’r at the Next Court of Oyer & Terminer…There to Testify the Truth on certain Indictments to be Exhibited against George Burroughs.” This illustrates the seriousness with which the authorities are treating the accusations and their determination to proceed with trials.
newspapers.summary: Today brings another accusation in Salem Village, expanding the reach of the trials to yet another family connected to the Proctors. Meanwhile, legal preparations continue for the upcoming court session, with summonses issued for witnesses in the case against Reverend George Burroughs. These developments underscore the widening scope and intensifying legal activity of the witchcraft crisis.
