Mary DeRich was accused today, adding another name to the growing list of the accused and further solidifying the focus on the Proctor family and their connections. Seven individuals linked to the Proctors have now faced accusations, highlighting a concentrated effort to investigate this particular social network.

The court is actively preparing for the next session, issuing summonses for witnesses like James Greenslit to testify against Reverend Burroughs. This indicates the legal proceedings are moving forward despite the ongoing examinations and confessions.

The summons for Greenslit is formally issued in the names of King William and Queen Mary, emphasizing the authority of the royal government behind these legal actions. The document explicitly threatens a hefty fine for failure to appear.

While Mary DeRich has been accused, there is no indication she has been formally indicted or is currently in jail; she appears to have been released after questioning. This highlights inconsistencies in the treatment of the accused.

The accusations continue to spread beyond Salem Town, with Mary DeRich residing in Salem Village, demonstrating that the crisis is affecting all parts of the Massachusetts Bay colony.

The ongoing legal activity suggests the authorities believe they have sufficient evidence to pursue convictions, despite the often-tenuous nature of the accusations and the reliance on spectral evidence.

newspapers.lede: The Court of Oyer and Terminer saw a change in leadership today as Attorney General Anthony Checkley assumed the role of prosecutor, replacing Thomas Newton. Checkley, approximately fifty years of age and from Boston, brings with him a legal background and a father-in-law who served as a minister. The transition occurs as the court moves deeper into the summer and prepares to hear more cases. As the magistrate’s record shows, Newton’s involvement had already been extensive, but Checkley now bears the responsibility for guiding these prosecutions. The implications of this change are unclear, but it suggests the court intends to continue, and perhaps intensify, its pursuit of those accused of witchcraft. It is a somber day in Salem Village, as anxieties continue to rise with each new accusation and examination. One resident, speaking privately, expressed fear that “God doth send us a sore trial, and we know not how to appease His wrath.”
newspapers.summary: Today marks a shift in the legal proceedings of the Salem Witch Trials. Thomas Newton has been replaced as prosecutor by Anthony Checkley, the Attorney General from Boston. This change comes as the court prepares for further examinations and trials, signaling an escalation of the legal response to the accusations.

Today marks a shift in the legal proceedings of the Salem Witch Trials. Thomas Newton has been replaced as prosecutor by Anthony Checkley, the Attorney General from Boston. This change comes as the court prepares for further examinations and trials, signaling an escalation of the legal response to the accusations.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer saw a change in leadership today as Attorney General Anthony Checkley assumed the role of prosecutor, replacing Thomas Newton. Checkley, approximately fifty years of age and from Boston, brings with him a legal background and a father-in-law who served as a minister. The transition occurs as the court moves deeper into the summer and prepares to hear more cases. As the magistrate’s record shows, Newton’s involvement had already been extensive, but Checkley now bears the responsibility for guiding these prosecutions. The implications of this change are unclear, but it suggests the court intends to continue, and perhaps intensify, its pursuit of those accused of witchcraft. It is a somber day in Salem Village, as anxieties continue to rise with each new accusation and examination. One resident, speaking privately, expressed fear that “God doth send us a sore trial, and we know not how to appease His wrath.”

Attorney General Anthony Checkley officially took over as prosecutor for the Court of Oyer and Terminer today, succeeding Thomas Newton. This transition comes at a critical juncture, as more examinations and trials are expected in the coming weeks, and may indicate a hardening of the court’s stance against the accused.

The shift in prosecution leadership follows the recent hanging of Bridget Bishop on June tenth, the first execution in the unfolding crisis. This earlier event set a precedent and has increased the fear and urgency surrounding the trials.

While the specific reasons for Newton’s replacement have not been publicly stated, it is understood that Checkley’s appointment reflects a desire for a more experienced legal advocate to guide the court's proceedings.

Accusations continue to circulate throughout Salem Village and neighboring communities, fueling the atmosphere of suspicion and fear. The focus remains on identifying individuals believed to be practicing witchcraft and harming others.

Local ministers continue to play a central role in identifying and condemning those accused, often relying on spectral evidence and the testimonies of afflicted individuals. This evidence, though controversial, is currently accepted by the court.

Residents are increasingly divided, with some supporting the trials and others expressing skepticism and concern about the fairness of the proceedings. These divisions are creating further tension within the community.

newspapers.lede: The crisis deepens as accusations now extend to neighboring towns. This Monday, authorities in Salem issued a warrant for the arrest of Mary Bridges Sr. of Andover, fueled by the confessions of others already in custody. Constable John Ballard of Andover has already brought her to Salem for examination. The warrant, as the magistrate’s record reads, charges her with witchcraft “Lately on the Body of Timothy Swan of Andover and others to theire great hurt.” This expansion beyond Salem Village suggests the afflicted girls’ accusations are gaining wider acceptance, and the investigations are broadening their scope. Meanwhile, here in Salem, Thomas Bradbury has offered a powerful testimony in defense of his wife, Mary Bradbury, stating they have been married fifty-five years. He describes her as “a loveing & faithfull wife,” industrious and charitable, hoping her long life will speak for itself. In his words, “her life and conversation hath been such amongst her neighbours, as gives a better & more reall Testimoney of her, then can bee exprest by words.”
newspapers.summary: Today brings more accusations and arrests as the reach of the witchcraft accusations expands beyond Salem Village to Andover and Haverhill. A warrant was issued for Mary Bridges of Andover, and warrants for Mary Green and Hannah Bromage of Haverhill, adding to the growing number of those imprisoned and facing examination.

Today brings more accusations and arrests as the reach of the witchcraft accusations expands beyond Salem Village to Andover and Haverhill. A warrant was issued for Mary Bridges of Andover, and warrants for Mary Green and Hannah Bromage of Haverhill, adding to the growing number of those imprisoned and facing examination.

The crisis deepens as accusations now extend to neighboring towns. This Monday, authorities in Salem issued a warrant for the arrest of Mary Bridges Sr. of Andover, fueled by the confessions of others already in custody. Constable John Ballard of Andover has already brought her to Salem for examination. The warrant, as the magistrate’s record reads, charges her with witchcraft “Lately on the Body of Timothy Swan of Andover and others to theire great hurt.” This expansion beyond Salem Village suggests the afflicted girls’ accusations are gaining wider acceptance, and the investigations are broadening their scope. Meanwhile, here in Salem, Thomas Bradbury has offered a powerful testimony in defense of his wife, Mary Bradbury, stating they have been married fifty-five years. He describes her as “a loveing & faithfull wife,” industrious and charitable, hoping her long life will speak for itself. In his words, “her life and conversation hath been such amongst her neighbours, as gives a better & more reall Testimoney of her, then can bee exprest by words.”

As detailed in the lede, Mary Bridges Sr. of Andover has been arrested on charges of witchcraft and transported to Salem for examination, broadening the scope of the investigations beyond Salem Village and indicating that accusations are spreading rapidly through the region.

Warrants were also issued today for Mary Green and Hannah Bromage of Haverhill, both accused of afflicting Timothy Swan, Mary Walcot, and Ann Putnam, further extending the reach of the accusations into new communities.

Thomas Bradbury testified today in defense of his wife, Mary Bradbury, describing her as a devoted and hardworking woman after fifty-five years of marriage, hoping her character will outweigh the accusations against her.

The growing number of arrests and examinations is straining the resources of the magistrates and the jail, raising concerns about the ability to fairly and efficiently process all the cases.

The accusations against Mary Bridges, Mary Green, and Hannah Bromage follow a pattern of linking accused individuals to the afflictions of others, particularly Timothy Swan, a frequent complainant in these cases.

The speed with which warrants are being issued suggests a sense of urgency and a belief among the authorities that swift action is necessary to address the perceived threat of witchcraft.

newspapers.lede: The meeting house was filled today as Mary Toothaker, a widow of Salem Village, underwent a lengthy and intense examination. Magistrates Major Gidney, Reverend Hauthorn, Reverend Corwin, and Captain Higginson questioned her for hours, attempting to discern the source of the afflictions plaguing several young women. The magistrates noted that Toothaker seemed to cause physical harm to the afflicted simply by looking at them. When pressed to confess her involvement with the Devil, Toothaker offered a troubled account, claiming she had been “under great discontentedness & troubled w'th feare about the Indians,” and plagued by disturbing dreams. Most strikingly, she confessed to questioning the value of her own baptism, saying she “had wished She had not ben bapt'ed because She had not Improved it as She ought to have done.” According to the record, she admitted to attempting to pray, but feeling as though even that act was somehow hindering her spiritual well-being, and fearing the Devil had tempted her *not* to pray. She described a sensation of being unable to breathe, even during the examination.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the examination of Mary Toothaker took place before several magistrates. Her testimony is remarkable for its internal conflict and suggestion that even religious practice might be a source of temptation, adding another layer of complexity to the accusations and fears gripping the community.

Today in Salem Village, the examination of Mary Toothaker took place before several magistrates. Her testimony is remarkable for its internal conflict and suggestion that even religious practice might be a source of temptation, adding another layer of complexity to the accusations and fears gripping the community.

The meeting house was filled today as Mary Toothaker, a widow of Salem Village, underwent a lengthy and intense examination. Magistrates Major Gidney, Reverend Hauthorn, Reverend Corwin, and Captain Higginson questioned her for hours, attempting to discern the source of the afflictions plaguing several young women. The magistrates noted that Toothaker seemed to cause physical harm to the afflicted simply by looking at them. When pressed to confess her involvement with the Devil, Toothaker offered a troubled account, claiming she had been “under great discontentedness & troubled w'th feare about the Indians,” and plagued by disturbing dreams. Most strikingly, she confessed to questioning the value of her own baptism, saying she “had wished She had not ben bapt'ed because She had not Improved it as She ought to have done.” According to the record, she admitted to attempting to pray, but feeling as though even that act was somehow hindering her spiritual well-being, and fearing the Devil had tempted her *not* to pray. She described a sensation of being unable to breathe, even during the examination.

As detailed in the magistrate’s record, the examination of Mary Toothaker dominated the day, revealing a complex and unsettling testimony. Toothaker’s account differs from many others, focusing on internal struggles and a questioning of her own religious life, rather than direct pacts with the Devil.

The afflicted girls continued to exhibit their characteristic fits and accusations during the examination, claiming Toothaker’s gaze caused them physical distress. This pattern of behavior is increasingly central to the process of identifying accused witches.

Fear of Native American attacks remains a consistent undercurrent in many testimonies, including Toothaker’s, suggesting a link between the witchcraft accusations and broader anxieties about the frontier.

The magistrates are increasingly focused on uncovering the specific ways in which accused individuals may have yielded to Satan’s temptations, probing deeply into personal histories and religious practices.

The case of Mary Toothaker, like others before it, highlights the difficulty of obtaining consistent and straightforward confessions, even under intense questioning. Her responses are often hesitant and self-contradictory.

Despite the ongoing examinations and trials, the number of those imprisoned continues to grow, straining the resources of the court and the community.

newspapers.lede: The grip of fear and accusation tightens on Salem Village and beyond. Today, word spread of Philip and Mary English’s daring escape. Facing certain conviction and the loss of everything they owned, the couple fled with the aid of sympathetic friends, hiding amongst laundry in a cart to pass undetected. They’ve found sanctuary with Governor Benjamin Fletcher in New York, a place where Massachusetts warrants hold no power. But their escape came at a terrible cost. Sheriff Corwin immediately seized their property – fourteen buildings, twenty-one vessels, warehouses brimming with goods, and the finest home in Salem. As one witness described the scene, the English family left behind a fortune, while Corwin wasted no time in claiming it for himself. Meanwhile, inside the jail, John Proctor has put his thoughts to paper, writing to the ministers in Boston. He begs them to intervene, describing the torment used to extract confessions. He writes, “I would have you know that these that are in prison are not only afflicted with bodily torments, but also with such terrors of spirit, as none can imagine.” Proctor, a man who always believed he could solve his own problems, now confesses he is utterly powerless against this tide of hysteria.
newspapers.summary: Today brings news of escalating desperation and injustice. Philip and Mary English have fled Salem, seeking refuge in New York after Sheriff Corwin began seizing their vast estate. John Proctor, imprisoned and awaiting trial, has penned a desperate plea to Boston ministers. The trials continue, and the cycle of accusation and seizure intensifies.

John Proctor, imprisoned and awaiting trial, has penned a desperate plea to Boston ministers. The trials continue, and the cycle of accusation and seizure intensifies.

Facing certain conviction and the loss of everything they owned, the couple fled with the aid of sympathetic friends, hiding amongst laundry in a cart to pass undetected. They’ve found sanctuary with Governor Benjamin Fletcher in New York, a place where Massachusetts warrants hold no power. But their escape came at a terrible cost. Sheriff Corwin immediately seized their property – fourteen buildings, twenty-one vessels, warehouses brimming with goods, and the finest home in Salem. As one witness described the scene, the English family left behind a fortune, while Corwin wasted no time in claiming it for himself. Meanwhile, inside the jail, John Proctor has put his thoughts to paper, writing to the ministers in Boston. He begs them to intervene, describing the torment used to extract confessions. He writes, “I would have you know that these that are in prison are not only afflicted with bodily torments, but also with such terrors of spirit, as none can imagine.” Proctor, a man who always believed he could solve his own problems, now confesses he is utterly powerless against this tide of hysteria.

Philip and Mary English escaped Salem today, fleeing to New York with the help of friends and ministerial support. They sought refuge from the escalating trials and the certain seizure of their extensive property, which Sheriff Corwin immediately began to confiscate.

John Proctor, imprisoned and facing trial, has sent a desperate letter to the ministers in Boston, pleading for their intervention and describing the coercive tactics used to obtain confessions.

Sheriff Corwin continued his ruthless seizure of property, not only from the English family but also from the farm of John and Elizabeth Proctor, taking food, livestock, valuables, and even the iron pot used for cooking – leaving the Proctor children destitute.

The witch hunt has spread beyond Salem Village to Andover, where the use of the “touch test” is generating a new wave of accusations and confessions, demonstrating the crisis’s capacity to consume entire communities.

Captain John Alden Jr., a Boston merchant accused in May, escaped from jail with the assistance of allies, highlighting the growing dissent and skepticism toward the proceedings.

There are reports that Martha Carrier’s sons were tortured into testifying against their mother, a grim example of the lengths to which accusers are going to secure convictions.

August marks the peak of harvest season, a time of crucial labor and anticipated abundance, tragically juxtaposed with the ongoing executions and economic devastation sweeping through the region.

The hanging of George Burroughs today on the gallows – and his perfect recitation of the Lord’s Prayer – has shaken some in the crowd, prompting Cotton Mather to reaffirm the justice of the proceedings from horseback.

newspapers.lede: The accusations continue to spread beyond Salem Village itself. Today, Mary Post of Rowley was taken into custody on a warrant issued by Justice Dudley Bradstreet of Andover. The complaint against her comes from Timothy Swan, Ann Putnam Jr., and Mary Walcott, all familiar accusers. As the magistrate’s record reads, the warrant accuses Post of afflicting the complainants “by witchcraft.” This expands the reach of the investigation and demonstrates how accusations can travel with individuals between towns. Meanwhile, testimony against Reverend George Burroughs is gaining traction. Samuel Webber testified today that Burroughs once demonstrated extraordinary strength, claiming he “put his fingers into the Bung of a Barrell of Malasses and lifted it up, and carryed it Round him and sett it downe againe.” This claim of supernatural power, common in these trials, is being presented as evidence of Burroughs’s pact with the Devil. The court is clearly looking for proof beyond mere spectral evidence.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Mary Post of Rowley was arrested on charges of witchcraft, joining a growing number of accused. Testimony continues to focus on displays of unnatural strength, with a new claim against Reverend George Burroughs. Long-held grudges and disputes are also surfacing as evidence in these cases, and Sheriff Corwin has seized a substantial estate.

Testimony continues to focus on displays of unnatural strength, with a new claim against Reverend George Burroughs. Long-held grudges and disputes are also surfacing as evidence in these cases, and Sheriff Corwin has seized a substantial estate.

The accusations continue to spread beyond Salem Village itself. Today, Mary Post of Rowley was taken into custody on a warrant issued by Justice Dudley Bradstreet of Andover. The complaint against her comes from Timothy Swan, Ann Putnam Jr., and Mary Walcott, all familiar accusers. As the magistrate’s record reads, the warrant accuses Post of afflicting the complainants “by witchcraft.” This expands the reach of the investigation and demonstrates how accusations can travel with individuals between towns. Meanwhile, testimony against Reverend George Burroughs is gaining traction. Samuel Webber testified today that Burroughs once demonstrated extraordinary strength, claiming he “put his fingers into the Bung of a Barrell of Malasses and lifted it up, and carryed it Round him and sett it downe againe.” This claim of supernatural power, common in these trials, is being presented as evidence of Burroughs’s pact with the Devil. The court is clearly looking for proof beyond mere spectral evidence.

As detailed in the lede, Mary Post was arrested today in Rowley based on the accusations of Timothy Swan, Ann Putnam Jr., and Mary Walcott, highlighting the expanding geographical scope of the witchcraft accusations and the continued prominence of these three individuals as accusers.

Samuel Webber’s testimony regarding Reverend Burroughs’s alleged feat of strength is particularly significant. Webber’s account paints Burroughs as possessing unnatural power, a key element in determining guilt in these proceedings, and has been seized upon by the court as damning evidence.

Another deposition today, from John Rogers, reveals a seven-year-old dispute with Martha Carrier. Rogers claims to have lost pigs, one of which was found with its ears deliberately cut off. He attributes this to Carrier’s witchcraft, demonstrating how pre-existing animosities are being woven into the fabric of the accusations.

Rogers provided a disturbing detail in his testimony, stating he “had two large lusty Sowes w'ch frequented home daily that were lost & this deponent: found one of them dead Nigh the s'd Carriers house w'th both Eares cut of.” While the ear-cutting suggests a possible act of malice rather than supernatural intervention, it’s being presented as evidence of Carrier’s harmful intent.

Sheriff George Corwin undertook a massive seizure of property belonging to Philip and Mary English, who have fled to New York. The inventory lists assets totaling £1,183—an extraordinary sum for the time—including furniture, wine, grain, livestock, and merchandise.

The thoroughness of the seizure, extending even to household linens, demonstrates the severity of the legal consequences for those accused and the financial motivation for confiscating property. The English’s case illustrates the devastating impact the trials have on families and their livelihoods.

Mary Webber also testified today, recalling stories from Burroughs’s wife about strange occurrences in their home, including noises and a spectral figure resembling a white calf. These accounts, though less concrete than the claims of physical strength, contribute to the growing sense of dread and supernatural activity surrounding Burroughs.

newspapers.lede: The court heard damning testimony today against Reverend George Burroughs, the former minister of Salem Village. Witnesses described extraordinary displays of strength, claiming Burroughs could hold a seven-foot gun at arm’s length with a single finger – a feat no one else could replicate. Mercy Lewis, a young woman orphaned by the conflict in Maine, testified that Burroughs’s spirit transported her to a mountaintop where he revealed “all the kingdoms of the earth,” echoing the biblical temptation of Christ. According to Lewis, Burroughs then declared “the divell was his sarvant” and offered her worldly power in exchange for her allegiance. Ann Putnam Jr. went further, claiming Burroughs confessed to bewitching and murdering two of his previous wives, describing visions of their ghostly accusations. This convergence of testimony paints Burroughs not merely as a witch, but as a figure explicitly aligned with Satan and steeped in malice. The evidence presented today is powerfully suggestive, and the court appears increasingly convinced of his guilt.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a surge of new accusations, expanding the reach of the trials beyond Salem Town and Village. Testimony against Reverend George Burroughs intensifies, linking him to supernatural feats and even the Devil himself. While some accused recant their statements, the cycle of fear and accusation continues unabated.

Today brings a surge of new accusations, expanding the reach of the trials beyond Salem Town and Village. Testimony against Reverend George Burroughs intensifies, linking him to supernatural feats and even the Devil himself. While some accused recant their statements, the cycle of fear and accusation continues unabated.

The court heard damning testimony today against Reverend George Burroughs, the former minister of Salem Village. Witnesses described extraordinary displays of strength, claiming Burroughs could hold a seven-foot gun at arm’s length with a single finger – a feat no one else could replicate. Mercy Lewis, a young woman orphaned by the conflict in Maine, testified that Burroughs’s spirit transported her to a mountaintop where he revealed “all the kingdoms of the earth,” echoing the biblical temptation of Christ. According to Lewis, Burroughs then declared “the divell was his sarvant” and offered her worldly power in exchange for her allegiance. Ann Putnam Jr. went further, claiming Burroughs confessed to bewitching and murdering two of his previous wives, describing visions of their ghostly accusations. This convergence of testimony paints Burroughs not merely as a witch, but as a figure explicitly aligned with Satan and steeped in malice. The evidence presented today is powerfully suggestive, and the court appears increasingly convinced of his guilt.

As we heard in the lede, the accusations against George Burroughs reached a fever pitch today. Witnesses described not just supernatural power, but a direct connection to the Devil, with claims of spectral journeys and explicit offers of demonic pacts. This escalating rhetoric is intended to establish Burroughs as a leader of the alleged witchcraft conspiracy.

The geographic reach of the accusations is broadening. Mary Clarke of Haverhill was accused today, demonstrating that the panic is no longer confined to Salem Village and its immediate surroundings. This expansion likely reflects existing tensions and disputes within neighboring communities being channeled into accusations of witchcraft.

A significant development occurred with Margaret Jacobs, who recanted her earlier confession, stating that her testimony against her grandfather, George Jacobs Sr., and Reverend Burroughs was false and coerced. She pleaded for forgiveness, acknowledging she’d spoken “under great fear of death.” It’s a stark reminder that these confessions are often obtained through immense pressure.

A land dispute between the Carrier family and the Abbotts has now been twisted into evidence of witchcraft. Benjamin Abbott testified that Martha Carrier threatened him, saying she would "stick as Closs to [him] as the bark stooke to the Tree." He then developed unexplained sores, which he attributes to Carrier’s malice.

Testimony emerged today concerning a covenant of secrecy signed by Burroughs’s wife in 1680. John and Rebecca Putnam claimed Burroughs demanded his wife pledge never to reveal his private conversations, suggesting he had something to hide. This attempt to cast suspicion on his personal life further fuels the narrative of a man concealing dark secrets.

More accusations against George Burroughs detail visions of his dead wives. Ann Putnam Jr. testified that the spirits of Burroughs's first two wives appeared to her and accused him of murder, claiming he stabbed one under the arm and concealed the wound with sealing wax.

Additional testimony from Sarah Bibber and Mary Walcott corroborated accounts of Burroughs tormenting other afflicted individuals. They claim to have witnessed his spectral form pinching, choking, and otherwise afflicting the girls during examinations.

A warrant was issued for the arrest of Mary Clarke of Haverhill, based on the complaint of Robert and John Swan. This signals a continuation of the investigations beyond Salem and a willingness to pursue accusations wherever they may lead.

newspapers.lede: The court continues to hear testimony linking accused witch John Willard to various afflictions, moving beyond spectral evidence to claims of direct physical harm. Bray Wilkins, Willard’s grandfather-in-law, testified today that Willard’s intense stare caused a sudden and painful blockage, preventing him from urinating while in Boston. Wilkins described the experience vividly, stating, “my water was sodainly stopt, & I had no benefit of nature, but was like a man on a Rack.” This claim, linking a gaze to a physical ailment, reinforces the community’s belief in the power of “the evil eye” and the ability of witches to inflict harm through unseen means. Simultaneously, Philip Knight and Thomas Nichols testified that Willard openly called for the hanging of the accused, saying, “hang them. they ar all witches.” The prosecution is framing this as Willard attempting to deflect suspicion, but it’s a dangerous statement in these times.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, accusations continue to mount against John Willard, with testimony linking his gaze to physical ailments and his words to a desire for conviction. Further accusations against Martha Corey and George Jacobs Sr. are also being presented, adding to the growing number of those imprisoned and facing trial.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, accusations continue to mount against John Willard, with testimony linking his gaze to physical ailments and his words to a desire for conviction. Further accusations against Martha Corey and George Jacobs Sr. are also being presented, adding to the growing number of those imprisoned and facing trial.

The court continues to hear testimony linking accused witch John Willard to various afflictions, moving beyond spectral evidence to claims of direct physical harm. Bray Wilkins, Willard’s grandfather-in-law, testified today that Willard’s intense stare caused a sudden and painful blockage, preventing him from urinating while in Boston. Wilkins described the experience vividly, stating, “my water was sodainly stopt, & I had no benefit of nature, but was like a man on a Rack.” This claim, linking a gaze to a physical ailment, reinforces the community’s belief in the power of “the evil eye” and the ability of witches to inflict harm through unseen means. Simultaneously, Philip Knight and Thomas Nichols testified that Willard openly called for the hanging of the accused, saying, “hang them. they ar all witches.” The prosecution is framing this as Willard attempting to deflect suspicion, but it’s a dangerous statement in these times.

As we just heard, John Willard faces increasingly serious accusations. Bray Wilkins’ deposition details a physical ailment he believes was directly caused by Willard’s intense stare, linking the accused to a form of sympathetic magic and bolstering the idea that witchcraft can manifest in physical illnesses.

The prosecution is attempting to portray Willard as insincere in his condemnation of witchcraft. Testimony from Knight and Nichols suggests Willard openly advocated for the execution of the accused, potentially to distract attention from himself.

Martha Corey remains a central figure in the accusations. Elizabeth Hubbard testified today, affirming that Corey’s apparition afflicted her, as well as Mary Walcott, Ann Putnam, and Mercy Lewis. Hubbard described being tormented and almost choked, and stated she "verily believe[s] s'd Jacobs is a wizard."

George Jacobs Sr. is also facing a barrage of accusations. Mary Warren, Elizabeth Hubbard, Mary Walcott, and Sarah Bibber all testified to seeing Jacobs or his apparition tormenting the afflicted girls, and even physically harming them with a staff.

A search of George Jacobs Sr’s person revealed a strange object under his shoulder - described as a “tett” with a sharp point. Though no blood or corruption was found, its presence is being presented as further evidence of his involvement in witchcraft.

Summonses have been issued for witnesses to testify against George Jacobs Sr., including Joseph Flintt, John Waters Sr., and Captain Putnam, indicating the court intends to pursue this case vigorously.

Mary Daniel testified today regarding apparitions and afflictions, claiming she was visited by a woman who beat and pinched her, urging her not to reveal what she knew. The deposition details vivid experiences of spectral assault.

George Burroughs received a summons today, demanding the attendance of John Pierce and John Lane to testify in his case. This suggests the court is actively preparing for further trials and examinations.

newspapers.lede: The courtroom is crowded today for the trial of George Burroughs, the former minister of Salem Village. Burroughs stands accused of witchcraft, and the proceedings have drawn the attention of Reverend Cotton Mather, a prominent figure in the ongoing crisis. As the magistrate’s record shows, a summons was issued this morning for witnesses, including members of the Ruck family, to testify against him. The prosecution will likely focus on Burroughs’s strict household management and perceived secrecy. Hannah Harris testified that Burroughs “scolded his wife for speaking to others,” painting him as a controlling and isolated figure. Meanwhile, the reach of the accusations continues to expand beyond Salem Village and Andover. In the town of Rowley, several new names have emerged, including John Howard, John Jackson Senior and Junior, and most tragically, Margaret Scott, a 77-year-old widow. The accusations against Scott, in particular, seem rooted in long-held resentments within the Rowley community.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem, the trial of former minister George Burroughs began, attracting the attention of influential Cotton Mather. Beyond Salem Village, accusations are spreading, with several residents of Rowley now accused, including an elderly widow who relied on community charity. The trials are increasingly fueled by local resentments as much as spectral evidence.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem, the trial of former minister George Burroughs began, attracting the attention of influential Cotton Mather. Beyond Salem Village, accusations are spreading, with several residents of Rowley now accused, including an elderly widow who relied on community charity. The trials are increasingly fueled by local resentments as much as spectral evidence.

The courtroom is crowded today for the trial of George Burroughs, the former minister of Salem Village. Burroughs stands accused of witchcraft, and the proceedings have drawn the attention of Reverend Cotton Mather, a prominent figure in the ongoing crisis. As the magistrate’s record shows, a summons was issued this morning for witnesses, including members of the Ruck family, to testify against him. The prosecution will likely focus on Burroughs’s strict household management and perceived secrecy. Hannah Harris testified that Burroughs “scolded his wife for speaking to others,” painting him as a controlling and isolated figure. Meanwhile, the reach of the accusations continues to expand beyond Salem Village and Andover. In the town of Rowley, several new names have emerged, including John Howard, John Jackson Senior and Junior, and most tragically, Margaret Scott, a 77-year-old widow. The accusations against Scott, in particular, seem rooted in long-held resentments within the Rowley community.

The trial of George Burroughs is underway, with Cotton Mather in attendance. The prosecution is building a case based on Burroughs’s perceived controlling behavior and secretive nature, as revealed in testimony about his treatment of his wife and neighbors.

Accusations are now extending beyond Salem Village and Andover, reaching the town of Rowley. John Howard, John Jackson Sr., and John Jackson Jr. were all accused today, though none have yet been brought to trial.

Margaret Scott, a 77-year-old impoverished widow from Rowley, is among those accused. Her case highlights the vulnerability of the marginalized and how existing social tensions are being exploited by the accusations.

Samuel Wardwell of Andover, a farmer known for practicing fortune telling, has also been accused. His case demonstrates the blurring lines between traditional folk beliefs and the charge of diabolical witchcraft.

Disturbing evidence continues to emerge that calls into question the validity of the accusers’ testimony. According to depositions from Samuel Barton and John Houghton, Mercy Lewis admitted she may have been “out of her head” when naming Elizabeth Proctor, suggesting her spectral evidence was unreliable.

Daniel Elliott testified that he overheard an afflicted person admit the accusations were “for sport,” a shocking revelation suggesting deliberate fraud among the accusers. William Rayment Jr. added that an afflicted girl seemed to be “making a Jest of it” when questioned by Goodwife Ingersoll.

Elizabeth Proctor was convicted today, but her execution has been stayed because she is pregnant. This reprieve, based on English common law, will likely save her life, though it does not erase her conviction or the legal consequences she faces.

John Wise, a minister from Ipswich, led 28 neighbors in signing a petition defending John and Elizabeth Proctor, stating, “we never had the Least Knowledge of such Nefarious wickedness in either of them.” This demonstrates growing opposition to the trials outside of the immediate Salem Village factions.

newspapers.lede: The meetinghouse was noticeably emptier this morning as Reverend Samuel Parris preached a sermon titled “God’s Justice Upon the Covenant Breakers.” Parris, clearly aware of the growing skepticism, forcefully defended the Court of Oyer and Terminer and the recent executions as acts of God’s justice. He warned those who question the proceedings, stating, “And the man that will do presumptuously, and will not hearken unto the priest or the judge, even that man shall die.” The sermon, delivered in the shadow of five more scheduled executions – including former minister George Burroughs – feels less like a triumph and more like a desperate attempt to shore up a crumbling conviction. Families like the Nurses and the Proctors are absent, either imprisoned or refusing to attend while Parris preaches. As the magistrate’s record reads, the hanging of Rebecca Nurse “cracked the community’s moral certainty.” Even Goodwife Ingersoll whispers that Sarah Good’s gallows curse – “I am no more a witch than you are a wizard” – may hold some truth.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon defending the court and the recent executions, even as attendance at the meetinghouse dwindles and doubt spreads among the community. The prosecutions continue, but the theological foundation supporting them appears increasingly fragile, with some questioning the proceedings.

Today in Salem Village, Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon defending the court and the recent executions, even as attendance at the meetinghouse dwindles and doubt spreads among the community. The prosecutions continue, but the theological foundation supporting them appears increasingly fragile, with some questioning the proceedings.

The meetinghouse was noticeably emptier this morning as Reverend Samuel Parris preached a sermon titled “God’s Justice Upon the Covenant Breakers.” Parris, clearly aware of the growing skepticism, forcefully defended the Court of Oyer and Terminer and the recent executions as acts of God’s justice. He warned those who question the proceedings, stating, “And the man that will do presumptuously, and will not hearken unto the priest or the judge, even that man shall die.” The sermon, delivered in the shadow of five more scheduled executions – including former minister George Burroughs – feels less like a triumph and more like a desperate attempt to shore up a crumbling conviction. Families like the Nurses and the Proctors are absent, either imprisoned or refusing to attend while Parris preaches. As the magistrate’s record reads, the hanging of Rebecca Nurse “cracked the community’s moral certainty.” Even Goodwife Ingersoll whispers that Sarah Good’s gallows curse – “I am no more a witch than you are a wizard” – may hold some truth.

Reverend Parris delivered a forceful sermon defending the Court of Oyer and Terminer and the ongoing executions, attempting to reaffirm the theological basis for the prosecutions as attendance at the meetinghouse continues to decline. The sermon underscores the fragility of the current situation, as Parris feels the need to actively maintain conviction.

The upcoming execution of George Burroughs, a former minister and Parris’s predecessor, is particularly unsettling. If a minister can be accused of witchcraft, it suggests the Devil’s influence has reached the highest levels of spiritual authority within the community.

Several prominent families, including the Nurses and the Proctors, are now absent from services, either due to imprisonment or protest, highlighting the growing division within Salem Village. Francis Nurse, whose wife Rebecca was recently executed, will not return to the meetinghouse while Parris preaches.

More than one hundred people are currently imprisoned, awaiting trial. The Court has scheduled another mass execution for August 19th, with Burroughs, Proctor, Jacobs, Willard, and Carrier all set to hang.

Concerns about the validity of the confessions and the reliability of spectral evidence are spreading, with some questioning whether the court is truly discerning guilt from innocence. Parris dismisses these concerns, asserting the confessions are consistent and the afflicted girls are instruments of God’s revelation.

Ann Putnam Senior appears physically and emotionally exhausted, while her daughter Ann Putnam Junior seems changed by her role in the accusations and trials. The weight of the proceedings is clearly taking a toll on those most involved.

Giles Corey attended the sermon, maintaining his stoic silence despite his wife Martha’s imprisonment, indicating a quiet resistance to the proceedings. He is aware that he may be next.

The fact that Parris must actively preach to maintain conviction – rather than the conviction sustaining itself – reveals the brittleness of the theological framework supporting the trials and suggests a growing undercurrent of doubt within Salem Village.

newspapers.lede: The court heard testimony today concerning Phillip English, a merchant of Salem Town, as part of the ongoing investigation into witchcraft. William Beale, a man of Marblehead and aged over sixty, recounted a vision he experienced back in March. Beale described seeing a dark shape, seemingly a man, near the chimney in his sickroom. He was suffering from smallpox at the time, and his son James was also ill. According to Beale’s deposition, “I beeing broade Awake I then saw up on the south jaame of that Chimny A darke shade… & alsoe in the middlee of the darkness… I beeheld somethinge of the forme or shape of A man.” While Beale does not directly accuse English of causing the apparition, the testimony is being presented as evidence of spectral activity linked to the accused. The court is attempting to connect these earlier visions to the current wave of accusations, strengthening the case against those already imprisoned.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the accusations continue to widen, now including merchant Phillip English. Testimony was given regarding a spectral figure seen months ago, adding another layer to the already fraught claims of afflicted individuals and fueling the escalating crisis. This highlights how past events are being reinterpreted through the lens of spectral evidence.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, the accusations continue to widen, now including merchant Phillip English. Testimony was given regarding a spectral figure seen months ago, adding another layer to the already fraught claims of afflicted individuals and fueling the escalating crisis. This highlights how past events are being reinterpreted through the lens of spectral evidence.

The court heard testimony today concerning Phillip English, a merchant of Salem Town, as part of the ongoing investigation into witchcraft. William Beale, a man of Marblehead and aged over sixty, recounted a vision he experienced back in March. Beale described seeing a dark shape, seemingly a man, near the chimney in his sickroom. He was suffering from smallpox at the time, and his son James was also ill. According to Beale’s deposition, “I beeing broade Awake I then saw up on the south jaame of that Chimny A darke shade… & alsoe in the middlee of the darkness… I beeheld somethinge of the forme or shape of A man.” While Beale does not directly accuse English of causing the apparition, the testimony is being presented as evidence of spectral activity linked to the accused. The court is attempting to connect these earlier visions to the current wave of accusations, strengthening the case against those already imprisoned.

The testimony of William Beale regarding a spectral figure seen in March was presented today as evidence against Phillip English. Beale described a dark shape resembling a man appearing near his chimney while he lay sick with smallpox, and this is now being linked to the current investigations.

The examinations continue at a relentless pace, with magistrates pressing accused individuals for names of others involved in witchcraft. The pressure is mounting, and confessions, though often obtained through coercion, are still considered valuable evidence.

Concern is growing among some residents about the speed and severity of the proceedings. While many believe the court is doing God’s work, others worry about the possibility of condemning innocent people based on spectral evidence.

Several more individuals were examined today, though no new arrests were immediately announced. The names of those questioned remain largely undisclosed, adding to the atmosphere of secrecy and fear.

The jail in Salem Town is increasingly overcrowded, with limited resources available for the care of prisoners. Conditions are reportedly poor, and disease is a constant threat.

Despite the growing number of accusations, the afflicted girls continue to exhibit their characteristic fits and convulsions, reinforcing the belief among many that witchcraft is indeed at work in the community.

newspapers.lede: The trials continue under a dark cloud, but today a different sort of shadow falls – the shadow of doubt, expressed in writing by a fellow magistrate. Major Robert Pike of Salisbury sent a letter to Judge Jonathan Corwin, directly challenging the acceptance of spectral evidence. Pike argues that the Devil, a master of deceit, could easily assume the form of anyone, rendering testimony based on visions utterly unreliable. He writes, and this is a direct quote from his letter, “If spectral evidence be admitted, no person is safe.” This is a significant development, as it represents a public questioning of the legal basis for the convictions being handed down in Salem. While the court has so far shown little inclination to alter its course, this letter adds to a growing chorus of concern about the methods used to identify and condemn the accused. It’s a rare instance of an official outside of Salem Village daring to speak out against the proceedings, and its impact remains to be seen.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a rare voice of reason to the escalating crisis. Major Robert Pike, a magistrate from Salisbury, has formally questioned the use of spectral evidence in the witchcraft trials, challenging the very foundation upon which many convictions rest. This dissent, though unlikely to halt the proceedings, signals growing unease with the court’s reliance on visions and apparitions.

Pike argues that the Devil, a master of deceit, could easily assume the form of anyone, rendering testimony based on visions utterly unreliable. He writes, and this is a direct quote from his letter, “If spectral evidence be admitted, no person is safe.” This is a significant development, as it represents a public questioning of the legal basis for the convictions being handed down in Salem. While the court has so far shown little inclination to alter its course, this letter adds to a growing chorus of concern about the methods used to identify and condemn the accused. It’s a rare instance of an official outside of Salem Village daring to speak out against the proceedings, and its impact remains to be seen.

Major Robert Pike of Salisbury sent a letter to Judge Jonathan Corwin today, questioning the validity of spectral evidence. He believes the Devil could impersonate anyone, making visions unreliable and potentially condemning innocent people, as he wrote, “If spectral evidence be admitted, no person is safe.” This letter is a formal challenge to the court’s methods from a respected magistrate.

The court remains in session, and examinations and interrogations continue, despite the growing criticism of its procedures. No new arrests were publicly announced today, but the atmosphere in Salem Village remains tense and fearful.

Concerns about the reliability of accusers are beginning to circulate more widely, though these criticisms are largely confined to private conversations and are rarely expressed publicly.

Several ministers continue to preach sermons emphasizing the reality of witchcraft and the need for vigilance against Satan’s influence, reinforcing the prevailing climate of fear.

The families of those already convicted remain in distress, attempting to salvage what little property they can before it is seized by the court.

Travel to and from Salem Village is increasing as people come to witness the trials, and lodging houses are quickly filling up, further straining the resources of the community.

newspapers.lede: The weight of false accusation and coerced testimony grows heavier this Sunday in Salem Village. Elizabeth Johnson Jr. of Andover has been accused of witchcraft, joining the ever-increasing number facing judgment by the Court of Oyer and Terminer. Meanwhile, fifteen-year-old Margaret Jacobs, imprisoned for her testimony against her own grandfather, George Jacobs Sr., and Reverend George Burroughs, has penned a desperate letter attempting to undo the damage. She writes, “They told me if I did not confess I should be put down into the dungeon and would be hanged…” This reveals the brutal tactics used to extract confessions, even from the young and vulnerable. Compounding the tragedy, Sarah Churchill, a servant formerly employed by Jacobs, confessed to Sarah Ingersoll at the tavern that her earlier testimony was entirely fabricated. It's a disturbing inversion of justice, where falsehoods are rewarded and truth is punished, as the court seems intent on finding guilt rather than establishing facts.
newspapers.summary: Today brings more accusations and forced confessions, as the trials continue to spiral. Elizabeth Johnson Jr. is accused, Margaret Jacobs attempts to retract her testimony against her grandfather, and another servant, Sarah Churchill, admits her earlier claims were lies—highlighting the immense pressure placed on the accused and the fragility of the evidence.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today brings more accusations and forced confessions, as the trials continue to spiral. Elizabeth Johnson Jr. is accused, Margaret Jacobs attempts to retract her testimony against her grandfather, and another servant, Sarah Churchill, admits her earlier claims were lies—highlighting the immense pressure placed on the accused and the fragility of the evidence.

The weight of false accusation and coerced testimony grows heavier this Sunday in Salem Village. Elizabeth Johnson Jr. of Andover has been accused of witchcraft, joining the ever-increasing number facing judgment by the Court of Oyer and Terminer. Meanwhile, fifteen-year-old Margaret Jacobs, imprisoned for her testimony against her own grandfather, George Jacobs Sr., and Reverend George Burroughs, has penned a desperate letter attempting to undo the damage. She writes, “They told me if I did not confess I should be put down into the dungeon and would be hanged…” This reveals the brutal tactics used to extract confessions, even from the young and vulnerable. Compounding the tragedy, Sarah Churchill, a servant formerly employed by Jacobs, confessed to Sarah Ingersoll at the tavern that her earlier testimony was entirely fabricated. It's a disturbing inversion of justice, where falsehoods are rewarded and truth is punished, as the court seems intent on finding guilt rather than establishing facts.

Elizabeth Johnson Jr. was accused of witchcraft today, adding another name to the growing list of the condemned. She later confessed to being one of “about six score” witches, a number that continues to fuel the hysteria gripping the region, though she wouldn’t be formally exonerated for over three centuries – a full 329 years, until 2022.

Margaret Jacobs’ recantation is a damning indictment of the process. She admits she confessed only under threat of imprisonment and execution, sacrificing her grandfather’s life to save her own. Her letter is a stark reminder that many confessions were born not of guilt, but of fear.

The confession of Sarah Churchill, a servant who previously testified against her master George Jacobs Sr., reveals the complete unreliability of the evidence being presented. Her admission that her testimony was false underscores the atmosphere of fear and coercion that permeated the trials.

Also examined today was Margaret Scott, an elderly and impoverished woman. Witnesses describe her as defiant in the face of accusations, but she lacks the social standing or family connections to offer any protection against the court’s relentless pursuit of convictions.

The pattern of forced confessions and subsequent recantations is becoming increasingly clear. The court seems less interested in discovering the truth and more interested in securing convictions, no matter the cost to individual lives or the integrity of the legal process.

newspapers.lede: The examinations held today paint a grim picture of the accusations spreading through the community. In Andover, Abigail Faulkner Sr. now stands accused, and what makes this case particularly troubling is the testimony against her coming from her own daughters, Dorothy and Abigail Jr. It appears these young girls were compelled to condemn their mother, adding a new layer of heartbreak to the unfolding tragedy. As the magistrate’s record reads from the questioning of Sarah Carrier, a young girl claims she was made a witch at the age of six, stating, “Ever since I was six years old.” Carrier further alleges her mother made her touch a book, a common element in these confessions. Meanwhile, Elizabeth Johnson Jr.’s examination reveals a network of alleged witches including Goody Carrier and Goody Toothaker, with claims of attending meetings and participating in afflictions. These examinations, while yielding confessions, are increasingly reliant on the testimony of the afflicted and the accused themselves, creating a cycle of accusation and fear.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village and Andover, accusations continue to mount, and disturbing details emerge about the coercion of children to condemn their own family members. Examinations of Sarah Carrier and Elizabeth Johnson Jr. reveal claims of early recruitment into witchcraft and connections to other accused individuals. These testimonies, though obtained under duress, are driving the escalating crisis.

Today in Salem Village and Andover, accusations continue to mount, and disturbing details emerge about the coercion of children to condemn their own family members. Examinations of Sarah Carrier and Elizabeth Johnson Jr. reveal claims of early recruitment into witchcraft and connections to other accused individuals. These testimonies, though obtained under duress, are driving the escalating crisis.

The examinations held today paint a grim picture of the accusations spreading through the community. In Andover, Abigail Faulkner Sr. now stands accused, and what makes this case particularly troubling is the testimony against her coming from her own daughters, Dorothy and Abigail Jr. It appears these young girls were compelled to condemn their mother, adding a new layer of heartbreak to the unfolding tragedy. As the magistrate’s record reads from the questioning of Sarah Carrier, a young girl claims she was made a witch at the age of six, stating, “Ever since I was six years old.” Carrier further alleges her mother made her touch a book, a common element in these confessions. Meanwhile, Elizabeth Johnson Jr.’s examination reveals a network of alleged witches including Goody Carrier and Goody Toothaker, with claims of attending meetings and participating in afflictions. These examinations, while yielding confessions, are increasingly reliant on the testimony of the afflicted and the accused themselves, creating a cycle of accusation and fear.

Abigail Faulkner Sr. of Andover was accused of witchcraft today, a particularly disturbing development as her daughters, Dorothy and Abigail Jr., were reportedly coerced into condemning her. This highlights the immense pressure being placed on individuals, even within families, to confess or accuse others.

The examination of Sarah Carrier reveals claims of being involved in witchcraft since childhood, with her mother allegedly initiating her into it. Carrier stated her mother made her “set [her] hand to a book,” a recurring motif in the examinations.

Elizabeth Johnson Jr.'s testimony connects several individuals, including Goody Carrier and Goody Toothaker, to supposed gatherings and afflictions. She claims they were present at a “mock sacrament” and involved in harming others.

Both Carrier and Johnson Jr. describe a form of baptism by the Devil, with Johnson Jr. recalling being dipped in water in Goody Carrier’s well. This imagery reflects the deep-seated religious anxieties driving the accusations.

The examinations reveal promises made to those who allegedly entered into witchcraft, including safety, financial gain, and even a black dog, though these promises were rarely fulfilled, according to the testimonies.

Lawrence Lacey, Sarah Phelps, Mary Walcutt, Ann Putnam, and a child of Ephrahm Davis are named as individuals afflicted by Elizabeth Johnson Jr. and Goody Carrier, demonstrating the wide range of alleged victims.

Daniel Ems of Boxford is implicated in the examinations, accused of being a witch and contributing to the afflictions of Sarah Phelps, suggesting the reach of these accusations extends beyond Salem Village and Andover.

newspapers.lede: A remarkable turn of events unfolded this week with the escape of Philip and Mary English from Boston jail. Salem’s wealthiest merchant, Philip English, had been accused of witchcraft back in April, and both he and his wife were awaiting trial. With the help of Reverend Joshua Moody of Boston, the Englishes made their way to New York, where they have reportedly found sanctuary under the protection of Governor Fletcher. Moody, it seems, provided both practical assistance and a theological justification for their flight, preaching a sermon based on the verse, “If they persecute you in one city, flee to another.” As the magistrate’s record shows, the Englishes were among those named by several afflicted girls, but their flight has cast a shadow over the proceedings. Some whisper that Governor Phips may have tacitly allowed their escape, recognizing the growing unrest and the potential for further disruption. It is a bold move, and one that will undoubtedly be discussed for days to come.
newspapers.summary: Salem Village faces a shifting landscape today as prominent merchant Philip English and his wife Mary have fled to New York, escaping imprisonment on charges of witchcraft. This daring escape, possibly aided by local officials and clergy, raises questions about the limits of Governor Phips’ authority and the growing desperation of the accused.

Salem Village faces a shifting landscape today as prominent merchant Philip English and his wife Mary have fled to New York, escaping imprisonment on charges of witchcraft. This daring escape, possibly aided by local officials and clergy, raises questions about the limits of Governor Phips’ authority and the growing desperation of the accused.

A remarkable turn of events unfolded this week with the escape of Philip and Mary English from Boston jail. Salem’s wealthiest merchant, Philip English, had been accused of witchcraft back in April, and both he and his wife were awaiting trial. With the help of Reverend Joshua Moody of Boston, the Englishes made their way to New York, where they have reportedly found sanctuary under the protection of Governor Fletcher. Moody, it seems, provided both practical assistance and a theological justification for their flight, preaching a sermon based on the verse, “If they persecute you in one city, flee to another.” As the magistrate’s record shows, the Englishes were among those named by several afflicted girls, but their flight has cast a shadow over the proceedings. Some whisper that Governor Phips may have tacitly allowed their escape, recognizing the growing unrest and the potential for further disruption. It is a bold move, and one that will undoubtedly be discussed for days to come.

Philip and Mary English successfully fled to New York, escaping imprisonment on charges of witchcraft with assistance from Reverend Joshua Moody. Moody’s sermon, citing Matthew 10:23, provided a religious framework for their decision to seek safety elsewhere, and Governor Phips may have quietly consented to their departure.

The Englishes’ escape underscores the desperation of those accused, as facing trial now means risking death by hanging. Their wealth and social standing likely made them targets, but also afforded them the means to attempt an escape.

Governor Fletcher of New York has offered shelter to the Englishes. This action could strain relations between Massachusetts Bay and New York, as it directly challenges the authority of the Massachusetts court.

Reverend Moody’s support for the Englishes is notable. His willingness to aid accused individuals suggests a growing unease among some clergy regarding the trials and the evidence presented.

The timing of the escape, during a period of escalating executions, is significant. It reflects a growing belief among the accused that justice is unlikely to be found within the Massachusetts legal system.

While the trials continue, the increasing number of escapes and questions about the process suggest that the fervor surrounding the witch hunt may be beginning to wane.

newspapers.lede: The flight of Philip and Mary English marks a significant turn in the ongoing crisis. Philip English, Salem’s wealthiest merchant, and his wife, fearing for their lives, fled Salem this Friday. They made their way to Boston, seeking refuge in an unlikely place—hidden amongst dirty laundry at the home of George Hollard. From there, they continued on to New York, where Governor Fletcher had signaled a willingness to accept those fleeing the trials. As the magistrate’s record of earlier examinations shows, English had been accused of “afflicting” several individuals, and his wealth hadn't shielded him from suspicion. According to depositions, one accuser claimed English appeared as a specter, threatening harm. But wealth *did* buy him a path to safety unavailable to the poor. This escape route, from Salem Town to Boston to New York, directly followed the trade routes that had built his fortune, proving that even in the midst of hysteria, commerce and connections mattered.
newspapers.summary: Today, Salem Village lost another prominent citizen to fear. Philip English, a wealthy merchant accused of witchcraft, and his wife Mary, escaped to New York, highlighting how social standing and resources could offer protection even as the trials reached their most deadly phase. This flight underscores the desperate measures people took to avoid the court.

Philip and Mary English successfully fled Salem, traveling through Boston to New York with the aid of established commercial networks and the promise of sanctuary from Governor Fletcher. This escape demonstrates that wealth and social connections could provide a measure of protection against the accusations and legal proceedings of the court, something unavailable to most of the accused.

The English’s escape highlighted the growing desperation of those accused, as they were willing to abandon their homes, businesses, and community to avoid potential conviction and execution.

The fact that Governor Fletcher of New York was willing to accept refugees from the trials suggests a growing skepticism among neighboring colonies regarding the proceedings in Massachusetts Bay.

With five more accused scheduled to be hanged on August 19th, the pace of executions remains alarmingly high, suggesting the court shows no sign of slowing down.

Word is circulating that Reverend Samuel Willard of Boston is among those privately questioning the validity of spectral evidence, though he has not yet publicly denounced the court.

Local constables are actively searching for other potential escapees, increasing the tension and fear within Salem Village and Salem Town.

newspapers.lede: The accusations continue to mount, now extending into the town of Haverhill. This morning, warrants were issued for the arrest of Frances Hutchins and Ruth Wilford, both of Haverhill, based on complaints made by Timothy Swan of Andover, and the familiar afflicted girls, Mary Walcott and Anna Putnam of Salem Village. The official warrant, as the magistrate’s record reads, directs the constable of Haverhill to apprehend the women, stating that they “hath sorely afflicted them…in their bodies, by witchcraft.” Constable diligently seized Mrs. Hutchins, but reports difficulty locating Ruth Wilford. This expansion of the accusations beyond our immediate area raises concerns about the scope of this crisis and the potential for further unrest, as anyone could now be accused based on the testimony of those claiming affliction. Timothy Swan, it seems, is becoming a central figure in these new complaints, naming multiple individuals from both Haverhill and Andover.
newspapers.summary: Today brings news of accusations spreading beyond Salem Village, this time to Haverhill. Two women, Frances Hutchins and Ruth Wilford, have been named in complaints filed by afflicted individuals and are now subject to arrest warrants, demonstrating how the reach of these accusations continues to expand.

The official warrant, as the magistrate’s record reads, directs the constable of Haverhill to apprehend the women, stating that they “hath sorely afflicted them…in their bodies, by witchcraft.” Constable diligently seized Mrs. Hutchins, but reports difficulty locating Ruth Wilford. This expansion of the accusations beyond our immediate area raises concerns about the scope of this crisis and the potential for further unrest, as anyone could now be accused based on the testimony of those claiming affliction. Timothy Swan, it seems, is becoming a central figure in these new complaints, naming multiple individuals from both Haverhill and Andover.

As detailed in the warrant issued today, Frances Hutchins and Ruth Wilford of Haverhill have been accused of witchcraft by Timothy Swan and the afflicted girls, Mary Walcott and Anna Putnam. This demonstrates a widening net of accusations extending beyond Salem Village and Andover.

Constable of Haverhill successfully apprehended Frances Hutchins, but has so far been unable to locate Ruth Wilford despite diligent searching. This highlights the challenges in enforcing the warrants and bringing accused individuals before the court.

Timothy Swan of Andover is emerging as a prominent accuser, initiating complaints against multiple residents of both Haverhill and Andover. His involvement suggests a potential pattern of accusation in these neighboring communities.

The accusations against Hutchins and Wilford, like many others, rely solely on the testimony of those claiming to be afflicted by witchcraft, raising questions about the evidence being presented and the due process afforded to the accused.

The warrants issued today, in the name of King William and Queen Mary, underscore the legal basis – however questionable – upon which these proceedings are being conducted, framing the accusations as offenses against the Crown and its laws.

newspapers.lede: Today, August 19th, 1692, five individuals were sent to the gallows on Proctor’s Ledge, marking a grim peak in the escalating crisis. Reverend George Burroughs, a former minister of Salem Village, was among those executed, alongside John Proctor, George Jacobs Sr., John Willard, and Martha Carrier. As Burroughs stood with the rope around his neck, he recited the Lord’s Prayer, perfectly and without hesitation. This unexpected act deeply moved the crowd, causing many to question the proceedings. As the magistrate’s record reads, the crowd “was greatly moved,” and murmurs of doubt rippled through the onlookers. But the moment was quickly seized by Cotton Mather, who rode forward on horseback to address the assembled crowd. He declared, "People of Salem—people of God—hear me! The Devil is the father of lies... He can produce the appearance of holiness..." Mather argued that the Devil could imitate piety, and that Burroughs's flawless prayer was merely a deception. His intervention quelled the growing unrest, and the executions proceeded. This day demonstrates how fragile the consensus supporting the trials truly was, and how easily it could be swayed—or, in this case, reinforced—by a powerful voice.
newspapers.summary: Today saw the largest single-day execution of the Salem Witch Trials, with five people – Reverend George Burroughs, John Proctor, George Jacobs Sr., John Willard, and Martha Carrier – hanged on Proctor’s Ledge. The executions were nearly derailed when Burroughs flawlessly recited the Lord’s Prayer on the scaffold, but Cotton Mather intervened, arguing the Devil himself could mimic piety.

Five people – Reverend George Burroughs, John Proctor, George Jacobs Sr., John Willard, and Martha Carrier – were hanged today on Proctor’s Ledge, the largest single-day execution in the trials. The hangings signify the height of the crisis and the court’s unwavering commitment to prosecution.

Reverend George Burroughs’s perfect recitation of the Lord’s Prayer on the scaffold caused a significant disruption, shaking the crowd’s confidence in the proceedings. The event highlights the conflict between folk beliefs—that a witch could not recite the prayer—and the court’s legal justifications.

Cotton Mather played a crucial role in maintaining order, riding forward on horseback to explain that the Devil could mimic religious devotion. His intervention prevented a potential riot and ensured the executions went forward, demonstrating his considerable influence.

Constable John Willard, who initially enforced the arrests, was himself executed today after refusing to continue serving warrants. This illustrates the dangerous position of those involved in the legal process and the growing skepticism within the community.

Rebecca Eames of Boxford was accused of witchcraft during the executions, after allegedly causing a pinprick on a spectator’s foot. This illustrates the self-perpetuating nature of the hysteria, with accusations arising even amidst the grim spectacle of the hangings.

John Proctor, a prosperous farmer and tavern keeper, was known for his outspoken opposition to the trials. His defiance and blunt opinions likely contributed to his condemnation, showcasing the dangers of challenging the court's authority.

Cotton Mather is a young man of twenty-nine, wielding enormous authority as the son and grandson of prominent ministers. His dress – fine clothing, a large periwig – reflects his high social standing and intellectual power.

George Burroughs was a physically strong man, accustomed to frontier life, a stark contrast to the refined Boston clergy. His composure on the gallows and his perfect recitation of the Lord’s Prayer made a powerful impression on the crowd and fueled doubts about his guilt.

newspapers.lede: The air here in Salem Village feels heavier today, weighed down by sorrow and doubt. Yesterday, five more were hanged on Gallows Hill, including George Jacobs Sr., and today, his granddaughter Margaret Jacobs has revealed the desperate circumstances that led to her initial accusations against him. From within the Salem prison, Margaret wrote a letter, begging her grandfather’s forgiveness and admitting her confession was false. She explained she was threatened by the magistrates, stating, “I having, through the Magistrates Threatnings, and my own Vile and Wretched Heart, confessed several things contrary to my Conscience and Knowledg.” This is not merely a retraction of testimony, but a desperate plea for redemption from a young woman tormented by guilt. The letter details the terror of a “wounded Conscience” and her fear for her own life, as the afflicted persons continue to accuse. Despite her grandfather's forgiveness, Margaret remains imprisoned, awaiting her own trial. Meanwhile, Sheriff George Corwin has expanded his seizures of property, taking the estates of those accused, including Philip English, even before they've been formally convicted.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a heartbreaking development as Margaret Jacobs recants her testimony against her grandfather, George Jacobs Sr., just one day after his execution. This recantation, born of fear and regret, highlights the immense pressure placed on the accused and the tragic consequences of false confessions during the trials. Sheriff Corwin also continued seizing the property of the accused, even before convictions.
