This recantation, born of fear and regret, highlights the immense pressure placed on the accused and the tragic consequences of false confessions during the trials. Sheriff Corwin also continued seizing the property of the accused, even before convictions.

The air here in Salem Village feels heavier today, weighed down by sorrow and doubt. Yesterday, five more were hanged on Gallows Hill, including George Jacobs Sr., and today, his granddaughter Margaret Jacobs has revealed the desperate circumstances that led to her initial accusations against him. From within the Salem prison, Margaret wrote a letter, begging her grandfather’s forgiveness and admitting her confession was false. She explained she was threatened by the magistrates, stating, “I having, through the Magistrates Threatnings, and my own Vile and Wretched Heart, confessed several things contrary to my Conscience and Knowledg.” This is not merely a retraction of testimony, but a desperate plea for redemption from a young woman tormented by guilt. The letter details the terror of a “wounded Conscience” and her fear for her own life, as the afflicted persons continue to accuse. Despite her grandfather's forgiveness, Margaret remains imprisoned, awaiting her own trial. Meanwhile, Sheriff George Corwin has expanded his seizures of property, taking the estates of those accused, including Philip English, even before they've been formally convicted.

Margaret Jacobs formally recanted her testimony against her grandfather, George Jacobs Sr., and against George Burroughs, revealing she confessed falsely under duress. She wrote from prison, detailing the intense pressure from the magistrates and her own internal conflict, ultimately seeking forgiveness from her grandfather before his execution.

George Jacobs Sr. was executed yesterday, August 19th, along with four others, bringing the total number of hanged to nineteen. His granddaughter’s recantation comes too late to save him, but underscores the questionable nature of the evidence used in the trials.

Sheriff George Corwin continued his aggressive seizure of property belonging to the accused, including the entire estate of Philip English, despite English not yet being convicted. This action raises questions about the legality and fairness of the proceedings.

Margaret Jacobs’ letter describes her desperate situation in the Salem prison, calling it a “loathsome Dungeon” and expressing fear for her own life. She fears she, too, will be put to death based on the accusations of the afflicted.

The recantation of Margaret Jacobs is particularly devastating because it demonstrates how easily fear and coercion can lead to false accusations, destroying families and lives within the community.

Despite her recantation and her grandfather’s forgiveness, Margaret Jacobs remains in prison, awaiting her own trial and potential conviction, demonstrating the unrelenting nature of the accusations.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village and Salem Town is thick with unease, even after the executions of five people just two days ago. But today, a different kind of reckoning began. Reverend Increase Mather, a highly respected minister in Boston, has started to draft a document titled ‘Cases of Conscience,’ which directly addresses the validity of what’s known as spectral evidence. This is evidence based on visions, dreams, or apparitions experienced by accusers. Mather’s questioning of it could significantly change the course of these trials. As the magistrate’s record of earlier examinations shows, accusers frequently testified to being tormented by the spirits of the accused, saying, “I was sorely afflicted by the apparition of Goody [Osborne], and her spirit did pinch and choke me!” Mather believes these spectral experiences may not be reliable proof of a pact with the Devil, but rather products of the imagination or even demonic deception designed to mislead the court. His writing represents the first major intellectual challenge to the proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today, Reverend Increase Mather began work on a treatise questioning the reliability of spectral evidence – visions and dreams used to accuse people of witchcraft. This marks a critical shift in the trials, as a prominent minister challenges the very foundation upon which many convictions rest.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today, Reverend Increase Mather began work on a treatise questioning the reliability of spectral evidence – visions and dreams used to accuse people of witchcraft. This marks a critical shift in the trials, as a prominent minister challenges the very foundation upon which many convictions rest.

The atmosphere in Salem Village and Salem Town is thick with unease, even after the executions of five people just two days ago. But today, a different kind of reckoning began. Reverend Increase Mather, a highly respected minister in Boston, has started to draft a document titled ‘Cases of Conscience,’ which directly addresses the validity of what’s known as spectral evidence. This is evidence based on visions, dreams, or apparitions experienced by accusers. Mather’s questioning of it could significantly change the course of these trials. As the magistrate’s record of earlier examinations shows, accusers frequently testified to being tormented by the spirits of the accused, saying, “I was sorely afflicted by the apparition of Goody [Osborne], and her spirit did pinch and choke me!” Mather believes these spectral experiences may not be reliable proof of a pact with the Devil, but rather products of the imagination or even demonic deception designed to mislead the court. His writing represents the first major intellectual challenge to the proceedings.

Reverend Increase Mather’s work on ‘Cases of Conscience’ is the most significant development of the day, as it directly challenges the use of spectral evidence, which has been central to many convictions. He is urging caution and careful consideration of the origins of these visions before accepting them as proof of witchcraft.

The recent executions on August 19th continue to weigh heavily on the community, but Mather’s challenge to the court’s methods introduces a new dynamic. The sheer number of accusations and convictions following the hangings has created widespread fear and anxiety.

While Mather’s work is still in its early stages, its potential impact is considerable, as he is a highly respected figure whose opinion carries significant weight among the other ministers and magistrates.

The court is scheduled to meet again next week, and it remains to be seen how they will respond to Mather’s arguments, or if they will even consider revising their procedures.

Concerns about the safety of those imprisoned continue, with many families fearing further accusations and trials, even as doubts about the process begin to surface.

newspapers.lede: The wave of accusations expands today, this time centering on the town of Andover and the closely-knit Bridges and Post families. Sarah Bridges, just seventeen years old, along with her stepsister Hannah Post and several other relatives, were accused of witchcraft by Rose Foster and Martha Sprague. The accusations are building on each other, with Sarah implicating Hannah during her examination. As the magistrate’s record reads, a warrant was issued for their arrest, and that of Mary Marston and Mary Barker, stating they had “woefully afflicted & abused” Abigail Martin, Rose Foster, and Martha Sprague “by witchcraft.” This pattern – accusations stemming from pre-existing relationships and quickly expanding to include family members – is becoming tragically common. It’s a disturbing illustration of how fear and suspicion are tearing apart the fabric of these communities. The authorities aren’t just pursuing convictions, they are dismantling lives. Sheriff Corwin’s actions at the Proctor farm after John’s execution demonstrate a ruthless efficiency in stripping the family of everything they own.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village and Andover, the accusations continue to spread, now focusing intensely on the interconnected families of Bridges and Post. Several women were accused of witchcraft, and the authorities are systematically seizing the property of those convicted, leaving families destitute. This escalation points to a pattern of accusation driven by existing social networks.
newspapers.ttsFullText:

Several women were accused of witchcraft, and the authorities are systematically seizing the property of those convicted, leaving families destitute. This escalation points to a pattern of accusation driven by existing social networks.

The wave of accusations expands today, this time centering on the town of Andover and the closely-knit Bridges and Post families. Sarah Bridges, just seventeen years old, along with her stepsister Hannah Post and several other relatives, were accused of witchcraft by Rose Foster and Martha Sprague. The accusations are building on each other, with Sarah implicating Hannah during her examination. As the magistrate’s record reads, a warrant was issued for their arrest, and that of Mary Marston and Mary Barker, stating they had “woefully afflicted & abused” Abigail Martin, Rose Foster, and Martha Sprague “by witchcraft.” This pattern – accusations stemming from pre-existing relationships and quickly expanding to include family members – is becoming tragically common. It’s a disturbing illustration of how fear and suspicion are tearing apart the fabric of these communities. The authorities aren’t just pursuing convictions, they are dismantling lives. Sheriff Corwin’s actions at the Proctor farm after John’s execution demonstrate a ruthless efficiency in stripping the family of everything they own.

The core of today’s events lies in the accusations leveled against the Bridges and Post families of Andover. Sarah Bridges, Hannah Post, Mary Bridges Jr., Susanna Post, and others were all accused, revealing a pattern of kinship-based accusations that is rapidly unfolding in the town.

Sheriff Corwin continued the systematic seizure of John Proctor’s estate following his recent execution. All beer was emptied from the barrels at the tavern, food was confiscated, and even a pot of broth was thrown out—along with the pot itself.

The value of the seized Proctor estate is estimated to be over 300 pounds, with court and jail fees alone totaling approximately 150 pounds, leaving John Proctor’s children with nothing to support themselves.

Mary Parker previously accused Hannah Post, adding another layer to the existing network of accusations and suggesting a pre-existing animosity or dispute that may be fueling the charges.

William Barker Sr., Mary Marston, and Mary Barker were also named in warrants issued today, expanding the scope of the accusations beyond Andover and suggesting a wider reach for the perceived threat of witchcraft.

Mary Bridges Jr., at only thirteen years old, is among those accused, highlighting the vulnerability of children during this period and the devastating impact the trials are having on families.

The confiscated cattle from the Proctor farm were not simply sold, but also slaughtered or shipped to the West Indies for sale, indicating a deliberate attempt to eradicate any remaining value from the estate.

newspapers.lede: The meeting house was filled with tension today as John Jackson, a resident of Salem Village, was examined on accusations of witchcraft. Magistrate John Hawthorne and others questioned Jackson about his alleged torment of Martha Sprague and Rose Foster. As the questioning began, the afflicted girls reportedly fell into convulsions, prompting accusations that Jackson’s very presence caused their distress. Jackson maintained his innocence, pleading with God to keep him from such sin. According to the magistrate’s record, he declared, “I desire to Cry to God to keep both me & mine from this sin: I never did it since the day I was born.” However, Mary Warren testified that Jackson *had* afflicted her, and Sprague claimed he harmed her throat the previous night. A dramatic moment occurred when Jackson was asked to look upon the afflicted, and Sprague was struck down—though, remarkably, she recovered when Jackson touched her hand. The magistrates then called Hannah Post to testify, but Jackson insisted he knew nothing of a witch meeting she described in Andover.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, John Jackson Senior underwent a grueling examination before magistrates. Accused of witchcraft by several afflicted girls, Jackson vehemently denied the charges, even as the girls fell into fits during the proceedings. This examination highlights the reliance on spectral evidence and the precarious position of the accused.

Today in Salem Village, John Jackson Senior underwent a grueling examination before magistrates. Accused of witchcraft by several afflicted girls, Jackson vehemently denied the charges, even as the girls fell into fits during the proceedings. This examination highlights the reliance on spectral evidence and the precarious position of the accused.

Magistrate John Hawthorne and others questioned Jackson about his alleged torment of Martha Sprague and Rose Foster. As the questioning began, the afflicted girls reportedly fell into convulsions, prompting accusations that Jackson’s very presence caused their distress. Jackson maintained his innocence, pleading with God to keep him from such sin. According to the magistrate’s record, he declared, “I desire to Cry to God to keep both me & mine from this sin: I never did it since the day I was born.” However, Mary Warren testified that Jackson *had* afflicted her, and Sprague claimed he harmed her throat the previous night. A dramatic moment occurred when Jackson was asked to look upon the afflicted, and Sprague was struck down—though, remarkably, she recovered when Jackson touched her hand. The magistrates then called Hannah Post to testify, but Jackson insisted he knew nothing of a witch meeting she described in Andover.

John Jackson Senior’s examination was the central event of the day, with accusations of witchcraft leveled against him by afflicted girls like Mary Warren and Martha Sprague. The girls’ physical reactions were presented as evidence of Jackson’s guilt, despite his repeated denials and pleas of innocence.

The testimony of Hannah Post added another layer to the accusations, claiming Jackson was seen at a witch meeting in Andover. This broadened the scope of the alleged conspiracy and suggested a network of witches extending beyond Salem Village.

The magistrates relied heavily on the afflicted girls' reactions as proof of guilt. The fact that Martha Sprague was struck down while Jackson looked at her, yet seemingly recovered from his touch, was presented as a key piece of evidence.

The examination of John Jackson, like many others, relied on spectral evidence – the claims of visions and afflictions that could not be independently verified. This practice continued to fuel the cycle of accusations and fear.

Despite professing innocence, John Jackson faced a hostile environment, with the magistrates seemingly predisposed to believe the accusations leveled against him. This illustrates the difficulty faced by the accused in proving their innocence during this period.

The focus on Andover as a potential hotbed of witchcraft suggests the investigation may soon expand beyond Salem Village, potentially leading to more accusations and trials in neighboring communities.

newspapers.lede: The crisis in Andover deepened significantly today as accusations multiplied and confessions poured forth. Warrants were issued for Elizabeth Johnson Sr. and her daughter Abigail, based on the testimony of Samuel Martin and the afflicted girls. The pattern of accusation is becoming chillingly clear: families are being targeted as a unit, with one member's confession often leading to the implication of others. William Barker Sr., during his examination, gave a particularly detailed account of the demonic activity, claiming to have been in the devil’s service for three years and describing a gathering of no fewer than 307 witches. He stated, as the magistrate’s record reads, that “Satans design was to set up his own worship, abolish all the churches in the land.” Later, Barker and others signed a recantation, admitting they confessed under duress, saying they were “exceedingly astonished and amazed, and consternated and affrighted even out of our reason.” The sheer number of those implicated today suggests a desperate attempt by authorities to break the perceived network of witchcraft in the region.
newspapers.summary: Today in Andover, the accusations spread rapidly, ensnaring multiple members of the Johnson, Barker, and Marston families. Several confessed to witchcraft, describing gatherings of demons and compacts with the devil. These confessions, while adding to the growing panic, seem increasingly coerced as authorities press for admissions.

Today in Andover, the accusations spread rapidly, ensnaring multiple members of the Johnson, Barker, and Marston families. Several confessed to witchcraft, describing gatherings of demons and compacts with the devil. These confessions, while adding to the growing panic, seem increasingly coerced as authorities press for admissions.

The crisis in Andover deepened significantly today as accusations multiplied and confessions poured forth. Warrants were issued for Elizabeth Johnson Sr. and her daughter Abigail, based on the testimony of Samuel Martin and the afflicted girls. The pattern of accusation is becoming chillingly clear: families are being targeted as a unit, with one member's confession often leading to the implication of others. William Barker Sr., during his examination, gave a particularly detailed account of the demonic activity, claiming to have been in the devil’s service for three years and describing a gathering of no fewer than 307 witches. He stated, as the magistrate’s record reads, that “Satans design was to set up his own worship, abolish all the churches in the land.” Later, Barker and others signed a recantation, admitting they confessed under duress, saying they were “exceedingly astonished and amazed, and consternated and affrighted even out of our reason.” The sheer number of those implicated today suggests a desperate attempt by authorities to break the perceived network of witchcraft in the region.

As detailed in the lede, the Johnson, Barker, and Marston families of Andover were heavily implicated today, with accusations against Elizabeth Johnson Sr., Abigail Johnson, Mary Barker, Mary Marston, and William Barker Jr. all coming to light. This illustrates how accusations quickly spread through social networks.

William Barker Sr.’s confession is remarkable for its scale—he claims to have witnessed a gathering of 307 witches—and for his later recantation, which suggests the confessions are not always freely given. He described signing the devil’s book with blood, a common motif in these examinations.

Mary Marston, age 27, confessed to hearing a voice in the night asking permission to afflict others. According to the copy of her examination, she said she “heard a voice in ye nighttime which desired leave of her to afflict & she answerd yes.” This highlights the reliance on spectral evidence in these cases.

Young William Barker Jr., only fourteen years old, confessed to being in the devil’s snare and reported seeing a black dog in the woods. His brief explanation of his compact with the devil raises questions about the degree to which interrogators guided these young confessors.

The warrant for the arrest of Elizabeth Johnson Sr. and Abigail Johnson, as documented in the official record, relies entirely on the accusations of Samuel Martin and the testimony of the afflicted girls, demonstrating the weight given to these sources.

The swiftness of the arrests and examinations in Andover, following closely on the heels of the events in Salem Village, indicates a coordinated effort by magistrates to root out perceived witchcraft throughout the region.

newspapers.lede: The meeting house was filled today as Elizabeth Johnson, a woman of Salem Village, was examined by the magistrates. The proceedings were lengthy and, by all accounts, forceful. Johnson initially resisted direct accusations, but eventually admitted to seeing the Devil, describing his first appearance as “a white bird” that visited her in her own house. She claimed the Devil awoke her at night and demanded service, but insisted she never formally signed his book, only marking it with her finger. As the magistrate’s record reads, questioning continued relentlessly: “Come be thorow in all licklyhood – you have been long in this Snare the Sundry years.” She eventually conceded to being involved “for 3 Year or not above 4,” a timeframe seemingly dictated by the pressure of the interrogation. This confession, even with its limitations, will almost certainly be used to implicate others and advance the prosecutions.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Elizabeth Johnson Senior underwent a grueling examination before the magistrates. Her confession, while partial, adds to the growing body of evidence used to justify the prosecutions and fuels the escalating hysteria gripping the community. This examination underscores the intense pressure placed on the accused to confess.

This examination underscores the intense pressure placed on the accused to confess.

Johnson initially resisted direct accusations, but eventually admitted to seeing the Devil, describing his first appearance as “a white bird” that visited her in her own house. She claimed the Devil awoke her at night and demanded service, but insisted she never formally signed his book, only marking it with her finger. As the magistrate’s record reads, questioning continued relentlessly: “Come be thorow in all licklyhood – you have been long in this Snare the Sundry years.” She eventually conceded to being involved “for 3 Year or not above 4,” a timeframe seemingly dictated by the pressure of the interrogation. This confession, even with its limitations, will almost certainly be used to implicate others and advance the prosecutions.

Elizabeth Johnson’s examination was the central event of the day. She confessed to seeing the Devil and entering into a pact with him, though she maintained she never formally signed the Devil’s book, only marking it with her finger – a detail that may prove significant as the trials continue.

The ongoing examinations are clearly focused on extracting confessions, regardless of their veracity. The relentless questioning, as evidenced in Johnson’s case, suggests the magistrates are less interested in truth and more interested in securing admissions of guilt.

With five already hanged on August 19th, including Reverend George Burroughs, the community remains on edge. Fear and suspicion continue to spread, making reasoned discourse nearly impossible.

The number of imprisoned individuals continues to rise, straining the resources of Salem Village and surrounding towns. Conditions in the jail are reportedly deplorable.

Several new complaints and accusations were filed today, though the identities of the accusers and the accused remain largely unconfirmed at this time.

Locals whisper that the magistrates are becoming increasingly reliant on spectral evidence – visions and dreams reported by the afflicted girls – despite growing doubts about its reliability.

newspapers.lede: The wheels of justice, or what passes for it here in Salem, continue to turn. Today, August thirty-first, a warrant was issued by Deputy Governor William Stoughton, directing the sheriff of Essex County to assemble a new jury for the Court of Oyer and Terminer. Forty men, freeholders and freemen of the county, are to be summoned to Salem to hear the cases of those accused of witchcraft. The court is scheduled to reconvene on Tuesday, September sixth. The warrant itself is a formal document, declaring that the sheriff is “Required in their Ma'ties Name to Impannel and return Forty good and lawful men…” This makes clear the authority the Crown expects to be exercised here. It’s a grim reminder that despite the recent hangings, the court believes the threat remains, and more lives hang in the balance. The same Grand Jurors who served at the last session are also being recalled, suggesting the court intends to build upon previous findings.
newspapers.summary: Today, preparations are underway for the next session of the Court of Oyer and Terminer, with a warrant issued for a new jury of forty men. This indicates the trials will continue, despite five executions just two weeks ago, and underscores the ongoing belief in the need to root out witchcraft in the Massachusetts Bay.

Today, August thirty-first, a warrant was issued by Deputy Governor William Stoughton, directing the sheriff of Essex County to assemble a new jury for the Court of Oyer and Terminer. Forty men, freeholders and freemen of the county, are to be summoned to Salem to hear the cases of those accused of witchcraft. The court is scheduled to reconvene on Tuesday, September sixth. The warrant itself is a formal document, declaring that the sheriff is “Required in their Ma'ties Name to Impannel and return Forty good and lawful men…” This makes clear the authority the Crown expects to be exercised here. It’s a grim reminder that despite the recent hangings, the court believes the threat remains, and more lives hang in the balance. The same Grand Jurors who served at the last session are also being recalled, suggesting the court intends to build upon previous findings.

A new jury of forty men is being impaneled for the Court of Oyer and Terminer, as ordered by Deputy Governor Stoughton, and will convene on September sixth. This signals the continuation of the trials despite the recent executions and ongoing skepticism in some quarters.

The recall of the previous Grand Jury indicates the court intends to continue building upon established accusations and evidence, likely revisiting cases already presented.

The warrant specifically requests “freeholders and other Freemen” for the jury, meaning landowning men with a stake in the community are expected to participate in these grave proceedings.

The court’s insistence on a jury of “good and lawful men” underscores the perceived need for legitimacy, even as the proceedings are increasingly criticized for their reliance on spectral evidence.

With five people already hanged on August 19th, including Reverend George Burroughs, the atmosphere in Salem Village remains tense and fearful. The community is bracing for more trials and potentially more executions.

The continued operation of the Court of Oyer and Terminer demonstrates the strong support for these proceedings among the Massachusetts Bay’s political and religious leaders.

newspapers.lede: The village of Andover is overwhelmed by accusations today, as the wave of hysteria that began with the afflicted girls has expanded to encompass a dozen new names. Samuel Wardwell, a carpenter and known fortune-teller, confessed immediately to signing a pact with the Devil, telling the court he was “unhappy with his life” when he first made the agreement. However, when his confession was read back to him, he attempted to recant, declaring it all false. This retraction, tragically, all but sealed his fate — as is becoming tragically clear, confession offers a slim hope for survival, while recantation guarantees the gallows. Alongside Wardwell, his wife Sarah has also been accused and condemned. Mary Parker, a 55-year-old widow, maintains her innocence, simply stating, “I know nothing of witchcraft,” but faces mounting evidence against her. Even 19-year-old Mercy Wardwell is caught in the web, confessing in hopes of saving her father, though it appears to be a futile effort.
newspapers.summary: Today in Andover, accusations are flying. Several residents, including Samuel and Sarah Wardwell, Mary Parker, and Sarah Hawkes Jr., are accused of witchcraft, leading to confessions and deepening the crisis. Mercy Wardwell’s testimony implicates others in an attempt to save her father, while the case of Giles Corey continues its grim course.

Mercy Wardwell’s testimony implicates others in an attempt to save her father, while the case of Giles Corey continues its grim course.

The situation in Andover is rapidly escalating, with accusations multiplying and several individuals now in custody. Samuel Wardwell’s initial confession and subsequent recantation highlights the deadly paradox at the heart of these trials.

Mercy Wardwell confessed to witchcraft, implicating Mary Parker and Mary Lacey, possibly in an attempt to save her father, Samuel Wardwell, from prosecution. Her actions demonstrate the desperation gripping the accused and their families.

Mary Parker, a widow from Andover, steadfastly denies any involvement in witchcraft, maintaining her innocence despite the accusations leveled against her.

Sarah Hawkes Jr. confessed to participating in gatherings at Mr. Tyler’s house, indicating the accused are being pressured to admit to spectral meetings and dances.

Sarah Wardwell, wife of Samuel Wardwell, has been convicted alongside her husband and now faces the same grim fate, watching her husband’s likely execution from within the prison walls.

Stephen Johnson of Andover is among the few men accused in the Andover outbreak, suggesting the accusations are primarily focused on women and children.

Ann Putnam Sr.’s condition continues to decline, her afflictions lessening but her overall health failing, perhaps signaling a shift in the dynamic of the accusations.

Giles Corey remains defiant as the pressing continues, refusing to plead and accepting the excruciating punishment with a chilling determination, reportedly uttering the words “More weight.”

The case of Samuel Wardwell illustrates how traditional practices like fortune-telling, while frowned upon, are now being equated with demonic pacts and punishable by death.

newspapers.lede: The wave of accusations sweeping through Massachusetts Bay reached Andover today, with Mary Parker becoming the latest named in the unfolding crisis. Several accusers, including Hannah Bixby, Sarah Phelps, and Martha Sprague, identified Parker as afflicting them. Most disturbingly, Mercy Wardwell, a young woman already implicated and seemingly coerced into confessing witchcraft herself, also named Parker. This suggests a desperate attempt to deflect attention, perhaps even to save her own father. The Andover meetinghouse has become a focal point, with the so-called “touch test” yielding a flood of new names. As the magistrate’s record shows, the afflicted would feign fits and spasms upon being touched by the accused, providing what many considered proof of guilt. But even as the court pursues these claims, a surprising admission comes from Reverend Cotton Mather. In a letter to Chief Justice William Stoughton, Mather writes of his concern that “Innocent people being Accused” may occur, yet simultaneously requests the trial records to compose a detailed narrative defending the court’s actions.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the accusations continue to spread, now reaching Andover with the naming of Mary Parker. Meanwhile, Reverend Cotton Mather, a key intellectual supporter of the trials, has requested the court records – a move that suggests he is preparing a formal account, even as he acknowledges the possibility of wrongly accused individuals.

Meanwhile, Reverend Cotton Mather, a key intellectual supporter of the trials, has requested the court records – a move that suggests he is preparing a formal account, even as he acknowledges the possibility of wrongly accused individuals.

Several accusers, including Hannah Bixby, Sarah Phelps, and Martha Sprague, identified Parker as afflicting them. Most disturbingly, Mercy Wardwell, a young woman already implicated and seemingly coerced into confessing witchcraft herself, also named Parker. The Andover meetinghouse has become a focal point, with the so-called “touch test” yielding a flood of new names. As the magistrate’s record shows, the afflicted would feign fits and spasms upon being touched by the accused, providing what many considered proof of guilt. But even as the court pursues these claims, a surprising admission comes from Reverend Cotton Mather. In a letter to Chief Justice William Stoughton, Mather writes of his concern that “Innocent people being Accused” may occur, yet simultaneously requests the trial records to compose a detailed narrative defending the court’s actions.

The accusations against Mary Parker represent a significant expansion of the trials beyond Salem Village and into Andover, demonstrating the rapidly escalating scope of the hysteria. The reliance on spectral evidence and the “touch test” continues to drive these accusations, with Mercy Wardwell’s testimony highlighting the potential for false claims made under duress.

Cotton Mather’s request for the trial records is a complex development. While he acknowledges the possibility of innocent people being accused, his intention to write an official account raises questions about his true motives and whether he seeks to justify the proceedings.

The fact that Mather’s future publication, Wonders of the Invisible World, would receive the endorsement of William Stoughton underscores the close alignment between the intellectual and legal authorities driving the trials.

The continued use of coerced confessions, as evidenced by Mercy Wardwell’s testimony, raises serious concerns about the fairness and reliability of the evidence being presented in court.

The focus on Andover indicates that the magistrates and ministers are actively attempting to identify and prosecute witches in multiple communities, rather than limiting their efforts to Salem Village.

Despite growing anxieties, there is no indication that the court intends to slow down the pace of examinations and trials, meaning more accusations and potentially more executions are likely in the coming weeks.

newspapers.lede: The reach of the accusations broadened significantly today, as two women from Gloucester were formally accused of witchcraft. Ebenezer Babson, a resident of Gloucester, invited the young women from Salem Village—those who have been experiencing fits and claiming to be afflicted—to his home after seeing visions he attributed to supernatural forces. He hoped they could identify who was harming his mother, Eleanor Babson, and Mary Sargent, a local justice’s wife. As the magistrate’s record reads, a warrant was issued for the apprehension of Elizabeth Dicer and Margaret Prince, stating they had “griveously hurt & Tortured” the two women “by witchcraft.” Dicer, it’s worth noting, had previously been brought before the Essex County Court for calling a neighbor a “black-mouthed witch,” a charge that, while serious, didn’t lead to further action until now. Prince is described locally as having a “sharp tongue,” suggesting a reputation for being contentious, a characteristic that, in these times, can easily be twisted into evidence of something far more sinister. Both women were taken into custody and transported to Salem for examination.
newspapers.summary: Today brings the accusations of witchcraft beyond Salem Village, extending to the coastal town of Gloucester. Two women, Elizabeth Dicer and Margaret Prince, were named as tormentors by the afflicted girls, prompted by a local man seeking answers for his mother’s illness. This expansion demonstrates how fear is spreading throughout Massachusetts Bay.

Two women, Elizabeth Dicer and Margaret Prince, were named as tormentors by the afflicted girls, prompted by a local man seeking answers for his mother’s illness. This expansion demonstrates how fear is spreading throughout Massachusetts Bay.

The reach of the accusations broadened significantly today, as two women from Gloucester were formally accused of witchcraft. Ebenezer Babson, a resident of Gloucester, invited the young women from Salem Village—those who have been experiencing fits and claiming to be afflicted—to his home after seeing visions he attributed to supernatural forces. He hoped they could identify who was harming his mother, Eleanor Babson, and Mary Sargent, a local justice’s wife. As the magistrate’s record reads, a warrant was issued for the apprehension of Elizabeth Dicer and Margaret Prince, stating they had “griveously hurt & Tortured” the two women “by witchcraft.” Dicer, it’s worth noting, had previously been brought before the Essex County Court for calling a neighbor a “black-mouthed witch,” a charge that, while serious, didn’t lead to further action until now. Prince is described locally as having a “sharp tongue,” suggesting a reputation for being contentious, a characteristic that, in these times, can easily be twisted into evidence of something far more sinister. Both women were taken into custody and transported to Salem for examination.

Today’s accusations against Elizabeth Dicer and Margaret Prince mark the first time individuals outside of Salem Village have been formally accused during this outbreak. This expansion of the crisis suggests the fear of witchcraft is no longer contained to a single community.

Ebenezer Babson’s invitation of the afflicted girls to Gloucester is a key development, demonstrating how actively people are seeking explanations for misfortune through the lens of witchcraft, even inviting the source of the perceived affliction into their homes.

Elizabeth Dicer’s prior accusation of a neighbor being a “black-mouthed witch” reveals a pattern of labeling individuals with negative attributes that now, in the current climate, are being reinterpreted as evidence of witchcraft.

Margaret Prince is identified as the first resident of Gloucester to be accused, and local descriptions of her “sharp tongue” raise questions about how personality and reputation are contributing to these accusations.

Both Dicer and Prince were released on their own recognizance in December, meaning they were not held in jail but required to appear for trial—a trial that ultimately never happened, suggesting a potential shift in the legal approach to these cases.

The warrant for their arrest, signed by magistrates Bartho Gedney, John Hathorn, and Jonathan Corwin, confirms the official endorsement of these proceedings and the continued belief in spectral evidence.

newspapers.lede: The weight of accusations grows heavier this Friday as the afflicted continue to name names, now extending beyond the immediate area of Salem and Andover. Today, Jane Lilly of distant Malden, Joseph Emons, and Nicholas Frost of Manchester all found themselves accused. But the most dramatic events unfolded during the examination of Margaret Prince of Cape Ann. As the magistrate’s record reads, Prince was questioned about afflicting Widow Babson and William Serjant’s wife. The afflicted, Elizabeth Hubbard and Mary Warren, reportedly fell into fits, only to be “helped up” by a touch of Prince’s hand. Then, according to the deposition, a chilling claim emerged: “Margret Princes spectre had told her that she killd Mrs Duncun of Capan,” a statement Prince vehemently denied, insisting she “had never hurt sd Duncun nor Babson nor would not hurt anybody for a thousand worlds.” This incident highlights the reliance on spectral evidence and the desperation to find a cause for the ongoing afflictions.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village and beyond, the accusations continue to spread. Three new individuals – Jane Lilly of Malden, Joseph Emons of Manchester, and Nicholas Frost, a mariner also from Manchester – have been accused of witchcraft, demonstrating the widening reach of the crisis. Meanwhile, the examination of Margaret Prince revealed startling claims of spectral attacks and accusations.

Meanwhile, the examination of Margaret Prince revealed startling claims of spectral attacks and accusations.

But the most dramatic events unfolded during the examination of Margaret Prince of Cape Ann. The afflicted, Elizabeth Hubbard and Mary Warren, reportedly fell into fits, only to be “helped up” by a touch of Prince’s hand. Then, according to the deposition, a chilling claim emerged: “Margret Princes spectre had told her that she killd Mrs Duncun of Capan,” a statement Prince vehemently denied, insisting she “had never hurt sd Duncun nor Babson nor would not hurt anybody for a thousand worlds.” This incident highlights the reliance on spectral evidence and the desperation to find a cause for the ongoing afflictions.

As detailed in the lede, the accusations have expanded geographically with Jane Lilly of Malden, Joseph Emons, and Nicholas Frost of Manchester all named as witches today, indicating the crisis is no longer contained to the immediate Salem-Andover area.

Margaret Prince underwent a lengthy examination today, with accusers claiming her touch both afflicted and, paradoxically, healed them—a confusing pattern common in these cases.

Summonses were issued for witnesses to testify against Ann Pudeator and Alice Parker, indicating preparations for their trials are underway.

Mary Taylor was examined today after being accused of afflicting Mary Marshall; Taylor allegedly “beat” and attempted to convert Marshall to her own beliefs, according to Marshall’s testimony.

Witnesses were also summoned to testify against Mary Bradbury, further expanding the network of legal proceedings and drawing in individuals from neighboring towns like Amesbury and Andover.

The accusation of Joseph Emons is notable as Mary Warren, a former accused who later became an accuser, lodged the complaint, underlining the complex and shifting dynamics within the afflicted group.

newspapers.lede: The court at Salem continued its work today, delivering a verdict that shocked many observers. Despite a remarkable display of community support—more than one hundred neighbors testified on her behalf—Mary Bradbury of Salisbury was found guilty of witchcraft. It’s a stark reminder that even the strongest character witnesses seem powerless against the accepted evidence of spectral visions and the anxieties fueling these proceedings. Bradbury’s husband reportedly bribed the jailer, allowing her to escape and flee to Maine, where she lived for many years after. As one official document from today reads, a summons for witnesses was issued for several members of the Towne family, demanding their appearance before the Court of Oyer and Terminer. This relentless pursuit of accused witches shows no sign of slowing, even as doubts about the process grow among some.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Mary Bradbury was convicted of witchcraft despite over one hundred neighbors testifying to her good character. Meanwhile, accusations against Alice Parker expanded to include spectral attacks at sea, highlighting the widespread fear gripping the Massachusetts Bay Colony. These events demonstrate the trials’ relentless momentum, even in the face of overwhelming evidence of innocence.

Meanwhile, accusations against Alice Parker expanded to include spectral attacks at sea, highlighting the widespread fear gripping the Massachusetts Bay Colony. These events demonstrate the trials’ relentless momentum, even in the face of overwhelming evidence of innocence.

The court at Salem continued its work today, delivering a verdict that shocked many observers. Despite a remarkable display of community support—more than one hundred neighbors testified on her behalf—Mary Bradbury of Salisbury was found guilty of witchcraft. It’s a stark reminder that even the strongest character witnesses seem powerless against the accepted evidence of spectral visions and the anxieties fueling these proceedings. Bradbury’s husband reportedly bribed the jailer, allowing her to escape and flee to Maine, where she lived for many years after. As one official document from today reads, a summons for witnesses was issued for several members of the Towne family, demanding their appearance before the Court of Oyer and Terminer. This relentless pursuit of accused witches shows no sign of slowing, even as doubts about the process grow among some.

Mary Bradbury was convicted of witchcraft today despite an unprecedented outpouring of support from over one hundred neighbors who testified to her good reputation. This demonstrates how readily the court dismissed character evidence in favor of spectral accusations and confessions.

Alice Parker faced new accusations, including claims she used witchcraft to sink ships and drown sailors. This testimony speaks to the pervasive fear of the sea and the belief that witchcraft could explain unexplained maritime disasters.

Neighbors testified that Alice Parker bewitched Thomas Westgate, causing a prolonged illness, and that she practiced image magic against her enemies. This case, unlike many others, relied heavily on local grudges and suspicions rather than the afflicted girls’ visions.

A summons was issued today for multiple members of the Towne family – Mary, William, Samuel, Rebecca, and Elizabeth – requiring their appearance before the court tomorrow. This highlights the widening scope of the accusations and the increasing number of families caught up in the trials.

Thomas Bradbury, Mary’s husband, allegedly bribed the jailer to allow his wife to escape custody. She fled to Maine, where she lived for many years, demonstrating a rare instance of successfully evading the court’s reach.

The court’s continued insistence on spectral evidence, even when confronted with overwhelming character testimony, underscores the power of fear and belief in shaping the course of justice in Salem Village.

newspapers.lede: The crisis has exploded beyond Salem Village today, as Reverend Thomas Barnard of Andover invited the afflicted girls—believed to be Ann Putnam Junior and Mary Walcott—to conduct what became known as a ‘touch test’ at the Andover meetinghouse. Accused individuals were blindfolded and forced to touch the girls while they were experiencing fits. When the fits subsided, it was taken as irrefutable proof of guilt. Over forty residents of Andover were accused in a single day, a dramatic escalation of the proceedings. As one accused woman described the ordeal, ‘We were blindfolded, and our hands were laid upon the afflicted persons… then they said they were well and that we were guilty of afflicting them.’ This method, relying on a physical reaction rather than detailed testimony, has alarmed even some who previously supported the trials. Justice Dudley Bradstreet, who had issued approximately thirty warrants in Andover, refused to issue any more after witnessing the test and was immediately accused by the girls himself, forcing him and his wife to flee.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the witchcraft crisis. The practice of ‘touch tests’—bringing afflicted girls to identify witches through physical contact—spread rapidly to Andover, resulting in over forty new accusations in a single day. This expansion beyond Salem Village suggests the hysteria is gaining dangerous momentum.

The practice of ‘touch tests’—bringing afflicted girls to identify witches through physical contact—spread rapidly to Andover, resulting in over forty new accusations in a single day. This expansion beyond Salem Village suggests the hysteria is gaining dangerous momentum.

The crisis has exploded beyond Salem Village today, as Reverend Thomas Barnard of Andover invited the afflicted girls—believed to be Ann Putnam Junior and Mary Walcott—to conduct what became known as a ‘touch test’ at the Andover meetinghouse. Accused individuals were blindfolded and forced to touch the girls while they were experiencing fits. When the fits subsided, it was taken as irrefutable proof of guilt. Over forty residents of Andover were accused in a single day, a dramatic escalation of the proceedings. As one accused woman described the ordeal, ‘We were blindfolded, and our hands were laid upon the afflicted persons… then they said they were well and that we were guilty of afflicting them.’ This method, relying on a physical reaction rather than detailed testimony, has alarmed even some who previously supported the trials. Justice Dudley Bradstreet, who had issued approximately thirty warrants in Andover, refused to issue any more after witnessing the test and was immediately accused by the girls himself, forcing him and his wife to flee.

The Andover touch test resulted in over forty new accusations today, marking a significant expansion of the witchcraft crisis beyond Salem Village. The afflicted girls, brought from Salem specifically for this purpose, identified numerous individuals through physical contact, claiming their afflictions ceased when touched by a witch.

Justice Dudley Bradstreet of Andover, after presiding over the touch test and refusing to issue further warrants, was himself accused by the afflicted girls. He and his wife fled Andover, fearing for their safety, and remain unapprehended.

Henry Salter of Andover was accused today by Rose Foster, one of the afflicted girls, during the touch test. However, he was released and will not be tried, a rare occurrence given the current climate.

Testimony emerged today regarding Ann Pudeator. Samuel Pickworth testified he saw her move with extraordinary speed, claiming she “pasid by me as swifte as if a burd flue by me.” This aligns with other accusations of spectral evidence and unnatural movement.

John Best, Senior, testified that Ann Pudeator had afflicted his wife for years, “pinching & Bruseing of her Till her Earms & other parts of her Body Looked Black.” This account details long-standing animosity and physical harm, common themes in many of the accusations.

Mary Towne sent an excuse to the court today explaining that she and her family are too ill and weak to appear, stating, “we are in a Straing condicion and most of us can scars git of of our beads.” This plea highlights the physical and emotional toll the accusations are taking on the accused and their families.

Several witnesses provided character references for Mary Bradbury of Salisbury, attesting to her piety and good works. James Allen stated she “hath lived according to the rules of the gospell,” attempting to defend her reputation against the accusations.

newspapers.lede: The village of Andover is the focus of much attention this Monday as accusations continue to mount. Mary Osgood, wife of Captain John Osgood, a man of some prominence, confessed today to making a covenant with the Devil eleven years ago. According to the examination, she described a disturbing scene, saying she “saw the appearance of a cat… which yet she thought was a real cat,” and ultimately prayed to the Devil instead of God. This confession, along with those of Abigail Barker, Deliverance Dane, Eunice Frye, and Mary Tyler, all came after what’s being called a “touch test” – a practice where the afflicted girls would be brought near the accused, and any reaction from the girls was taken as proof of guilt. Mary Lovett Tyler, we hear, initially resisted confession, but was pressured by her brother and Reverend Emerson, who “flanked” her and contradicted any denial. These confessions, while seemingly bolstering the case for witchcraft, are raising questions about the methods being used to obtain them.
newspapers.summary: Today in Andover, several women confessed to witchcraft, adding to the growing number of accusations. These confessions, however, are now being questioned as witnesses reveal the intense pressure placed upon the accused. The crisis deepens as the afflicted continue to name new victims, even those of high standing in the community.

The crisis deepens as the afflicted continue to name new victims, even those of high standing in the community.

Mary Lovett Tyler, we hear, initially resisted confession, but was pressured by her brother and Reverend Emerson, who “flanked” her and contradicted any denial. These confessions, while seemingly bolstering the case for witchcraft, are raising questions about the methods being used to obtain them.

As the lede explained, Andover is experiencing a surge in accusations and confessions. Mary Osgood, Abigail Barker, Deliverance Dane, Eunice Frye, and Mary Tyler all confessed today, admitting to covenants with the Devil, spectral encounters, and attendance at gatherings at Five Mile Pond.

The accusation of Eunice Frye, the wife of Deacon Frye, is particularly noteworthy. Her inclusion among the accused demonstrates that even respected members of the community are not safe from suspicion, even those holding positions of religious authority.

Elizabeth Hubbard testified today against William Proctor, claiming he afflicted her on May thirty-first and again on this, the eighth of September. As the magistrate’s record reads, she stated that William Proctor “douth testify… that william procter did aflicte me this deponant.”

Ann Putnam Jr. also gave a deposition today, accusing Mary Bradbury of afflicting Timothy Swan in Andover. She claims to have witnessed Bradbury's “Appearance most grevious afflicting” Swan and believes her to be “a most dreadfull wicth.”

Edward Putnam and Ezekiel Cheever testified regarding the accusations against Martha Corey, detailing a complaint from Ann Putnam that Corey was tormenting her. They claim to have questioned Corey, but the deposition ends abruptly before providing a complete account of the interaction.

The deposition of Ann Putnam Jr. and Ezekiel Cheever, along with the testimony of Elizabeth Hubbard, indicates that accusations are now extending beyond direct confessions and relying heavily on the afflicted girls’ claims of spectral attacks.

Though not a new development, the sheer number of accusations today underscores the escalating panic and the growing difficulty in discerning truth from fabrication within the ongoing trials.

newspapers.lede: The Court of Oyer and Terminer met today and found Alice Parker guilty of witchcraft. The conviction rested largely on the testimony of the young women claiming to be afflicted, as well as accusations of image magic and harming sailors at sea. Parker maintained her innocence, but the court found the spectral evidence compelling enough to condemn her. As Ann Putnam, Junior, testified today, “ever sence the begining of May 1692 I have ben afflected by a woman which tould me hir name was Mis Bradbery.” This pattern of accusations, often linking present afflictions to past grievances, is becoming increasingly common. Another deposition, from James Carr, detailed an incident from twenty years prior, claiming a strange affliction followed a visit to the widow Maverick's house while William Bradbery was courting her. Carr stated, “I was taken affter a strange maner as if #[every] liveing creature did run about every part of my body redy to tare me to peaces.” Such long-held grudges, combined with spectral claims, are fueling the ongoing hysteria.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Alice Parker was convicted of witchcraft and sentenced to hang. The trials continue to rely heavily on the testimony of afflicted girls and accusations of spectral evidence, with claims of harm stretching back decades. This conviction underscores the escalating crisis and the growing number facing execution.

This conviction underscores the escalating crisis and the growing number facing execution.

Parker maintained her innocence, but the court found the spectral evidence compelling enough to condemn her. Carr stated, “I was taken affter a strange maner as if #[every] liveing creature did run about every part of my body redy to tare me to peaces.” Such long-held grudges, combined with spectral claims, are fueling the ongoing hysteria.

Alice Parker was found guilty of witchcraft today and sentenced to death by hanging, adding to the growing list of condemned. The trial highlights the court’s reliance on spectral evidence and testimony from the afflicted girls as sufficient grounds for conviction.

Testimony continued against Mary Bradbury, with Ann Putnam, Junior, claiming Bradbury tormented her and others during her examination, even causing physical harm. Putnam also reported a vision of her deceased uncle, John Carr, accusing Bradbury of murder and demanding vengeance.

James Carr gave a deposition recounting an incident from approximately twenty years ago, involving a perceived slight and subsequent physical illness he attributed to the Bradburys. This deposition illustrates how past disagreements are now being interpreted through the lens of witchcraft.

The court continues to accept depositions detailing events from years, even decades, past as evidence of ongoing witchcraft. This reliance on historical grievances raises questions about the validity and fairness of the proceedings.

The afflicted girls continue to be central figures in the trials, with their accusations heavily influencing the court's decisions. Their claims of being tormented by the accused remain largely unchallenged.

With five already hanged last month, the pace of convictions and sentencing shows no sign of slowing. The community remains deeply divided, and fear continues to grip Salem Village.

newspapers.lede: The accusations continue to spread, now reaching into Salem Town. This morning, Henry Brag came before the magistrates claiming his son, William, was being afflicted by the touch of Hannah Carroll and Sarah Cole. This echoes the very first accusations here in Salem Village, where young girls began to cry out against others, claiming spectral torment. Brag’s testimony suggests a similar dynamic at play. As the magistrate’s record reads, Mary Marshall testified against Elizabeth Colson, claiming she was “knocking me downe Strikeing of me deafe and Dumm Tortering my body in most parts.” These reports of physical torment, whether real or perceived, fuel the growing hysteria. Meanwhile, Rebecca Jacobs faces continued scrutiny, with Elizabeth Hubbard repeating accusations against her. But a glimmer of reason appears in a petition from Rebecca Fox, Jacobs’s mother, who argues her daughter has been “broken & distracted in her mind” for over twelve years, questioning the validity of any confession made under such circumstances.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Town, accusations expanded beyond the initial circle of afflicted girls. Henry Brag accused Hannah Carroll and Sarah Cole, mirroring the pattern seen in Salem Village—a father acting on behalf of his child. Though these women were apprehended, they were quickly released and never formally tried.

Today in Salem Town, accusations expanded beyond the initial circle of afflicted girls. Henry Brag accused Hannah Carroll and Sarah Cole, mirroring the pattern seen in Salem Village—a father acting on behalf of his child. Though these women were apprehended, they were quickly released and never formally tried.

The accusations continue to spread, now reaching into Salem Town. This morning, Henry Brag came before the magistrates claiming his son, William, was being afflicted by the touch of Hannah Carroll and Sarah Cole. This echoes the very first accusations here in Salem Village, where young girls began to cry out against others, claiming spectral torment. Brag’s testimony suggests a similar dynamic at play. As the magistrate’s record reads, Mary Marshall testified against Elizabeth Colson, claiming she was “knocking me downe Strikeing of me deafe and Dumm Tortering my body in most parts.” These reports of physical torment, whether real or perceived, fuel the growing hysteria. Meanwhile, Rebecca Jacobs faces continued scrutiny, with Elizabeth Hubbard repeating accusations against her. But a glimmer of reason appears in a petition from Rebecca Fox, Jacobs’s mother, who argues her daughter has been “broken & distracted in her mind” for over twelve years, questioning the validity of any confession made under such circumstances.

As we discussed in the lede, accusations have broadened to include Hannah Carroll and Sarah Cole of Salem Town, both accused by Henry Brag on behalf of his son. They were apprehended but released without trial, a relatively uncommon outcome given the current climate.

Testimony continues to mount against those already accused. Mary Marshall detailed brutal attacks she claims Elizabeth Colson inflicted upon her, outlining specific injuries and claiming Colson even dislocated her shoulder.

William Arnold reported a curious incident involving Elizabeth Colson, stating she fled from officers attempting to arrest her and that his dog refused to approach her – a detail that could be interpreted as supernatural protection.

Elizabeth Hubbard reiterated her claims against Rebecca Jacobs, stating she witnessed Jacobs afflicting other girls during her earlier examination, and still feels her influence.

Rebecca Fox has formally petitioned the court on behalf of her daughter, Rebecca Jacobs, arguing that her daughter has suffered from mental distress for years and that any confession should be viewed with skepticism.

The afflicted girls continue to be central to the proceedings, their testimonies often driving the accusations and investigations, even as questions arise about the nature of their experiences.

newspapers.lede: This Thursday, the atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with religious fervor and fear. Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon centered on the condemnation of those found guilty of witchcraft, specifically focusing on Martha Corey. He publicly excommunicated Corey from the church, severing her spiritual ties to the community and solidifying her status as an outcast. Parris drew a connection between the accused and biblical warnings of evil, reading from Revelation: “These shall make War with the Lamb, & the Lamb shall overcome them.” This service wasn’t just a religious rite; it was a public affirmation of the court’s judgment, broadcast directly to the assembled parishioners. The pronouncements from the pulpit reinforced the belief that these trials were a righteous battle against the forces of darkness, and that the afflicted girls were instruments of God’s will. The gravity of the situation is made all the more stark by the recent executions, making this a day of both judgment and dread.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Reverend Samuel Parris publicly denounced and excommunicated Martha Corey from the church, following her conviction as a witch. This act, delivered from the pulpit, underscores the increasing severity of the trials and the deep entanglement of religious authority with the legal proceedings.

Today in Salem Village, Reverend Samuel Parris publicly denounced and excommunicated Martha Corey from the church, following her conviction as a witch. This act, delivered from the pulpit, underscores the increasing severity of the trials and the deep entanglement of religious authority with the legal proceedings.

This Thursday, the atmosphere in Salem Village is thick with religious fervor and fear. Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon centered on the condemnation of those found guilty of witchcraft, specifically focusing on Martha Corey. He publicly excommunicated Corey from the church, severing her spiritual ties to the community and solidifying her status as an outcast. Parris drew a connection between the accused and biblical warnings of evil, reading from Revelation: “These shall make War with the Lamb, & the Lamb shall overcome them.” This service wasn’t just a religious rite; it was a public affirmation of the court’s judgment, broadcast directly to the assembled parishioners. The pronouncements from the pulpit reinforced the belief that these trials were a righteous battle against the forces of darkness, and that the afflicted girls were instruments of God’s will. The gravity of the situation is made all the more stark by the recent executions, making this a day of both judgment and dread.

Reverend Parris’s excommunication of Martha Corey today represents a peak in the intertwining of religious and legal authority during the trials, demonstrating how the church actively endorsed and participated in the prosecution of the accused.

The recent executions continue to cast a long shadow over the village, with five people hanged on August nineteenth, including Reverend George Burroughs, a former minister of Salem Village.

The court's focus remains on identifying and punishing those believed to be practicing witchcraft, and the number of accusations and examinations continues to be high.

Testimony from the afflicted girls—Abigail Williams, Ann Putnam Jr., and others—remains central to the court's proceedings, despite growing skepticism from some observers.

Concern is growing among some in the Massachusetts Bay Colony regarding the use of spectral evidence, but the court has not yet indicated any intention to alter its evidentiary standards.

The local jail is overcrowded with the accused, many of whom have been imprisoned for months awaiting trial or sentencing.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Boston is tense this Friday, as news spreads of escapes from the jail. Captain John Alden Jr., a man of standing in the community, has fled to New York, aided by those who believe his imprisonment unjust. Alden, as many recall, was accused in May during a visit to Salem Village, the afflicted girls claiming he afflicted them despite not knowing him. The magistrate’s record shows the absurdity of the situation—bystanders had to *point out* which man was Alden before the girls could even pretend to recognize him. This escape, along with others—Edward and Sarah Bishop also broke jail this week—demonstrates a breakdown in public confidence and a growing resistance to the proceedings. As one observer noted, though not for publication, “It seems the walls of the jail are not as strong as the walls of silence surrounding these accusations.” Alden's flight is a clear signal that not everyone accepts the court’s judgments.
newspapers.summary: Today brings news of jailbreaks in Boston, with Captain John Alden Jr. among those escaping custody. This highlights growing doubts about the trials and the willingness of some to help the accused, even as the executions continue to cast a dark shadow over Massachusetts Bay.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today brings news of jailbreaks in Boston, with Captain John Alden Jr. among those escaping custody. This highlights growing doubts about the trials and the willingness of some to help the accused, even as the executions continue to cast a dark shadow over Massachusetts Bay.

The atmosphere in Boston is tense this Friday, as news spreads of escapes from the jail. Captain John Alden Jr., a man of standing in the community, has fled to New York, aided by those who believe his imprisonment unjust. Alden, as many recall, was accused in May during a visit to Salem Village, the afflicted girls claiming he afflicted them despite not knowing him. The magistrate’s record shows the absurdity of the situation—bystanders had to *point out* which man was Alden before the girls could even pretend to recognize him. This escape, along with others—Edward and Sarah Bishop also broke jail this week—demonstrates a breakdown in public confidence and a growing resistance to the proceedings. As one observer noted, though not for publication, “It seems the walls of the jail are not as strong as the walls of silence surrounding these accusations.” Alden's flight is a clear signal that not everyone accepts the court’s judgments.

Captain John Alden Jr. escaped from Boston jail today and is believed to be traveling to New York. His flight, facilitated by sympathetic individuals, underscores the increasing skepticism surrounding the witch trials and the willingness of some to defy the authorities.

Edward and Sarah Bishop also escaped from Boston jail this week, suggesting a coordinated effort or, at the very least, a shared desperation among those accused. This further strains the already overextended jail system and raises questions about security.

Jailkeeper John Arnold submitted an account for expenses today, detailing costs for chains, repairs to the jail, blankets, and the keeping of prisoners. The account, as documented in the records, lists payments for the care of Sarah Good, Sarah Osborne, Rebecca Nurse, and others, highlighting the financial burden the trials place on the colony.

The continued imprisonment of individuals like John Procter and his wife Elizabeth is noted in the jailkeeper’s account, indicating they remain in custody despite months having passed since their initial examinations.

While no executions are scheduled for today, the recent spate of hangings—five individuals were executed on August 19th—continues to weigh heavily on the community and fuel anxieties about the ongoing trials.

Susanna Martin of Amsbury and Bridget Bishop remain imprisoned, as indicated by the jailkeeper’s financial record, and their cases, like those of many others, remain unresolved.

newspapers.lede: The anxieties gripping Massachusetts Bay deepened today with the daring escape of Captain John Alden from a Boston jail. Alden, a veteran of King William’s War and son of the famed Mayflower passenger, had been arrested in late May after accusations surfaced during a visit to Boston. His flight, aided by friends, is a clear act of defiance against the court, and speaks to a growing distrust of the proceedings. As one observer noted, the accusations have become “a most terrible and grievous thing,” creating fear and uncertainty throughout the colony. But the accusations continue. Today, Joan Penney of Gloucester became the latest named in complaints—a pattern we’re seeing where accusations arise from pre-existing land disagreements. The afflicted girls, meanwhile, continue to testify against those accused, offering accounts of spectral attacks. According to the testimonies given today, Mary Walcott affirmed that Ann Foster of Andover “has afflicted her…by biting pinching & choaking of her.”
newspapers.summary: Today brings news of escape and accusation. Captain John Alden, a respected military officer, fled from a Boston jail where he was held on suspicion of witchcraft. Meanwhile, in Gloucester, Joan Penney has been accused, highlighting the spread of these charges beyond Salem Village and the role of local disputes.
newspapers.ttsFullText:

Meanwhile, in Gloucester, Joan Penney has been accused, highlighting the spread of these charges beyond Salem Village and the role of local disputes.
