Alden, a veteran of King William’s War and son of the famed Mayflower passenger, had been arrested in late May after accusations surfaced during a visit to Boston. His flight, aided by friends, is a clear act of defiance against the court, and speaks to a growing distrust of the proceedings. As one observer noted, the accusations have become “a most terrible and grievous thing,” creating fear and uncertainty throughout the colony. But the accusations continue. Today, Joan Penney of Gloucester became the latest named in complaints—a pattern we’re seeing where accusations arise from pre-existing land disagreements. The afflicted girls, meanwhile, continue to testify against those accused, offering accounts of spectral attacks. According to the testimonies given today, Mary Walcott affirmed that Ann Foster of Andover “has afflicted her…by biting pinching & choaking of her.”

Captain John Alden successfully escaped from jail in Boston, where he’d been held since May on charges related to witchcraft. His escape underscores the growing doubts surrounding the trials and the perceived injustice of the legal process.

Joan Penney of Gloucester was accused of witchcraft today by Zebulon Hill, a case that illustrates the spread of accusations beyond Salem Village and the influence of local disputes. Unlike many of the Salem cases, hers may not proceed to trial as the Court of Oyer and Terminer has been dissolved.

A summons was issued for witnesses to testify against Wilmot Redd, a woman already under indictment. The list of potential witnesses includes people from Marblehead, indicating the court is actively seeking testimony from a wider geographical area.

The afflicted girls—Mary Walcott, Mary Warren, and Elizabeth Hubbard—provided further testimony against Ann Foster of Andover, describing spectral attacks and affirming their belief she is a witch.

These accusations in Gloucester, like many others, appear rooted in neighborhood disagreements, suggesting that personal feuds may be fueling some of the charges leveled against individuals.

The court continues to rely heavily on spectral evidence—visions and experiences reported by the afflicted girls— despite growing concerns about its reliability and potential for manipulation.

newspapers.lede: This Sunday, the Salem Village Church witnessed a stark demonstration of spiritual judgment. Martha Corey, a woman who has maintained her innocence throughout the proceedings, was formally excommunicated by Reverend Samuel Parris before the assembled congregation. Excommunication, in the strict theology of the time, meant to cast Corey out of the community of believers—a spiritual death sentence. It’s a chilling prelude to her scheduled execution on September 22nd. As the magistrate’s record reads, accusations continue to mount against others as well. Testimony given today details afflictions experienced by young women, claiming spectral attacks and physical harm. Mary Warren, for example, affirmed to the grand inquest that Samuel Wardwell “hath often afflicted her,” and even afflicted her “now before the grand Inquest.” This pattern of testimony, relying heavily on what the accusers perceive as supernatural harm, fuels the ongoing crisis.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Martha Corey was formally excommunicated from the church, a severe spiritual punishment preceding her scheduled execution. Testimony continues against several accused, including Wilmott Redd and Samuel Wardwell, with accusers detailing afflictions and spectral evidence before the court. This escalation of accusations and condemnations marks a grim period in the trials.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, Martha Corey was formally excommunicated from the church, a severe spiritual punishment preceding her scheduled execution. Testimony continues against several accused, including Wilmott Redd and Samuel Wardwell, with accusers detailing afflictions and spectral evidence before the court. This escalation of accusations and condemnations marks a grim period in the trials.

This Sunday, the Salem Village Church witnessed a stark demonstration of spiritual judgment. Martha Corey, a woman who has maintained her innocence throughout the proceedings, was formally excommunicated by Reverend Samuel Parris before the assembled congregation. Excommunication, in the strict theology of the time, meant to cast Corey out of the community of believers—a spiritual death sentence. It’s a chilling prelude to her scheduled execution on September 22nd. As the magistrate’s record reads, accusations continue to mount against others as well. Testimony given today details afflictions experienced by young women, claiming spectral attacks and physical harm. Mary Warren, for example, affirmed to the grand inquest that Samuel Wardwell “hath often afflicted her,” and even afflicted her “now before the grand Inquest.” This pattern of testimony, relying heavily on what the accusers perceive as supernatural harm, fuels the ongoing crisis.

As we discussed, Martha Corey was excommunicated today, severing her ties to the church and, in the eyes of many, condemning her soul. This act underscores the intense religious fervor driving the trials and the belief that witchcraft is a profound spiritual offense demanding severe punishment.

Testimony was given against Wilmott Redd, claiming a curse placed on a neighbor. Sarah Dodd swore under oath that Redd wished ill upon Mrs. Simse, and shortly thereafter, “it fell out with Mrs. Simse…according s’d Redd’s wish.” This illustrates how seemingly minor disputes could be twisted into evidence of witchcraft.

Samuel Wardwell faced further accusations of witchcraft and physical assault. Martha Sprague testified that Wardwell pinched, stuck pins into, and struck her down, even while under warrant to appear in court. Multiple witnesses now claim to have seen Wardwell, or his “Apperition,” inflict harm on others.

Elizabeth Hubbard offered testimony against Sarah Buckley, stating that she witnessed Buckley’s “Apperance torment and afflete Mary Walcott and Ann Putnam” during Buckley’s examination in May. While Hubbard admits she cannot say Buckley ever directly harmed her, she firmly believes Buckley is a witch.

The court continues to rely heavily on the testimony of the afflicted girls, accepting their descriptions of invisible attacks as evidence. This reliance on spectral evidence—visions and apparitions—remains a central, and deeply problematic, aspect of the proceedings.

The pace of accusations and examinations remains relentless. With five already executed last month, the community is gripped by fear and suspicion, and the courts show no sign of slowing down.

newspapers.lede: The crisis in Salem Village took a dramatic turn this Monday as the afflicted girls named Sarah Noyes Hale, wife of Reverend John Hale of Beverly, as a witch. Hale, a minister who had previously lent his support to the court and attended examinations, now finds himself facing the unthinkable – the accusation of his own wife. This development is sending shockwaves through the community, as it demonstrates that the accusations are no longer limited to outsiders or those on the fringes of society. It is reaching into the very heart of the religious leadership. As the magistrate’s record reads from depositions taken today, accusations continue to mount against individuals in Gloucester and beyond, adding to the growing sense of panic. Hale, witnessing this firsthand, is reportedly devastated, realizing, according to those present, that if the system can identify an innocent woman like his wife as a witch, then the entire process is fatally flawed. This moment marks a turning point, as the foundations of the trials begin to crumble from within.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a chilling escalation to the Salem trials. Reverend John Hale’s wife, Sarah, has been accused of witchcraft, shaking the faith of one of the proceedings’ strongest early supporters. Alongside this, a flood of new accusations continues to spread fear through Gloucester and beyond, and a desperate plea for mercy emerges from condemned prisoner Mary Easty.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today brings a chilling escalation to the Salem trials. Reverend John Hale’s wife, Sarah, has been accused of witchcraft, shaking the faith of one of the proceedings’ strongest early supporters. Alongside this, a flood of new accusations continues to spread fear through Gloucester and beyond, and a desperate plea for mercy emerges from condemned prisoner Mary Easty.

The crisis in Salem Village took a dramatic turn this Monday as the afflicted girls named Sarah Noyes Hale, wife of Reverend John Hale of Beverly, as a witch. Hale, a minister who had previously lent his support to the court and attended examinations, now finds himself facing the unthinkable – the accusation of his own wife. This development is sending shockwaves through the community, as it demonstrates that the accusations are no longer limited to outsiders or those on the fringes of society. It is reaching into the very heart of the religious leadership. As the magistrate’s record reads from depositions taken today, accusations continue to mount against individuals in Gloucester and beyond, adding to the growing sense of panic. Hale, witnessing this firsthand, is reportedly devastated, realizing, according to those present, that if the system can identify an innocent woman like his wife as a witch, then the entire process is fatally flawed. This moment marks a turning point, as the foundations of the trials begin to crumble from within.

Reverend John Hale’s wife, Sarah Hale, was accused of witchcraft today, fundamentally altering the course of the trials. This accusation, coming after months of Hale's support for the proceedings, has shaken his belief in the process and will likely lead to a significant shift in his position.

Mary Easty, facing execution, penned a powerful petition not for her own life, but for the lives of others. She wrote, “I petition your honours not for my own life for I know I must die and my appointed time is set but the Lord he knows it is that no more innocent blood be shed.” This selfless plea highlights the growing awareness of the injustice unfolding in Salem.

Accusations have expanded beyond Salem Village to include several women in Gloucester. Ann Dolliver, daughter of magistrate Reverend John Higginson, Mary Rowe, Phoebe Day, Rachel Vinson, and Sarah Parker were all named by the afflicted girls today.

Captain John Alden Jr., son of the Mayflower passenger, escaped from jail today after being held for fifteen weeks. He reportedly denounced the accusers as “a company of poor distracted, or possessed creatures.”

Mary Bradbury, convicted of witchcraft on September 6th, managed to escape before her execution. Reports suggest her husband bribed the jailer and transported her to Maine.

Elizabeth Proctor, convicted but reprieved due to her pregnancy, is experiencing the full consequences of the court’s judgment. Even though she will not be executed immediately, her property has been seized, leaving her and her children destitute.

Depositions from Rowley detail further accusations against Margaret Scott, including claims of physical torment and involvement in the deaths of livestock. Witnesses testified to unusual feats of strength, like lifting a full barrel of molasses with only two fingers, further fueling suspicions.

The deposition of Thomas Greenslit corroborates the claims of extraordinary strength attributed to George Burroughs, detailing incidents from years prior during the Indian Wars.

newspapers.lede: The pattern of accusation broadened significantly today, reaching into the homes of families in Andover and demonstrating a disturbing willingness to accept the testimony of children against their parents. Nine-year-old Abigail Faulkner Jr. and her sister Dorothy were coerced into implicating their mother, Abigail Faulkner Sr. This tactic, forcing children to accuse, is becoming tragically common in the Andover examinations. Simultaneously, three Tyler sisters – Hannah, Martha, and Joannah – faced accusations based on touch tests conducted by Ralph Farnum and Hannah Foster. The youngest, eleven-year-old Joannah Tyler, provided a detailed, though clearly coerced, account of her involvement with witchcraft during her examination today. As the magistrate’s record reads, she claimed, “Good'e falkner pe'swaded her first & the black Man w'th her & he asked me if I would Sett my hand to his book…” This revelation underscores the intense pressure applied to these young accusers and the fantastical narrative being constructed around them. Adding to the day’s turmoil, Captain John Alden Jr., who had been held on suspicion of involvement in the recent ransoming of captives, managed to escape from jail in Boston and is believed to have fled to New York.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village and Andover, the trials continue to ensnare even the youngest members of the community. Three Tyler sisters and nine-year-old Abigail Faulkner Jr. have been accused, with children pressured to testify against their own parents. Meanwhile, Captain John Alden Jr. has escaped custody, seeking refuge in New York.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village and Andover, the trials continue to ensnare even the youngest members of the community. Three Tyler sisters and nine-year-old Abigail Faulkner Jr. have been accused, with children pressured to testify against their own parents. Meanwhile, Captain John Alden Jr. has escaped custody, seeking refuge in New York.

The pattern of accusation broadened significantly today, reaching into the homes of families in Andover and demonstrating a disturbing willingness to accept the testimony of children against their parents. Nine-year-old Abigail Faulkner Jr. and her sister Dorothy were coerced into implicating their mother, Abigail Faulkner Sr. This tactic, forcing children to accuse, is becoming tragically common in the Andover examinations. Simultaneously, three Tyler sisters – Hannah, Martha, and Joannah – faced accusations based on touch tests conducted by Ralph Farnum and Hannah Foster. The youngest, eleven-year-old Joannah Tyler, provided a detailed, though clearly coerced, account of her involvement with witchcraft during her examination today. As the magistrate’s record reads, she claimed, “Good'e falkner pe'swaded her first & the black Man w'th her & he asked me if I would Sett my hand to his book…” This revelation underscores the intense pressure applied to these young accusers and the fantastical narrative being constructed around them. Adding to the day’s turmoil, Captain John Alden Jr., who had been held on suspicion of involvement in the recent ransoming of captives, managed to escape from jail in Boston and is believed to have fled to New York.

As described in the lede, the accusations against Abigail Faulkner Jr. and the Tyler sisters highlight a dangerous trend: the use of child testimony to condemn parents and the acceptance of accusations based on flimsy evidence like touch tests. These events in Andover reveal the accelerating hysteria gripping the region.

Captain John Alden Jr.’s escape from Boston jail is a striking development. He was accused while engaged in the difficult task of negotiating the release of captives taken during the raid on York, and his flight suggests a lack of confidence in the legal process.

The examination of Joannah Tyler offers a chilling glimpse into the interrogation techniques used by the magistrates. Her confession, detailing promises of fine clothes and eternal servitude to the Devil, appears to be the result of intense pressure and leading questions.

The presence of “Goody Faulkner” – likely Goodwife Faulkner, the mother of Abigail Faulkner Jr. and Dorothy – as a figure in Joannah Tyler’s alleged vision suggests a deliberate effort to connect different accused individuals and bolster the case for widespread witchcraft.

The fact that all three Tyler sisters confessed, despite later being found not guilty, demonstrates the overwhelming pressure to confess and the difficulty of resisting the accusations once they were made.

This Tuesday marks a continuation of the peak execution period that began last month with the hanging of several individuals, including Reverend George Burroughs, further fueling the fear and panic within the community.

newspapers.lede: The weight of accusation fell heavily on the Faulkner family of Andover today. Abigail Faulkner Sr., age forty, was convicted of witchcraft by the Court of Oyer and Terminer, but her punishment was stayed due to her pregnancy. This highlights a grim paradox of the trials: pregnancy offered a temporary reprieve, though it did not erase the charge. As the depositions reveal, the tragedy extends to her children. Twelve-year-old Dorothy Faulkner confessed to the crime, and court records show she was “persuaded” to accuse her own mother. According to a summary of the examinations, Dorothy and her younger sister Abigail Jr., along with Martha Tyler, Johannah Tyler, Sarah Wilson Jr., and Joseph Draper, all “confessed” to being led into witchcraft “by hir meanse” – by Abigail Faulkner Sr. The desperation within this family, and the way children were drawn into the accusations, is a harrowing illustration of the crisis gripping our communities. Rose Foster testified that she had been “most greviously afflected and tormented by Abigail Falkner,” a claim repeated by multiple afflicted individuals throughout the day.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village and Andover, the accusations continue to spread, ensnaring entire families. Abigail Faulkner was convicted despite being pregnant, while her twelve-year-old daughter Dorothy confessed, seemingly coerced into condemning her own mother. Several others also faced conviction, and one man, Giles Corey, chose a horrific fate to protect his family’s estate.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village and Andover, the accusations continue to spread, ensnaring entire families. Abigail Faulkner was convicted despite being pregnant, while her twelve-year-old daughter Dorothy confessed, seemingly coerced into condemning her own mother. Several others also faced conviction, and one man, Giles Corey, chose a horrific fate to protect his family’s estate.

The weight of accusation fell heavily on the Faulkner family of Andover today. Abigail Faulkner Sr., age forty, was convicted of witchcraft by the Court of Oyer and Terminer, but her punishment was stayed due to her pregnancy. This highlights a grim paradox of the trials: pregnancy offered a temporary reprieve, though it did not erase the charge. As the depositions reveal, the tragedy extends to her children. Twelve-year-old Dorothy Faulkner confessed to the crime, and court records show she was “persuaded” to accuse her own mother. According to a summary of the examinations, Dorothy and her younger sister Abigail Jr., along with Martha Tyler, Johannah Tyler, Sarah Wilson Jr., and Joseph Draper, all “confessed” to being led into witchcraft “by hir meanse” – by Abigail Faulkner Sr. The desperation within this family, and the way children were drawn into the accusations, is a harrowing illustration of the crisis gripping our communities. Rose Foster testified that she had been “most greviously afflected and tormented by Abigail Falkner,” a claim repeated by multiple afflicted individuals throughout the day.

As the lede explained, Abigail Faulkner Sr. was convicted of witchcraft today but will not be immediately executed because she is pregnant with her seventh child. Her case underscores the arbitrary nature of survival during the trials; while pregnancy offered a temporary stay of execution, it did not absolve her of guilt in the eyes of the court, and her neighbors faced the gallows on the same evidence.

Giles Corey refused to enter a plea today, choosing instead to face the ordeal of peine forte et dure—pressing with stones—rather than allow the court to seize his property. This defiant act, though ultimately fatal, was a calculated attempt to protect his estate for his sons-in-law.

Dorothy Faulkner, only twelve years old, was accused and confessed today, and court records indicate she was strongly influenced to condemn her mother, Abigail Faulkner Sr. This is one of the most disturbing instances of children being used to bolster accusations against their parents.

Sarah Wilson Sr. and Sarah Wilson Jr. of Andover were both accused by the afflicted girls today, adding another mother-daughter pair to the growing list of families torn apart by these accusations. Both women confessed, and their testimonies will likely be used against others.

Mary Parker was convicted of witchcraft today despite consistently proclaiming her innocence, stating plainly, “I know nothing of witchcraft.” Her steadfast denial, unlike the confessions of others, offered her no protection.

Margaret Scott, at seventy-seven years of age, was also convicted today, making her the oldest person condemned in these proceedings. She faces execution alongside Parker and others.

There was documented confusion during Mary Parker’s trial between her and another woman named Alice Parker, raising concerns about the reliability of the proceedings and the court’s ability to keep track of individual cases amidst the escalating number of accusations.

Several depositions were taken today from afflicted individuals like Rose Foster and Ann Putnam Jr., all detailing how Abigail Faulkner Sr. had allegedly tormented them through witchcraft, further solidifying the case against her in the court’s eyes.

newspapers.lede: The shadow of Gallows Hill looms large over Salem Village this Thursday. Yesterday, the church officially cast out Giles Corey, severing him from the covenant community before his scheduled pressing tomorrow. This act of excommunication, performed while Corey remained defiant and refused to plead to the charges against him, highlights the absolute power wielded by both the church and the courts. As the magistrate’s record shows, Corey’s silence was interpreted not as innocence, but as contempt. He refused to answer questions, preferring to bear the weight of the stones rather than offer a defense. The Reverend Samuel Parris noted, in a letter to a colleague, that Corey’s obstinacy was “a most grievous affront to the Court of Heaven, and a demonstration of his utter wickedness.” This rejection from the church, coupled with the brutal sentence of pressing, reveals a justice system that offered little room for mercy or due process. Corey, like his wife Martha before him, faced death not only as an accused witch, but as an outcast from God’s grace.
newspapers.summary: Today brings news of deepening tragedy as Giles Corey was excommunicated from the Salem Village church just one day before his scheduled pressing. This act of spiritual condemnation, combined with his impending physical punishment, underscores the complete rejection of Corey by both religious and legal authorities.

Corey, like his wife Martha before him, faced death not only as an accused witch, but as an outcast from God’s grace.

Yesterday, Giles Corey was excommunicated by the Salem Village church, a final condemnation before his pressing tomorrow. This act demonstrates the complete rejection of Corey by both the religious and legal authorities, emphasizing the severity of the accusations against him and the inflexible nature of the proceedings.

The sentence of pressing itself remains a grim spectacle. It was imposed upon Corey because he refused to enter a plea, a right afforded to all defendants, but one he chose to forfeit in his silence.

Corey’s wife, Martha, was herself excommunicated on September fourteenth, indicating a pattern of religious condemnation alongside legal prosecution.

The continued executions and imprisonments are straining the resources of the local jail and the patience of some villagers, though open dissent remains rare.

The court is scheduled to hear further examinations in Salem Town tomorrow, and a large crowd is expected to gather.

Rumors persist of more accusations being prepared, extending the reach of the trials beyond the immediate families already entangled in the crisis.

newspapers.lede: Today, the unthinkable happened in the fields near the Salem jail. Giles Corey, eighty-one years old and accused of witchcraft, was pressed to death. Corey refused to enter a plea of guilty or not guilty, a tactic that, under English law, prevented any trial and protected his estate from seizure by Sheriff Corwin. For two days, stones were piled upon his chest, increasing in weight with each refusal to speak. Witnesses report his final words were simply, “More weight.” As Robert Calef recorded, “Giles Corey’s tongue was pressed out of his mouth; the Sheriff, with his cane, forced it in again.” This barbarous act demonstrates a chilling intersection of legal procedure, personal greed, and unwavering belief in spectral evidence. It’s a day that will forever stain the history of Salem Village, and raises serious questions about the justice being meted out here.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, an unthinkable act occurred. Eighty-one-year-old Giles Corey was pressed to death for refusing to enter a plea in his witchcraft trial. Corey’s silence, a calculated move to protect his family’s property, marks a dark turn in the trials and highlights the troubling financial motivations at play.

Today in Salem Village, an unthinkable act occurred. Eighty-one-year-old Giles Corey was pressed to death for refusing to enter a plea in his witchcraft trial. Corey’s silence, a calculated move to protect his family’s property, marks a dark turn in the trials and highlights the troubling financial motivations at play.

Today, the unthinkable happened in the fields near the Salem jail. Giles Corey, eighty-one years old and accused of witchcraft, was pressed to death. Corey refused to enter a plea of guilty or not guilty, a tactic that, under English law, prevented any trial and protected his estate from seizure by Sheriff Corwin. For two days, stones were piled upon his chest, increasing in weight with each refusal to speak. Witnesses report his final words were simply, “More weight.” As Robert Calef recorded, “Giles Corey’s tongue was pressed out of his mouth; the Sheriff, with his cane, forced it in again.” This barbarous act demonstrates a chilling intersection of legal procedure, personal greed, and unwavering belief in spectral evidence. It’s a day that will forever stain the history of Salem Village, and raises serious questions about the justice being meted out here.

As we heard in the lede, Giles Corey died today after refusing to enter a plea. By remaining silent, Corey strategically prevented a conviction, ensuring his farm would pass to his sons-in-law and not be seized by Sheriff Corwin. He endured two to three days of agonizing pressure for this outcome.

The circumstances surrounding Corey’s death are particularly grim. He was stripped naked, a board placed on his chest, and stones added incrementally. Witnesses claim he repeatedly demanded "More weight!" as the pressure intensified.

This wasn't an isolated event divorced from Corey’s past. In 1676, he was convicted of beating his indentured servant, Jacob Goodale, to death with a stick—though he avoided a murder charge. Thomas Putnam has now brought this incident forward, claiming a specter revealed Corey’s past crime.

Sheriff George Corwin appears to have a clear financial interest in these convictions. He has been seizing the property of accused witches—livestock, food, furniture—and profiting directly from their misfortune. After Corey’s death, he went to the farm to claim payment for jail expenses.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer, created specifically to try these cases, is comprised of the colony’s elite. Six of the nine judges are related by marriage, raising concerns about impartiality and the potential for collusion.

Samuel Sewall, one of the judges, noted in his diary that “much pains was used with him two days,” seemingly downplaying the horrific nature of the ordeal and framing it as a simple matter of persuasion.

Corey’s wife, Martha, is currently awaiting her own execution, scheduled for three days from now on September twenty-second. The tragedy of this family is heartbreaking, and underscores the devastating impact of these trials on individuals and families.

The legal basis for pressing Corey—peine forte et dure—allowed the court to compel a plea through physical torture. By refusing to plead, Corey understood he was choosing a gruesome death to protect his family’s legacy.

newspapers.lede: The weight of these trials is felt acutely today, not just in the preparations for another execution, but in the words of a woman facing death herself. Mary Easty, imprisoned and awaiting the noose on September twenty-second, wrote a petition to the court, a document considered by many to be the most poignant of the entire period. Remarkably, she doesn’t ask for her own life to be spared. Instead, she implores the magistrates to consider the possibility of error, writing, “I petition your Honours not for my own life, for I know I must die… but if it be possible, that no more innocent blood be shed.” This plea, coming from a condemned woman, speaks volumes about the growing unease surrounding these proceedings. Alongside this, a warrant for the arrest of Joan Penny, a widow from Gloucester, was issued today, accusing her of witchcraft against Mary Hill of Salem. The accusations continue, and the cycle of fear and accusation shows no signs of abating.
newspapers.summary: Today, as the hangings continue, Mary Easty, facing her own execution in just two days, penned a remarkable petition. She didn’t plead for her life, but for an end to the trials themselves, fearing more innocent people would be condemned. Simultaneously, a warrant was issued for the arrest of Joan Penny of Gloucester, accused of witchcraft against Mary Hill.

Today, as the hangings continue, Mary Easty, facing her own execution in just two days, penned a remarkable petition. She didn’t plead for her life, but for an end to the trials themselves, fearing more innocent people would be condemned. Simultaneously, a warrant was issued for the arrest of Joan Penny of Gloucester, accused of witchcraft against Mary Hill.

Mary Easty, imprisoned and awaiting the noose on September twenty-second, wrote a petition to the court, a document considered by many to be the most poignant of the entire period. Remarkably, she doesn’t ask for her own life to be spared. Instead, she implores the magistrates to consider the possibility of error, writing, “I petition your Honours not for my own life, for I know I must die… but if it be possible, that no more innocent blood be shed.” This plea, coming from a condemned woman, speaks volumes about the growing unease surrounding these proceedings. Alongside this, a warrant for the arrest of Joan Penny, a widow from Gloucester, was issued today, accusing her of witchcraft against Mary Hill of Salem. The accusations continue, and the cycle of fear and accusation shows no signs of abating.

As we discussed earlier, Mary Easty wrote a moving petition from her cell today, not seeking to save herself, but to prevent further bloodshed. Her words highlight a growing awareness of the possibility of condemning innocent individuals, and it's a testament to her character that her final thoughts were for others facing similar accusations.

A warrant was issued today for the arrest of Joan Penny, a widow from Gloucester, based on the complaint of Zebulon Hill. The charge, as the magistrate’s record reads, is that she “feloniously committed Severall acts of witchcraft” against Mary Hill, causing her “great hurt & Torment.”

The court acted swiftly on the complaint against Penny, with Constable John Chote of Ipswich reporting that he has seized her and brought her to Salem to face examination. This demonstrates the speed with which accusations are pursued and individuals are brought into the legal process.

The warrant for Penny’s arrest was signed by several magistrates including Bartholomew Gedney, John Hathorne, Jonathan Corwin, and John Higginson, underlining the broad support for these investigations within the local authorities.

While no executions occurred today, the atmosphere remains heavy with anticipation, as Mary Easty’s impending execution looms over the village and the petition serves as a haunting reminder of the human cost of these trials.

The continued issuance of warrants, like the one for Joan Penny, indicates that the number of accusations is not diminishing, despite the recent hangings and growing doubts among some observers.

newspapers.lede: The court met today to examine Joan Penny, another woman accused of afflicting others with witchcraft. The magistrates, seeking evidence of guilt, instructed her to recite the Lord’s Prayer. As the magistrate's record reads, Penny progressed well until reaching the petition for forgiveness. At the line, “...forgive us our trespasses as we forgive them that trespass against us,” she interjected with a simple, yet telling phrase: “so do I.” This addition, a clear statement of her own willingness to offer forgiveness, was considered remarkable – not as a sign of piety, but as an oddity in the context of the accusations against her. It’s a detail that, while seemingly minor, speaks to the intense scrutiny and peculiar interpretations of everyday actions happening now. The court continued with further questioning, attempting to discern whether this response indicated a pact with the Devil or simply a stubborn spirit.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the court continued examinations, focusing on Joan Penny. While accused of witchcraft, her ability to recite the Lord’s Prayer without error—except for a pointed addition at a key phrase—was noted by the magistrates and recorded for posterity. This small detail highlights the strange logic at play during these trials.

This small detail highlights the strange logic at play during these trials.

The court met today to examine Joan Penny, another woman accused of afflicting others with witchcraft. The magistrates, seeking evidence of guilt, instructed her to recite the Lord’s Prayer. As the magistrate's record reads, Penny progressed well until reaching the petition for forgiveness. At the line, “...forgive us our trespasses as we forgive them that trespass against us,” she interjected with a simple, yet telling phrase: “so do I.” This addition, a clear statement of her own willingness to offer forgiveness, was considered remarkable – not as a sign of piety, but as an oddity in the context of the accusations against her. It’s a detail that, while seemingly minor, speaks to the intense scrutiny and peculiar interpretations of everyday actions happening now. The court continued with further questioning, attempting to discern whether this response indicated a pact with the Devil or simply a stubborn spirit.

Joan Penny’s examination dominated today’s proceedings, with the court fixating on her recitation of the Lord’s Prayer and her pointed addition of “so do I” when asking for forgiveness. This moment was deemed “remarkable” by the magistrates, further fueling the investigation into her alleged witchcraft.

The recent wave of executions continues to cast a pall over Salem Village. Five individuals, including Reverend George Burroughs, were hanged on August 19th, and fear remains high that more will follow.

Though no new arrests were made today, multiple individuals remain imprisoned, awaiting examination or trial. The jail is reportedly overcrowded and conditions are worsening.

Local ministers continue to preach sermons emphasizing the dangers of witchcraft and the importance of rooting out evil from the community, further heightening public anxiety.

Several witnesses have traveled to Salem Village from neighboring towns to offer testimony, contributing to the growing body of evidence—and accusations—surrounding the alleged witchcraft.

There is growing, though quiet, discussion among some residents regarding the reliability of spectral evidence, but these concerns are largely suppressed by the authority of the court and the ministers.

newspapers.lede: Today, on September 22nd, 1692, the hangman’s noose fell on eight more accused witches at Gallows Hill. The largest single execution of the trials took place during the height of the harvest season, a grim counterpoint to the labor of bringing in the crops. Among those executed was Martha Corey, a seventy-two-year-old woman who, even in the face of death, maintained her sharp wit and challenged her accusers. As the magistrate’s record shows, she was labeled a “gospel woman” by some, yet the afflicted girls claimed she was a “gospel witch.” Mary Easty, a farmer’s wife from Topsfield, penned a moving farewell petition from prison, pleading “I petition your honours not for my own life, for I know I must die… but that no more innocent blood be shed.” Her words stand as a heartbreaking testament to the injustice of these proceedings. The executions concluded today, but the repercussions will echo through this community for generations.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a tragic end to the Salem witch trials. Eight people – Martha Corey, Mary Easty, Alice Parker, Ann Pudeator, Margaret Scott, Wilmot Reed, Samuel Wardwell, and Mary Parker – were hanged at Gallows Hill, the largest single-day execution of the crisis. This brings the total number of deaths by hanging to nineteen, and concludes the executions, though the legal ramifications will continue.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a tragic end to the Salem witch trials. Eight people – Martha Corey, Mary Easty, Alice Parker, Ann Pudeator, Margaret Scott, Wilmot Reed, Samuel Wardwell, and Mary Parker – were hanged at Gallows Hill, the largest single-day execution of the crisis. This brings the total number of deaths by hanging to nineteen, and concludes the executions, though the legal ramifications will continue.

Today, on September 22nd, 1692, the hangman’s noose fell on eight more accused witches at Gallows Hill. The largest single execution of the trials took place during the height of the harvest season, a grim counterpoint to the labor of bringing in the crops. Among those executed was Martha Corey, a seventy-two-year-old woman who, even in the face of death, maintained her sharp wit and challenged her accusers. As the magistrate’s record shows, she was labeled a “gospel woman” by some, yet the afflicted girls claimed she was a “gospel witch.” Mary Easty, a farmer’s wife from Topsfield, penned a moving farewell petition from prison, pleading “I petition your honours not for my own life, for I know I must die… but that no more innocent blood be shed.” Her words stand as a heartbreaking testament to the injustice of these proceedings. The executions concluded today, but the repercussions will echo through this community for generations.

Eight individuals – Martha Corey, Mary Easty, Alice Parker, Ann Pudeator, Margaret Scott, Wilmot Redd, Samuel Wardwell, and Mary Parker – were hanged today at Gallows Hill, marking the largest single execution of the Salem witch trials and bringing the total number of deaths by hanging to nineteen.

Mary Easty’s farewell petition, written from prison, is considered one of the most eloquent and humane documents of the entire crisis, as she pleaded not for her own life, but for an end to the bloodshed.

Ann Pudeator, a Salem Town nurse and midwife, had her medical supplies confiscated and presented as evidence of witchcraft, demonstrating how the trials weaponized everyday objects and reframed healing practices as demonic.

Samuel Wardwell of Andover confessed to witchcraft, then recanted his confession, but was executed nonetheless, highlighting the deadly trap of confession and recantation during the trials.

Rev. Nicholas Noyes, standing at the foot of the gallows, famously declared the eight executed individuals “eight firebrands of Hell,” revealing the prosecutors’ unwavering conviction even as doubts began to grow.

The execution of Margaret Scott, a 77-year-old impoverished widow, illustrates how the trials preyed on the vulnerable and isolated members of the community, as she had no family or neighbors to petition on her behalf.

Alice Parker’s case is significant because she was a Salem Town resident with no connection to the internal disputes of Salem Village, demonstrating how the accusations spread beyond local conflicts.

Mercy Wardwell’s attempt to save her father, Samuel, by falsely accusing Mary Parker tragically failed, resulting in both their executions – a grim example of the devastating consequences of coerced confessions.

newspapers.lede: The fallout from the recent executions continues to ripple through Essex County. Just three days after Mary Parker of Andover was hanged as a witch, Sheriff Corwin dispatched an officer to her farm to seize her remaining property. This included corn, hay, and cattle – essential provisions for her sons and their families as winter approaches. According to reports, Parker’s sons claimed there was little left to take. Whether this was due to genuine poverty, or a desperate attempt to protect what little they had by concealing it, remains unclear. This seizure highlights a disturbing pattern: the court isn’t simply condemning individuals, but dismantling their lives and leaving their families destitute. As the magistrate’s record of similar cases shows, the authorities seem intent on stripping the accused and their kin of everything, regardless of their wealth or need. One can only imagine the desperation felt by those left behind, facing starvation with the cold months ahead.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the consequences of the trials expanded beyond the accused to their families. The farm of recently executed Mary Parker was seized, revealing the court’s reach extended to confiscating even the meager possessions of the condemned, regardless of their financial standing. This underscores the devastating impact of the trials on ordinary people.

The farm of recently executed Mary Parker was seized, revealing the court’s reach extended to confiscating even the meager possessions of the condemned, regardless of their financial standing. This underscores the devastating impact of the trials on ordinary people.

The fallout from the recent executions continues to ripple through Essex County. Just three days after Mary Parker of Andover was hanged as a witch, Sheriff Corwin dispatched an officer to her farm to seize her remaining property. This included corn, hay, and cattle – essential provisions for her sons and their families as winter approaches. According to reports, Parker’s sons claimed there was little left to take. Whether this was due to genuine poverty, or a desperate attempt to protect what little they had by concealing it, remains unclear. This seizure highlights a disturbing pattern: the court isn’t simply condemning individuals, but dismantling their lives and leaving their families destitute. As the magistrate’s record of similar cases shows, the authorities seem intent on stripping the accused and their kin of everything, regardless of their wealth or need. One can only imagine the desperation felt by those left behind, facing starvation with the cold months ahead.

The seizure of Mary Parker’s farm after her execution demonstrates the court’s willingness to confiscate the property of the accused, even those with limited means, leaving her sons to face hardship this winter.

Mary Parker was a widow from Andover, not a prominent merchant or landowner in Salem Village, indicating the trials targeted people from all walks of life, not just the wealthy or influential.

The speed with which the seizure occurred – only three days after the execution – shows how efficiently the court’s orders were carried out, and how little time families had to prepare for the loss of their livelihood.

The sons’ claim of having nothing of value raises questions about whether they attempted to hide assets to protect their family, or if they truly possessed very little to begin with.

This case, like others, underscores the broader economic impact of the witch trials, disrupting families and communities beyond the immediate condemnation of the accused.

newspapers.lede: The crisis continues, and today’s developments show the trials’ reach extending beyond accusations and trials into the daily lives of families. Selectmen from Andover have formally petitioned the court at Ipswich, seeking guidance on how to care for the children of Samuel and Elizabeth Wardwell, who were recently imprisoned on charges of witchcraft. The petition details the children’s vulnerability, stating they are “incapable of provideing for themselves, and are now in a suffering condition.” The Selectmen ask the court to allow them to place the children with families who can offer care and to access the Wardwells’ estate to provide for their immediate needs. As the petition reads, the town officials humbly request the court’s direction, hoping to “inform us what is our duty in this case, and to give … order so to dispose of them that their necessityes may be releived.” This plea underscores the widening consequences of the accusations, affecting not just the accused, but also their innocent children.
newspapers.summary: Today in Andover, town officials petitioned the court regarding the welfare of the children of Samuel and Elizabeth Wardwell, recently arrested for witchcraft. This highlights the broader social impact of the trials, as families are torn apart and the community struggles to care for those left behind.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Andover, town officials petitioned the court regarding the welfare of the children of Samuel and Elizabeth Wardwell, recently arrested for witchcraft. This highlights the broader social impact of the trials, as families are torn apart and the community struggles to care for those left behind.

The crisis continues, and today’s developments show the trials’ reach extending beyond accusations and trials into the daily lives of families. Selectmen from Andover have formally petitioned the court at Ipswich, seeking guidance on how to care for the children of Samuel and Elizabeth Wardwell, who were recently imprisoned on charges of witchcraft. The petition details the children’s vulnerability, stating they are “incapable of provideing for themselves, and are now in a suffering condition.” The Selectmen ask the court to allow them to place the children with families who can offer care and to access the Wardwells’ estate to provide for their immediate needs. As the petition reads, the town officials humbly request the court’s direction, hoping to “inform us what is our duty in this case, and to give … order so to dispose of them that their necessityes may be releived.” This plea underscores the widening consequences of the accusations, affecting not just the accused, but also their innocent children.

The Andover Selectmen formally petitioned the court today, requesting instructions on how to care for the children of Samuel and Elizabeth Wardwell, who are currently imprisoned on charges of witchcraft, leaving the children destitute and in need of support. This highlights the growing social disruption caused by the trials and the strain placed on communities to care for those displaced by the accusations.

The court is currently sitting in Ipswich, continuing examinations and hearings related to the ongoing cases. Though no executions are scheduled for today, the atmosphere remains tense as more accusations surface.

Concern for the well-being of children orphaned or left destitute by the trials is growing, and the Andover petition may set a precedent for other towns facing similar situations.

The magistrate’s record shows a continued pattern of spectral evidence being admitted in court, relying heavily on the afflicted individuals’ visions and claims of being tormented by the spirits of the accused.

Several individuals remain imprisoned, awaiting trial or sentencing, including Rebecca Nurse, Martha Carrier, and John Proctor, whose cases have garnered significant attention.

While the number of new accusations has slowed, fear and suspicion still permeate the community, and the possibility of further arrests remains a constant threat.

newspapers.lede: After months of escalating accusations and executions, Governor William Phips intervened in the witchcraft trials today. He issued a directive restricting the admissibility of spectral evidence—testimony based on visions or dreams of the accused tormenting the afflicted. For weeks, the courts had relied heavily on these visions, accepting them as proof of guilt. But mounting doubts about their reliability, coupled with growing concerns about the fairness of the proceedings, finally prompted the Governor to act. As the magistrate’s record shows, Phips stated that he was "much disturbed to see such evidence admitted," and feared it was leading to unjust convictions. This new restriction doesn’t overturn past verdicts, but it effectively halts new arrests, as accusations now require corroborating physical evidence. The atmosphere here in Salem Village is notably different this evening—a hesitant relief mixed with lingering fear.
newspapers.summary: Governor William Phips took a significant step today, restricting the use of spectral evidence in witchcraft trials. This means accusations based solely on visions or dreams will no longer be accepted in court, effectively ending new arrests in Salem Village and Salem Town. This decision marks a turning point in the trials.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Governor William Phips took a significant step today, restricting the use of spectral evidence in witchcraft trials. This means accusations based solely on visions or dreams will no longer be accepted in court, effectively ending new arrests in Salem Village and Salem Town. This decision marks a turning point in the trials.

After months of escalating accusations and executions, Governor William Phips intervened in the witchcraft trials today. He issued a directive restricting the admissibility of spectral evidence—testimony based on visions or dreams of the accused tormenting the afflicted. For weeks, the courts had relied heavily on these visions, accepting them as proof of guilt. But mounting doubts about their reliability, coupled with growing concerns about the fairness of the proceedings, finally prompted the Governor to act. As the magistrate’s record shows, Phips stated that he was "much disturbed to see such evidence admitted," and feared it was leading to unjust convictions. This new restriction doesn’t overturn past verdicts, but it effectively halts new arrests, as accusations now require corroborating physical evidence. The atmosphere here in Salem Village is notably different this evening—a hesitant relief mixed with lingering fear.

Governor Phips’s decision to restrict spectral evidence is the most important development of this day, effectively ending the wave of new arrests that have gripped Salem Village and Salem Town. The courts will now require more than just visions or dreams to convict someone of witchcraft.

While the new order stops further arrests based on spectral evidence alone, it does not retroactively invalidate the convictions of those already condemned. The fates of those currently imprisoned, and those already executed, remain unchanged.

The restriction comes after five people were hanged in August, including Reverend George Burroughs, a respected minister from Salem Village, and marks a growing awareness of the dangers of relying on unreliable testimony.

The afflicted girls, including Abigail Williams and Ann Putnam Jr., continue to exhibit their fits, but without the ability to support their accusations with spectral evidence, the court’s attention is shifting.

The General Court is scheduled to meet next month, and there is speculation that the recent developments may be discussed, potentially leading to a broader review of the trials.

Despite the Governor’s actions, fear and suspicion still permeate the community, and many remain convinced that witchcraft is a genuine threat.

newspapers.lede: The air in Salem Village feels different today. The Court of Oyer and Terminer, the body responsible for prosecuting these cases of witchcraft, is effectively finished. Governor Phips, facing mounting pressure and even accusations leveled at his own wife, has begun to dismantle the legal framework that fueled this hysteria. Lady Mary Phips, the governor’s wife, was “cried out upon” by one of the afflicted girls, a development that struck many as the final, undeniable proof that the accusations had spiraled out of control. As the magistrate’s record reads from a complaint filed today, Mary Brown of Reading details her torment, claiming Sarah Cole of Lynn has “threatened me sorely what Shee would do to me” and caused her “grat paine.” But these complaints, once readily accepted, are now meeting with skepticism and doubt. The accusations continue, but the court that once received them is no more. The long, dark days of examination and conviction are drawing to a close, leaving behind a community fractured and deeply wounded.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in Salem. The Court of Oyer and Terminer is dissolving, and while 150 accused remain imprisoned, no new trials will be held. Key figures like Mary Warren and Mercy Lewis are withdrawing from the drama, while others, like Thomas Putnam, find their power rapidly eroding. The crisis is ending, but the scars will remain.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a turning point in Salem. The Court of Oyer and Terminer is dissolving, and while 150 accused remain imprisoned, no new trials will be held. Key figures like Mary Warren and Mercy Lewis are withdrawing from the drama, while others, like Thomas Putnam, find their power rapidly eroding. The crisis is ending, but the scars will remain.

The air in Salem Village feels different today. The Court of Oyer and Terminer, the body responsible for prosecuting these cases of witchcraft, is effectively finished. Governor Phips, facing mounting pressure and even accusations leveled at his own wife, has begun to dismantle the legal framework that fueled this hysteria. Lady Mary Phips, the governor’s wife, was “cried out upon” by one of the afflicted girls, a development that struck many as the final, undeniable proof that the accusations had spiraled out of control. As the magistrate’s record reads from a complaint filed today, Mary Brown of Reading details her torment, claiming Sarah Cole of Lynn has “threatened me sorely what Shee would do to me” and caused her “grat paine.” But these complaints, once readily accepted, are now meeting with skepticism and doubt. The accusations continue, but the court that once received them is no more. The long, dark days of examination and conviction are drawing to a close, leaving behind a community fractured and deeply wounded.

The Court of Oyer and Terminer has been dissolved, bringing an end to the trials. Governor Phips, spurred by the accusation against his own wife and growing concerns about the evidence used, has halted new cases and begun to dismantle the court. This is a seismic shift, after nearly seven months of accusations, examinations, and executions.

Mary Warren has retreated into silence, effectively disappearing from the afflicted circle. She no longer actively participates in the accusations, carrying with her the weight of her testimony and the betrayal of those she implicated. Her silence speaks volumes, a stark contrast to the public apologies of some and the disappearances of others.

Mercy Lewis, like Warren, is fading from prominence as the court dissolves. Stripped of the framework that gave voice to her fears, she is returning to her life as a servant in the Putnam household. Though the accusations fade, the trauma of the past year – and the earlier catastrophe at Falmouth – will likely stay with her.

Reverend Samuel Parris is losing influence as the community’s conviction wanes. His attempts to defend the court, such as the “Christ the Judge” sermon he delivered last month, are falling on increasingly deaf ears. His position as minister of Salem Village is now deeply precarious.

Thomas Putnam finds himself increasingly isolated and despised. With the court disbanding and the Porter faction regaining power, his influence is rapidly diminishing. He is left with a backlog of unfiled complaints and a community turning against him.

Tituba remains imprisoned, a forgotten casualty of the trials. Though a general pardon has been issued, no one will pay her jail fees, leaving her to languish in confinement. Even Samuel Parris, the man whose ministry her confession initially validated, refuses to assist her, ultimately selling her to an unknown buyer to cover her debts.

Families of the accused are still facing financial ruin. The Proctor family, for example, has incurred approximately 150 pounds in prison fees and property seizures, a devastating sum that will take years to recover. Similar burdens fall on the families of those who were executed or remain imprisoned.

The changing seasons add to the atmosphere of unease. Daylight hours are rapidly decreasing, and the return of darkness is a stark reminder of the gloom that accompanied the beginning of the trials. The landscape is preparing for winter, mirroring the community's own retreat inward.

newspapers.lede: The crisis in Salem Village and the surrounding communities took a dramatic turn today in Cambridge. Increase Mather, president of Harvard College and arguably the most influential minister in New England, read his treatise 'Cases of Conscience Concerning Evil Spirits' to a gathering of his peers. The core of Mather's argument is a challenge to the very evidence being used to convict accused witches. He contends the Devil, as “the father of lies,” can project the appearance of an innocent person, thereby making spectral evidence inherently untrustworthy. As Mather put it, “It were better that ten suspected witches should escape, than that one innocent person should be condemned.” This wasn’t a lone voice of dissent; fourteen ministers signed the document, lending their considerable weight to Mather’s argument. The implications are enormous. Governor Phips, already facing mounting pressure and with accusations now reaching into prominent families, has been given a theological justification to reconsider the proceedings and potentially dissolve the Court of Oyer and Terminer. The atmosphere today wasn’t one of celebration, but of a long-held breath finally being released.
newspapers.summary: Today, a pivotal moment in the Salem trials. Increase Mather presented his ‘Cases of Conscience’ to a gathering of ministers, arguing spectral evidence is unreliable and that it’s better to let ten suspected witches escape than condemn one innocent person. This powerful statement, endorsed by fourteen ministers, directly challenges the court's foundation and signals a potential turning of the tide.

The crisis in Salem Village and the surrounding communities took a dramatic turn today in Cambridge. Increase Mather, president of Harvard College and arguably the most influential minister in New England, read his treatise 'Cases of Conscience Concerning Evil Spirits' to a gathering of his peers. The core of Mather's argument is a challenge to the very evidence being used to convict accused witches. He contends the Devil, as “the father of lies,” can project the appearance of an innocent person, thereby making spectral evidence inherently untrustworthy. As Mather put it, “It were better that ten suspected witches should escape, than that one innocent person should be condemned.” This wasn’t a lone voice of dissent; fourteen ministers signed the document, lending their considerable weight to Mather’s argument. The implications are enormous. Governor Phips, already facing mounting pressure and with accusations now reaching into prominent families, has been given a theological justification to reconsider the proceedings and potentially dissolve the Court of Oyer and Terminer. The atmosphere today wasn’t one of celebration, but of a long-held breath finally being released.

Increase Mather’s ‘Cases of Conscience’ delivered a devastating blow to the evidentiary foundation of the Salem trials. The treatise argues that spectral evidence is unreliable because the Devil can assume the form of an innocent person, and that it is better to risk letting ten guilty witches escape than to condemn one innocent.

Fourteen ministers endorsed Mather’s work, signifying widespread concern about the validity of the trials and the reliance on spectral evidence. This widespread support gives Governor Phips a pathway to act without appearing to undermine the judicial process.

Also today, the formal accusation of Sarah Cole of Lynn adds another name to the growing list of the accused. She is one of the last formally accused individuals before Governor Phips is expected to take action.

The deposition of Sarah Cole, taken today, details an alleged nighttime encounter with several women, including the wife of Abraham Cole, and an incident involving a board with nails used to inflict harm upon her child.

Increase Mather argues that condemning an innocent person is a sin that "cries out to God from the ground," emphasizing the immense moral weight of potentially executing someone wrongly accused.

The argument against spectral evidence centers on the Devil’s deceptive nature; Mather points to scripture stating the Devil is “the father of lies,” and can even appear as “an angel of light” to mislead.

The situation is complicated by the fact that Cotton Mather, Increase’s son, has publicly defended the court in his writings, creating a visible tension that reflects the divided conscience of the colony.

Governor Phips is now positioned to use Mather’s treatise as justification to halt the trials and potentially restructure the court, as the theological and legal grounds for continuing the current course are rapidly eroding.

newspapers.lede: This Monday, October sixth, the court continues to grapple with the sheer number of people in custody. Rather than keeping everyone imprisoned in Salem jail, officials are increasingly relying on a system of recognizances. These are legal pledges, essentially bail arrangements, where local men promise to hold the accused and ensure their appearance in court. Today, Walter Wright and Francis Faulkner posted a bond of five hundred pounds sterling for thirteen-year-old John Sawdy, stating they’ve taken him “Into th’r Majesties Goale” and will be responsible for his care. Similar recognizances were filed for Dorothy and Abigail Faulkner, aged ten and eight, by John Osgood and Nathaniel Dane, as well as for Stephen and Abigail Johnson, and Mary Lacey, a fifteen-year-old girl. As one such document reads, these men are “holden & firmely Bownd Joyntly & Sevirally” to the King and Queen, guaranteeing the accused will appear when summoned. This practice suggests the jail is overwhelmed and that the authorities are attempting to distribute the responsibility for the accused amongst the community, though it does little to address the underlying accusations.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem, a flurry of legal activity centers on the custody of accused witches. Several recognizances—essentially guarantees of appearance in court—were posted for young women and a boy, transferring their custody from the jail to private individuals. This highlights the strain on the jail and a reliance on local families to oversee the accused.

This highlights the strain on the jail and a reliance on local families to oversee the accused.

Rather than keeping everyone imprisoned in Salem jail, officials are increasingly relying on a system of recognizances. These are legal pledges, essentially bail arrangements, where local men promise to hold the accused and ensure their appearance in court. Today, Walter Wright and Francis Faulkner posted a bond of five hundred pounds sterling for thirteen-year-old John Sawdy, stating they’ve taken him “Into th’r Majesties Goale” and will be responsible for his care. Similar recognizances were filed for Dorothy and Abigail Faulkner, aged ten and eight, by John Osgood and Nathaniel Dane, as well as for Stephen and Abigail Johnson, and Mary Lacey, a fifteen-year-old girl. This practice suggests the jail is overwhelmed and that the authorities are attempting to distribute the responsibility for the accused amongst the community, though it does little to address the underlying accusations.

As detailed in the lede, multiple recognizances were signed today, transferring custody of several young people—John Sawdy, Dorothy Faulkner, Abigail Faulkner, Stephen Johnson, Abigail Johnson, and Mary Lacey—from the Salem jail to the care of local men. This demonstrates the growing logistical challenges of housing so many accused individuals.

The recognizances all involve a substantial financial bond of five hundred pounds sterling, indicating the seriousness with which the court views these cases and the potential financial risk to those posting the bond.

The ages of those released into private custody are notable. Several are children – eight, ten, thirteen, eleven, and fifteen – raising questions about the fairness and justice of the proceedings, even by the standards of the time.

The recognizances all name King William and Queen Mary, emphasizing the Crown’s authority and the legal basis for the court’s actions, even as the proceedings continue to unfold in what many see as an increasingly chaotic manner.

The recognizances were all executed in Salem, but involve residents of Andover, demonstrating that the reach of the accusations and the court extends beyond Salem Village and Town.

newspapers.lede: Here in Salem Village, the hangings have stopped for the moment, but the air remains thick with fear and accusation. Today, however, a different kind of battle is being waged – a battle of ideas. Thomas Brattle, a wealthy merchant from Boston and a Fellow of the Royal Society, has sent a letter to an unnamed clergyman, systematically dismantling the logic behind these trials. Brattle’s critique is circulating rapidly among the educated elite of Boston, and it’s a scathing indictment of the methods being used here. He challenges the very foundation of the evidence presented, pointing out inconsistencies and raising serious doubts about the validity of the accusations. As Brattle writes, questioning the so-called "touch test," “I cannot but condemn this method of the Justices…because I am fully persuaded that it is sorcery, and a superstitious method.” He compares relying on spectral evidence to “pistolling the shadow” rather than catching the substance. This letter isn’t just a personal opinion; it’s a carefully reasoned argument from a man known for his intellect and his commitment to reason, and it carries significant weight. It represents a growing chorus of skepticism that may finally force a reconsideration of these proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem, a powerful critique of the witchcraft trials emerged from Boston. Thomas Brattle, a respected merchant and scientist, circulated a detailed letter questioning the methods used by the Court of Oyer and Terminer, marking a turning point in public opinion and adding pressure on Governor Phips to intervene.

Today in Salem, a powerful critique of the witchcraft trials emerged from Boston. Thomas Brattle, a respected merchant and scientist, circulated a detailed letter questioning the methods used by the Court of Oyer and Terminer, marking a turning point in public opinion and adding pressure on Governor Phips to intervene.

Here in Salem Village, the hangings have stopped for the moment, but the air remains thick with fear and accusation. Today, however, a different kind of battle is being waged – a battle of ideas. Thomas Brattle, a wealthy merchant from Boston and a Fellow of the Royal Society, has sent a letter to an unnamed clergyman, systematically dismantling the logic behind these trials. Brattle’s critique is circulating rapidly among the educated elite of Boston, and it’s a scathing indictment of the methods being used here. He challenges the very foundation of the evidence presented, pointing out inconsistencies and raising serious doubts about the validity of the accusations. As Brattle writes, questioning the so-called "touch test," “I cannot but condemn this method of the Justices…because I am fully persuaded that it is sorcery, and a superstitious method.” He compares relying on spectral evidence to “pistolling the shadow” rather than catching the substance. This letter isn’t just a personal opinion; it’s a carefully reasoned argument from a man known for his intellect and his commitment to reason, and it carries significant weight. It represents a growing chorus of skepticism that may finally force a reconsideration of these proceedings.

Today, Thomas Brattle, a Boston merchant and member of the Royal Society, circulated a detailed letter critiquing the Salem witch trials, a document that systematically dismantles the evidentiary methods of the Court of Oyer and Terminer. This letter is considered a milestone in American literature and represents a critical moment when written opposition reached a tipping point.

Brattle’s letter specifically attacks the use of spectral evidence, arguing it’s unreliable and based on illusions – little more than “pistolling the shadow,” as he puts it. He highlights that the afflicted girls often claimed to be touched by the specters of the accused, even when no actual physical contact occurred.

He points out the inconsistencies in the confessions obtained, noting that many accused individuals later recanted their statements, raising questions about the methods used to extract them. He also questions why those who confessed were often spared, while those who maintained their innocence were condemned.

Brattle challenges the validity of the “touch test,” noting that the afflicted girls would fall into fits at anyone’s touch, not just the accused, exposing it as superstitious and lacking any scientific basis.

The letter also points out that the accusations have reached into the colony’s own leadership, including the wife of Reverend Hale and the mother-in-law of Judge Corwin, raising concerns about the impartiality of the court.

Brattle's critique isn't solely about the methods; he also questions the consistency of the confessions, suggesting they appear coached or follow a pre-determined pattern. This observation further undermines the authenticity of the evidence presented.

This letter, combined with Increase Mather’s Cases of Conscience from last week, is placing significant pressure on Governor Phips to take action and potentially dissolve the Court of Oyer and Terminer. The voices of reason are growing louder.

newspapers.lede: Governor William Phips took decisive action today, ordering that no further individuals be imprisoned on charges of witchcraft without his express consent. This follows weeks of mounting criticism of the trials, sparked by Increase Mather’s publication of *Cases of Conscience* and Thomas Brattle’s scathing letter detailing inconsistencies in the evidence. Phips, who had been occupied with military duties in Maine, returned to Boston earlier this month to a province gripped by fear and accusation. He described the initial situation in a letter to the Privy Council, noting that upon his arrival, “some scores of poor people were taken with preternaturall torments… some scalded with brimstone, some had pins stuck in their flesh.” He appointed what he believed to be a prudent court, but grew concerned as the accusations broadened, including even persons of good reputation. Today’s order doesn’t reverse past convictions, but it effectively stops the flow of new cases into the system. As he explained in his letter, the court initially “began their proceedings with the accusations of the afflicted and then went upon other humane evidences to strengthen that.” Now, those accusations will no longer automatically lead to imprisonment.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the Salem trials. Governor William Phips has issued an order halting all new arrests for witchcraft, a direct response to growing concerns about the proceedings and mounting pressure from both within and outside Massachusetts Bay. While the court remains seated, its power to expand the circle of accusation has been significantly curtailed.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a turning point in the Salem trials. Governor William Phips has issued an order halting all new arrests for witchcraft, a direct response to growing concerns about the proceedings and mounting pressure from both within and outside Massachusetts Bay. While the court remains seated, its power to expand the circle of accusation has been significantly curtailed.

Governor William Phips took decisive action today, ordering that no further individuals be imprisoned on charges of witchcraft without his express consent. This follows weeks of mounting criticism of the trials, sparked by Increase Mather’s publication of *Cases of Conscience* and Thomas Brattle’s scathing letter detailing inconsistencies in the evidence. Phips, who had been occupied with military duties in Maine, returned to Boston earlier this month to a province gripped by fear and accusation. He described the initial situation in a letter to the Privy Council, noting that upon his arrival, “some scores of poor people were taken with preternaturall torments… some scalded with brimstone, some had pins stuck in their flesh.” He appointed what he believed to be a prudent court, but grew concerned as the accusations broadened, including even persons of good reputation. Today’s order doesn’t reverse past convictions, but it effectively stops the flow of new cases into the system. As he explained in his letter, the court initially “began their proceedings with the accusations of the afflicted and then went upon other humane evidences to strengthen that.” Now, those accusations will no longer automatically lead to imprisonment.

Governor Phips’s order halting new arrests represents the most significant development of the day. While the existing court will continue to hear cases already in progress, the administrative change effectively freezes the expansion of the trials. The pipeline of accusations, fueled by the afflicted girls, has been severed at its source.

Thirty-two residents of Ipswich have signed a petition challenging the use of spectral evidence and the treatment of those accused. This demonstrates growing dissent outside of Salem Village and highlights concerns about the methods employed by the court.

A petition from Andover, signed by John Osgood and eight others, details the hardship faced by families of those imprisoned. They appeal for relief, citing the “exceeding great Charges and expences” and the coming winter’s hardship for those already incarcerated.

Governor Phips has penned a letter to the Earl of Nottingham defending his handling of the crisis. He attempts to portray himself as a cautious leader who responded responsibly to an unprecedented situation, rather than one who enabled the excesses of the court.

Despite the Governor’s order, the afflicted girls continue to accuse others. However, without the authority to imprison, those accusations will not lead to further arrests, at least not without Phips’s direct intervention.

The jails remain full. Though no new prisoners will be added, the fate of those already convicted and awaiting execution remains uncertain as the court continues its work.

newspapers.lede: The pressures surrounding the Salem Witch Trials continue to mount, even as the executions slow. Today, Reverend Samuel Willard, minister of Boston’s Third Church, released a pamphlet criticizing the reliance on spectral evidence, the visions the afflicted girls claim to see of those tormenting them. Willard published anonymously, using the initials ‘P.E. and J.A.’ and falsely claiming the pamphlet originated in Philadelphia. This deception underscores how dangerous it had become to publicly question the court’s methods. Willard argued that accepting spectral evidence meant accepting the Devil himself as the primary witness. As he put it, the court was essentially letting “the Devil, turned informer,” dictate guilt or innocence. He highlighted the logical flaw: if Satan could freely impersonate anyone, then these visions proved nothing about the accused. This line of reasoning echoed the concerns of Increase Mather and Thomas Brattle, but Willard felt compelled to shield his identity to express them.
newspapers.summary: Today, Reverend Samuel Willard of Boston published a carefully disguised critique of the trials, questioning the validity of spectral evidence. His pamphlet, published under false names and a fabricated location, reveals the risks of speaking out against the proceedings—even for a respected minister—as the crisis continues.
