Today, Reverend Samuel Willard of Boston published a carefully disguised critique of the trials, questioning the validity of spectral evidence. His pamphlet, published under false names and a fabricated location, reveals the risks of speaking out against the proceedings—even for a respected minister—as the crisis continues.

The pressures surrounding the Salem Witch Trials continue to mount, even as the executions slow. Today, Reverend Samuel Willard, minister of Boston’s Third Church, released a pamphlet criticizing the reliance on spectral evidence, the visions the afflicted girls claim to see of those tormenting them. Willard published anonymously, using the initials ‘P.E. and J.A.’ and falsely claiming the pamphlet originated in Philadelphia. This deception underscores how dangerous it had become to publicly question the court’s methods. Willard argued that accepting spectral evidence meant accepting the Devil himself as the primary witness. As he put it, the court was essentially letting “the Devil, turned informer,” dictate guilt or innocence. He highlighted the logical flaw: if Satan could freely impersonate anyone, then these visions proved nothing about the accused. This line of reasoning echoed the concerns of Increase Mather and Thomas Brattle, but Willard felt compelled to shield his identity to express them.

Reverend Samuel Willard’s pamphlet is a significant, though cautious, challenge to the court’s acceptance of spectral evidence, questioning the very foundation upon which many convictions have been based. Willard’s argument that the Devil could impersonate anyone renders these visions unreliable and opens the door to false accusations, a position gaining traction among some observers.

The use of pseudonyms and a false place of publication – Philadelphia – by Willard demonstrates the level of fear and self-preservation present among those who dared to voice dissent. Even a respected minister like Willard could not openly criticize the proceedings without risking repercussions.

Willard’s critique builds upon earlier concerns raised by Increase Mather and Thomas Brattle regarding the dangers of relying on spectral evidence, signaling a growing chorus of opposition to the court’s methods.

The trials remain in a crisis phase, with five people hanged this August, including George Burroughs, demonstrating the continued power of the court and the gravity of the accusations.

Though the pace of executions has slowed, several remain imprisoned, awaiting trial or sentencing, and the atmosphere in Salem Village remains tense and fearful.

The magistrate’s record shows continued examinations of accused individuals, often relying heavily on the testimony of the afflicted girls and their descriptions of spectral attacks.

newspapers.lede: Concern is growing beyond Salem Village as residents of Andover formally appeal to the government for a more just handling of the witchcraft accusations. Twenty-six men signed a petition delivered this day to Governor Phips and the Massachusetts Council, requesting a review of the cases against their wives and daughters. They acknowledge the seriousness of witchcraft and state their desire to see true offenders brought to justice, but also express worry that some accused individuals may have been falsely accused by what they describe as “distempered persons.” As the petition reads, they hope that if any have been “misrepresented,” the truth may come to light, adding that most of those accused have “never gave the least occasion…to their neerest relations.” This collective plea for fairness represents one of the first organized attempts by families to defend those caught in the web of accusations, and suggests a rising tide of anxiety about the proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today in Andover, twenty-six men petition the Massachusetts government, pleading for a fair review of the witchcraft accusations leveled against their wives and daughters. This petition signals growing concern among those affected by the trials, and a desire for due process, however limited, in the face of mounting accusations.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Andover, twenty-six men petition the Massachusetts government, pleading for a fair review of the witchcraft accusations leveled against their wives and daughters. This petition signals growing concern among those affected by the trials, and a desire for due process, however limited, in the face of mounting accusations.

Concern is growing beyond Salem Village as residents of Andover formally appeal to the government for a more just handling of the witchcraft accusations. Twenty-six men signed a petition delivered this day to Governor Phips and the Massachusetts Council, requesting a review of the cases against their wives and daughters. They acknowledge the seriousness of witchcraft and state their desire to see true offenders brought to justice, but also express worry that some accused individuals may have been falsely accused by what they describe as “distempered persons.” As the petition reads, they hope that if any have been “misrepresented,” the truth may come to light, adding that most of those accused have “never gave the least occasion…to their neerest relations.” This collective plea for fairness represents one of the first organized attempts by families to defend those caught in the web of accusations, and suggests a rising tide of anxiety about the proceedings.

Twenty-six men from Andover presented a formal petition to the government today, seeking a review of the witchcraft accusations against their wives and daughters. They expressed a desire for justice but also voiced concern that some individuals may have been falsely accused, highlighting the growing distress within the community.

The petition from Andover specifically references accusations made by individuals they describe as “distempered persons,” indicating a growing awareness that the accusers themselves may be unreliable.

This petition represents a significant development as it is one of the first collective efforts by family members to defend the accused, suggesting a growing resistance to the unchecked accusations.

The crisis continues unabated, with no respite from the examinations and imprisonments occurring in Salem Village and surrounding towns.

While this petition seeks redress, it is important to remember that the magistrates and ministers continue to believe they are acting to protect the Massachusetts Bay Colony from a grave spiritual threat.

The recent series of executions in August—including the controversial hanging of Reverend George Burroughs—has undoubtedly fueled the anxiety and desperation of those seeking a fairer process.

newspapers.lede: This Sunday, unsettling news arrives from Andover. Several women previously convicted on their own confessions—including Mary Osgood, Deliverance Dane, and Abigail Barker—have now declared those confessions to be false. Reverend Increase Mather has reported their statements, explaining the women felt pressured into admitting to dealings with the devil. Mary Osgood, as Mather relays, stated her confession was “wholly false, and that she was brought to the said confession by the violent urging and unreasonable pressings that were used toward her.” She maintains she never signed the devil’s book, nor afflicted anyone. Osgood explained that when questioned about the timing of supposed events, she simply offered a date from a time when she was ill, desperate to satisfy her interrogators. This coordinated recantation throws a shadow over the entire process, suggesting the court may have relied on testimony obtained through intimidation rather than genuine evidence. The implications for the ongoing trials are considerable, and it remains to be seen how the magistrates will respond to this direct challenge to their authority.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a significant challenge to the court's proceedings as several women from Andover publicly recant their confessions of witchcraft. These recantations, delivered through Reverend Increase Mather, allege coercion and false testimony, raising serious questions about the validity of the trials and the methods used to obtain confessions. Meanwhile, a husband petitions for his wife's release from prison.

These recantations, delivered through Reverend Increase Mather, allege coercion and false testimony, raising serious questions about the validity of the trials and the methods used to obtain confessions. Meanwhile, a husband petitions for his wife's release from prison.

This Sunday, unsettling news arrives from Andover. Several women previously convicted on their own confessions—including Mary Osgood, Deliverance Dane, and Abigail Barker—have now declared those confessions to be false. Reverend Increase Mather has reported their statements, explaining the women felt pressured into admitting to dealings with the devil. Mary Osgood, as Mather relays, stated her confession was “wholly false, and that she was brought to the said confession by the violent urging and unreasonable pressings that were used toward her.” She maintains she never signed the devil’s book, nor afflicted anyone. Osgood explained that when questioned about the timing of supposed events, she simply offered a date from a time when she was ill, desperate to satisfy her interrogators. This coordinated recantation throws a shadow over the entire process, suggesting the court may have relied on testimony obtained through intimidation rather than genuine evidence. The implications for the ongoing trials are considerable, and it remains to be seen how the magistrates will respond to this direct challenge to their authority.

As we heard in the lede, a group of women from Andover have formally recanted their confessions of witchcraft, claiming they were coerced through “violent urging and unreasonable pressings.” Reverend Increase Mather delivered their collective statement, significantly undermining the credibility of the examinations and the confessions themselves.

Nicholas Rice of Reading has petitioned the General Court for the release of his wife, Sarah Rice, who has been imprisoned in Boston since June first. He asserts she is an elderly and infirm woman who deserves nursing care, not a jail cell, and that no evidence supports the accusations against her.

The recanting women specifically deny signing the devil’s book, being baptized by the devil, or ever afflicting the young accusers. Mary Osgood explained that she simply gave the date of a past illness when pressed for a timeline of events, hoping to end the interrogation.

This is not the first instance of recanted testimony. However, this coordinated effort from multiple women, brought to light by a respected minister like Increase Mather, represents a far more substantial challenge to the court's authority than previous individual reversals.

The situation highlights the intense pressure placed on those accused and the questionable tactics employed during examinations. The court’s reliance on spectral evidence, combined with relentless questioning, appears to have led to numerous false confessions.

The magistrate’s record shows a pattern of leading questions and acceptance of ambiguous statements as proof of witchcraft, further fueling the hysteria and endangering the lives of those accused.

While the court has yet to respond publicly to the recantations, the news is sure to circulate quickly throughout Salem Village and beyond, potentially influencing public opinion and raising doubts about the fairness of the trials.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village remains thick with fear and religious conviction this Thursday. Reverend Samuel Parris preached at the meeting house, attempting to offer solace to his congregation amidst the ongoing trials. His sermon, titled “The Devil’s Rage Against the Saints,” centers on the biblical Song of Solomon, specifically the line “Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth.” Parris appears to be framing the spiritual struggle against demonic forces as a passionate, even romantic, pursuit of divine affection. As the magistrate’s record shows, accusations continue to fly, and the number of those imprisoned and condemned grows daily. Parris wrote of this passage, “Cant. 2. Let him Kiss me with the kisses of his Mouth &c.: for thy Love is […],” suggesting a desire for intense spiritual connection as a shield against evil. This sermon reflects the community’s desperate attempt to understand the source of their troubles through a theological lens—a lens that, tragically, continues to fuel the accusations and convictions.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon focused on divine love and the perceived battle against the Devil, reflecting the ongoing anxiety and religious fervor gripping the community as the trials continue and executions mount. The sermon underscores the deep spiritual concerns motivating the pursuit of witchcraft.

Today in Salem Village, Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon focused on divine love and the perceived battle against the Devil, reflecting the ongoing anxiety and religious fervor gripping the community as the trials continue and executions mount. The sermon underscores the deep spiritual concerns motivating the pursuit of witchcraft.

Reverend Samuel Parris preached at the meeting house, attempting to offer solace to his congregation amidst the ongoing trials. His sermon, titled “The Devil’s Rage Against the Saints,” centers on the biblical Song of Solomon, specifically the line “Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth.” Parris appears to be framing the spiritual struggle against demonic forces as a passionate, even romantic, pursuit of divine affection. As the magistrate’s record shows, accusations continue to fly, and the number of those imprisoned and condemned grows daily. Parris wrote of this passage, “Cant. 2. Let him Kiss me with the kisses of his Mouth &c.: for thy Love is […],” suggesting a desire for intense spiritual connection as a shield against evil. This sermon reflects the community’s desperate attempt to understand the source of their troubles through a theological lens—a lens that, tragically, continues to fuel the accusations and convictions.

Reverend Samuel Parris delivered a sermon today emphasizing divine love as a defense against the Devil, a message intended to reassure his congregation as the witch trials continue to escalate. The sermon’s focus on passionate spiritual connection offers insight into the religious thinking of the time.

Though no trials or executions occurred today, the jail remains full, and the court is preparing for further examinations of accused individuals in the coming days.

Public anxiety is high as Salem Village residents fear they—or their neighbors—may be targeted by accusations of witchcraft, creating a climate of mistrust and suspicion.

Several families continue to petition the authorities for the release of loved ones, arguing against the spectral evidence used to secure convictions. These pleas, however, have so far been unsuccessful.

The recent wave of executions, including those of Bridget Bishop, Sarah Good, and Reverend George Burroughs, has intensified the fear and desperation within the community.

Support for the trials remains strong among some influential members of the Massachusetts Bay government, who see the suppression of witchcraft as a matter of religious and social order.

newspapers.lede: This Wednesday, October 29th, 1692, Governor William Phips took the momentous step of dissolving the Court of Oyer and Terminer, the special commission established to try those accused of witchcraft. The decision comes after months of escalating trials, executions, and growing concern among influential figures in Boston. Thomas Brattle’s letter criticizing the use of spectral evidence, coupled with arguments from Reverend Increase Mather, appear to have finally persuaded Phips to intervene. Mather, in his recently published *Cases of Conscience*, argued that, “better that ten suspected witches escape than one innocent person condemned.” The Governor’s own wife, Abigail Phips, having been accused, likely also weighed heavily on his decision. Chief Justice William Stoughton, however, vehemently opposed the dissolution, believing the court’s work unfinished. He reportedly confronted Phips, insisting convicted witches remained at large. Phips replaced the Oyer and Terminer with the Superior Court of Judicature, a court that will not admit spectral evidence, effectively ending the convictions that have plagued this colony.
newspapers.summary: Today, Governor William Phips dissolved the Court of Oyer and Terminer, halting the Salem witch trials and ending the immediate threat of execution. While over 150 remain imprisoned, the legal process that led to nineteen hangings and one death by pressing has been dismantled, marking a turning point in this crisis.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today, Governor William Phips dissolved the Court of Oyer and Terminer, halting the Salem witch trials and ending the immediate threat of execution. While over 150 remain imprisoned, the legal process that led to nineteen hangings and one death by pressing has been dismantled, marking a turning point in this crisis.

This Wednesday, October 29th, 1692, Governor William Phips took the momentous step of dissolving the Court of Oyer and Terminer, the special commission established to try those accused of witchcraft. The decision comes after months of escalating trials, executions, and growing concern among influential figures in Boston. Thomas Brattle’s letter criticizing the use of spectral evidence, coupled with arguments from Reverend Increase Mather, appear to have finally persuaded Phips to intervene. Mather, in his recently published *Cases of Conscience*, argued that, “better that ten suspected witches escape than one innocent person condemned.” The Governor’s own wife, Abigail Phips, having been accused, likely also weighed heavily on his decision. Chief Justice William Stoughton, however, vehemently opposed the dissolution, believing the court’s work unfinished. He reportedly confronted Phips, insisting convicted witches remained at large. Phips replaced the Oyer and Terminer with the Superior Court of Judicature, a court that will not admit spectral evidence, effectively ending the convictions that have plagued this colony.

Governor Phips today dissolved the Court of Oyer and Terminer, bringing an end to the trials that have gripped Salem Village and the Massachusetts Bay Colony for months. The court had convicted twenty-nine people of witchcraft, resulting in nineteen executions by hanging and the brutal death of Giles Corey under the press.

The dissolution was not a sudden change of heart, but the result of mounting pressure. Reverend Increase Mather’s arguments against spectral evidence, combined with Thomas Brattle’s public critique, created a climate of doubt that Governor Phips could no longer ignore.

Chief Justice William Stoughton reacted with fury to the Governor’s decision, believing the court was doing God’s work. He even went so far as to sign death warrants for those acquitted by the new Superior Court, but Governor Phips thankfully intervened with reprieves.

While the court is dissolved, over 150 accused witches remain imprisoned, awaiting further legal proceedings. The Superior Court of Judicature will begin hearing cases in January, but with the exclusion of spectral evidence, it is expected that most will be acquitted.

The decision to dissolve the court was as much a political calculation as a moral one. Governor Phips understood that the trials were damaging the colony’s reputation and relationship with England, and he sought to restore order and stability.

Stoughton, refusing to accept the change, declared that “we were in a way to have cleared the land” of witches, revealing his unwavering belief in the guilt of the accused and a disturbing lack of remorse for the lives lost. He would go on to a long and powerful career in colonial government.

newspapers.lede: The crisis in Salem continues to shift, though not toward resolution, but toward doubt. Today, Thomas Barrett of Chelmsford formally petitioned Governor William Phips for the release of his daughter, Martha Sparks. She has been held in Boston prison for twelve months and five days, originally committed by Deputy Governor Thomas Danforth on suspicion of witchcraft. Barrett’s petition, a desperate plea for his daughter and grandchildren, states plainly that “noe Evidence hath appeared against her in any Such matter.” He details the hardship of caring for her two young children and his ailing wife, adding to his family’s distress. Barrett emphasizes that no one currently accuses his daughter, and no bond has been set for prosecution. As the magistrate’s record shows, this petition is one of many now circulating, reflecting a growing sense that the legal proceedings have gone too far and are based on flimsy evidence. It comes just days after the Court of Oyer and Terminer was dissolved, leaving dozens languishing in jail.
newspapers.summary: Today brings news of a petition for the release of Martha Sparks, who has been imprisoned for over a year on charges of witchcraft, but against whom no evidence has been presented. This development signals a growing questioning of the trials and the lengthy detentions without proof, as the court has recently been dissolved.

The fact that Martha Sparks has been incarcerated for over a year without a formal accusation or bond for prosecution is now being publicly questioned, as evidenced by her father’s appeal to Governor Phips.

Barrett’s petition details the significant personal hardship inflicted on his family by his daughter’s imprisonment, including the financial burden of caring for her young children and his own wife’s illness.

With the court dissolved, the fate of those still imprisoned remains uncertain, and petitions like Barrett’s are becoming more frequent as families seek some resolution.

The petition makes no mention of spectral evidence—the type of testimony that fueled many of the initial accusations—suggesting a growing skepticism toward such claims.

Henry Sparks, Martha’s husband, is currently serving in the military, further complicating the family’s situation and potentially influencing Barrett’s urgency in seeking her release.

newspapers.lede: The wave of accusations that gripped Salem and its surrounding communities appears to be finally receding. Today, November 5th, saw the last formally recorded accusations of witchcraft in this crisis. James Stevens of Gloucester sent for the afflicted girls from Salem Village, hoping they would identify the source of his sister Mary Fitch’s illness. He claimed the girls immediately pointed to Rebecca Dike, wife of a substantial landowner. As the magistrate’s record reads, Stevens stated he “did desire to know who afflicted my sister,” believing her sickness to be supernatural in origin. Shortly after, Esther Elwell, a woman from a prominent Gloucester family, and then Abigail Rowe, a fifteen-year-old girl, were also accused by Stevens and the girls. These accusations, however, came after the Court of Oyer and Terminer had been dissolved, and with growing skepticism about spectral evidence, prosecution proved impossible. These women, like so many others, were accused but never brought to trial.
newspapers.summary: Today marks the end of formal accusations in the Salem crisis. Three women – Abigail Rowe, Esther Elwell, and Rebecca Dike – were all accused on or around this date by the same accuser, James Stevens, and the afflicted girls. Though the Court of Oyer and Terminer has dissolved, local accusations persist, even as doubt grows.

James Stevens accused Rebecca Dike, Esther Elwell, and Abigail Rowe today, all with similar claims of affliction and identification by the Salem Village girls. This represents the final batch of formal accusations in the Salem witch trials, signaling a turning point in the crisis.

Rebecca Dike, married to a landowner in Gloucester, was accused after the dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer. This highlights how local accusations continued even as the official legal process faltered.

Esther Elwell, from a well-known Gloucester family, was also accused by Stevens and the girls. Her case, like the others made today, was ultimately dropped due to the increasing unreliability of spectral evidence.

Abigail Rowe, at just fifteen years of age, is the youngest of those accused from Gloucester. She was never tried and, like Dike and Elwell, was released.

The fact that all three accusations stem from one accuser, James Stevens, suggests a possible pattern of influence or personal grievance driving these late claims.

The ban on spectral evidence, though not formally announced today, is increasingly impacting the ability to pursue these accusations, paving the way for the eventual release of those imprisoned.

newspapers.lede: Reverend John Hale, the minister of Beverly and a consistent advocate for the prosecution of witches, experienced a personal crisis this Friday. Seventeen-year-old Mary Herrick has accused his wife, Sarah Hale, of witchcraft. This accusation, coming as it does against a woman he knows to be devout, has shaken Hale to his core. For months, Reverend Hale had attended examinations and affirmed the testimony of the afflicted girls, lending his considerable influence to the court. Now, facing the possibility of his wife’s condemnation, he is reassessing everything he thought he knew. As he would later write, “such was the darkness of that day that we walked in clouds and could not see our way.” The accusation against Sarah Hale, alongside the recent accusation against Lady Phips, is increasingly seen as one of the key events that brought the trials to a halt.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a crucial shift in the Salem trials. Reverend John Hale of Beverly, a staunch supporter of the court, now finds his own wife accused, triggering a profound change of heart and contributing to the growing doubt that will soon end the proceedings.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a crucial shift in the Salem trials. Reverend John Hale of Beverly, a staunch supporter of the court, now finds his own wife accused, triggering a profound change of heart and contributing to the growing doubt that will soon end the proceedings.

The accusation against Sarah Hale represents a turning point in the Salem trials, as Reverend John Hale, formerly a strong supporter of the court, begins to doubt the proceedings. His personal experience with the accusations is prompting a reconsideration of the evidence and the methods used to secure convictions.

Reverend Hale’s initial support for the trials was significant, as he frequently attended examinations and validated the claims of the afflicted girls. His change of heart carries considerable weight within the community and among the other ministers involved.

Mary Herrick, the accuser in this case, is a seventeen-year-old girl whose testimony has been central to several recent examinations. Her continued accusations, even against respected members of the community like Sarah Hale, are fueling growing skepticism.

The court is already showing signs of strain, with many questioning the validity of spectral evidence and the reliability of the afflicted girls. This new development will almost certainly accelerate the growing calls for a halt to the proceedings.

Though Sarah Hale has not been formally arrested, the accusation itself carries immense social and legal consequences, subjecting her to public scrutiny and suspicion within Salem Village and Beverly.

While the formal dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer is still days away, today’s events strongly suggest that the peak of the witch hunt has passed, and a period of reassessment is beginning.

newspapers.lede: The news from England, though arriving slowly, is now definitive: King James II has been replaced by William and Mary. For those who saw James’s Catholicism as a threat to Protestant faith and, indeed, to the entire order of things, this is a moment of providential deliverance. Reverend Samuel Willard of Boston wrote in a letter this past spring, “’Tis a sweet deliverance that God hath sent us, in casting out that Popish Usurper.” But this “sweet deliverance” comes with practical difficulties here in Massachusetts Bay. The old charter under which we governed was revoked years ago, and no new one has yet been issued by the new monarchs. This legal vacuum is keenly felt as we attempt to rebuild after the trials, and the lack of clear authority hampers any efforts toward reconciliation or justice. The Court of Oyer and Terminer, responsible for prosecuting the accused, was dissolved last month, largely due to growing doubts about the validity of spectral evidence, but also because of the uncertainty surrounding the government itself.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point, though not here in Salem Village. News of the revolution in England – the crowning of William and Mary – has finally solidified, shifting the political landscape of Massachusetts Bay and adding to the legal confusion following the removal of Governor Andros. This change in power, while welcome to many Puritans, complicates the already fraught situation following the dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer.

As the news of William and Mary’s ascension becomes fully accepted, it underscores the precarious legal situation in Massachusetts Bay. The absence of a valid charter leaves the colony in a state of uncertainty, complicating efforts to address the aftermath of the recent trials and hindering any attempts to establish a stable legal framework.

The dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer last month continues to be the most immediate impact felt here in Salem Village. Without a functioning court, there is no clear path for reviewing the convictions or addressing the grievances of those affected by the accusations.

Many residents remain deeply divided over the events of the past year. While some believe the trials were a necessary defense against the Devil’s influence, others are increasingly skeptical, questioning the methods and motives of the accusers and magistrates.

The economic impact of the trials is beginning to be felt more acutely. Families torn apart by accusations and imprisonment face financial hardship, and the disruption to trade and agriculture has created a sense of instability.

Reverend Samuel Parris remains a controversial figure. Though he has not been formally accused of wrongdoing, his role in initiating the accusations against Tituba and others continues to be debated within the community.

Local officials are struggling to maintain order and address the lingering anxieties and resentments that permeate Salem Village. The absence of strong central authority exacerbates these challenges.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere in Salem Village is shifting from accusation to aftermath. Though the trials officially ended last month, the legal repercussions continue. Today, Jonathan Prescot and John Horton posted a two-hundred-pound bond guaranteeing the appearance of Ann Sears, accused of witchcraft, at the next session of the court. This is a significant step, indicating a move toward due process, even for those already imprisoned. Meanwhile, Abigail Faulkner Sr., condemned to death but currently spared due to pregnancy, has submitted a desperate petition to Governor William Phips. In her own words, she writes, “I know my selfe altogether Innocent & Ignorant of the crime of witchcraft w’ch is layd to my charge.” Faulkner’s petition is particularly poignant as she details the suffering of her husband, whose health deteriorated after the accusations began, and the hardship faced by her six children. It’s a plea based not on legal technicalities, but on the human cost of the trials. As the magistrate’s record of the recognizance shows, these actions suggest that some officials are now willing to reconsider the cases before them.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem, we see the beginnings of a legal winding-down after the dissolution of the special Court of Oyer and Terminer. While prisoners remain in jail, recognizances – essentially bail agreements – are being posted for some, and petitions for pardon are being submitted by those condemned, highlighting growing doubts about the recent trials.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem, we see the beginnings of a legal winding-down after the dissolution of the special Court of Oyer and Terminer. While prisoners remain in jail, recognizances – essentially bail agreements – are being posted for some, and petitions for pardon are being submitted by those condemned, highlighting growing doubts about the recent trials.

In her own words, she writes, “I know my selfe altogether Innocent & Ignorant of the crime of witchcraft w’ch is layd to my charge.” Faulkner’s petition is particularly poignant as she details the suffering of her husband, whose health deteriorated after the accusations began, and the hardship faced by her six children. It’s a plea based not on legal technicalities, but on the human cost of the trials. As the magistrate’s record of the recognizance shows, these actions suggest that some officials are now willing to reconsider the cases before them.

As detailed in the lede, Jonathan Prescot and John Horton have posted a significant bond to secure the appearance of Ann Sears, accused of witchcraft, at future court proceedings. This recognizance demonstrates a shift toward allowing accused individuals a chance to defend themselves legally, rather than relying solely on spectral evidence and forced confessions.

Abigail Faulkner Sr. has formally petitioned Governor Phips for a pardon, appealing to his sense of justice and outlining the devastating impact the accusations have had on her family. Her petition relies heavily on the recantations of previous accusers and the claim of her own innocence.

The posting of recognizances, like the one for Ann Sears, suggests that the authorities are beginning to acknowledge the possibility of error in the trials, or at least the need for more solid evidence before proceeding with executions.

The fact that multiple condemned individuals are now seeking pardons indicates a growing belief among the accused that the tide may be turning in their favor, even after convictions were secured.

Faulkner’s description of her husband’s failing health, caused by “greife and sorrow,” reveals the wider suffering caused by the witch hunt, extending beyond the accused to their families and communities.

While the Court of Oyer and Terminer has been dissolved, the process of reviewing cases and determining the fate of those imprisoned is still underway, and these petitions and recognizances are part of that slow, evolving process.

newspapers.lede: The chill of December is settling over Massachusetts Bay, and with it, a growing sense of unease regarding the recent trials. Though the Court of Oyer and Terminer was dissolved just weeks ago, the legal fallout continues. Today, a recognizance—essentially a financial guarantee—was recorded for Martha Sparks of Chelmsford, keeping her bound over for appearance at a future court session. This means she remains imprisoned in Boston, still accused of witchcraft. The document, as the magistrate’s record reads, details a bond of two hundred pounds, “to be leavied on his Goods or Chattells Lands or Tennements” if Sparks fails to appear. Simultaneously, a delegation of men from Andover has formally petitioned Governor Phips, detailing the “great sufferrings” of their wives and neighbors held in Salem Prison. They write, “we are very sensible of the extream danger the Prisoners are in of perishing, if they are not speedily released.” This petition underscores the desperate conditions within the jail and the growing anxiety among families left waiting for answers.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, legal proceedings continue even as doubt about the trials grows. Martha Sparks remains in Boston Gaol, and her accusers still maintain she is practicing witchcraft. Meanwhile, families from Andover have petitioned Governor Phips, pleading for the release of their wives and neighbors before winter brings further hardship.

As detailed in the recognizance recorded today, Martha Sparks continues to be held in Boston Gaol, accused of witchcraft and bound over for future legal proceedings. This action confirms that despite the dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer, accusations are still being pursued, and individuals remain imprisoned.

A petition from Andover, signed by John Osgood and seven others, has been sent to Governor Phips, requesting the release of their wives and neighbors imprisoned in Salem. The petitioners cite the harsh winter conditions and the deteriorating health of those incarcerated as urgent reasons for their appeal.

The petition from Andover also subtly asserts the innocence of the accused, stating they “think we have sufficient grounds to make such a plea for them.” This is a notable, though cautious, challenge to the prevailing assumptions fueling the trials.

The continued requirement of recognizances for accused individuals demonstrates that the legal mechanisms for pursuing witchcraft accusations remain active, even with the special court disbanded.

The geographic scope of the petitions—reaching from Andover to Boston—highlights the widespread impact of the trials beyond Salem Village itself.

The pleas for release based on the onset of winter reflect a growing concern for basic human welfare, suggesting a shift in focus from spectral evidence to practical considerations.

newspapers.lede: The dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer last month has left a wake of uncertainty and hardship in its path. While the executions have ceased, the legal and financial fallout continues to ripple through the community. Today, George Herrick, who served as a marshal and deputy sheriff throughout the trials, formally petitioned Governor William Phips for financial aid. Herrick details how his duties—serving warrants, transporting prisoners, and attending countless examinations—have left him impoverished and unable to provide for his family. He writes, “Nesessity hath forcd me to lay downe my Place and must Certainly come to Wante if not in some Measure suplyd.” This plea highlights the economic toll the trials took not just on the accused and their families, but on those employed by the court itself. Alongside this, legal documents show that recognizances—essentially promises to appear in court—were issued for Bethia Carter and Jane Lilly, both accused of witchcraft. These recognizances, with bonds of two hundred pounds each, demonstrate an attempt to maintain some semblance of legal process even as doubts about the trials grow.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the aftermath of the trials continues as individuals seek financial assistance and legal safeguards are put in place for those accused. George Herrick, a former court officer, petitions the government for support, while recognizances are issued to ensure the appearance of accused women at future court proceedings. These actions reveal a system grappling with its recent actions and attempting to manage the ongoing consequences.

These actions reveal a system grappling with its recent actions and attempting to manage the ongoing consequences.

As detailed in the lede, George Herrick has petitioned the government for financial assistance, claiming he is unable to support his family due to his service during the trials, having spent nearly a year serving warrants and transporting prisoners.

Recognizances were issued today for Bethia Carter of Wooburn, and Jane Lilly of Reading, requiring them to appear at the next court session. John Pierson, George Lilly, and Ruben Lilly each pledged two hundred pounds as surety for Jane Lilly’s appearance.

Similar recognizance bonds were also established for Bethia Carter, with John Pierson and George Lilly guaranteeing her appearance in court.

These recognizances suggest a continued, if cautious, effort to pursue legal proceedings against those accused, even after the main court has been suspended.

The petitions and recognizances indicate a shifting focus from conviction to managing the aftermath of the trials, including addressing financial hardship and ensuring future legal compliance.

The recognizance documents specify that these legal obligations fall under the authority of King William and Queen Mary, reinforcing the Crown’s ultimate oversight of justice in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

newspapers.lede: The dismantling of the special Court of Oyer and Terminer last month has not resolved all matters surrounding the accusations of witchcraft. Today, Samuel Ray of Salem posted a fifty-pound bond guaranteeing the appearance of Dorothy Good at the next session of the court. This “Recognizance for Dorothy Good,” as the document reads, essentially holds her financially responsible until a final judgment is reached. While out on bail, Dorothy remains legally bound to the court’s decision. But the most heartbreaking news concerns her young daughter, Dorcas. Dorcas Good, just four or five years old at the time of her arrest in March, endured nearly eight months chained in a Boston jail. Her father’s petition reveals the extent of the trauma, stating she “hath ever since been very chargeable, having little or no reason to govern herself.” The long-term psychological damage inflicted on this child is a stark reminder of the trials' devastating impact on the most vulnerable.
newspapers.summary: Today brings news of the legal aftermath of the trials. While the court has been dissolved for over a month, the financial burdens of imprisonment and legal bonds remain, and we learn more about the lasting damage inflicted upon even the youngest accused, Dorcas Good. This highlights the human cost extending far beyond the executions.

This bond ensures she will face judgment when the courts reconvene, even though the specific charges against her have not been dismissed.

The condition of Dorcas Good, the young daughter of Dorothy Good, is now publicly known. Held for months in chains, she is described as permanently psychologically damaged, unable to function normally after her imprisonment.

The financial implications of the trials continue to linger. Posting a bond of fifty pounds was a significant sum in 1692, demonstrating the economic strain the legal proceedings placed on individuals and the community.

The case of Dorcas Good underscores the profound and lasting trauma inflicted on accused individuals, particularly children, extending far beyond any immediate legal outcome.

Though the peak of the accusations has passed, legal processes are still unfolding, with bonds being posted and future court appearances anticipated—a sign that the community is far from fully resolving the crisis.

newspapers.lede: After months of trials and escalating accusations, the Massachusetts Bay Colony is attempting to establish a clearer legal framework for addressing witchcraft. This Sunday, December fourteenth, the General Court passed “A Bill against Conjuration, Witchcraft and Dealing with Evil and Wicked Spirits.” The bill, as it’s formally titled, outlines specific offenses—conjuring spirits, disturbing graves, and practicing witchcraft that harms others—and prescribes the death penalty for those “duly and lawfully convicted.” The language of the bill is stark. It declares that anyone found guilty “shall suffer pains of death as a Felon or Felons.” This isn’t a cessation of concern, but an attempt to codify the response. Simultaneously, William Hobbs, previously committed to Boston Goal on suspicion of witchcraft, has secured his release by posting a two-hundred-pound bond. According to the recognizance document, Hobbs must appear before the court at the next session, a standard legal procedure. This combination of legislation and bond suggests a move toward a more predictable, though still severe, legal process.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the crisis. While fear of witchcraft remains, the Massachusetts General Court has formally enacted a new bill concerning its prosecution, and William Hobbs of Topsfield has posted bond guaranteeing his appearance in court. These actions suggest a shift from extraordinary trials to more conventional legal proceedings.

Today marks a turning point in the crisis. While fear of witchcraft remains, the Massachusetts General Court has formally enacted a new bill concerning its prosecution, and William Hobbs of Topsfield has posted bond guaranteeing his appearance in court. These actions suggest a shift from extraordinary trials to more conventional legal proceedings.

It declares that anyone found guilty “shall suffer pains of death as a Felon or Felons.” This isn’t a cessation of concern, but an attempt to codify the response. The Massachusetts General Court today formalized a new law concerning witchcraft, outlining specific offenses and mandating the death penalty for conviction, as the legislative record shows. This bill aims to provide a more defined legal basis for prosecuting those accused of witchcraft, though the penalties remain extraordinarily harsh.

William Hobbs of Topsfield was released from Boston Goal after posting a two-hundred-pound bond, guaranteeing his appearance at the next court session. This suggests a shift from imprisonment without trial to a more standard legal process, albeit within the context of the ongoing witch hunt.

The new bill specifically addresses not only the practice of witchcraft but also related acts like grave robbing and the consultation of evil spirits. This broadened scope indicates a desire to address the perceived roots of witchcraft, not just its manifestations.

Although the Court of Assistants has been dissolved and the special commission disbanded, the passage of this bill confirms that the authorities still view witchcraft as a serious threat requiring legal intervention.

The recognizance for William Hobbs was signed by Justices James Russell and Samuel Heyman, indicating continued oversight from members of the Governor’s Council. This highlights that even as the extraordinary trials have ended, established authorities remain involved in the prosecution of suspected witches.

The language of the bill, with phrases like “Invocation or Conjuration of any evil and wicked Spirit,” reflects the deep-seated religious beliefs and fears that fueled the witch hunt throughout 1692.

newspapers.lede: The focus today is on securing the future testimony of Margaret Prince, a widow accused of witchcraft. Officials are clearly concerned that, with the Court of Oyer and Terminer now dissolved, accused individuals might flee before they can be fully tried. This Monday, December 15th, Thomas Prince and Richard Tarr both posted a bond of two hundred pounds—a substantial sum—guaranteeing Margaret Prince’s appearance at the next court session. The official record, as the magistrate’s record reads, details the legal language of this “Recognizance,” stating that if she fails to appear, her accusers can seize the assets of Prince and Tarr. The document itself is quite a mouthful, stating, “Whereas Margarett Prince Widdow Of Gloster aboves'd: is suspected & Accused of Committing Acts of Witchcrafts.” This legal maneuver highlights the ongoing effort to maintain some control over the situation even as the immediate crisis abates, and reveals the lengths to which the authorities will go to ensure witnesses – or accused – appear in court.

newspapers.lede: The legal proceedings may have paused, but the shadow of accusation hasn’t fully lifted from Salem Village. This Saturday, John Osgood and Deacon John Frie formally entered into a recognizance – a financial guarantee – to ensure Mary Osgood, John’s wife, will appear before the justices if required. The document, recorded by Sheriff George Corwin, details a bond of two hundred pounds, to be levied on their property should Mary fail to appear. The legal language is precise, stating that Mary “is Suspected & Accused of #[Witchcraft] Committing Divers Acts of Witchcrafts.” As the magistrate’s record reads, the recognizance is conditioned on Mary “mak[ing] her Personall Appearance before the Justices” at the next session of the court. While the Court of Oyer and Terminer was dissolved in November, this bond suggests authorities still believe there may be a need to revisit the charges against her, or at least maintain the possibility of future legal action. It’s a clear indication that the effects of the trials continue to ripple through the community.

newspapers.lede: The pace of events has slowed considerably in the wake of the court’s dissolution last month, but legal proceedings continue. This Sunday, Frances Hutchins of Haverhill was released on recognizance—essentially a promise to appear in court—after posting a two-hundred-pound bond. Samuel Hutchins and Joseph Kingsbury, both described as husbandmen, pledged their property as surety for her appearance. The official record, as the magistrate’s record reads, details the terms of this agreement: “Whereas francess Hutchens Widdow of Haverell afores’d is Suspected of and Accused of Committing Divers Acts of Witchcrafts If therefore the Said frances Hutchens afores’d: Shall & do make her Personall Appearance…” This isn’t an acquittal, but a continuation of legal process, suggesting the authorities are attempting to manage existing accusations rather than pursue new ones with the same fervor as before. It’s a small step, but notable given the extraordinary events of the past year, and a clear indication that the legal focus is now on securing appearances at future court sessions.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Frances Hutchins secured her release from prison with a financial bond, acknowledging potential legal consequences if she fails to appear at the next court session. This action signals a shift from arrests and examinations to legal procedures, as the court attempts to manage remaining accusations.

The recognizance for Frances Hutchins is a formal legal document, demonstrating a shift towards standard court procedure rather than the earlier, more ad hoc examinations.

No new examinations were conducted today, continuing the trend since the court was suspended in late October. This suggests a deliberate pause in actively seeking out new accusations.

While legal proceedings continue for those already accused, like Frances Hutchins, there are no reports of fresh arrests or complaints filed today in Salem Village.

The financial bond required of Hutchins and her sponsors represents a significant sum for the time, indicating the seriousness with which the authorities still view the charges against her.

The quiet of this Sunday stands in stark contrast to the frenzied activity of earlier months, signaling a possible turning point in the crisis, though legal matters are far from resolved for those still accused.

newspapers.lede: The wheels of justice, or what remains of them, are turning once more. Even as the special Court of Oyer and Terminer that oversaw the recent trials has been effectively dismantled, the colonial government is moving forward with a planned session of the Court of Assizes and General Goal Delivery, scheduled to begin in Salem on January third. Today, warrants were issued from Boston, dispatched to towns throughout Essex County – Lynn, Ipswich, Salem itself, Gloucester, Marblehead, Topsfield, Beverly, Haverhill, Andover, and Newbury – instructing local officials to assemble townspeople and select jurors. These warrants, signed by court clerk Jona’ Elatson, detail the qualifications for jurors: they must be freeholders with either a substantial real estate or personal estate. As one warrant to the town of Ipswich reads, officials are to choose “eleven good & lawfull men… each whereof to have a reall estate of fourty shillings… or a personall Estate of fifty pounds.” This is a clear effort to return to a more conventional legal process, though the shadow of the recent trials undoubtedly hangs over these preparations.
newspapers.summary: Today, December twenty-third, 1692, the government is preparing for a new session of the Court of Assizes, despite its recent dissolution due to growing doubts about the trials. Warrants have been sent to towns across Essex County, ordering them to select and send jurors to Salem for the January session. This signals an attempt to re-establish legal order after a period of intense upheaval.

Today, December twenty-third, 1692, the government is preparing for a new session of the Court of Assizes, despite its recent dissolution due to growing doubts about the trials. Warrants have been sent to towns across Essex County, ordering them to select and send jurors to Salem for the January session. This signals an attempt to re-establish legal order after a period of intense upheaval.

The wheels of justice, or what remains of them, are turning once more. Even as the special Court of Oyer and Terminer that oversaw the recent trials has been effectively dismantled, the colonial government is moving forward with a planned session of the Court of Assizes and General Goal Delivery, scheduled to begin in Salem on January third. Today, warrants were issued from Boston, dispatched to towns throughout Essex County – Lynn, Ipswich, Salem itself, Gloucester, Marblehead, Topsfield, Beverly, Haverhill, Andover, and Newbury – instructing local officials to assemble townspeople and select jurors. These warrants, signed by court clerk Jona’ Elatson, detail the qualifications for jurors: they must be freeholders with either a substantial real estate or personal estate. As one warrant to the town of Ipswich reads, officials are to choose “eleven good & lawfull men… each whereof to have a reall estate of fourty shillings… or a personall Estate of fifty pounds.” This is a clear effort to return to a more conventional legal process, though the shadow of the recent trials undoubtedly hangs over these preparations.

As detailed in the lede, warrants were sent today to eleven towns across Essex County – Lynn, Ipswich, Salem, Gloucester, Marblehead, Topsfield, Beverly, Haverhill, Andover, and Newbury – directing them to select jurors for the Court of Assizes and General Goal Delivery, set to convene in Salem on January third. The warrants specify property requirements for potential jurors.

The warrants consistently require towns to choose men who possess either a real estate holding of forty shillings annually, or a personal estate valued at fifty pounds. This suggests a desire to ensure jurors are men of some means and standing in the community.

The number of jurors requested from each town varies, ranging from three in Topsfield and Gloucester to nine in Newbury, reflecting the relative size and population of each community.

The timing of these warrants, just weeks after the effective end of the Court of Oyer and Terminer, suggests a deliberate attempt to reassert legal authority and demonstrate that the government is still functioning.

The Court of Assizes and General Goal Delivery was a regular part of the colonial legal system, distinct from the special court appointed to handle the witchcraft accusations, indicating a move towards normalcy.

It remains to be seen whether the new court will address the cases of those still imprisoned on suspicion of witchcraft, or if it will focus on more conventional criminal matters.

The continued legal activity, even in a modified form, offers a small measure of stability to a community deeply shaken by the events of the past year.

newspapers.lede: After months of heightened fear and intense accusations, the wheels of justice are slowly turning once more. Today, the towns of Lynn and Newbury both completed the selection of grand jurors and trial jurors for the court scheduled to convene in Salem on January third. Samuel Ingals, the constable of Lynn, reported that Robert Potter Senior and Benjamin Rednap were chosen for the grand jury, while Cornet Johnson, Lieutenant Person, John Witt, and Benjamin Collins will serve on the trial jury. According to the official return from Newbury, signed by constable Samuel Hills, Thomas Hale, Richard Browne, and Richard Bartlett Senior will comprise their grand jury, with Sergeant John Kent, Sergeant Joseph Little, Sergeant John Hale, John Emery Junior, Benajah Titcomb, and John Ordway selected for trial duty. As the magistrate’s record reads from Newbury, at a town meeting convened by the constable, these men were “chosen to Serve as jurors at a Court of assises & Gen’ll Goale Delivery.” This methodical selection process represents a significant shift from the earlier, more chaotic proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today brings news not of accusations or examinations, but of legal preparations for the upcoming court session in January. Towns across Essex County are selecting jurors, signaling a return to formal legal processes after the controversial Court of Oyer and Terminer was dissolved in late October. This is a crucial step toward reestablishing order and addressing the lingering effects of the witch trials.

This is a crucial step toward reestablishing order and addressing the lingering effects of the witch trials.

The selection of jurors in both Lynn and Newbury demonstrates a concerted effort to reestablish a formal legal process following the dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer. This court, responsible for prosecuting those accused of witchcraft, was widely criticized for its reliance on spectral evidence and questionable procedures.

The jury lists from both towns include prominent citizens – constables, sergeants, and respected landowners – indicating a desire to restore public confidence in the legal system.

The upcoming court session in January will likely address not only outstanding witchcraft cases, but also other criminal matters, as indicated by the phrasing “assises & Gen’ll Goale Delivery” in the Newbury return.

The careful documentation of the jury selection process, with official returns signed by town constables, highlights a commitment to transparency and accountability.

While the specter of the recent trials still hangs over the county, these developments suggest a move toward a more measured and conventional approach to justice.

newspapers.lede: The work of rebuilding legal order continues as towns across the province assemble and select jurors. Today, reports came in from Marblehead, Topsfield, Andover, and Wenham, each town naming men to serve on both the grand jury and the jury of trials. The process, while routine, feels particularly significant after the extraordinary court that dissolved in November. As the magistrate’s record from Andover reads, town officials are acting “In Obediance unto this Above Riten worant,” attempting to resume normal governance. Constable Ephraim Foster confirms they have “Asembled the free holders & other the Inhabitance of Our Town Togither” to fulfill their civic duty. This methodical selection of jurors signals a clear intention to move forward with established legal procedures, even as the memory of the recent trials remains fresh in everyone’s minds. The reports confirm that legal mechanisms, though strained, are still functioning in the wake of the crisis.
newspapers.summary: Today, December 30th, 1692, towns across Massachusetts Bay are fulfilling legal obligations by selecting jurors for upcoming court sessions. These returns demonstrate a return to normal legal processes after the extraordinary trials concluded last spring, though the shadow of the witch hunt still lingers over the region.

Today, December 30th, 1692, towns across Massachusetts Bay are fulfilling legal obligations by selecting jurors for upcoming court sessions. These returns demonstrate a return to normal legal processes after the extraordinary trials concluded last spring, though the shadow of the witch hunt still lingers over the region.

The work of rebuilding legal order continues as towns across the province assemble and select jurors. Today, reports came in from Marblehead, Topsfield, Andover, and Wenham, each town naming men to serve on both the grand jury and the jury of trials. The process, while routine, feels particularly significant after the extraordinary court that dissolved in November. As the magistrate’s record from Andover reads, town officials are acting “In Obediance unto this Above Riten worant,” attempting to resume normal governance. Constable Ephraim Foster confirms they have “Asembled the free holders & other the Inhabitance of Our Town Togither” to fulfill their civic duty. This methodical selection of jurors signals a clear intention to move forward with established legal procedures, even as the memory of the recent trials remains fresh in everyone’s minds. The reports confirm that legal mechanisms, though strained, are still functioning in the wake of the crisis.

Today’s reports from multiple towns – Marblehead, Topsfield, Andover, and Wenham – document the formal selection of grand jurors and trial jurors, demonstrating a return to standard legal procedure following the disbanding of the special court last month.

In Marblehead, William Woods and Richard Reed were named for the grand jury, while William Beale and Richard Groce will serve on the jury of trials, as documented in the town’s return.

Topsfield chose Jacob Towne for the grand jury and John Prichett and Corporal John Curtiow for the trial jury, according to the town’s recorder, Ephraim Dorman.

Andover selected Samuel Marble and Henry Holt, Senior, to serve on the jury of trials, fulfilling the requirements of the warrant as attested by Constable Ephraim Foster.

Wenham named Richard Hutten and Samuel Kimball to the grand jury, and James Friend and John Abbey to the jury of trials, as recorded by Constable William Fairfield.

These returns, though seemingly mundane, are crucial evidence that the legal framework of Massachusetts Bay is being restored after the turbulent events of 1692, offering a glimpse of hope for a more stable future.

newspapers.lede: The year ends not with a cessation of legal proceedings, but a shift in focus. Though the extraordinary court convened to try the accused witches has been dissolved, the wheels of justice continue to turn. Today, towns across the county are officially naming jurors for the Court of Assizes and General Goal Delivery, scheduled to convene here in Salem on January third. According to the return from the town of Rowley, Constable Robert Greenough reports that “sir In pursuance of a Warrant… wee have made Choyse of these men following,” including Captain Joseph Boynton and James Dickison for the grand jury. This signals a return to standard legal procedure, even as the recent trials cast a long shadow. The constable’s report also details jurors selected for the trial jury, John Pickard and John Platts. Meanwhile, a mittimus – a warrant for transfer – has been issued for five women, Lydia Dustin, Sarah Dustin, Mary Colson, Elizabeth Colson, and Sarah Cole, held in Cambridge, to be brought to Salem for the upcoming session.
newspapers.summary: Today, December thirty-first, sixteen ninety-two, towns across Essex County are fulfilling their legal obligations by selecting jurors for the upcoming court session at Salem. While the special Court of Oyer and Terminer has been dissolved, routine legal business continues, and preparations are underway for the Court of Assizes and General Goal Delivery. A few individuals remain in custody pending trial.

A few individuals remain in custody pending trial.

Though the extraordinary court convened to try the accused witches has been dissolved, the wheels of justice continue to turn. Today, towns across the county are officially naming jurors for the Court of Assizes and General Goal Delivery, scheduled to convene here in Salem on January third. According to the return from the town of Rowley, Constable Robert Greenough reports that “sir In pursuance of a Warrant… wee have made Choyse of these men following,” including Captain Joseph Boynton and James Dickison for the grand jury. This signals a return to standard legal procedure, even as the recent trials cast a long shadow. The constable’s report also details jurors selected for the trial jury, John Pickard and John Platts. Meanwhile, a mittimus – a warrant for transfer – has been issued for five women, Lydia Dustin, Sarah Dustin, Mary Colson, Elizabeth Colson, and Sarah Cole, held in Cambridge, to be brought to Salem for the upcoming session.

As the lede explained, towns throughout Essex County are completing the vital task of selecting jurors for the Court of Assizes and General Goal Delivery, which will meet here in Salem on January third. Returns have been filed from Gloucester, Haverhill, and Rowley, detailing the names of those chosen to serve on both the grand jury and the jury of trials.

Five women – Lydia Dustin, Sarah Dustin, Mary Colson, Elizabeth Colson, and Sarah Cole – are being transferred from the Cambridge jail to Salem, under the authority of a mittimus issued today. They will await trial at the upcoming Court of Assizes.

John Alden, a mariner from Boston who previously escaped from jail while accused of witchcraft, has entered into a recognizance – a legal agreement – securing his future appearance in court. He, along with two shopkeepers, have pledged two hundred pounds if he fails to appear.

The returns from each town follow a similar pattern, demonstrating the standardized legal processes being followed throughout the county. Each constable confirms having assembled the freeholders and inhabitants to fulfill the warrant’s requirements.

While the special court focused on spectral evidence is no longer in session, the Court of Assizes will address a range of legal matters, including those still pending from the recent cases, and new offenses that may arise.

newspapers.lede: The new year begins not with celebration, but with the slow, painful work of reckoning. The Court of Oyer and Terminer has been dissolved since late October, but the wounds it inflicted remain raw. Abigail Faulkner, convicted last September but saved by her pregnancy, recently gave birth to a daughter she named Ammi Ruhamah – Hebrew for “my people had mercy.” It’s a poignant statement, a plea for the grace she and her family desperately need. As she petitioned Governor Phips, she explained her husband was an invalid and her children had ‘little or nothing to subsist on.’ Phips granted her reprieve, but life will be a struggle. The act of naming her child a testament to divine mercy speaks volumes about the desperation and hope that linger in the wake of the trials. While some find relief, others are left to grapple with the consequences of their actions, or the actions taken in their name.
newspapers.summary: Today we look at the aftermath of the Salem trials. While the court has been dissolved for months, the consequences linger. Some, like Abigail Faulkner, have been spared execution but face hardship. Others, like Ann Putnam Sr., carry their convictions to the grave. And the ripple effects continue to shape the lives of those involved – and those who remain silent.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today we look at the aftermath of the Salem trials. While the court has been dissolved for months, the consequences linger. Some, like Abigail Faulkner, have been spared execution but face hardship. Others, like Ann Putnam Sr., carry their convictions to the grave. And the ripple effects continue to shape the lives of those involved – and those who remain silent.

The new year begins not with celebration, but with the slow, painful work of reckoning. The Court of Oyer and Terminer has been dissolved since late October, but the wounds it inflicted remain raw. Abigail Faulkner, convicted last September but saved by her pregnancy, recently gave birth to a daughter she named Ammi Ruhamah – Hebrew for “my people had mercy.” It’s a poignant statement, a plea for the grace she and her family desperately need. As she petitioned Governor Phips, she explained her husband was an invalid and her children had ‘little or nothing to subsist on.’ Phips granted her reprieve, but life will be a struggle. The act of naming her child a testament to divine mercy speaks volumes about the desperation and hope that linger in the wake of the trials. While some find relief, others are left to grapple with the consequences of their actions, or the actions taken in their name.

Abigail Faulkner was officially pardoned thanks to her pregnancy, giving birth to her seventh child, Ammi Ruhamah, on March 20th. She named the child as a statement of gratitude for the mercy shown to her and her family, recognizing that her unborn child saved her life from the gallows.

Ann Putnam Sr. died this week, seven years after the trials, never offering a public apology for her role in the accusations. She reportedly believed Rebecca Nurse's specter killed her babies, a conviction she held until her death, and her passing leaves her children orphaned in a village that blames their parents.

Constable Joseph Herrick, who executed the first arrest warrants, has seemingly become a skeptic. He now returns to more mundane duties, like settling disputes over livestock, having witnessed the devastating consequences of the accusations he once carried out.

Cotton Mather continues to defend the trials in his writings, despite growing criticism. His book, ‘Wonders of the Invisible World,’ seeks to justify the proceedings as necessary to combat Satan, a position increasingly at odds with the changing sentiments of the colony.

Dorothy Good, imprisoned as a young child, has been permanently damaged by her ordeal. She requires constant care and is considered mentally unstable, a tragic consequence of the harsh conditions she endured in the Salem jail.

Dr. William Griggs, whose initial diagnosis sparked the crisis, died in 1698 without publicly reflecting on his role in the events. We have no record of whether he reconsidered his assessment of the afflicted girls or the deaths that followed.

Elizabeth Proctor, also saved by her pregnancy, gave birth to a son named John Proctor III on January 27th while still imprisoned. She faces a legal battle to reclaim her husband’s confiscated estate, a testament to the enduring hardships faced by those impacted by the trials.

Families of the executed reportedly retrieved their loved ones' bodies from shallow graves at Proctor’s Ledge at night, denying them the proper burial rites afforded to even the poorest members of the community. This act of defiance and grief underscores the profound injustice of the executions.

newspapers.lede: The legal wheels continue to turn, though slowly, even after the abrupt end of the trials last fall. This Friday, the town of Beverly formally returned its list of jurors to the court, preparing for potential future sessions. According to the official record, John Louit senior and Robert Cue were chosen for the grand jury, while Samuel Morgan and Edmond Gale will serve on the jury of trials. Constable John Conant signed the return, assuring the authorities that these men had been duly summoned. This is a procedural step, and does not indicate any immediate resumption of trials. However, it shows a continued – if tentative – effort to maintain some semblance of legal order. Meanwhile, Reverend John Higginson of Marblehead and Reverend Samuel Cheever offered testimonies concerning Sarah Buckley, stating they had "never observed…any thing that was unsuitable to a Conversation becoming the Gospel." This suggests a growing desire among some to review the accusations and consider the character of those accused.
newspapers.summary: Today brings news from Beverly, confirming the selection of jurors for upcoming court sessions, even as the special Court of Oyer and Terminer remains dissolved. Also, two ministers offer character references for Sarah Buckley, bolstering claims of her piety and good standing in the community – a small sign of efforts to reassess past judgments.
