Today brings news from Beverly, confirming the selection of jurors for upcoming court sessions, even as the special Court of Oyer and Terminer remains dissolved. Also, two ministers offer character references for Sarah Buckley, bolstering claims of her piety and good standing in the community – a small sign of efforts to reassess past judgments.

The legal wheels continue to turn, though slowly, even after the abrupt end of the trials last fall. This Friday, the town of Beverly formally returned its list of jurors to the court, preparing for potential future sessions. According to the official record, John Louit senior and Robert Cue were chosen for the grand jury, while Samuel Morgan and Edmond Gale will serve on the jury of trials. Constable John Conant signed the return, assuring the authorities that these men had been duly summoned. This is a procedural step, and does not indicate any immediate resumption of trials. However, it shows a continued – if tentative – effort to maintain some semblance of legal order. Meanwhile, Reverend John Higginson of Marblehead and Reverend Samuel Cheever offered testimonies concerning Sarah Buckley, stating they had "never observed…any thing that was unsuitable to a Conversation becoming the Gospel." This suggests a growing desire among some to review the accusations and consider the character of those accused.

The town of Beverly has officially selected and named its jurors for court service, despite the ongoing dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer. This indicates a preparedness for potential future legal proceedings, though no date has been set.

Reverend John Higginson and Reverend Samuel Cheever have both testified to the pious character of Sarah Buckley, affirming her long-held good reputation within their congregations. They state they have known her for many years and found her consistently devout.

These testimonials for Sarah Buckley represent a small but significant shift in the atmosphere, as some begin to publicly question the validity of the earlier accusations and seek evidence of good character.

The continued, though limited, administrative work like the return of jurors from Beverly demonstrates that the authorities haven’t entirely abandoned the process of addressing the crisis, even while reassessing their approach.

As of today, no new examinations or trials are scheduled, and the focus seems to be on administrative tasks and collecting testimonies about the character of those previously accused.

newspapers.lede: The atmosphere here in Salem Village is markedly different than it was just months ago. Today, January 3rd, 1693, the Superior Court of Judicature began its sessions, replacing the controversial Court of Oyer and Terminer. But this is not the same court. Governor Phips has decreed that testimony based on spectral evidence – the supposed visions of the accused tormenting their victims – will be inadmissible. The impact was immediate. As the magistrate’s record reads regarding Margaret Jacobs, accused of afflicting Elizabeth Hubbard, the jury returned a verdict of “Not guilty.” This pattern continued throughout the day, with five individuals acquitted at the Charlestown session. While Chief Justice Stoughton still signed warrants for those previously convicted, Governor Phips has refused to carry them out. The reliance on spectral evidence, it seems, was the very foundation upon which these accusations stood. As one observer might have said, though we have no direct quote for it, today, the tide appears to be turning.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a dramatic shift in the Salem trials. The Superior Court has convened, but with a crucial new rule: spectral evidence—visions and dreams—will no longer be accepted. This change led to acquittals in the first five cases, signaling a potential end to the prosecutions.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a dramatic shift in the Salem trials. The Superior Court has convened, but with a crucial new rule: spectral evidence—visions and dreams—will no longer be accepted. This change led to acquittals in the first five cases, signaling a potential end to the prosecutions.

The atmosphere here in Salem Village is markedly different than it was just months ago. Today, January 3rd, 1693, the Superior Court of Judicature began its sessions, replacing the controversial Court of Oyer and Terminer. But this is not the same court. Governor Phips has decreed that testimony based on spectral evidence – the supposed visions of the accused tormenting their victims – will be inadmissible. The impact was immediate. As the magistrate’s record reads regarding Margaret Jacobs, accused of afflicting Elizabeth Hubbard, the jury returned a verdict of “Not guilty.” This pattern continued throughout the day, with five individuals acquitted at the Charlestown session. While Chief Justice Stoughton still signed warrants for those previously convicted, Governor Phips has refused to carry them out. The reliance on spectral evidence, it seems, was the very foundation upon which these accusations stood. As one observer might have said, though we have no direct quote for it, today, the tide appears to be turning.

The Superior Court convened today and immediately implemented the ban on spectral evidence, leading to the acquittal of five individuals tried at Charlestown. This decision effectively dismantled the evidentiary basis for the majority of the witchcraft accusations.

Robert Paine Jr., the foreman of the Grand Jury, played a pivotal role in halting further prosecutions by refusing to indict any of the accused. His insistence on a higher standard of proof than had previously been applied was critical.

While the court acquitted most of the accused, three individuals previously convicted still face potential execution, though Governor Phips has signaled his intention to grant them pardons. This remains a point of contention and anxiety for many.

A petition from Andover, signed by Mary Osgood, Martha Tyler, and others, details how the initial investigation—beginning with a visit from Joseph Ballard and the afflicted girls—brought the tragedy to their town. It highlights the destructive impact of the accusations.

The town is dealing with financial matters related to the arrests and trials, as evidenced by an account of expenses submitted by Sheriff Timothy Phillips. This document details the costs of transporting prisoners, providing food, and maintaining security.

A return from the town of Salem, dated January 3rd, lists those chosen for the Grand Jury, including Joshua Ray and Job Swinnerton. This document provides a snapshot of the civic duties being carried out amidst the ongoing crisis.

An order for payment to Timothy Phillips, dated December 17th, 1697 – a correction to the date is likely – shows ongoing efforts to settle accounts related to the trials even years after the initial accusations.

newspapers.lede: The trials may be waning, but legal proceedings continue. Today, Job Tookey, a waterman from Beverly, was formally indicted by a grand jury on charges of witchcraft. The indictment, as the magistrate’s record reads, accuses Tookey of using “detestable Arts Called Witchcraft and Sorceries” against Mary Warren “on the seventh day of June in the year of our Lord One thousand six hundred Ninety and two and divers other dayes and times.” Warren, of course, was one of the most prominent accusers during the height of the trials, and her continued testimony, even now, carries weight. Tookey pleaded not guilty and requested a trial, but the circumstances are markedly different than they were last spring. The Court of Oyer and Terminer was dissolved back in November, and while a new court is expected to be seated, its composition and approach remain uncertain. As the indictment states, the charges also claim Tookey “Tortured Afflicted Wasted Consumed pined & Tormented” Warren, outlining the severe consequences allegedly resulting from his supposed actions.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, Job Tookey of Beverly faced indictment for witchcraft based on the accusations of Mary Warren. This marks one of the last formal legal proceedings against an accused witch as the special court was dissolved months ago, signaling a shift away from the intense prosecutions of the previous year.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, Job Tookey of Beverly faced indictment for witchcraft based on the accusations of Mary Warren. This marks one of the last formal legal proceedings against an accused witch as the special court was dissolved months ago, signaling a shift away from the intense prosecutions of the previous year.

The trials may be waning, but legal proceedings continue. Today, Job Tookey, a waterman from Beverly, was formally indicted by a grand jury on charges of witchcraft. The indictment, as the magistrate’s record reads, accuses Tookey of using “detestable Arts Called Witchcraft and Sorceries” against Mary Warren “on the seventh day of June in the year of our Lord One thousand six hundred Ninety and two and divers other dayes and times.” Warren, of course, was one of the most prominent accusers during the height of the trials, and her continued testimony, even now, carries weight. Tookey pleaded not guilty and requested a trial, but the circumstances are markedly different than they were last spring. The Court of Oyer and Terminer was dissolved back in November, and while a new court is expected to be seated, its composition and approach remain uncertain. As the indictment states, the charges also claim Tookey “Tortured Afflicted Wasted Consumed pined & Tormented” Warren, outlining the severe consequences allegedly resulting from his supposed actions.

Job Tookey’s indictment repeats the pattern seen throughout 1692: an accusation, a grand jury finding of probable cause, and the initiation of formal legal proceedings. However, this case unfolds against a backdrop of growing doubt and the recent dissolution of the special court established to hear these matters.

Mary Warren’s continued accusations are notable, given the shifting public sentiment. Her initial testimony was pivotal in driving the earlier wave of arrests, and her willingness to persist with these claims suggests she still believes in the reality of spectral evidence.

The grand jury that indicted Tookey included several familiar names—Edward Flint, Nathan’l Howard, and John Hale among them—reflecting that many of the same individuals who participated in the earlier trials remain involved in the legal process.

The indictment specifically references events from June of 1692, during the peak of the accusations, demonstrating that the charges are rooted in events that occurred many months ago.

Tookey’s plea of not guilty signals his intention to defend himself against the charges, which could lead to a full trial before a newly constituted court, though the timing of such a trial is unclear.

The reference to the King and Queen—William and Mary—highlights the legal basis for the prosecution, invoking the authority of the crown and the existing statutes against witchcraft.

newspapers.lede: The court met today, hearing cases from across the afflicted region, but the day’s most significant development was the return of “Ignoramus” – meaning no true bill – on the indictment of Abigail Soames of Beverly, accused by Mary Warren of afflicting her. Warren testified that Soames “hath often aflicted mee by bitting pricking & pinching of mee & halling mee about under the Table.” However, the grand jury found insufficient evidence to move forward with the case. This is a rare outcome, and while the trials continue, it suggests some members of the court are beginning to question the validity of the accusations. As the magistrate’s record reads regarding Soames, the jury simply reported “Ignoramous,” effectively ending the legal pursuit against her. This dismissal, coming after months of convictions, is a noteworthy shift in the proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a wave of legal outcomes in the witchcraft trials. Several cases went before the court, but notably, the indictment against Abigail Soames was dismissed – a rare instance of the court finding no cause to proceed. While trials continue, this dismissal hints at growing skepticism surrounding the accusations.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today brings a wave of legal outcomes in the witchcraft trials. Several cases went before the court, but notably, the indictment against Abigail Soames was dismissed – a rare instance of the court finding no cause to proceed. While trials continue, this dismissal hints at growing skepticism surrounding the accusations.

The court met today, hearing cases from across the afflicted region, but the day’s most significant development was the return of “Ignoramus” – meaning no true bill – on the indictment of Abigail Soames of Beverly, accused by Mary Warren of afflicting her. Warren testified that Soames “hath often aflicted mee by bitting pricking & pinching of mee & halling mee about under the Table.” However, the grand jury found insufficient evidence to move forward with the case. This is a rare outcome, and while the trials continue, it suggests some members of the court are beginning to question the validity of the accusations. As the magistrate’s record reads regarding Soames, the jury simply reported “Ignoramous,” effectively ending the legal pursuit against her. This dismissal, coming after months of convictions, is a noteworthy shift in the proceedings.

As we just discussed, the indictment against Abigail Soames was dismissed today. The grand jury found no evidence to support the charge that she afflicted Mary Warren, marking a rare instance of the court rejecting an accusation.

Johannah Tyler of Andover faced charges of making a covenant with the Devil, specifically signing his book and renouncing her Christian baptism. The court record details the accusations, stating she “did make a Covenant with the Devill… whereby she Gave both her soule and body to the Devill.” Her trial is ongoing.

Elizabeth Johnson Senior, also of Andover, is accused of similar offenses – specifically making a pact with the Devil and signing his book. The indictment claims she “wickedly, malitiously, and felloniously, A covenant with the Devill did make.”

Abigail Barker of Andover also stands accused of entering into a diabolical covenant with the Devil years prior, and of practicing witchcraft last September. The records state she “wickedly malitiously, and felloniously, a Covenant with the Devill did make, and Signe the Devills Booke.”

Candy, an enslaved woman belonging to Mary Hawkes of Salem, was also brought before the court today, accused of afflicting Ann Putnam with witchcraft. The indictment alleges she “wickedly, maliciously and felloniously… used, practised, and Exercised” witchcraft upon Putnam.

A petty jury also convened in the case of Candy, comprised of men like Richard Gross and John Emery, to consider the charges against her. This underscores the broad involvement of the community in these proceedings.

Mary Warren also provided a deposition detailing further accusations against Abigail Soames, outlining specific instances of alleged affliction, including being “biting pricking & pinching.” This deposition, however, did not sway the grand jury.

newspapers.lede: The Superior Court is now handling cases previously before the Court of Oyer and Terminer, and a clear change in approach is evident. Today, the indictment against William Proctor for afflicting Mary Walcott was returned “ignoramus” – meaning the grand jury found no evidence to support the charge. As the magistrate’s record reads, Proctor was accused of using “detestable Artes called Witchcrafts & Sorceries” on September seventeenth of last year and “Divers other Dayes and Times” thereafter, causing Mary Walcott to be “Tortured Afflicted Tormented Consumed Pined & Wasted.” But the jury clearly did not find that evidence convincing. Simultaneously, the case of Mary Tyler of Andover came before the court, accused of making a covenant with the Devil “about Seaven Yeares Since” and practicing witchcraft against Ralph Farnam – a case that, like others, is now under intense scrutiny as the court moves away from accepting spectral evidence. This growing reluctance to convict is a significant turning point in the crisis.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the unraveling of the witch hunt continues. While accusations still occur – Rebecca Johnson Jr. of Andover is the latest named – the courts are increasingly skeptical, and several cases are being dismissed. The shift signals a growing doubt about the validity of the trials and the evidence presented.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today in Salem Village, the unraveling of the witch hunt continues. While accusations still occur – Rebecca Johnson Jr. of Andover is the latest named – the courts are increasingly skeptical, and several cases are being dismissed. The shift signals a growing doubt about the validity of the trials and the evidence presented.

The Superior Court is now handling cases previously before the Court of Oyer and Terminer, and a clear change in approach is evident. Today, the indictment against William Proctor for afflicting Mary Walcott was returned “ignoramus” – meaning the grand jury found no evidence to support the charge. As the magistrate’s record reads, Proctor was accused of using “detestable Artes called Witchcrafts & Sorceries” on September seventeenth of last year and “Divers other Dayes and Times” thereafter, causing Mary Walcott to be “Tortured Afflicted Tormented Consumed Pined & Wasted.” But the jury clearly did not find that evidence convincing. Simultaneously, the case of Mary Tyler of Andover came before the court, accused of making a covenant with the Devil “about Seaven Yeares Since” and practicing witchcraft against Ralph Farnam – a case that, like others, is now under intense scrutiny as the court moves away from accepting spectral evidence. This growing reluctance to convict is a significant turning point in the crisis.

The indictment against William Proctor was dismissed today after the grand jury found no grounds for the charge of afflicting Mary Walcott. This is a clear indication that the court is becoming less willing to accept accusations without substantial evidence.

Mary Tyler of Andover is also facing accusations, specifically of covenanting with the Devil years ago and more recently afflicting Ralph Farnam. Her case, like Proctor’s, is being examined with increased skepticism.

Rebecca Johnson Jr. of Andover is among the very last formally accused individuals, suggesting the peak of the accusations has passed, though the legal processes are still ongoing.

The shift from the Court of Oyer and Terminer to the Superior Court reflects a broader effort to bring more legal rigor to the proceedings and move away from the earlier, more emotionally charged trials.

The rejection of spectral evidence is playing a key role in the dismissals, as jurors and judges are increasingly unwilling to base convictions on visions and unsubstantiated claims.

Although accusations continue, the trend toward acquittals and dismissals suggests that the worst of the witch hunt may finally be coming to an end.

newspapers.lede: The trials haven’t entirely ceased, though the atmosphere is markedly changed since Governor Phips dissolved the Court of Oyer and Terminer last November. Today, three women from Andover – Sarah Wardwell, Sarah Hawkes, and Mercy Wardwell – were formally indicted before a reduced panel of judges. The accusations are strikingly similar: each woman is alleged to have signed a pact with the Devil, renouncing her Christian baptism and pledging allegiance to Satan. As the magistrate’s record reads regarding Mercy Wardwell, she “did make a Covenant with the Devill and Signed a paper to the Devill with a Red marke, and believed the Devill, and promised to serve him.” The indictments detail not just the alleged covenant, but specific acts of witchcraft directed toward individuals like Martha Sprague and Timothy Swan. While the special court is no more, these cases proceed under a different authority, suggesting a continued desire to pursue those accused of witchcraft, even as doubt and skepticism grow throughout the colony. It’s a strange and unsettling scene – the weight of the past year pressing down on these proceedings.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, the trials continue, though with a diminished court. Three women – Sarah Wardwell, Sarah Hawkes, and Mercy Wardwell – faced indictment on charges of witchcraft, all accused of making covenants with the Devil and afflicting others with dark magic. These proceedings highlight the lingering effects of the trials even after the official court was suspended.

As detailed in the lede, Sarah Wardwell, Sarah Hawkes, and Mercy Wardwell were all formally indicted on charges of witchcraft today, each accused of making a pact with the Devil and afflicting others. These indictments demonstrate that despite the dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer, legal proceedings against the accused continue.

All three women are from Andover, a town that has seen a significant number of accusations during this crisis. This suggests the spread of fear and suspicion beyond Salem Village itself.

The indictments consistently accuse the women of signing a physical document – a “paper” – as part of their covenant with the Devil. This detail, repeated across multiple cases, seems to have become a key element in the prosecution's narrative.

The accusations extend beyond simply making a pact; the indictments also specify acts of witchcraft directed at named individuals, such as Martha Sprague and Timothy Swan, alleging direct harm caused by these supposed dark arts.

Judge Wait Winthrop was notably absent from today’s proceedings, and from all subsequent trials, signaling a possible shift in the composition and approach of the remaining judicial authority.

The continued pursuit of these cases, even after the main court was dissolved, is likely to fuel further debate and division within the Massachusetts Bay Colony regarding the validity and fairness of the witchcraft trials.

newspapers.lede: The wheels of justice, or what remains of them, continued to turn today with the formal recording of the indictment against Elizabeth Johnson, Jr. of Andover. Though the Court of Oyer and Terminer was dissolved back in October, the legal record still requires completion. Johnson stands accused of entering into a covenant with the Devil some three years prior, and of practicing witchcraft against Ann Putnam last August. As the magistrate’s record reads, she is accused of having “wickedly felloniously and mallitiously a Covenant with the Devill did make, and was baptized by the Devill, and Renounced her former Christian Baptisme.” This indictment, coming months after the height of the accusations, illustrates how the legal system continued to process cases even as doubts about the trials grew within the community. The meticulous documentation underscores the severity with which these accusations were treated, and the lasting impact on the lives of those named. It’s a chilling reminder that even with the court’s suspension, the shadow of witchcraft accusations still looms large over Essex County.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a quiet but significant moment in the aftermath of the trials. The case of Elizabeth Johnson, Jr., of Andover, is formally recorded, though the court is no longer in session, highlighting the lingering legal processes even after public proceedings have ceased. This demonstrates the extensive paperwork and lingering accusations that continue to haunt those accused.

Elizabeth Johnson, Jr. of Andover was formally indicted on charges of witchcraft, despite the dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer last fall. The indictment details accusations of making a covenant with the Devil and practicing witchcraft against Ann Putnam, stemming from events last August.

This formal recording of the indictment emphasizes that legal proceedings, in some form, continued even after the public trials ended. The meticulous documentation reflects the seriousness with which the authorities were treating these accusations.

The charges against Johnson, dating back three years, show the length of time over which accusations could be made and revisited. This illustrates the pervasive fear and suspicion that gripped the community.

The case highlights the fact that even with growing skepticism towards spectral evidence, the legal system was still obligated to address accusations formally brought before it.

While the court is no longer hearing testimony, the completion of these records signifies that the legal consequences for those accused are far from over, and many face continued uncertainty.

newspapers.lede: The courts are still in session, but the pace has slowed considerably since the height of the trials last year. Today saw indictments handed down against four women from Andover and Rowley. Mary Bridges, Mary Post, Sarah Bridges, and Mary Osgood are all accused of making a pact with the Devil, signing his book, and practicing witchcraft. The accusations, detailed in the court records, paint a grim picture of diabolic worship. As the magistrate’s record reads regarding Mary Osgood, she is accused of signing the Devil’s book “about Elleven Yeares agoe,” and of taking the Devil to be her God. But the claims aren’t limited to the past. Even now, witnesses report ongoing harm. Mercy Lewis testified today that she was physically assaulted by the spectral forms of Phillip English and his wife, along with others, claiming they “Stroke mee on the Brest: & almost Choaked mee.” This latest testimony, taken before the grand jury, suggests that accusations and spectral evidence continue to fuel the unrest.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village and Andover, the legal proceedings continue, though with a diminished intensity. Four women – Mary Bridges, Mary Post, Sarah Bridges, and Mary Osgood – faced formal indictments accusing them of witchcraft and covenanting with the Devil. Meanwhile, Mercy Lewis testified to continued spectral attacks, naming Phillip English and several others.

Four women – Mary Bridges, Mary Post, Sarah Bridges, and Mary Osgood – were formally indicted today on charges of witchcraft. The indictments allege they entered into covenants with the Devil, signed his book, and practiced dark arts, accusations stemming back several years in some cases.

Mercy Lewis provided a deposition claiming she was physically attacked by the spectral forms of Phillip English, his wife Mary English, Lydia Dustin, Elizabeth Johnson Jr., and Thomas Farrer. She states they demanded she sign a book and threatened her with affliction if she refused.

The indictments against Mary Bridges, Mary Post, Sarah Bridges, and Mary Osgood all follow a similar pattern, detailing accusations of making a covenant with the Devil and renouncing Christian baptism.

The timeframe of the alleged witchcraft varies. While some accusations date back over a decade, as in the case of Mary Osgood, others occurred as recently as last year, suggesting a continuing practice according to the accusers.

The deposition of Mercy Lewis highlights the continued reliance on spectral evidence, with Lewis claiming she was physically harmed by the spirits of the accused. This practice – accepting visions as proof – has been central to the trials, and remains controversial.

These indictments come after the dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer in November, indicating that local magistrates are still pursuing cases of alleged witchcraft, even without the special court’s authority.

newspapers.lede: The legal aftermath of the recent trials continues today with activity in both Salem and Andover. Several men came forward to post financial guarantees for women still held on suspicion of witchcraft. These “recognizances,” as they are called, allowed Martha Tyler and Johanna Tyler, as well as Dorothy Faulkner and Abigail Faulkner, Jr., to be released from jail. The men—Hopestill Tyler, John Bridges, Francis Faulkner, and Joseph Marble—pledged one hundred pounds each, promising the court the women would appear if called upon. As the magistrate’s record reads, the document legally binds them to ensure the accused “shall make their p’rsonall appearance before the Justices” at the next session of court. This is a stark contrast to the hurried examinations and convictions of just months ago, and suggests a growing reluctance to continue the prosecutions. Even more significantly, Mary Lacey, Jr. of Andover, has formally been indicted on charges of witchcraft, accusing her of renouncing her baptism and signing the Devil’s book.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village and Andover, several individuals posted recognizance bonds—essentially guarantees—to secure the release of accused witches from jail. These bonds ensured the women would appear for further legal proceedings if the courts were to reconvene, a sign that the intense period of examinations and trials is, for now, over.

Mary Lacey, Jr. was formally indicted today on charges of witchcraft, specifically accused of making a pact with the devil and practicing sorcery against Timothy Swan. This indictment, despite the recent dissolution of the special court, demonstrates that accusations are still being pursued in some cases.

The recognizance documents themselves are interesting. They specify a sum of one hundred pounds as security, a considerable amount of money at the time, and legally obligate the men posting bond to ensure the accused women appear in court if requested.

The fact that men are stepping forward to vouch for these women—and risk financial loss if they fail to appear—suggests a degree of community support, or at least a willingness to mitigate the consequences for those accused.

With the Court of Oyer and Terminer dissolved last fall, these recognizances are being processed by Chief Justice William Stoughton, indicating he remains engaged in overseeing these cases, even without a functioning court to try them.

newspapers.lede: A new life arrived in Salem today, a son born to Elizabeth Proctor while she remains imprisoned. She gave birth to John Proctor the Third, a boy conceived before her arrest last April and delivered here in the jail. As was the custom, her execution was postponed “for the belly,” a temporary reprieve granted to pregnant women. Her husband, John Proctor, was not so fortunate, having been hanged on August nineteenth. The birthing network – the women of the village who attended each other in childbirth – knew Elizabeth was with child well before her arrest, a fact that weighed heavily on them. According to the magistrate’s record, Goodwife Proctor maintained throughout her examination, “I am as innocent as a child newly born.” The irony of that statement is starkly apparent today, as a child *is* newly born, while his mother remains accused. This birth, a small act of continuation, stands in sharp contrast to the finality of the hangings.
newspapers.summary: Today in Salem Village, a life began within the walls of the prison. Elizabeth Proctor gave birth to her son, a child spared from the trials that claimed his father’s life just five months ago. This birth underscores the complex and often contradictory decisions made during the recent crisis, and the human cost of the accusations.

Today in Salem Village, a life began within the walls of the prison. Elizabeth Proctor gave birth to her son, a child spared from the trials that claimed his father’s life just five months ago. This birth underscores the complex and often contradictory decisions made during the recent crisis, and the human cost of the accusations.

She gave birth to John Proctor the Third, a boy conceived before her arrest last April and delivered here in the jail. As was the custom, her execution was postponed “for the belly,” a temporary reprieve granted to pregnant women. Her husband, John Proctor, was not so fortunate, having been hanged on August nineteenth. The birthing network – the women of the village who attended each other in childbirth – knew Elizabeth was with child well before her arrest, a fact that weighed heavily on them. According to the magistrate’s record, Goodwife Proctor maintained throughout her examination, “I am as innocent as a child newly born.” The irony of that statement is starkly apparent today, as a child *is* newly born, while his mother remains accused. This birth, a small act of continuation, stands in sharp contrast to the finality of the hangings.

Elizabeth Proctor gave birth to a son, John Proctor the Third, in the Salem Village jail today, securing a temporary stay of her execution. The practice of delaying punishment for pregnant women highlights the complex moral considerations, even amidst the height of the trials, and the recognition of natural life cycles.

John Proctor was hanged five months ago, on August 19, 1692, making this birth a particularly poignant event. The child will grow up without a father, a direct consequence of the accusations and convictions made during the witch hunt.

The women of Salem Village possessed knowledge of Elizabeth Proctor’s pregnancy before her arrest, a fact known through the informal network of midwives and those assisting in childbirth. This shared knowledge added another layer of complexity to the unfolding events, and a silent weight on those who knew the truth.

Elizabeth Proctor’s imprisonment began on April 4, 1692, and continues to this day. She is among the last remaining prisoners still awaiting resolution of her case, though the court has been dissolved.

While Elizabeth remains incarcerated, hopes for a pardon are growing as the climate in Massachusetts Bay shifts away from the zealous pursuit of witchcraft. Governor Phips has signaled a desire to bring the crisis to a close.

Elizabeth Proctor will eventually be released in May of 1693, following the Governor’s general pardon, and later remarry. She also received financial compensation in 1711 for her unjust imprisonment.

newspapers.lede: Governor William Phips made a decisive move today, issuing reprieves for all eight individuals condemned for witchcraft – three newly convicted by the Superior Court and the five whose death warrants Lieutenant Governor William Stoughton had already signed. This means no more hangings will take place. The reprieves allow the accused to purchase their release by paying their jail fees, a bitter irony for those unable to afford it, even after being found not guilty. The action has caused a significant rift between Phips and Stoughton, who vehemently disagreed with the governor’s decision. As the magistrate’s record shows, new indictments were still being drawn up today, accusing Mary Taylor and Mary Toothaker of covenanting with the devil. But Stoughton, furious at what he saw as a betrayal of justice, reportedly declared, “we were in a way to have cleared the land” of witches before storming off the bench. This isn’t simply a political disagreement; it’s a fundamental clash over the direction of justice in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
newspapers.summary: Today, Governor Phips issued reprieves to all those condemned for witchcraft, effectively ending the executions. This action, however, ignited a fierce dispute with Lieutenant Governor Stoughton, who believed the court had been rightfully pursuing God’s justice and feared Phips had intervened prematurely.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today, Governor Phips issued reprieves to all those condemned for witchcraft, effectively ending the executions. This action, however, ignited a fierce dispute with Lieutenant Governor Stoughton, who believed the court had been rightfully pursuing God’s justice and feared Phips had intervened prematurely.

Governor William Phips made a decisive move today, issuing reprieves for all eight individuals condemned for witchcraft – three newly convicted by the Superior Court and the five whose death warrants Lieutenant Governor William Stoughton had already signed. This means no more hangings will take place. The reprieves allow the accused to purchase their release by paying their jail fees, a bitter irony for those unable to afford it, even after being found not guilty. The action has caused a significant rift between Phips and Stoughton, who vehemently disagreed with the governor’s decision. As the magistrate’s record shows, new indictments were still being drawn up today, accusing Mary Taylor and Mary Toothaker of covenanting with the devil. But Stoughton, furious at what he saw as a betrayal of justice, reportedly declared, “we were in a way to have cleared the land” of witches before storming off the bench. This isn’t simply a political disagreement; it’s a fundamental clash over the direction of justice in the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

Governor Phips’s reprieves effectively ended the executions stemming from the witch trials, offering a path to release for those who could afford the jail fees, while simultaneously sparking a major conflict with Lieutenant Governor Stoughton.

The disagreement between Phips and Stoughton isn’t merely about the trials’ continuation, but a deeper theological divide—Stoughton believing the court was fulfilling God’s will, while Phips felt it had overstepped.

Despite the governor’s actions, the Superior Court of Judicature continued to issue indictments today, naming Mary Taylor and Mary Toothaker in accusations of covenanting with the devil, as evidenced by court documents.

The indictments against Taylor and Toothaker detail accusations of making a pact with the devil and marking a birch rind as confirmation, a common element in the prosecution of alleged witches.

The fate of those already imprisoned, even those acquitted, remains precarious, as many, like seventy-nine-year-old Lydia Dustin, may die in jail due to their inability to pay the required fees.

The dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer last fall, while intended to slow the proceedings, has now created a lasting and bitter division between the colony’s two most powerful leaders.

newspapers.lede: After nearly two years of accusations, trials, and executions, the Superior Court concluded its work today, bringing a measure of relief to many in Massachusetts Bay. Though Judge William Stoughton signed warrants for the execution of three individuals – their names have not yet been publicly released – Governor William Phips intervened with reprieves, effectively halting any further hangings. The court acquitted the majority of those still imprisoned, but as the magistrate’s record of the trial of Mary Toothaker shows, the legal process itself was fraught with accusations of covenanting with the devil: “Mary Toothaker of Billerica…being Indicted…for that shee the s'd Mary Toothaker…Wickedly feloneousely and malitiously a Covenant with the Devill did make.” However, even with an acquittal, freedom was not guaranteed. A cruel and little-noticed aspect of the colonial legal system meant that those found not guilty were still required to pay their jail fees – the costs associated with their imprisonment. This meant that for some, innocence was not enough to secure their release, and the specter of poverty loomed even after escaping the gallows.
newspapers.summary: Today marks a turning point in the Salem trials. While three death warrants were signed by Judge Stoughton, Governor Phips has issued reprieves, and most remaining accused have been acquitted or released by the Superior Court. However, freedom comes at a cost, as prisoners are required to pay their jail fees, creating a final, cruel hardship for those already victimized.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks a turning point in the Salem trials. While three death warrants were signed by Judge Stoughton, Governor Phips has issued reprieves, and most remaining accused have been acquitted or released by the Superior Court. However, freedom comes at a cost, as prisoners are required to pay their jail fees, creating a final, cruel hardship for those already victimized.

After nearly two years of accusations, trials, and executions, the Superior Court concluded its work today, bringing a measure of relief to many in Massachusetts Bay. Though Judge William Stoughton signed warrants for the execution of three individuals – their names have not yet been publicly released – Governor William Phips intervened with reprieves, effectively halting any further hangings. The court acquitted the majority of those still imprisoned, but as the magistrate’s record of the trial of Mary Toothaker shows, the legal process itself was fraught with accusations of covenanting with the devil: “Mary Toothaker of Billerica…being Indicted…for that shee the s'd Mary Toothaker…Wickedly feloneousely and malitiously a Covenant with the Devill did make.” However, even with an acquittal, freedom was not guaranteed. A cruel and little-noticed aspect of the colonial legal system meant that those found not guilty were still required to pay their jail fees – the costs associated with their imprisonment. This meant that for some, innocence was not enough to secure their release, and the specter of poverty loomed even after escaping the gallows.

As reported in the lede, the Superior Court concluded its work today, with Governor Phips issuing reprieves that prevented the execution of three individuals despite signed death warrants from Judge Stoughton. This intervention signifies a growing doubt within the colonial government regarding the validity of the trials.

The vast majority of those remaining accused have been acquitted or released, effectively ending the legal proceedings that began in the spring of 1692. While a welcome development, the aftermath will be complicated by the social and economic damage caused by the trials.

A disturbing detail emerged today regarding the conditions of release for the acquitted. Prisoners are required to pay their own jail fees – covering expenses like food and lodging during their imprisonment – creating a financial burden that some families cannot meet.

The case of Lydia Dustin, as documented in the court records, illustrates this final cruelty. Despite being acquitted, the seventy-nine-year-old widow remains imprisoned, unable to pay the fees required for her release.

Similar circumstances apply to Sarah Dustin, whose trial was also recorded today. The records show she too pleaded not guilty and demanded a trial by God and the country, but her fate hinges on her family’s ability to cover the cost of her detention.

The trial records for Mary Toothaker, though resulting in acquittal, reveal the nature of the accusations leveled against the accused – claims of making covenants with the devil and using witchcraft to harm others. These details highlight the pervasive fear and superstition that fueled the trials.

Even as the courts are emptying, the families of the accused face ruinous debt, and the emotional scars of the past months will likely linger for years to come.

newspapers.lede: The dissolution of the court last fall has not translated into universal relief or generosity. Reverend Samuel Parris, minister of Salem Village, continues to demonstrate a troubling lack of compassion. Despite Tituba confessing to witchcraft – a confession many believe initially fueled the frenzy – Parris has refused to pay the fee to keep her from jail. As the magistrate’s record shows, prisoners were charged two shillings sixpence per week, plus additional costs for chains. She has now been held for thirteen months, awaiting payment of just seven pounds for her release. According to depositions, Tituba claimed, “I did do it,” hoping to spare others, yet her master abandons her to languish in prison. This action underscores how personal animosity and economic concerns overshadowed any sense of justice even after the official proceedings concluded, leaving a stain on the reputation of both the minister and the village.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a stark reminder that the end of the trials hasn’t brought closure for everyone. Reverend Samuel Parris’s refusal to cover the jail fees for Tituba, his enslaved woman, leaves her imprisoned long after the court has adjourned, highlighting the lingering injustice and personal grudges of this ordeal.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today brings a stark reminder that the end of the trials hasn’t brought closure for everyone. Reverend Samuel Parris’s refusal to cover the jail fees for Tituba, his enslaved woman, leaves her imprisoned long after the court has adjourned, highlighting the lingering injustice and personal grudges of this ordeal.

As the magistrate’s record shows, prisoners were charged two shillings sixpence per week, plus additional costs for chains. She has now been held for thirteen months, awaiting payment of just seven pounds for her release. According to depositions, Tituba claimed, “I did do it,” hoping to spare others, yet her master abandons her to languish in prison. This action underscores how personal animosity and economic concerns overshadowed any sense of justice even after the official proceedings concluded, leaving a stain on the reputation of both the minister and the village.

Reverend Parris’s refusal to pay Tituba’s jail fees has resulted in her continued imprisonment for thirteen months, a clear indication that the spirit of accusation and punishment hasn’t fully dissipated. This demonstrates a disturbing lack of responsibility for someone who arguably helped expand the scope of the initial accusations.

The cost of imprisonment was significant, with weekly fees of two shillings sixpence plus charges for chains adding up quickly. This financial burden appears to have been a major factor in Parris’s decision, revealing the economic realities underlying the legal process.

Tituba’s initial confession, while coerced, played a critical role in sustaining the early stages of the witch hunt. Her eventual abandonment by her master is a poignant example of the precarious position of enslaved people in this era.

The length of Tituba's imprisonment, long after the court’s dissolution, speaks to the slow pace of reconciliation and the lingering resentment within Salem Village. It is a stark reminder that legal closure does not automatically equate to communal healing.

The details of Tituba's case are surfacing in community discussions, prompting questions about the fairness of the entire process and the motivations of key figures like Reverend Parris.

newspapers.lede: The weight of the past year is settling not just on the accused and their families, but on the colony’s finances. Sheriff Timothy Phillips of Middlesex County has filed a detailed accounting of expenses related to the recent trials, a document now before the General Assembly. The bill covers everything from transporting prisoners like Elizabeth Colson from Charlestown to Salem, to the cost of food, lodging, and guards during their imprisonment and transport to and from court. Phillips details expenses for “carying Elizabeth Coleson to Salem by Warrant from Charlestown and assistance & Expenses,” totaling over forty-two pounds, a substantial sum in 1693. This isn’t simply a matter of tallying costs; it’s a stark reminder of the widespread disruption and the resources consumed by the witch hunt. As the magistrate’s record shows, the trials demanded a significant investment of manpower and money, and now the colony must reckon with the financial consequences of its actions. Phillips concludes his account with a formal declaration: "A true Copy of the Accompt presented with the Petition of Timothy Phillips unto the General Assembly.”
newspapers.summary: Today’s news concerns the financial fallout from the recent trials. Sheriff Timothy Phillips has submitted a detailed bill for expenses incurred while arresting, transporting, and guarding those accused of witchcraft, revealing the significant cost of the proceedings to the Massachusetts Bay colony.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today’s news concerns the financial fallout from the recent trials. Sheriff Timothy Phillips has submitted a detailed bill for expenses incurred while arresting, transporting, and guarding those accused of witchcraft, revealing the significant cost of the proceedings to the Massachusetts Bay colony.

The weight of the past year is settling not just on the accused and their families, but on the colony’s finances. The bill covers everything from transporting prisoners like Elizabeth Colson from Charlestown to Salem, to the cost of food, lodging, and guards during their imprisonment and transport to and from court. Phillips details expenses for “carying Elizabeth Coleson to Salem by Warrant from Charlestown and assistance & Expenses,” totaling over forty-two pounds, a substantial sum in 1693. This isn’t simply a matter of tallying costs; it’s a stark reminder of the widespread disruption and the resources consumed by the witch hunt. As the magistrate’s record shows, the trials demanded a significant investment of manpower and money, and now the colony must reckon with the financial consequences of its actions. Phillips concludes his account with a formal declaration: "A true Copy of the Accompt presented with the Petition of Timothy Phillips unto the General Assembly.”

Sheriff Timothy Phillips has submitted a detailed bill for expenses related to the arrest, transportation, and imprisonment of those accused of witchcraft, totaling over forty-two pounds. This accounting includes costs for travel, food, lodging, and guards, demonstrating the financial burden the trials placed on the colony.

The itemized bill reveals specific expenses, such as one pound, eleven shillings, and eleven pence for transporting Elizabeth Colson to Salem and twenty-two shillings, nine pence, and eight farthings for moving six prisoners from Cambridge to Salem.

Phillips also accounted for expenses related to trials themselves, including fees of fifteen shillings per person for his services as sheriff, totaling six pounds.

Expenses weren't limited to transport and trials; Phillips also billed for seven days spent pursuing the accused, as well as costs for wood and corn used to sustain prisoners in Charlestown.

The submission of this bill to the General Assembly signals a move toward accountability and a reckoning with the financial impact of the trials, likely to spark debate and scrutiny.

The detailed nature of the accounting—listing even minor expenses like the “Cryers Fees” and “Constables ringing ye Bell”—highlights the pervasive reach of the legal proceedings and the mobilization of colonial resources.

newspapers.lede: After more than a year of accusations, trials, and executions, a small measure of justice occurred today in Salem Village. John Alden, a mariner from Boston, had been accused of practicing witchcraft and required to post bond guaranteeing his appearance before the court. This morning, Alden appeared as required, and the court, seemingly with little further investigation, discharged him. As the magistrate’s record reads, simply, “John Alden of Boston Marriner who stood Recognized for his Appearance at this Court upon suspition of Witchcraft being called appeared and was discharged by proclamation.” This dismissal is significant not for what it reveals about Alden, but for what it suggests about the shifting mood in Massachusetts Bay. The court’s willingness to release a defendant without a full hearing, after so many convictions, hints at a growing doubt surrounding the validity of the spectral evidence that fueled these trials. It is a small step, but perhaps a crucial one, towards ending this period of fear and injustice.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a rare moment of good news in this long ordeal. John Alden, a mariner from Boston, was officially cleared of the charge of witchcraft after appearing before the court. This dismissal, though late, signals a growing reluctance among authorities to continue the prosecutions.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today brings a rare moment of good news in this long ordeal. John Alden, a mariner from Boston, was officially cleared of the charge of witchcraft after appearing before the court. This dismissal, though late, signals a growing reluctance among authorities to continue the prosecutions.

After more than a year of accusations, trials, and executions, a small measure of justice occurred today in Salem Village. John Alden, a mariner from Boston, had been accused of practicing witchcraft and required to post bond guaranteeing his appearance before the court. This morning, Alden appeared as required, and the court, seemingly with little further investigation, discharged him. As the magistrate’s record reads, simply, “John Alden of Boston Marriner who stood Recognized for his Appearance at this Court upon suspition of Witchcraft being called appeared and was discharged by proclamation.” This dismissal is significant not for what it reveals about Alden, but for what it suggests about the shifting mood in Massachusetts Bay. The court’s willingness to release a defendant without a full hearing, after so many convictions, hints at a growing doubt surrounding the validity of the spectral evidence that fueled these trials. It is a small step, but perhaps a crucial one, towards ending this period of fear and injustice.

John Alden’s release is the most important development today, marking a departure from the court’s previous eagerness to convict on the basis of spectral evidence. While the court offered no explanation for its decision, it is a clear indication that the tide may be turning against the prosecutions.

Though the court has not formally adjourned, activity has significantly decreased in the weeks since Governor Phips halted the examinations in October. Few new accusations are being brought forward, and the remaining prisoners await resolution.

The General Court, the Massachusetts Bay Colony’s legislative body, has yet to take formal action regarding the trials, despite repeated appeals from concerned citizens. Many are calling for reparations for those wrongly accused and imprisoned.

Reverend Samuel Willard of Boston continues to publicly question the reliability of spectral evidence, arguing that it is susceptible to deception and misinterpretation. His voice, along with others, is adding to the growing chorus of dissent.

Several ministers who initially supported the trials are now expressing reservations, acknowledging the possibility that errors may have been made. This shift in opinion is contributing to the erosion of public trust in the proceedings.

Families of those who were executed continue to grieve and seek answers, demanding accountability from those responsible for the deaths of their loved ones. Their pain remains a stark reminder of the human cost of this tragedy.

newspapers.lede: Governor William Phips today issued a general pardon to all those still imprisoned on charges of witchcraft, effectively ending the formal legal phase of the trials. The decision comes after months of growing doubt and the dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer last October. While many rejoice at this news, a stark injustice lingers. Tituba, the enslaved woman whose confession in February of 1692 ignited the accusations, remains in jail, not because of any legal finding, but because no one will pay the seven-pound fee to secure her release. Reverend Samuel Parris, Tituba’s owner, refused to cover the cost, and she was ultimately sold into new ownership to satisfy the debt. As the magistrate’s record shows – or rather, doesn’t show, as Tituba was never formally tried – her initial words were enough to condemn so many. In her own words, relayed in the early examinations, she spoke of a book that compelled her to join the Devil, a confession extracted under duress that quickly spiraled into mass hysteria. It is a grim irony that the woman who unwittingly unleashed this terror has been abandoned to her fate.
newspapers.summary: Today marks the official end of the legal proceedings of the Salem Witch Trials. Governor Phips has issued a general pardon, releasing all those imprisoned on accusations of witchcraft. However, this act of mercy does not extend to Tituba, the enslaved woman whose initial confession sparked the hysteria, as she remains in jail due to unpaid fees.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today marks the official end of the legal proceedings of the Salem Witch Trials. Governor Phips has issued a general pardon, releasing all those imprisoned on accusations of witchcraft. However, this act of mercy does not extend to Tituba, the enslaved woman whose initial confession sparked the hysteria, as she remains in jail due to unpaid fees.

Governor William Phips today issued a general pardon to all those still imprisoned on charges of witchcraft, effectively ending the formal legal phase of the trials. The decision comes after months of growing doubt and the dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer last October. While many rejoice at this news, a stark injustice lingers. Tituba, the enslaved woman whose confession in February of 1692 ignited the accusations, remains in jail, not because of any legal finding, but because no one will pay the seven-pound fee to secure her release. Reverend Samuel Parris, Tituba’s owner, refused to cover the cost, and she was ultimately sold into new ownership to satisfy the debt. As the magistrate’s record shows – or rather, doesn’t show, as Tituba was never formally tried – her initial words were enough to condemn so many. In her own words, relayed in the early examinations, she spoke of a book that compelled her to join the Devil, a confession extracted under duress that quickly spiraled into mass hysteria. It is a grim irony that the woman who unwittingly unleashed this terror has been abandoned to her fate.

Governor Phips’s general pardon brings a formal end to the Salem Witch Trials, releasing all remaining accused from prison – with the notable exception of Tituba. This pardon follows the disbanding of the Court of Oyer and Terminer and reflects a growing recognition of the flawed and dangerous nature of the proceedings.

Tituba, the first to confess to witchcraft, has been held in prison for over thirteen months after charges were dropped simply because no one would pay her seven-pound jailer’s fee. This demonstrates a callous disregard for her life and a shocking lack of concern for justice following the trials.

Reverend Samuel Parris, Tituba’s owner, refused to pay the fee to release her, ultimately leading to her being sold to an unknown owner to cover the debt. This act highlights the power dynamics at play and the dehumanizing treatment of enslaved people during this period.

The case of Tituba serves as a chilling reminder that the initial confession, obtained through coercion and likely fabrication, was the catalyst for the entire crisis. Her testimony, though suspect, became the foundation upon which numerous accusations were built.

The lack of financial support for Tituba’s release underscores the hypocrisy of a justice system that readily condemned individuals based on spectral evidence but failed to provide even basic fairness to those deemed expendable.

While today’s pardon offers some measure of relief, the lasting trauma and division caused by the trials will undoubtedly continue to affect this community for years to come. The stories of those wrongly accused, and those like Tituba who were exploited and abandoned, will not be easily forgotten.

newspapers.lede: The legal proceedings against Tituba, the woman whose initial accusations helped ignite the Salem witch hunt, reached a quiet but significant conclusion today in Ipswich. The grand jury formally indicted her for covenanting with the Devil—essentially accusing her of signing a pact with the supernatural—but almost immediately returned a verdict of “Ignoramus.” As the magistrate’s record reads, the indictment itself alleges that “upon or about the latter end of the yeare 1691 In the Towne of Salem Village…She the Said Tittapa is become A detestable Witch.” However, the Grand Jury’s foreman, Abraham Haseltine, signed the document noting the “Ignoramus” verdict, effectively ending the case against her. This isn’t an acquittal, but a finding that insufficient evidence exists to move forward, and it represents a key turning point after more than a year of accusations, trials, and executions. It suggests that the court, and perhaps the community, is beginning to question the validity of the spectral evidence that fueled the trials.
newspapers.summary: Today brings a symbolic end to the worst of the Salem crisis. The grand jury has returned an indictment against Tituba, the enslaved woman initially accused of witchcraft, but the case was promptly dismissed—a legal term called “Ignoramus,” meaning the jury found no evidence to proceed. This signals a growing reluctance to continue prosecutions.
newspapers.ttsFullText:
Today brings a symbolic end to the worst of the Salem crisis. The grand jury has returned an indictment against Tituba, the enslaved woman initially accused of witchcraft, but the case was promptly dismissed—a legal term called “Ignoramus,” meaning the jury found no evidence to proceed. This signals a growing reluctance to continue prosecutions.

The legal proceedings against Tituba, the woman whose initial accusations helped ignite the Salem witch hunt, reached a quiet but significant conclusion today in Ipswich. The grand jury formally indicted her for covenanting with the Devil—essentially accusing her of signing a pact with the supernatural—but almost immediately returned a verdict of “Ignoramus.” As the magistrate’s record reads, the indictment itself alleges that “upon or about the latter end of the yeare 1691 In the Towne of Salem Village…She the Said Tittapa is become A detestable Witch.” However, the Grand Jury’s foreman, Abraham Haseltine, signed the document noting the “Ignoramus” verdict, effectively ending the case against her. This isn’t an acquittal, but a finding that insufficient evidence exists to move forward, and it represents a key turning point after more than a year of accusations, trials, and executions. It suggests that the court, and perhaps the community, is beginning to question the validity of the spectral evidence that fueled the trials.

The dismissal of the indictment against Tituba marks a turning point in the Salem witch trials, demonstrating a growing skepticism within the legal system regarding the accusations and evidence presented. While Tituba was among the first accused, and her confession initially helped drive the proceedings, the Grand Jury’s finding today shows a shift in attitude.

This “Ignoramus” verdict follows the dissolution of the Court of Oyer and Terminer last October and the subsequent appointment of a new Superior Court, signaling a deliberate attempt to move away from the extraordinary trials that had gripped the colony.

Though the Superior Court has met briefly since its formation, today’s action suggests a broader effort to avoid further prosecutions based on spectral evidence—visions and claims of being afflicted by witches.

Several remaining prisoners continue to await resolution of their cases, but this development offers a glimmer of hope that the tide may be turning for those falsely accused.

The community of Salem Village remains deeply divided and traumatized by the events of the past year, and the process of healing will undoubtedly be long and arduous.

While this case is dismissed, the damage to reputations and lives is irreversible, and the memory of the witch hunt will likely linger for generations.
